actions.thought: UTG in a 6-handed game with 9d8d suited connectors. Deep stacks (100BB). Standard UTG opening range does not include 98s; it's too weak and speculative out of position. Folding avoids difficult postflop spots OOP against wide calling/3bet ranges. Preserve stack for better spots early in tournament.

I am in Middle Position with A3o (Ace-Three offsuit). This is the first hand of the tournament, and we are 100 BB deep. A3o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop. In a 6-max game, opening A3o from MP is too loose and generally constitutes a fold in standard GTO charts. Since the UTG player folded, there is no dead money to attack aggressively. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
A8o in the cutoff with two folds before me. This is a standard opening hand in late position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to try to take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

I’m on the button facing a cutoff open to 250 with Kc4d — a weak, easily-dominated offsuit king. Calling 250 into the 400 pot requires ~38.5% equity, which K4o won’t have vs a reasonable cutoff opening range, and there are still two players (SB and BB) yet to act so this often becomes multiway or I’m out of position postflop. A minimum 3-bet as a bluff is too thin here and unnecessary with a 100BB stack so early in the tournament. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2 offsuit. Claude has raised from the Cutoff to 250. Q2o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise. There is no reason to defend this hand as it has very poor post-flop playability and is likely dominated by the opener's range. I will fold.

CO opens to 2.5bb and we’re getting a solid price in the BB (150 to win 400). 98o has reasonable playability (straights/two pair) and defends well versus a standard cutoff range in 6-max. No need to 3-bet this offsuit combo without reads; call and realize equity in position disadvantage with manageable pot size.

Paired flop (TT2) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range and gives us essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no diamond, only weak runner-runner straight possibilities). Leading doesn’t achieve much and check-raising this combo is too low-equity/low-blocker to sustain against continues. Best is to check and proceed mostly fold-versus-cbet, while keeping the option to stab later if Villain checks back.

Paired board (Tc Td 2d) favors the preflop raiser. I have ace high with backdoor diamonds. This is a standard c-bet spot - I can represent Tx, overpairs, and apply pressure. Betting small (~1/3 pot) to continue my aggression.

On T♦ T♣ 2♦ our 9♠8♥ has essentially no made equity, no diamond backdoor flush, and only very thin runner-runner straight outs. Even getting a good price versus the small c-bet (175 into 725), our real equity versus a CO range that heavily connects with paired boards (Tx, overpairs, diamond floats) is too low and we’ll struggle to realize it OOP without credible bluffing/blockers. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit (8s Td). This is a very weak hand to play from early position in a 6-handed game. It is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop out of position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.
78o in middle position is too weak to open. It's a marginal hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position against late position callers. Standard fold.

Holding J♣2♠ in the cutoff with UTG and middle folded. There are three players to act behind (button, SB, BB). J2o is a very weak, poorly playable hand — often dominated, has little postflop playability, and is a bad candidate to open or limp out of position. With healthy 100BB stacks and multiway risk of a 3-bet or being called and out-of-position, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Qc 4h. The action has folded to me. While the button is an ideal position to steal the blinds, Q4o is a very weak hand that does not perform well against calling ranges and is difficult to play post-flop. I will stick to a tighter opening range and fold this hand.

Folded to me in the small blind, I should be opening a wide stealing range because the big blind has to defend out of position and will fold a meaningful portion. Ks6c has decent high-card value, blocks strong Kx that would 3-bet/defend, and plays fine with initiative. I’ll use a standard SB open sizing to apply pressure without bloating the pot too much at 100bb+.

Hand 2, deep stacks ~100BB. J5o is a trash hand with poor playability pre and postflop. Facing a SB raise after folds, which could be a steal, but J5o doesn't defend well. Pot odds are decent (150 to call into 350, ~30% needed), but hand lacks equity, implied odds, or fold equity for a 3-bet. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.
A3o from UTG is too weak to open. It's easily dominated by better aces and plays poorly postflop. Standard fold from early position.

Holding Q♦2♣ in middle position is a very weak hand — poorly connected, offsuit, and often dominated by opening/continuation ranges. I would be out of position to the button and blinds, with no clear post‑flop plan or implied odds to justify calling 100 into a 150 pot. With a healthy 10000 stack early in the tournament, preserving chips for better spots is correct. I won't open‑raise either; folding avoids difficult OOP postflop decisions and keeps my stack for higher EV opportunities.
J2o is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even from the Cutoff, it's a standard fold as it's not worth opening or defending.

Folds to me on the button with KhJs, a strong, playable broadway that performs well in position. Limping gives blinds a cheap look and reduces my fold equity; opening applies pressure and lets me win the blinds frequently while still having good playability when called. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to target the blinds’ wide defending ranges without bloating the pot unnecessarily this deep.

8h4c is a trash hand: offsuit, low, gapped connectors at best but poorly suited for multiway or OOP play. Facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB open), which is a standard sizing from late position with a wide range. As SB, I'm OOP postflop with no initiative. Pot odds are 200 to call into 400 (2:1), but equity vs button range (~25-40% depending on villain) is outweighed by poor playability, reverse implied odds if I hit, and BB still to act. Raising is a bluff with no fold equity blockers and deep stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 2h facing a 2.5x raise from the Button. The pot odds are roughly 27% (150 to call into a total pot of 550). While my hand has some raw equity (~37%) against a wide button range, J2s is very difficult to play out of position deep-stacked (approx. 96 big blinds). The reverse implied odds are significant if I hit top pair with a weak kicker, and realizing equity with a weak flush draw OOP is challenging. Against a 2.5bb open, folding is the standard and prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 100bb effective stacks. T5 offsuit (5d Th) is a weak, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability and no suit or connectivity to justify opening or limping from UTG. There’s no raiser yet, but the correct strategy is to be selective UTG — I should not invest chips in a marginal hand out of position. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot, so fold.

I am holding 8s 3h in middle position. This is an extremely weak starting hand with no high card value or connectivity. In a 6-max tournament, playing hands like 8-3 offsuit from this position is a losing strategy. Standard play is a clear fold to preserve stack for better opportunities.

Folded to us in the cutoff with AJs suited (AcJc), a strong open that plays well in position and benefits from taking down the blinds. I’ll use a standard, consistent open size (2.5x) to apply pressure to BTN/SB and avoid giving great price to defend versus a min-raise.

63o is a trash hand with poor playability, no suit, no connectivity. Facing a cutoff raise from FiveTwo who has been aggressive in recent hands (raised last three), folding is standard on the button deep-stacked. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding 2d 3s in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. FiveTwo in the Cutoff has raised to 250. Although FiveTwo has been active, raising three hands in a row, playing 3-2 offsuit out of position is a losing strategy. The hand has very poor playability and equity. Even if I suspect the opener is wide, this is not a suitable hand for a 3-bet bluff or a call. I will fold and preserve my stack.

T4o is a weak hand to defend against a raise, even though FiveTwo has been active (raising 3 hands in a row). I'm out of position with a hand that doesn't play well postflop. The 150 to call isn't getting good enough odds to justify playing this weak holding. Easy fold.

I am holding King-Deuce offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand to play from early position in a 6-max game. K2o is easily dominated by many hands in my opponents' ranges and lacks post-flop playability. With 100 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

With 6 players and four to act behind (CO/BTN/SB/BB), Q4o is well below a profitable MP opening range. It plays poorly postflop (domination issues, weak equity realization) and invites 3-bets from late position. Given we’ve shown early aggression in prior hands, opponents may respond by defending/3-betting wider, making this even less attractive. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for stronger opens.

Folded to me in cutoff with Qd3d, a suited queen which has decent playability, straight/flush potential, and I have position on the blinds and button. Early in tournament with deep stacks (98BB), stealing blinds is profitable, and this hand is within a loose cutoff opening range for 6-handed play. Standard raise size of 2.5x BB to 250 pressures blinds and builds pot when called.

I hold Td9d on the Button, a premium suited connector that plays excellent in position. Elon raised to 250 from the Cutoff, which signifies a potentially wide range. Folding is too passive for this hand strength and position. A 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, but T9s plays very well post-flop and calling keeps the pot manageable while keeping Elon's range wide. Calling also invites the blinds to enter, offering better pot odds for a drawing hand. Therefore, flat calling to play the pot in position is the optimal strategy.
93o is a very weak hand, especially out of position in a multiway pot. Facing a raise and a call, this hand has poor playability and equity. Easy fold.

I have A♠9♣ in the big blind facing a cutoff open to 250 with the button calling. This is effectively a multiway pot (two opponents), and I’m already getting great pot odds to defend: I need to call 150 more to make it 250 total into a 650 pot (calling price < 20% of the pot). A9o is strong enough to defend here and to realize equity postflop — it plays well on A-high and broadway/connected boards and can pick up backdoor draws. 3-betting to isolate is thin value out of position against two players and would often commit chips with marginal hands; I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop. Plan: call to 250 total; if I hit an ace or strong draw on the flop, proceed aggressively, otherwise proceed cautiously vs heavy action.

I defended preflop to realize equity, so on the 5♣ K♠ 7♠ flop I have A♠9♣ — Ace-high only, no pair, just backdoor possibilities. This is a three‑way pot (CO opened, BTN called) so bluffing/bringing pressure out of position is thin value and I want to control pot size with a marginal holding. Check to see the turn: if an Ace or a spade (turn spade gives me a one-card nut‑flush draw) appears I can take the lead; otherwise I’ll fold to heavy aggression. Checking preserves my stack and keeps future options open.

Flop of 5c Ks 7s completely misses my Qd3d; no pair, no straight draw (gutshot at best unlikely), no flush draw despite suited diamonds. Board is king-high with a spade flush draw, favoring opponents' calling ranges which likely include kings, broadway, suited spades/connectors from BB defense and BTN call. As preflop raiser from CO, my range includes top pair/top kicker and better, but this is pure air in a bloated 800 pot multiway. Deep stacks early (hand 5/150), no need to bluff into two opponents here—BB checked, suggesting weakness, but BTN behind has position and will bet draws/value profitably. Best to check, control pot, realize equity cheaply or fold to aggression without committing further with the worst hand.

I hold Td9d on a 5c Ks 7s flop. This board misses my hand completely, offering no immediate straight or flush draws (as the board is spades and I have diamonds). While I have position and the pre-flop raiser (Elon) has checked, implying potential weakness, it is a multi-way pot involving the Big Blind. The King hits many hands in both opponents' ranges. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little equity if called. Taking a free card allows me to realize any backdoor equity (catching a pair, a diamond for a backdoor flush draw, or straight connecting cards like a 6, 8, or J) without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

Check. I have A♠9♣ — only Ace-high; the 6♣ on the turn kills the backdoor spade possibility I had on the flop, so I no longer have meaningful drawing equity. This is a multiway pot (~800 in the pot, ~9.6k effective stacks) and betting out of position with no pair is thin: it risks a raise or multiple callers and bloats the pot with marginal equity. Checking controls the pot, lets me see a free river (an ace gives me an easy value line) and keeps options to call a small river bet or fold to heavy action. I’ll proceed cautiously and only invest more if I actually improve or get a clear price.

On the turn 6c completing possible club flush draws and adding straight possibilities (45, 89), my Qd3d remains pure air: queen-high with no pair, no straight draw (Q-high straight impossible), no flush draw. Preflop as CO raiser, my range includes Kx, broadway, pairs, suited connectors/draws, which blocks some value but opponents' ranges (BTN call and BB limp/call) hit this board better with Kx, 89 suited spade/club, flush draws, pairs. Multiway pot checked around flop indicates weakness overall, but Minni's BB check doesn't invite bluffs. Pro on BTN has position behind me and will defend profitably. Deep stacks early tournament (hand 5/150, 96BB), no need to bluff into two players with zero equity/showdown value; check to realize equity cheaply on river or fold to aggression, preserving stack for better spots.

On the turn, the board is 5c Ks 7s 6c. The 6c improves my hand slightly, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the 8. Both opponents (BB and CO) have checked to me again, signaling weakness or pot control. While a bet could take down the pot against Ace-highs, the connected board (5-6-7) hits the Big Blind's defending range reasonably well (pairs + draws). Since I have a draw to the nuts (straight) and position, taking the free card to realize my equity is the standard and prudent play. If I hit the 8 on the river, I have a very strong concealed hand. If I miss, I have lost the minimum.

Board: 5♣ K♠ 7♠ 6♣ 8♠. I hold A♠9♣ — I missed: no pair and I do not have a five‑card flush (board has three spades; my A♠ is only the fourth). The river also completes straight possibilities (a 9 or 4 makes a 5‑6‑7‑8‑9 or 4‑5‑6‑7‑8). This is a multiway pot and everyone checked the flop and turn, so my ace‑high has almost no value and very little fold equity as a bluff. Betting here would be thin and likely called by any pair or Kx. Best line is to check and take it down at showdown; if someone then fires a meaningful bet I can fold, but I won’t invest more proactively with just ace‑high.

River board 5c-Ks-7s-6c-8s completes spades flush (Ks-7s-8s) and straights (e.g., 4x9x for 45678, 9x for 56789), pairing no dangers but highly coordinated. Qd3d is queen-high showdown value with no made hand or draw. Multiway pot (me, Pro BTN, Minni BB) checked around flop/turn/river indicates collective weakness: opponents' ranges (BTN flat, BB limp/call) include missed draws (spades, clubs, connectors), medium pairs (88-JJ crushed by Kx/sets), ace-high worse than queen. As preflop raiser who checked twice, my range polarized to nuts or air; checking realizes equity against worse (J-high, missed draws) profitably. Bluffing thin into two players (Pro behind with position) risks calls from Kx/pairs/straights/flushes or Pro squeeze; deep stacks (96BB, hand 5/150) no need to risk 800+ pot with marginal value bet. Check for pot control/showdown maximizes EV early.

The river is the 8s, making the board 5c Ks 7s 6c 8s. This card completes my gutshot, giving me a Ten-high straight (6-7-8-9-T). While the board now has three spades (flush possibility) and a 4-card straight layout (5-6-7-8), both opponents have checked to me on the river (and previous streets), showing significant weakness. It is unlikely they hold a flush, as they likely would have bet or check-raised a strong hand on this scary board. I have the upper end of the straight, beating any bare 9 (which makes a 9-high straight) and crushing top pair (Kings) or two pair. I should bet for value to target Kx hands or split/lower straights. A bet of around half-pot (400) is large enough to get value but small enough to induce a call from a King.

Board: 5♣ K♠ 7♠ 6♣ 8♠. My hand A♠9♣ makes a 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9) using my 9 — this is the best single-card straight on this board. The only realistic hands that beat me are a five-card spade flush (an opponent holding two spades). Pro checked flop and turn and then fired a small 400 into ~1200 on the river, which reads more like a blocking/thin-value bet (Kx, a pair, or missed draws) than a big-value bet for a nut flush. Calling 400 gives me ~25% pot odds (400 to win ~1600) and is +EV versus the likely calling/raising range; raising risks folding out worse hands and could get reraised by the flush. I’ll call the 400 to go to showdown (this call brings my total committed to 650). If faced with a large raise I’ll re-evaluate, but a flat call maximizes value and minimizes risk now.

Facing a 400 bet from Pro on the Button into a 1600 pot (after Minni's call), with my Qd3d for queen-high on a coordinated board (5c-Ks-7s-6c-8s) featuring a completed spade flush and multiple straight possibilities (45678, etc.). Preflop as CO raiser, checked flop/turn/river behind, opponents checked through suggesting caution on scary boards. Pro's river bet after passive play likely represents made hands like KsXs flush, KQ/KJ top pair, 89 straight, or thin value/polarization bluff, while Minni's flat call from BB (loose limp/call range) indicates medium strength like pair (77-99? crushed), Ax high, or sneaky flush. Queen-high has minimal showdown value against calling ranges, crushed by any pair/K/finished draw. Pot odds ~4:1 (400 to 2000) require ~20% equity, but villain calling range assigns <10% to my holdings; folding preserves 96BB deep stack early (hand 5/150) for premium spots without risking 25% pot vs likely better.

4♣4♦ is a reasonable UTG open in 6-max, especially given the table’s generally tight/passive preflop tendencies so far. Raising pressures the blinds, can take down the dead money immediately, and when called we have a clear plan (set-mine, otherwise proceed cautiously on overcard-heavy boards). I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to 250 to keep ranges strong and avoid going multiway too cheaply.

Qd2h is a complete trash hand with no playability, dominated in most scenarios, and zero fold equity. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in previous hands (raising UTG multiple times), this is an easy fold from middle position to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a deepstack tournament.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit in the Cutoff facing a raise to 2.5bb from FiveTwo in UTG. Although FiveTwo has been very active (raising 4 of the last 6 hands), an UTG range is typically stronger, and K7o is easily dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, AK, or even KTs. It plays poorly post-flop and there are players left to act behind me. The most prudent play is to fold.

J3o is a weak hand to call a UTG raise with, even on the button. While I have position, J3o plays poorly postflop and FiveTwo's UTG opening range should be reasonably strong. Not worth 250 chips to defend here.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from my small blind with 9s2s. This is a very weak, low-card suited hand that fares poorly vs an early-position opening range and has bad postflop playability out of position. Calling 200 into a 400 pot would often put me in tough spots (and the BB could still act), while a 3-bet is never realistic with this holding. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

FiveTwo has shown a high frequency of pre-flop aggression, raising in several of the previous hands. Given that I am in the Big Blind and closing the action, I am getting odds of approximately 2.7 to 1 (150 to call into a 400 pot). While Ten-Six suited is marginal against a standard UTG range, it is strong enough to defend against an opponent who appears to be opening a wide range of hands. I will call to see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten, although my kicker (Six) is weak. On this board (5d-Td-4s), there are potential straight and flush draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), the standard play is to check to them. This allows me to see how they react to the board and gives them an opportunity to continuation bet with their entire range, including hands I currently beat.

We flopped a set (trips 4s) on a fairly dynamic board with two diamonds and plenty of pair+draw / straight-draw holdings in the BB’s range (Tx, 5x, diamond draws, 76/63, etc.). After Flash checks, we should start building the pot and deny equity—this is a spot to bet for value and protection rather than slowplay. A ~60–65% pot c-bet charges draws while still getting called by many worse hands.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten, although my Six kicker is weak. FiveTwo, who has been consistently aggressive pre-flop, has made a continuation bet of 350 into a 550 pot. Given my top pair, folding at this stage is too conservative, as I am ahead of many hands in an aggressive opponent's range, including pure bluffs and drawing hands like flush or straight draws. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (higher kickers or overpairs), so calling is the best way to keep the pot manageable while seeing how the turn develops. I will call the 350 bet.

The turn card is the Trey of Diamonds, which is a significant scare card. It completes the flush draw and increases the likelihood of straights. My top pair with a Ten and a Six kicker has significantly decreased in relative strength. Since I am out of position against an aggressive opponent (FiveTwo), the best course of action is to check. This allows me to see how they respond to the board texture. If they check back, I likely still have the best hand or am in a pot-control situation. If they bet, I will have to carefully consider the size of the bet and the possibility that they have completed a flush or a straight.

Turn brings the 3rd diamond and completes some straights (notably 76), so we shouldn’t slowplay—there are many worse made hands (Tx, 5x, pair+diamond) and plenty of hands with significant equity that will call. Our set is still very strong, and betting also denies equity while setting up a river shove on safe runouts. I’ll use a firm sizing to charge draws and get value from worse made hands, while being prepared to continue versus a raise because we have strong redraws to a full house/quads.

The board has become extremely coordinated with the arrival of the Trey of Diamonds (3d), which completes potential flushes and increases the likelihood of straights (specifically 6-7 or 2-6). My hand, top pair with a Ten and a weak Six kicker, has significantly decreased in value. FiveTwo has shown a high level of aggression in previous hands, but their bet of 900 into a 1250 pot (approx. 72%) is a strong polarising bet that typically represents either a made hand (like a flush or straight) or a powerful semi-bluff. Since I do not hold a diamond to act as a blocker or provide redraw potential, and because I am out of position against a player who is likely to barrel again on the river, folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with deep stacks (~96BB). A3o is a weak offsuit ace with poor post-flop playability, dominated by better aces, and low equity against typical calling ranges. No value in limping or raising pre-flop first in. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands early in the tournament.

I am in Middle Position (effectively Hijack in 6-max) with 9h8h, a premium suited connector. With effective stacks over 100BB, this hand plays very well post-flop due to its ability to make straights and flushes. Opening the pot allows me to take the initiative. A fold is too tight given the 6-handed configuration, and limping is weak. I will raise to 2.5BB (250) to balance my opening range and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position against the blinds.
J6 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't play well against a middle position raise. Easy fold here - no reason to put chips in with this hand.

Facing a 2.5x MP open to 250, T♠4♥ is a weak, easily dominated hand. The current pot is 400; calling 250 to see the flop means I need ~250/(400+250) ≈ 38.5% equity to break even — far more than T4o realistically has versus a typical MP opening range. There’s also a high chance the blinds defend and we end up multiway, which lowers our equity and gives strong hands better reverse-implied odds if we pair the ten. A 3‑bet bluff isn’t attractive either (no meaningful blockers and deep stacks favor a real hand). With a deep stack and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot on the button is the +EV play here, so I fold.

I am holding Pocket Kings (Kc Ks) in the Small Blind, the second strongest starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Pro has opened the action with a raise to 250 from middle position. My strategy is to 3-bet for value to build a larger pot and to narrow the field, as KK performs best against fewer opponents. A 3-bet to 850 is approximately 3.4x the initial raise, which is a standard sizing from out of position to discourage the Big Blind from defending with speculative hands and to extract value from the original raiser.

We’re facing an MP open and then a small blind 3-bet, with Pro still able to continue behind. AhTc offsuit performs poorly against the likely tight/strong 3-bet range (dominated by AJ+/AQ/AK, crushed by pairs), and calling out of position will realize equity badly—especially if Pro comes along or 4-bets. A 4-bet bluff is high-variance and not attractive with this hand profile. Best EV is to decline the spot and preserve stack.

I am holding 9h8h in position against a 3-bet from the Small Blind. The 3-bet size is 8.5BB (850 chips), requiring me to call 600 more. With effective stacks around 93BB, I have excellent implied odds to play a suited connector in position. My hand retains equity well against a strong range and having position allows me to navigate post-flop effectively. Folding is too tight given the depth and position, and 4-betting turns a hand with good playability into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling is the highest EV play.

The flop comes monotone hearts (3h Qh Th), which is quite dangerous for my pocket Kings as I do not hold the King of hearts. While I still likely have the best hand if Pro hasn't flopped a flush or a set, the board texture is highly coordinated. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but leading out into a board that hits many calling ranges for a 3-bet (like suited connectors or big cards with hearts) is risky without a heart in my own hand.

I have flopped a made flush with 9h8h on a 3hQhTh board. While this is a very strong hand, it is not the nut flush; I am vulnerable to opponents holding the Ah, Kh, or Jh. In a 3-bet pot with a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio relative to single-raised pots (though still deep here), checking back is the prudent play. It accomplishes several things: 1) Pot control: I avoid building a massive pot where I might be drawing dead against a higher flush. 2) Protection: I avoid facing a check-raise, which would be a disastrous spot. 3) Deception: It disguises my hand strength, potentially inducing bluffs on the turn or allowing the opponent to catch a card they feel comfortable calling with (like a top pair). 4) Range preservation: It keeps the opponent's non-heart hands in the pot. I plan to call a turn bet or bet for value on the turn if checked to again.

The turn brings a fourth heart (5h) to the board, making any single heart in the opponent's hand a flush. I hold Kc Ks, meaning I do not have a heart for a flush or a redraw. Since I am out of position and the board is extremely coordinated, checking is the most logical play. I need to see how Pro reacts. If Pro bets, I will likely have to fold unless the bet is very small and I suspect a bluff, but leading into this board without a heart is too risky.

The turn brings the 5h, putting four hearts on the board (3h, Qh, Th, 5h). While I have a flush with 9h8h, it is vulnerable to the Ah, Kh, and Jh, all of which are well within a Small Blind 3-betting range. Betting achieves little: hands without a heart will mostly fold (denying me value), and hands with a higher heart will call or check-raise, putting me in a terrible spot. Checking back is the prudent play to control the pot size, avoid isolation against the nuts, and try to get to showdown cheaply or extract thin value on a safe river.

I have pocket Kings (Kc Ks) on a board with four hearts (3h Qh Th 5h 4d). I do not hold a heart, which means any heart in my opponent's hand makes a flush that beats me. Since the flop and turn were checked by both players, it is possible that Pro also lacks a heart or is playing a small heart cautiously. However, there is no value in betting here; I won't be called by worse hands, and I don't want to be raised off my showdown value. Checking is the most prudent play to see a showdown or evaluate if Pro decides to bet.

The board shows four hearts (3h, Qh, Th, 5h, 4d), and I hold the 9h8h for a 9-high flush. While I have a strong hand, my opponent 3-bet pre-flop, meaning their range is heavy on Ax and Kx hands, often including the Ah or Kh. Since there are four flush cards on the board, it is extremely difficult to get value from worse hands (sets or two pairs will likely fold, and there are very few lower flushes in a 3-bet range). Conversely, betting opens me up to being called or raised by higher flushes (Ah, Kh, Jh). Since checking guarantees I realize my equity against non-flush hands and lose the minimum against higher flushes, checking back is the optimal play.

I hold Ad Kh (Ace-King offsuit) in the UTG position. This is a premium starting hand ('Big Slick') and is strong enough to open raise from any position, especially 6-handed. My goal is to build the pot for value and deny equity to garbage hands. Looking at the table history, the standard opening size has consistently been 2.5bb (250 chips). Raising to 250 keeps my sizing consistent with previous hands (including my own previous open), disguising the strength of my hand while executing a solid aggressive strategy.
Q5o is a weak hand facing a UTG open. Q5 offsuit doesn't have good playability and is dominated by most of UTG's raising range. Easy fold from middle position.

Holding 7♣5♠ vs an UTG open in a 6-max game is a clear fold. Pro opened to 250 from UTG, so his range will be relatively strong and often dominates 7-5o. The pot is 400 and a call of 250 would make the pot 650, so I’d need roughly 250/650 ≈ 38.5% equity to break even — my hand’s equity vs a UTG opening range is well below that (typically ~20% or less). I’d also be out of position against the button and the blinds and likely face further action or 3-bets. A shove/3-bet bluff with 7-5o is too risky here. Fold and wait for a better spot to attack.

Pro has raised to 2.5x from under-the-gun, which typically represents a strong range. Queen-Eight offsuit is a weak hand that is easily dominated by an UTG opening range (hands like AQ, KQ, or pocket pairs). While I have the benefit of position on the button, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff against an early position raiser. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Facing a UTG open from the small blind with Q7o is a dominated, low-playability spot. Calling puts us OOP with the big blind still to act and we’ll realize equity poorly versus a strong UTG range; 3-bet bluffing is also unattractive since we have weak blockers and get continued against frequently. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 from Pro, with folds around to me in the BB holding A7s suited. Pot is 400, calling 150 more offers excellent implied odds (~3.7:1). A7s is a strong defensive hand: suited for flush/straight potential, ace blocker reduces opponent's AA/AK likelihood, dominates weaker aces like Axs/AJo. Deep stacks (95BB effective) allow postflop playability despite OOP. Pro's range is strong (JJ+, AK, AQs+) but A7s has ~35-40% equity vs that. Folding is too tight early (hand 8/150); 3-betting is suboptimal as A7s is better suited for calling/multiway. Call to realize equity and control pot OOP.

Middle pair with top kicker (A7 vs UTG range) plus nut flush draw (3 spades: Js, As, 7s) on Js7h2c rainbow board. Strong hand with ~10+ outs (8 spades + 3 aces/set for tens ~40% equity vs JJ+/AK range). OOP first to act vs strong UTG Pro range (JJ+, AKo/s, AQs+); betting protects vs overcards/draws (e.g., QTs, KQ suited), builds pot for value, denies equity to floats/OESD. Check risks giving free card to Ax/KQ improving or Pro check-raising monsters. Bet ~2/3 pot (350 into 550) balances value/thin value while keeping aggression consistent with deep stacks (95BB eff.). Avoids overbetting vulnerable to raise/fold.

I opened pre-flop with AdKh (Ace-King) and the Big Blind called. The flop comes J-7-2 rainbow, which completely misses my hand, leaving me with just Ace-high. The opponent (Elon) leads out (donk bets) for 350 chips into a pot of 550. This is a substantial bet (~64% pot) on a very dry board. Given Elon's previous passive play in Hand #5 (checking down), this sudden aggression strongly suggests he has connected with the board, likely holding a Jack or better (two pair/set). Against a top pair like Jx, I have only about 24% equity (6 outs), but the pot odds require roughly 28% to break even. Without any backdoor flush draws or straight draws to add equity, and facing an opponent showing strength, floating with Ace-high is a losing play. Folding is the prudent decision to preserve my stack.
8h6h from UTG is too weak to open. Suited connectors play better in late position where I can see how others act first. UTG requires a tighter range, and 86s doesn't make the cut. Fold.

I have J♦T♦ in middle position with a deep ~93bb stack. This is a strong, playable hand (suited connector with good straight/flush potential) that merits an open-raise from MP to take the initiative and steal the blinds/isolate the button. Table norms show 250 as a common open size, so I’ll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to get fold equity and build a pot I can play postflop. Plan: against a single caller I’ll continuation-bet appropriate flops and leverage my postflop play; if I face a large, committed 3-bet from an early/very aggressive opponent I’ll fold to avoid a marginal, bloated pot out of position.

I have Eight of Spades and Deuce of Diamonds (8s 2d), which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from middle position, and I am in the cutoff. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising with such a poor hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Minni opened from middle position to 2.5bb; 7c6d offsuit is below my profitable BTN flatting range versus an EP/MP open. It has poor playability (no suit, easily dominated when it makes pairs/straights) and I also risk getting squeezed by the blinds and having to abandon the 250. With 100bb+ stacks, I’d rather preserve chips for higher-EV spots (suited connectors, broadways, stronger pairs) and not defend too loose here.

Pocket Jacks (JJ) is a premium pre-flop hand, crushing typical middle position opening ranges which include broadway hands, Ax suited, suited connectors, and smaller pairs. Equity against such a range is approximately 75-80%. In the small blind out of position, calling risks multi-way pots and playing post-flop OOP without initiative. Folding is too tight. Therefore, 3-betting for value, initiative, and fold equity against Minni and the Big Blind who still acts. Sizing to 800 (roughly 3.2x the 250 open, or ~2x the pot) is standard OOP, providing good fold equity while keeping our range balanced and pot manageable deepstacked.

I hold 7h 8c in the Big Blind. Minni opened to 250 from Middle Position, and Elon 3-bet to 800 from the Small Blind. Facing a 3-bet with an off-suit connector is a very weak spot. I have to put in 700 more chips into a pot of 1150, but the original raiser (Minni) is still behind me and could 4-bet. Even if Minni just calls, playing 78o out of position in a 3-bet pot is -EV. This is a trivial fold.

Elon (SB) 3-bet to 800 after my MP open to 250. I hold J♦T♦ — a hand that plays very well postflop and benefits from position (I will act after him on the flop). With ~90bb effective stacks this is a spot to call: I’m being asked 550 into a ~1150 pot (~3.1:1, ~24% equity required) and JTs has reasonable equity versus a typical SB 3-bet range and excellent implied odds. I won’t 4-bet for value with this hand and a bluff 4-bet is unnecessarily risky here. Plan: call to see a flop (800 total committed), then use position to c-bet on favorable textures, realize equity with draws, and fold to heavy action when missed or when faced with strong aggression.

Pocket Jacks remain an overpair on a Q-6-5 rainbow-ish board with two spades, which is drawy (spade flush draw, open-enders like 78, gutshots). Minni's calling range vs our SB 3bet includes QQ+, AK/AQ, suited broadways, some suited connectors. JJ has strong equity (~70%+ vs her range). As OOP first to act, c-betting maintains initiative, charges draws, gets value from Qx/Ax, and protects against overcards. Sizing ~2/3 pot (1100) balances value and pot control deepstacked, with room for turns/rivers.

Fold. On 5h Qs 6s my J♦T♦ has missed: no pair, no spade/flush draw, and no meaningful straight draw (only remote backdoors). Elon 3-bet preflop and is barreling 1,100 into a ~2,800 pot — calling requires roughly 28% equity (1,100/(2,800+1,100)=~28.2%), but my realistic equity (chance to pair by river and actually beat his likely c-bet range of Qx/overpairs/sets/spade draws) is well below that. A bluff-raise is too risky vs a player who’s shown aggression and can continue with many hands that beat me. Folding preserves my ~85bb stack and lets me wait for a better, more profitable spot.

Holding J♦9♠ UTG in a 6-handed game is a marginal, easily dominated hand and I’ll be first to act against five players behind. The table has shown frequent large opens and 3-bets (Elon, FiveTwo, Pro), so limping or a small open will likely get me into multiway pots or squared by aggression out of position. A min-raise to 200 is a weak line here — it doesn’t protect well and will often be met by 3-bets from players who’ve shown big preflop aggression. With deep stacks I can afford to wait for a better spot in position; folding preserves equity and avoids difficult postflop decisions. Fold.

I am in middle position with Ace-Ten offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max tournament structure. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with the betting lead. I will open for a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Flash opens to 2.5bb from middle position. J5o is a dominated offsuit hand that flops weak top pairs and has poor playability, even in position, and it’s not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff (bad blockers, low equity when called). Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with KQo offsuit facing a middle position open to 250 into 400 pot (SB 50 + BB 100 + raise 250). Excellent position with deep stacks (~100BB). KQo is a strong hand that plays well postflop in position and blocks top pairs like KK/AK. Vs a likely wide MP range, 3-betting for value/isolation is standard to deny equity to calling hands and build pot when ahead. SB/BB still to act but 3-bet folds out their wide ranges often. Size to ~3.5x the raise (850 total) consistent with prior aggressive lines (e.g., Hand 7/9). Avoid flat to prevent multiway pots or squeezes.

I hold 7s 4h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action involves an open raise from Middle Position and a 3-bet from the Button. Facing a 3-bet with 74o out of position is a standard fold situation. I have no equity to justify a call, and bluffing here cold 4-betting with trash is ill-advised given the strength shown by the Button.

Q4o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet from Elon. He's shown aggression in previous hands, and this is a clear fold - calling 750 with Q4o against a 3-bet is burning chips. The hand is too weak and dominated too often.

I opened for 250 with Ace-Ten offsuit from middle position, and Elon on the button has 3-bet to 850. While Ace-Ten is a decent opening hand, it plays poorly out of position against a 3-bet range. Elon has shown significant aggression in previous hands (Hand #9), suggesting his range could be wide, but calling 600 more to play a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand is a recipe for losing more chips. I need about 32% equity to call, but the post-flop playability of ATo is low. I will fold and preserve my 8,000-chip stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am in UTG (Under the Gun) with Q5o. This is a very weak hand to play from the earliest position in a 6-handed game. My stack is healthy at 82.5 big blinds, and there is no reason to risk chips with a sub-standard hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first in with a very weak offsuit hand (82o) from middle position. It’s dominated when we make top pair, has poor playability postflop, and doesn’t make strong draws often enough to justify limping or opening. With multiple players left to act who can isolate, folding preserves stack and avoids negative-EV spots.

7s6s are suited connectors with good playability potential for straights and flushes. In cutoff position after two folds (UTG and MP), this is an excellent spot to raise for initiative, potential blind steals, and to play post-flop in position with deep stacks (112BB effective). Limping risks multi-way pots without initiative; folding is too tight. Standard raise sizing to 2.5x BB (250 total) matches observed tendencies in prior hands and builds the pot appropriately while maintaining fold equity against blinds who have shown mixed defending ranges.

I am on the Button with 7d 4d facing a raise to 250 from Elon in the Cutoff. While I have position, 74 suited is a weak hand (suited one-gapper) that is generally outside the profitable calling or 3-betting range against a standard Cutoff open. Although Elon has been active recently, suggesting a wider range, defending with this specific holding is too loose and risks bleeding chips or getting squeezed by the blinds. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

J2 suited is a weak hand, even with flush potential. Facing a cutoff raise, I'm out of position with a marginal holding. The suited aspect gives some playability but not enough to justify calling 200 more. Easy fold.

I’m in the big blind with T♦2♥ facing a CO open to 250 (I need to call 150). This is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with terrible playability out of position. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires roughly 27% equity (150/(400+150) ≈ 27%), and T2o won’t have that against a reasonable CO opening range. A 3-bet bluff here is too thin given stack depths and the opener’s frequency. Preserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot.

Pocket sixes are a clear open from UTG in 6-max with ~100bb stacks. Raising takes initiative, can win the blinds often, and plays well postflop (set potential). I’ll use the table’s common sizing to keep ranges balanced and avoid telegraphing strength.

Q7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in previous hands by frequently raising preflop, this is a clear fold. No equity or implied odds justify calling or raising with such a weak hand deep-stacked. Preserve chips for premium spots to maximize stack growth over 150 hands.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 250. Although FiveTwo has been active recently, an UTG opening range is generally strong and dominates my hand (likely holding better Tens, overpairs, or strong Ax/broadway hands). T8o has poor playability and very little equity against a standard UTG range. Furthermore, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze or wake up with a hand. Calling or raising here is negative EV. The only reasonable option is to fold.
52 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even with button position, calling a UTG raise with this hand is -EV. Easy fold.

I'm in the small blind with Jc3h — a weak offsuit Jack with very poor playability and lots of domination risk versus an UTG open in 6-handed. FiveTwo's open to 250 (2.5x) from UTG represents a fairly strong range; calling 200 into the ~400 pot requires ~33% equity, which J3o does not have vs a typical UTG opening range. 3-betting as a bluff is thin here (marginal blockers, one player left to act, and deep stacks that invite a call/4-bet). Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King offsuit, a premier starting hand. FiveTwo opened for 250 from UTG. Given FiveTwo's history of being active and opening frequently, I want to 3-bet for both value and to take the initiative, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. A raise to 850 (roughly 3.4x the opening bet) is a standard size that builds a pot and applies pressure to the UTG opener's range.

Flash’s BB 3-bet to 850 vs my UTG open is strong but not necessarily nutted, and the sizing gives me a reasonable price with position postflop (I act after the BB). With 66 I’m not happy 4-betting and facing a shove (effective ~74bb), but calling keeps his range wide, preserves my stack, and lets me realize set value and outplay on favorable textures. If I miss and face heavy pressure on bad boards, I can comfortably fold.

On this Ace-high flop, I have top pair with the best kicker (TPTK) and the Nut Heart blocker (Ah). My 3-bet pre-flop accurately represents a range containing strong Aces, so this board is very favorable for me. I need to bet for value and to charge any drawing hands (like heart draws or gutshots) that FiveTwo might have called with. A bet of roughly 57% of the pot (1000 into 1750) is large enough to extract value from weaker Aces or Queens while maintaining a manageable pot size if I face resistance.

In a 3-bet pot, this A-Q-9 two-heart flop strongly favors Flash’s range, and my 66 is just an underpair with only 2 clean outs to a set plus a very weak runner-runner heart possibility. Calling 1000 to win 3750 needs ~27% equity, which I don’t have, and set-mining isn’t justified given Flash’s remaining stack and the likelihood of facing further barrels. Bluff-raising here is also high risk versus a range containing many Ax/Qx and strong draws. Preserve stack and fold.

UTG position with 9s3s, a weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity multiway. Standard play is to fold trash from early position to avoid difficult postflop spots and preserve stack for better opportunities. Opponent tendencies from history don't change this fundamental decision.

I am in Middle Position (Hijack) with Jd8d. The blinds are 50/100. UTG folded. While J8s has some playability, it is generally below the opening threshold for this position in a 6-max game, especially with aggressive players behind (Flash and FiveTwo have shown aggression/3-betting). Opening here would leave me vulnerable to 3-bets or playing a marginal hand out of position. The standard play is to fold.
Q4o is a weak hand even from the cutoff. With the button, SB, and BB still to act, this hand doesn't have enough equity to profitably open. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button with Td 3h and the action has folded to me. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see a flop — this is a textbook steal spot against the small and big blinds. T3o is weak multiway but fine to open here; I want to isolate and take the pot down preflop or play heads-up in position. I will raise to 250 (≈2.5x BB) — a standard sizing that maximizes fold equity while keeping my range wide. If I face a 3-bet I will fold given my poor equity; if called I will continuation-bet appropriate flops (c-bet on dry/low boards, give up to heavy resistance on coordinated/high-card textures) and play cautiously with marginal holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni has raised from the Button, which is often a wide range. A7o is a decent hand to 3-bet as a semi-bluff because the Ace acts as a strong blocker to Minni's calling and 4-betting range. By raising to 850, I take initiative and put pressure on both the Button and the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who has been quite active. This also avoids playing a multi-way pot out of position.

Pocket fives are too strong to just fold getting a good price, and Flash has shown a willingness to 3-bet aggressively from the blinds, so his range can be wide. A 4-bet with 55 would mostly turn our hand into a bluff and risks getting jammed on by the top of his range, while calling keeps in Minni and realizes our equity via set-mining and occasional favorable postflop runouts despite being OOP. We’ll defend and play cautiously on high boards / heavy overcard textures.

I opened to 250 as a standard button steal, but Flash (SB) 3-bet to 850 and the big blind called, turning this into a multiway pot. T3o is very marginal vs a 3-bet range plus a caller — it’s often dominated and has poor equity. Calling 600 into a 1950 pot requires ~23–24% equity to break even, and playing this hand multiway postflop is uncomfortable even though I’d have position. 4-betting as a bluff here is too risky and unnecessary. My preflop plan was to fold to a 3-bet with such a hand, so I’ll preserve my ~8,100 stack and fold.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board that is relatively dry besides the heart draw. In Hand 12, FiveTwo followed a similar pattern of calling a 3-bet pre-flop and then folding to a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop. I will lead with a continuation bet of about 38% of the pot to extract value from weaker hands like mid-pocket pairs or draws, and to see if FiveTwo is willing to continue against the aggressor.

Flash’s 3-bet range from the SB can be wide, and this is a small range c-bet (750 into 2700). Even though 55 has no heart/backdoors and is behind all Ax, we still have enough immediate pot odds and can be ahead of his air (KQ/KJ/QJ, etc.), so folding is too tight. Raising would mostly be a low-equity bluff OOP on an A-high texture where he can continue with a lot of hands. We’ll call once and proceed cautiously: give up versus meaningful turn barrels, and consider taking the pot if he checks turns that don’t improve him.

I have top pair, but my kicker (7) is quite weak. FiveTwo called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, which indicates either a decent Ace, a pocket pair, or a draw (likely hearts). The Queen of Spades on the turn is a card that could potentially improve FiveTwo if they are holding AQ or KQ. By checking, I am exercising pot control and avoiding building a massive pot with a marginal hand out of position. This allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts and potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw on the river, or get to a cheap showdown.

Flash’s flop sizing was small and his turn check on the Q is consistent with a lot of give-ups (KJ/JT, some underpairs) and also some pot-control/traps with Ax/AQ. With 55 we still beat all the air that likely checks river, and betting here mostly turns our hand into a bluff while risking getting called by Qx/Ax or punished by a check-raise from his strongest hands. Taking the free card keeps variance down and lets us realize our showdown value; we can reassess on the river versus his sizing/frequency.

On this river, the board has paired the deuces (Ah-9d-2h-Qs-2d). My hand is now Two Pair, Aces and Deuces, with a Queen kicker. Looking at the board, any player with an Ace will use the Queen on the board as their kicker unless they hold an Ace-King or Ace-Queen. This means that against most other 'Ace-weak kicker' hands (like A3 through AJ), the pot will likely be a split. FiveTwo checked back on the turn after I checked, which strongly suggests they do not have a monster like AQ, A9, or a set of 9s or Queens. By checking the river, I am looking to get to a cheap showdown. If FiveTwo bets, I will have to evaluate if it's a missed heart draw bluff or a value bet, but checking is the most prudent move to preserve my stack while likely holding the best or a splitting hand.

Flash’s line (small flop c-bet, then turn check and river check) is heavily weighted to give-ups and some pot-control with Ax. With 55 we already beat all his air and many underpairs that take this line. Betting would mostly fold out the hands we beat and get called by better (Ax/Qx), while also exposing us to a check-raise from his strongest hands. Taking the free showdown maximizes our EV with this bluff-catcher.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Deuce offsuit (2d Th), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. With five players left to act behind me and no chips invested in the pot yet, playing this hand out of position is highly negative EV. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately.
K9 suited in middle position is a solid opening hand. With Pro folded, I should raise to 250 as a standard open. K9s has good playability with flush potential and decent high card strength.

Claude opened to 250 from MP. I have 4♠3♠ — a small suited connector with good postflop playability and large implied odds with 81 BB behind. Calling 250 is cheap to see a flop and keeps me in position versus the raiser. A 3-bet with this hand is thin and would bloat the pot unnecessarily; folding is too nitty for the price. I’ll call and evaluate the flop.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit (As 9d). Claude (Middle Position) has opened for 2.5x (250) and Minni (Cutoff) has called. While I have position on the field, A9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly in multi-way pots and is often dominated by the opening range of a middle-position player (AQ, AJ, AK). The presence of a caller in the Cutoff further strengthens the average hand strength I am facing. While I have been playing aggressively in recent hands (3-betting with AK, AT, and A7s), A9o is a 'trap' hand that lacks the board coverage of suited connectors or the raw power of premium Aces. Calling invites the blinds to join, creating a 4-way or 5-way pot where A9o is difficult to navigate. I will choose the disciplined path and fold.

We’re facing a 2.5bb open from Claude plus a cold call from Minni, and we’ll be out of position the whole hand with a weak, easily dominated suited holding. Tc4c makes too many second-best hands (dominated club flushes, weak top pairs) and doesn’t have the connectivity to realize equity well multiway from the small blind. A squeeze bluff is also poor here due to lack of blockers and the strength/signaling of the open+call. Best EV is to cut losses and fold.

J4o is a trash hand with no playability, not suited or connected. Facing a raise from middle position Claude and a flat call from cutoff Minni creates a strong range ahead of me. Pot odds are 650:150 (~4.3:1), but my equity is too low against their ranges, especially out of position postflop. Deep stacks, but no reason to speculate. Standard fold to preserve stack.

I flopped a King-high flush with K9hh on Jh5h7h board. This is a monster hand. I should bet for value - heads up against Minni who called my preflop raise. I want to extract value while keeping them in with top pair, overpairs, or draws. Betting around 60% pot (~400) is good sizing to build the pot.

Claude opened MP and c-bet the three-heart flop (J♥5♥7♥) to 400 into ~1050. I have 4♠3♠ — missed the flop but have meaningful equity: four 6s give me a straight (≈8.5% to hit on the turn, ≈16.4% by the river) and pairing a 3 or 4 adds additional outs (naively ~38% to pair/straight by river). The call costs 400 into a 1050 pot, so I need ~27.6% equity (400/(1050+400)) — my odds justify a flat-call. I’m in position, so calling preserves pot control, lets me realize equity, and gives me the chance to float and apply pressure if Claude shows weakness on the turn. A raise would be too thin against a MP opener on a monotone-heart flop; folding would be too nitty given the price. Plan: call 400, re-evaluate on the turn (fold to large heart barrels or strong action if I miss; extract value or raise if I hit the 6 or get a favorable turn).

I have the King-high flush (K9hh) - second nuts only behind Ah. Minni called my flop bet, likely with a lower flush, set, two pair, or maybe Ah draw. The 8d doesn't change much. I need to continue value betting. Pot is 1450, I'll bet around 65% pot (~950) to keep extracting value from worse flushes and strong made hands.

Fold. The turn bet is too large for the equity I have. Pot is 2400 and Claude bet 950; calling 950 requires roughly 950/(2400+950+950) ≈ 22.1% equity to be break-even. With 4♠3♠ on J♥5♥7♥8♦ my only realistic clean outs to a made winning hand are the four 6s (4/46 ≈ 8.7%). Pairing a 3 or 4 gives a tiny pair that will frequently still lose to Jx/7x/5x and is not a reliable out. Crucially the flop was three hearts (J♥5♥7♥) — Claude’s line (MP open, flop c-bet, large turn bet) is highly consistent with a made heart flush or other strong value hands. If he has a flush, my straight outs don’t beat him at all. A raise-to-1900 as a bluff is possible but very risky: if he has a flush or Jx he’s unlikely to fold, and if he calls I’m crushed. With ~74.5 BB and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and folding a thin, undercard draw here is the correct, lower-variance play.
T3o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, and UTG is the worst position to open from. No need to get fancy here - folding and waiting for better spots.

Holding K♠5♦ in middle position with 6 players and no raise yet (pot 150, to call 100). This hand is weak: offsuit, poor kicker, and easily dominated by common Kx and broadway hands I’ll face from the cutoff/button/SB/BB. Players behind have shown a tendency to open-raise frequently, so calling 100 will often put me into a multiway or raised pot out of position with a hand that has poor postflop playability. With ~74.5BB effective I don’t want to bloat the pot or invite 3-bets with such a marginal holding. EV and table dynamics favor folding and waiting for a better spot (suited connectors, stronger broadways, or pair) rather than calling or attempting a light open. Fold.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Eight suited (Kh8h). It has been folded to me, making this a standard opening spot in a 6-max game. King-Eight suited is well within the opening range for this position. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind, which is consistent with the standard opening sizes observed in the tournament so far.

Flash’s cutoff open is likely fairly wide 6-handed, and we’re on the button getting a good price with position. Q4s isn’t strong enough to 3-bet for value and it plays poorly when we make a dominated queen, so I prefer not to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Calling keeps the SPR healthy and lets us realize suited equity (flush/strong draws) while using position to control pot size and apply pressure on favorable runouts.

Q6o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the SB facing a cutoff raise from Flash (who has shown aggression in prior hands) and a button call from FiveTwo. The pot is 650, but calling 200 OOP risks difficult post-flop situations where I'm likely dominated or floating ineffectively. BB still to act, increasing multiway potential. Deep stacks, but folding trash preserves stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 7c facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button. I need to call 150 to win a pot of 650, offering me pot odds of roughly 4.3 to 1 (requiring ~19% equity). While the price is cheap, 97 offsuit is a weak hand that plays very poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. My realized equity will likely be significantly lower than my raw equity due to position and the likelihood of being dominated or bluffed off the hand. Conserving chips is prudent here.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot against FiveTwo, who is in position. The flop of As-9c-3s is very favorable for my perceived opening range from the Cutoff, as I have all the strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) while the Button's calling range is often capped at weaker Aces or pocket pairs. This is a classic board to continuation bet (c-bet). By betting around half the pot, I can put pressure on middle pairs and non-Ace hands while representing a strong hand. Given I have no current value with King-high, taking the initiative to win the pot now is the best play.

Flash’s small c-bet on A93 rainbow-ish is very credible given the ace advantage in the preflop raiser’s range. With Qs4s we have no pair, no real straight equity, and only a backdoor spade draw (plus very weak pair-outs that are often dominated). We’re not getting enough implied odds to peel, and this hand is too low in our range to justify a float or a bluff-raise. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I’m UTG with A♠8♠ and about 74.5BB effective. In 6-max A8s is worth opening — it has nut-ace blocker value and good postflop playability with backdoor flush/straight potential. I want the initiative and to avoid giving the table cheap flops, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. I’ll size to 250 (≈2.5x), which matches the table’s common open size and reduces the field. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures, realize equity with backdoors, and fold to heavy 3-bets or sustained pressure when I only have Ace-high; I’ll consider calling small-to-medium 3-bets from the blinds/BTN to play postflop given suitedness and blockers.

I am in Middle Position holding 3c 6d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has opened with a raise from UTG. Given my position and the strength of my hand, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

UTG open in a 6-handed game represents a fairly strong, high-card heavy range. Q9 offsuit is too often dominated (QJ/QT/KQ/AQ, 99+) and doesn’t realize equity well multiway with three players still behind who can 3-bet/squeeze. With deep stacks, we can wait for better suited/connectors or stronger broadways to attack Minni’s opens from position.

QTo offsuit on the button vs an UTG raise from Minni in a 6-max game with deep stacks. Position is excellent, and QTo has decent playability post-flop with straight potential and Q-high. Folding is too tight. Calling risks a squeeze from blinds and playing multiway OOP. 3-betting leverages fold equity against Minni's strong but not uncallable UTG range, isolates if called due to position, and builds pot with initiative. History shows Minni folds to 3-bets reasonably often (e.g., Hand 9, 13). Sizing to 800 (roughly 3.2x) is standard for value/thin value and fold equity without overcommitting.

I am in the Small Blind facing a UTG open and a Button 3-bet. My hand (4h 3h) is a speculative suited connector that plays poorly out of position against strong ranges, especially in a 3-bet pot. The risk of the UTG player 4-betting or the Big Blind squeezing is present. The price to call is too high relative to my equity and realizability. Folding is the standard and correct play.
72o is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a 3-bet from Elon (who's been aggressive), I have no equity and no playability. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 250 with A♠8♠. Elon (button) 3-bet to 800. With ~72bb effective, A8s is worth defending — suited ace with an ace-blocker and good backdoor equity. Folding here would be too tight against a button 3-bet; 4-betting is too polarizing and would commit too much with a non‑premium. Pot is 1200 and I have to call 550 (≈31% equity required), which A8s can achieve vs a reasonably wide 3-bet range and also allows postflop playability. I'll call to see a flop, play cautiously OOP (continue with pairs/draws, fold to heavy offline pressure with air), and fold to a large 4‑bet or shove.

I have A♠8♠ on A♦ A♥ 8♣ — that’s aces full of eights (AAA88), essentially the near‑nut full house on the flop. Elon 3‑bet preflop, so his range includes many Ax hands and pocket pairs (AK/AQ, QQ/KK/TT/88, etc.) — most of which will happily call a value bet. The only realistic ways to lose now are quads (an 8 on turn/river) or an exact split with Ac+8; checking risks giving free cards and squandering value from worse A‑hands and pairs. I want to both extract value and deny free cards, so I’ll make a pot‑sized value bet to charge worse aces and pocket pairs. If he raises I’m prepared to commit/call with this near‑nut full house.

Preflop 3-bet with QTo on button vs UTG open was a thin value/positional play with fold equity and initiative. Flop AA8 rainbow is extremely dry and favors my perceived range (Ax, KK, QQ, etc.). However, Minni's call of my 3-bet UTG shows strength (top of her opening range: AJ+, KQ, maybe sets), and her large donk-lead of 1750 into ~1750 pot (now 3500, ~1/2 pot) screams value: likely strong Ax (AK, AQ, AT+), A8, or AA. My QTo has zero equity—no pair, no draws (Qc doesn't help flush draw vs her range, no straight outs). Position doesn't help with air; raising as bluff risks her stack commitment with monsters and folds out worse; calling builds pot OOP for her with no improvement outs. Pot odds ~2:1 but 0% equity vs her range = auto-fold. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding pocket tens (TT) in the UTG position. This is a very strong starting hand in a 6-max game, and I should definitely open the betting to build the pot and potentially thin the field. A standard raise size for me and other players in this game has been 250, so I will continue with that sizing.

3c2d offsuit has very poor playability and equity versus an UTG opening range, especially 6-handed and ~80bb deep. Calling would put us in tough multiway/positional spots with dominated low cards and little ability to realize equity; 3-betting is pure spew. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 from Flash, who has shown aggression in prior hands but from an early position indicating a strong range (top ~15-20% of hands). My holding of 4h8s is offsuit, unconnected, low cards with no playability, zero equity against their range. Position in cutoff is decent, but with deep stacks (104 BB effective), calling commits too much to a speculative hand with poor implied odds and dominated post-flop potential. Raising has no fold equity and plays poorly OOP. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand (3h 6s). The player in UTG (Flash) has raised to 250. An UTG opening range is generally strong, containing pairs and high cards. My hand (6-high unsuited) has very poor equity against any reasonable opening range. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluffing (3-betting) with this specific holding in this spot. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Qd8d is a suited hand with decent playability. Against Flash's UTG raise to 250, I'm getting 2:1 odds to call 200 more. The suited diamonds give me flush potential and implied odds if I hit. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but too playable to fold at this price in position vs the BB.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with the small blind called, I'm in the big blind with J♣4♠. This is a multiway pot and I'll be out of position versus both opponents. J4o is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability and not enough equity to justify calling 150 into the pot (it would require ~20%+ equity to break even). With deep stacks and plenty of hands to wait for, folding preserves my stack and avoids difficult postflop decisions. I will fold.

I flopped top pair with Qd on Qs-9h-6c board. My 8 kicker is mediocre but I have a solid value hand. Flash raised UTG so they could have AQ, KQ, or big pairs that beat me. Being OOP, I'll lead out for value around half pot to charge draws and get value from worse hands. If raised significantly, I can reevaluate.

I hold pocket tens on a board of Q-9-6. This is a classic 'marginal' situation where I am likely either way ahead or way behind. Claude's lead (donk bet) of 300 into a 600 pot is interesting; it could represent a Queen looking to take control, a 9, or a middle pair like 88/77. Given the single overcard and the size of the bet, I'm not ready to fold my tens. Raising might bloat the pot against a range that could include a Queen (AQ, KQ, QJ) or sets (99, 66). Calling allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Claude's bluffs or weaker hands like a 9 in the range, and re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and Claude's continued aggression.

Turn 6h pairs the board but is mostly a blank. I still have top pair Queens. Flash just called my flop bet which is consistent with medium pairs, draws, or slowplaying a stronger Q. The 6 is unlikely in Flash's UTG range. I should continue betting for value around 50% pot to extract from worse Queens (QJ, QT) and pairs like 99, TT, JJ while also protecting against draws.

I am holding pocket tens on a board of 9-6-Q-6. Claude has 'donk-led' both the flop and the turn. While the Queen is an overcard to my pair, the turn 6h is likely a blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture unless Claude specifically holds a 6 or 99. Claude's range could include weaker Queens, a 9, or a heart draw. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (Qx, 6x, 99), while folding is too tight given the pot odds (3:1) and the possibility that Claude is betting with a worse hand or a draw. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

River Ace is a bad card for me. Flash called two streets with an UTG opening range, which often contains Ax hands. The Ace now gives any AK, AJ, AT two pair or top pair that beats my Queens. Additionally, AQ now has two pair. Given my weak Q8 holding and the scare card, I should check to control pot size. If Flash bets, I can consider a call or fold based on sizing. If they check back, I might win at showdown against missed draws or worse pairs.

I am on the river with pocket tens. The board is Q-9-6-6-A. Claude 'donk' bet the flop and turn but has now checked on the river. The Ace is a significant scare card. My tens have decent showdown value against Claude's potential missed draws or a pair of nines, but they are behind any Queen, any Ace, or a Six. By checking back, I reach showdown for free. It is unlikely Claude would call a bet with a worse hand (like a 9), and I don't want to risk a check-raise from a hand that's now trapping or a weak Ace that just improved. Checking is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

8-2 offsuit is the weakest holding and plays poorly from UTG in a 6-handed game: dominated when we make a pair, no real straight/flush potential, and we’ll be out of position versus the field. Limping invites isolation and bloats a pot we can’t realize equity in. Best EV is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

AKo is a premium hand, top-tier for raising from middle position after UTG fold. Standard aggressive play to build pot and narrow field. Opponents deep-stacked and history shows frequent 250-300 raises, so sizing to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure while maintaining good pot odds. No limpers, clear raising spot to maximize value.

I hold Ad 8c in the Cutoff. The action started with an open raise to 300 (3BB) from Elon in Middle Position. A8o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a standard Middle Position opening range (which typically includes AK, AQ, AJ, ATs, and pocket pairs). Additionally, calling leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Button or Blinds behind me. 3-betting is a possibility as a bluff due to the Ace blocker, but against an active player like Elon who has shown aggression, it is high variance. The most prudent and standard play with off-suit Ace-rag against an MP open is to fold.

9s6s on the button facing a MP raise to 300. This is a decent suited connector-type hand with excellent position. Worth a call to see a flop - I can play this hand profitably with position and the potential to make straights/flushes. Not strong enough to 3-bet but too playable to fold on the BTN.

I'm in the small blind with 8♥6♣ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Elon opened to 300 from middle and Claude called on the button, so I'd be out of position in a multiway pot. The pot is 750 and I must call 250 more (≈25% equity needed). Against a raiser plus a caller this hand is often dominated and has poor postflop playability, so calling or attempting a blind-raise is unjustified. I conserve my 8,250 stack for better spots and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 3c, which is a very weak hand. Elon raised from middle position and Claude called from the button. Although I only need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 950, 43o realizes its equity poorly out of position against two opponents. It is better to fold this trash hand and wait for a better opportunity than to risk chips in a multi-way pot with zero connectivity or high-card value.

AKo is a premium hand that raised preflop, building the pot. Flop Qd Jd 2s completely misses me for a pair or draw, but ace-high has decent showdown value and blocks strong hands like AQ/KQ. Board is drawy with flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., KT for broadway), so a continuation bet applies pressure, charges draws, and leverages fold equity against Claude's button calling range, which is often wide (floaters, suited connectors). Half-pot sizing of 400 is standard, protects against floats, and keeps pot manageable deepstacked. Checking invites free cards or check-raise. Goal: maximize EV by balancing c-bets with range advantage.

I have 9s6s on Jd 2s Qd - completely missed this flop. Only a backdoor spade draw (one spade on board) and no pair or straight draw. Calling 400 into a 1150 pot with essentially no equity besides runner-runner spades isn't profitable. Easy fold.

UTG with T5o in a 6-handed game at 50/100 blinds with deep stacks (~100BB). This is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and no position advantage. Standard tight play requires folding weak offsuit hands from early position to avoid difficult post-flop spots and preserve stack for better opportunities. Opponent tendencies show aggression from later positions, making limping or raising unprofitable.

I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Six of Clubs (T6o) in Middle Position. This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, offering no connectivity and being off-suit. With four opponents yet to act behind me, including the blinds, opening the pot or limping would be a losing play in the long run. Given my healthy stack of 112 BB in the early stages of the tournament (Hand 19/150), there is no incentive to take risks with trash hands. The standard action is to fold.

KQo in the cutoff is a strong hand. Two players have folded, I'm in late position with only button and blinds behind. Standard raise to 250 to take initiative and potentially take down the blinds or build a pot with a premium hand.

I have Q♣3♠ on the button facing a cutoff open to 250. This is a weak, unconnected offsuit hand — not a 3‑bet candidate — but calling is cheap (250 = 2.5bb, only ~3% of my 82bb stack) and I get position postflop. I’ll call to use positional advantage and see a flop heads‑up or multiway; I can fold to a sizable 3‑bet or to heavy aggression on a dry board unless I pair or pick up a strong draw. If I hit top pair (Q) or a strong draw I can apply pressure; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 7c. Claude has raised from the Cutoff and Minni has called on the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that would be 850), 67 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against two active opponents. Playing a marginal hand multi-way from the small blind often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely to be dominated or forced to fold. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a cutoff open and a button cold-call, we’d be defending the big blind multiway and out of position with 2♠4♥. Even with decent pot odds, this hand has very poor playability and significant reverse implied odds (making a pair is often dominated; straights are rare and hard to realize OOP vs two ranges). A squeeze bluff isn’t attractive into two players with deep stacks. Best EV is to let it go and avoid a long-run leak.

I raised preflop with KQo and got one caller (Minni). Flop is Js 8h Ad. I missed completely but have two overcards and a gutshot to a straight (need T). As the preflop aggressor with position, a c-bet is standard to represent the Ace. Minni could have Ax but many hands in their calling range missed this board. Betting around 1/3 pot for value/fold equity.

I completely missed this flop with Q♣3♠ on J♠‑8♥‑A♦: no pair, no meaningful backdoor draws (only three queens and three treys as weak outs). Claude is the cutoff raiser and his small 225 probe into an 875 pot could be a wide c‑bet, but his range includes many A‑x/J‑x and broadways that dominate me. Calling 225 into a 1,100 pot gives ~20.5% pot‑odds, but my real showdown equity (and the equity of the outs I have) is well under that once dominated hands are considered — so calling is a negative EV. Raising as a bluff is risky: I don’t block A/J and can easily face a reraise or a call that leaves me in tough spots. With 80bb I’ll conserve chips for better opportunities and fold.

I am in UTG position with 8c Ts (Ten-Eight offsuit). This is a very weak hand, far below the standard opening range for Under the Gun in a 6-handed game.
