I have five players acting behind me, increasing the likelihood that someone wakes up with a better hand or 3-bets me. With a healthy stack of 112 BBs, there is no pressure to make loose plays. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

J3o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position with 4 players left to act behind me. This is not in my opening range from this position - too easily dominated and poor playability post-flop. Folding is the correct play.

Cutoff with As5s and ~80bb effective. UTG and Claude folded so this is a standard open-raise spot — A5s has good playability and valuable A‑blockers. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the button and blinds, and play postflop in position, so I’ll open to a standard 2.5x (250). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold preflop; against single callers or small 3-bets I’ll continue to see flops and c-bet favorable boards, using backdoor spade equity and position to realize value.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a strong starting hand. Minni (Cutoff) has opened with a raise to 250. Given my position and the strength of my hand, I have two primary options: calling to see a flop with position or 3-betting to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately. Looking at Minni's stack size (7750), a 3-bet to around 750-800 is standard to apply pressure. However, in this specific tournament context and early stage, I will put in a 3-bet to 750. This defines my hand strength, exerts positional pressure, and can force folds from the blinds (FiveTwo and Elon) or even Minni herself.

8♠6♠ is too weak to continue versus a CO open and BTN 3-bet while out of position with the BB still to act. Calling would realize equity poorly (dominated/forced to fold on many boards and facing high c-bet frequency), and 4-betting as a bluff is unattractive here because we have no relevant blockers and would be taking a high-variance line with a hand that doesn’t play well when called/jammed on. Best play is to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

Pocket Kings is an extremely strong premium hand, second only to AA. Minni opened from cutoff (wide range possible), Flash 3-bet from button (excellent 3-bet spot, often light or polarized including bluffs and strong hands). SB folded, leaving Minni behind me. Calling risks Minni 4-betting or us going multiway postflop where KK is vulnerable. 4-betting isolates vs Flash's button range (which KK crushes), gets value from worse, and pressures Minni. Sizing to 2000 (~2.7x the 3-bet) is standard deepstack 4-bet size, building the pot while leaving room for postflop play. Stacks are deep (~100BB), so no need to over-size. Dominates typical calling ranges.

I opened from the cutoff to 250 with A5s for initiative and playability, but the action just got 3-bet by the button to 750 and then 4-bet by the big blind to 2000. With ~77.5bb effective, this 4-bet is substantial and heavily narrows the raiser’s range toward strong pairs and broadway combos; A5s, while having an ace blocker and suitedness, is frequently dominated and plays poorly facing a large 4-bet (especially if the button wakes up and peels). Calling 1,750 more to play a likely multi-way or awkward heads-up pot out of position is a marginal line, and 4-betting or shoving would be too committal and high-variance here. I stick to my preflop plan (fold to large 3/4-bets) and preserve my ~77bb stack for better spots.

Elon's cold 4-bet from the Big Blind after my 3-bet indicates a very strong range, likely consisting of QQ+, AK, and potentially some bluffs. However, Ace-Jack offsuit is significantly behind this value range and does not play well against a 4-bet. Despite having position, the cost to call (1250 more) is high, and I risk being dominated or facing a difficult post-flop situation. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.
Q3o is a weak hand to open from UTG. Standard UTG opening ranges don't include Q3 offsuit - it's too weak and out of position against 5 players. Easy fold.

8♥3♥ is a very weak, unsuited low connector with poor playability out of position. There are still multiple players to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) and the table has shown aggressive raisers/3-bettors — a raise here is likely to get called or 3-bet, and calling 100 to see a flop OOP is negative EV. With a healthy 77.5bb stack I should conserve chips for hands with better post‑flop playability and fold this weak holding preflop.

Action is folded to me in the Cutoff. While 9h 6h is a suited hand with some connectivity, it is slightly below the standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game, especially with active players like Elon and FiveTwo still to act behind me. Given my current stack and the tournament stage, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger opening candidate rather than risk a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position against the Button.

Folds to me on the Button with Ac4h. In a 6-max setup this is a profitable open: we have an ace (good blocker vs strong holdings), reasonable playability, and we can pick up the blinds often. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply and puts us in tougher multiway spots. I’ll use a standard small steal sizing to keep risk down while still applying pressure.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a button raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in prior hands, the pot odds to call 200 into 600 are not sufficient given the likelihood of domination and tough postflop spots OOP. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 3d facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). The pot odds are approximately 27% (calling 150 to win 400), which is mathematically tempting for any suited hand. However, J3s is at the very bottom of the suited Jack range. It suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds (hitting a Jack with a weak kicker) while playing out of position against an opponent who has demonstrated aggression and barreling capabilities (e.g., Hand #6). Defending too wide here can lead to bleeding chips in marginal spots. A fold is the disciplined, low-variance decision.

UTG with J♠4♠ — a weak, poorly connected suited hand with a bad kicker and high likelihood of being dominated. In 6-max/UTG I should be fairly tight; earlier hands show frequent and sometimes large preflop aggression (multiple players open-raising and 3-betting). Facing the prospect of callers and potential squeezes from the button and blinds, opening or even completing here is unnecessarily spewy with deep stacks. Preserve my 7.75k stack for better spots and stronger holdings — fold.

I am in middle position with pocket sixes (6d 6s). This is a solid opening hand in a 6-max game after the UTG player has folded. I will put in a standard open-raise of 2.5x the big blind to 250. This allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the blinds and antes, or set-mine if I get called.

Flash’s 2.5x open from middle position is likely fairly wide, but 6♣2♣ is too weak to defend profitably here. It has poor playability versus squeezes from the button/blinds, makes dominated flushes/weak pairs, and doesn’t have the robust straight potential of better suited connectors. With no blockers and no clear exploit to 3-bet bluff, the highest-EV line is to preserve chips and fold.

Preflop on the button with JcTh offsuit facing a middle position raise to 250 into a 400 pot. JTo has good playability, straight potential, and position allows profitable postflop play. Flash has been aggressive preflop in previous hands (e.g., Hands 6,10,12,15,17), suggesting a wide range we can exploit with position and implied odds. Deep stacks (~100BB) support calling for multiway potential or heads-up play. Folding is too tight; 3-betting offsuit is unnecessary without a read for frequent folds. Pot odds are favorable (400:250 ~1.6:1 direct, better implied). SB/BB may defend wide, but button position dominates postflop.

I hold Queen-Three offsuit (Qd 3s) in the Small Blind. The action involves a raise to 250 from Flash in Middle Position and a call from Elon on the Button. Q3o is a statistically weak hand with very poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot where I would be out of position post-flop against two opponents. Although the pot odds are roughly 4.25:1, the reverse implied odds and difficulty of realizing equity with this hand make calling unprofitable. A bluff raise (squeeze) is ill-advised with such weak holdings and no relevant blockers. Folding is the only standard play.

Holding 4d3h in the BB facing a raise to 250 and a call. This is a very weak offsuit hand with minimal potential. Even with decent pot odds (150 into 650), 43o plays terribly postflop - hard to make straights, no flush draw, and any pair will be bottom pair with weak kicker. Easy fold.

I started the hand with a pre-flop raise to 250 with pocket sixes and was called by Elon on the button. The flop comes Kd Ts 2d, which is quite unfavorable for my pair of sixes as there are two overcards and a diamond draw present. Being out of position against Elon, who has shown a willingness to call pre-flop, I should proceed with caution. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or have significant equity against me. Checking is the most prudent play to control the size of the pot and see how Elon reacts to this board.

Heads-up on the flop with middle pair (tens) and a jack kicker, holding JcTh on TsKd2d board. Flash, the preflop raiser from middle position, checks to me in position—an unusual sign of weakness from an aggressive opponent who frequently continuation bets (e.g., Hands 6,12,15,17). My hand has showdown value, blocks some of villain's top pair combos (holding a T), and faces a polarized checking range: bluffs/missed draws (like AdXd, QJ suited) or monsters slowplaying (unlikely given villain's history). Deep stacks (100+BB) and position favor a thin value bet to charge draws (diamond flush draws, QJ gutshots) and weaker pairs, building the pot while denying equity. Pot odds are irrelevant since checked to us. Half-pot bet (325-400) balances value/protection without overcommitting. Checking back risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and misses value from Ax/high cards that call a bet.

I have pocket sixes on a board of Kd-Ts-2d. After raising pre-flop and being called by Elon on the button, the flop has brought two overcards and a diamond draw. I checked the flop to control the pot size and gauge Elon's strength. Elon has now bet 350 into a 650 pot. My underpair is very vulnerable here; I am likely behind a King, a Ten, or even a diamond draw that has significant equity against me. Since I am out of position and have very limited potential to improve (only two sixes left in the deck), calling is a negative expectation play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand for early position in a 6-max game. Given that I have a healthy stack and it's early in the tournament, there's no reason to play such a marginal hand from this position. Standard tight-aggressive play dictates a fold here.

K7o is a dominated, low-connectivity hand that performs poorly when called or 3-bet. From middle position with aggressive opponents left to act (CO/BTN/SB capable of isolating and 3-betting), opening or limping will frequently put us in tough spots and reverse-implied-odds situations. With ~79bb, preserving stack for better opens is higher EV than attempting a thin steal here.

Cutoff position after two early folds with 9d Th, a suited connector with good playability and potential for strong post-flop hands. Deep stacks (122BB effective). Standard to open-raise here for initiative, to potentially isolate blinds or take them down pre-flop. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative; folding is too tight for this spot. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is a standard sizing to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against blinds and button.

I hold 9h 6s on the Button. The player in the Cutoff (Elon) has raised to 250. My hand is unsuited, disconnected, and very weak (9-high). Despite having the best position, the hand strength is far too low to call a raise or attempt a 3-bet bluff, especially with the blinds still left to act. The pot odds do not justify a call. Folding is the only standard and correct play.

Q3o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a cutoff raise. Out of position with a dominated queen and no real playability, this is a clear fold. Not worth calling 200 more to play OOP with such a marginal holding.

Elon (cutoff) opened to 250. I’m in the big blind with 5♥T♠ — a marginal but defensible hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call (about 27% break-even equity), and stacks are deep (~76bb), so I prefer to defend and see a flop rather than folding or 3-betting with a weak holding. Plan: call to realize equity; continue on flops where I hit a pair or have strong draws, otherwise give up to heavy aggression. Total to commit = 250.

Board: 2♥ J♠ J♣, my hand: T♠5♥. I have no pair and only a weak backdoor heart draw — essentially no meaningful equity vs any decent holding. Pot is 550 and stacks are deep (~75bb). Preflop I defended to see a flop; on this dry, paired board the correct line is pot control. I should not bluff-raise or barrel here with such a weak holding because I have very little fold equity and would be out of position vs likely better pairs or jacks. I will check and concede the initiative; if Elon bets I will fold to a standard c-bet or only call extremely small probes that give me good odds to see a free turn. If he checks back I get to see a turn for free and can reassess with a chance to hit something or bluff if the texture changes.

Heads-up on a paired J♠ J♣ 2♥ flop as preflop raiser from cutoff with position on Minni who limped-called preflop from the big blind and checked. My 9♦ T♥ has a gutshot straight draw to the Q (for T-J-Q), plus backdoor heart flush draw and two overcards. This board connects decently with my opening range (broadway hands like KT+, JTs, suited connectors that can flop sets, two-pair, or strong draws), while Minni's limp-call range is wide but weaker, often low pairs, suited junk, or weak broadways unlikely to have JJ or exactly Jx. Her check indicates weakness. Continuation betting exploits my range advantage, denies equity to her floats/draws, charges better draws, and has good fold equity against her capped range. Pot is 550; betting 300 (~55% pot) is an effective sizing that's not too thin for value when ahead but still profitable as a bluff given my playability and blockers to straight draws (T blocks some QT). Checking risks giving a free card to improve her hand or realize equity cheaply. Goal to maximize EV long-term in tournament.

Pot is 850 and Elon bets 300; calling 300 commits me to a pot of 1150, so I need ≈26.1% equity to break even. With T5o on a 2♥ J♠ J♣ board I have only 6 outs (three tens, three fives) and my two-card equity to improve by the river is ≈24.1%, which is below the required pot‑odds. Many of my outs are ‘dirty’ (a made Jack or better from Elon still beats me, and he opened preflop and c‑bet a paired board so his range is weighted to Jx/strong hands). I’m out of position, stacks are deep but calling or bluff‑raising here is negative EV and will leave me in tough spots on later streets. Fold and preserve chips for a better, more defensible spot.

We’re UTG 6-handed and holding 8♣3♦ offsuit, which has very poor playability and dominated equity against any reasonable continuing range. Limping creates multiway spots out of position with no high-card value and weak connectivity; opening would be burning chips since we can’t profitably continue versus 3-bets and will realize equity poorly postflop. Best stack-preserving, EV-maximizing action is to fold.

Preflop in middle position with Jc6h offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor high card strength, dominated potential, and low playability postflop. No raises yet, just blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100), but entering the pot with this hand is -EV due to position and multiway risks. Opponents have shown aggression in prior hands, but folding trash preserves deep stack (125BB) for premium spots in tournament.

I am in the Cutoff with 8h 3h. This is a very weak suited hand (bottom 15-20% of hands). Although I have position on the blinds, there are still three players left to act, including the Button. Opening here would be a loose steal attempt with very little equity if called. Given the early stage of the tournament and the weakness of the hand, the disciplined play is to fold.

On the button with Q5o and it's folded to me. This is a prime stealing spot - I have position and only need to get through the blinds. Q5o is a reasonable button open in this situation. Standard 2.5x raise to steal.

I’m in the small blind with A♡T♣ facing a button open to 250. With ~74 BB effective ATo is worth defending but it’s marginal to 3-bet out of position — a min 3-bet to 400 is too weak and a larger 3-bet commits too much with a hand that plays much better seeing a flop. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves fold equity postflop (Ace-blocker helps) and lets me play flop texture before committing more. I will call the extra 200 (total 250). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold or re-evaluate based on size; heads-up to the flop I’ll proceed cautiously OOP, looking to realize equity or float favorable boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 84o, which is a very weak starting hand. While I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 to win a pot of 600), the hand has extremely poor playability, especially out of position against two opponents including the Button raiser. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I called preflop with A♥T♣ to see a flop OOP and keep pots controllable. The flop 2♠-3♣-4♣ gives me no pair — just A-high — and only a backdoor club possibility (I need both turn and river to be clubs, ~4% to complete). This is a very coordinated, low board that hits many portions of the button’s raising range (small pairs, suited connectors, some two-club combos), so leading out OOP with a marginal hand is unattractive: I’d be building the pot with poor equity and without a plan to comfortably continue if I face resistance. Checking keeps the pot small, lets Claude c-bet into me (where I can call small/medium c-bets to realize equity or fold to large barrels), and preserves the option to take the pot away on favorable turns (A or T or a club runner). Plan: check, call modest c-bets to float or realize equity, fold to large turn/river aggression with no improvement.

Flop is 2s 3c 4c - low connected board with a club flush draw. I have Q5o which gives me a gutshot to the wheel (need an Ace). Minni checked to me. This is a good spot to c-bet - this low board doesn't connect well with typical BB defending ranges, and I have position plus a backdoor straight. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often.

I called preflop with A♥T♣ and checked the flop to keep the pot controllable. Claude’s 200 into 800 is a very small c-bet (≈25% pot) that a wide BTN raising range uses frequently — small pairs, suited connectors and two-club combos are all in range. My Ace is a useful blocker to many Ace-containing value combos and I have a backdoor club (need both turn+river clubs, ≈5% to complete), so I prefer to call and float rather than folding or turning a weak hand into a big bluff OOP. Raising out of position with only A-high would bloat the pot with poor equity and limited fold equity. Plan: call the 200 to realize equity and see a turn; if I hit an A, T, or club I can apply pressure, otherwise fold to strong turn/river aggression.

I called the small flop c-bet to keep the pot controllable and realize equity with my A‑high, planning to fold to meaningful turn/river aggression if I didn’t improve. The 7♥ on the turn changes nothing for me — I still have no pair, and the backdoor club route is dead (I needed both turn+river clubs). Claude’s small flop c‑bet signals a wide raising range that contains lots of hands that beat A‑high on this low, connected board (small pairs, 3/4/5-type hands, some 7x, and club combos). Betting out OOP here would bloat the pot with almost no value and limited fold equity; raising would be even worse. With 7,050 behind and a 1,000‑chip pot, the prudent line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful turn/river aggression, only continuing if I see a cheap river or pair/ten/club improvement that changes the hand.

The 7h on the turn doesn't change much. I now have an open-ended straight draw - I need an Ace (for the wheel A-2-3-4-5) or a 6 (for 2-3-4-5-6). That's 8 outs. Minni has checked again after calling my flop bet. I should continue betting as a semi-bluff - I have good equity if called and can win the pot now if Minni folds. Betting around half pot to apply pressure.

Claude (BTN) has been aggressive this hand — small flop c‑bet then a 500 turn into a ~1000 pot. My A♥T♣ missed: no pair, the backdoor club draw is dead, and I have only 6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Tens) — roughly ~13% equity to improve on the river. Calling 500 to win a 1500 pot requires ~25% equity, so a call is +EV only if I had many more outs or fold equity, which I do not. Raising OOP here with only Ace‑high would bloat the pot with very poor equity and limited fold equity versus a BTN continuer. Given the pot odds, my lack of improvement, and the desire to preserve 7050 chips for better spots, the correct play is to fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with A4 offsuit. This is a standard fold. The hand has poor post-flop playability, dominated by any other ace, weak kicker, offsuit, no straight potential. Early position requires tight range to avoid difficult spots OOP. Stacks are deep, but no reason to enter pot with marginal hand here. Preserve stack for better opportunities.

I hold 7h 9s in middle position (UTG+1). This is a weak offsuit hand (one-gapper) with very low playability and equity. Opening from this position with four players left to act is a losing strategy. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

T6o is a weak hand, even from the cutoff. While I'm in late position with only 3 players behind, this hand doesn't play well postflop and is below a standard cutoff opening range. With a healthy stack of 124 BBs, no need to take marginal spots. Folding.

I'm on the button with 4♥5♣ — a weak but connected hand that plays well in position. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call. With only the SB and BB left, I want to apply pressure and use position to take the pot or play the flop with initiative. I’ll open-raise rather than limp: 3x the big blind to 300 gives me a reasonable steal size (bigger than the min 200 so I don’t just invite the blinds for cheap) but keeps post‑3‑bet decisions manageable with a 70BB stack. If I get called I plan to use position to c‑bet on favorable flops and pot‑control on bad boards; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold this marginal holding.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen of hearts, a very strong starting hand. Minni has opened from the button for 300. This is a standard button steal attempt or value open with a wide range. KQs is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will 3-bet here to 1000 for value, to gain the initiative, and to discourage the Big Blind (FiveTwo) from entering the pot. This sizing (approx 3.3x) is standard when out of position.

We’re in the BB facing a BTN open and a SB 3-bet to 1000, with the original raiser still behind us. 9c5s is far too weak and dominated to continue: it has poor high-card value, makes weak pairs, and plays terribly multiway/in a 3-bet pot. Even though the immediate pot odds (900 to win 1400) look tempting, our realization is very low and we’ll often be forced to fold postflop or lose bigger pots when we pair. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive given our hand quality and the BTN’s ability to continue behind. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

Fold. Flash’s sizable 3‑bet to 1,000 after my 300 open is a strong/polarized action from the SB. 4♥5♣ is a weak, offsuit connector with poor playability and lots of domination against a typical SB 3‑bet range (high pairs, broadways, strong suited hands). Calling the extra 700 (1000 total) into ~1,400 would require ~33% equity vs his range — I’m well below that and have limited implied odds. A 4‑bet is out of the question with this holding. I preserve my ~67BB stack and wait for a better spot in position.

I am in UTG position with 5c 3d, which is an extremely weak unsuited hand (5-3 offsuit). Playing this hand from early position is statistically a losing play due to the high likelihood of being dominated and playing out of position against 5 other players. With a healthy stack of 110 BBs, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

AJo in middle position is a strong opening hand. Pro folded UTG, so I'm first to act. I should raise to my standard sizing of 2.5x (250) to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or isolate.

6♦2♦ is a garbage hand to play raised-from-early range. Claude opened to 250 from an earlier seat so his range is relatively strong and will dominate my low suited cards. To call 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38.5% equity (250/(400+250)); 6-2s is far below that vs an opener’s range and will be difficult to play postflop, especially if the button or blinds come along. I’ll preserve my 6,750 stack for better spots — fold.

I am on the button with 4h 6c, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has opened with a raise to 250 from middle position. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that there are still players behind me in the blinds, calling or raising is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket deuces play best as a set-mining call. We’re ~75bb deep with Claude covering, so the implied odds are there to call 200 more and try to flop a set, even though we’ll be out of position. 3-betting 22 from the SB vs a MP open is generally a low-EV bluff (no blockers, poor playability when called), and folding would be too tight given price/implied odds. Plan postflop: mostly fit-or-fold; continue strongly on set flops, otherwise proceed cautiously OOP.

T8o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability multiway. Facing a middle position raise to 250 (2.5x) and a small blind call, putting us three ways to the flop. While pot odds are favorable (150 to call into 750 pot), the hand has low equity against typical raising and calling ranges. Claude shows aggression from MP, and FiveTwo defends SB wide. Deep stacks, but folding preserves stack for premium spots. No strong reason to speculate here.

We completely whiffed: underpair to a highly connected, high-card flop (T-J-Q) with no backdoor flush and very poor equity vs Claude’s MP opening range. As the preflop caller OOP, our best line is to check and let him continuation-bet his range; leading would rarely fold out better and just builds a pot we can’t realize equity in. Plan: check/fold to most bets, only consider continuing vs a very small sizing with backdoor equity (which we don’t have).

I flopped middle pair (Jacks) with top kicker plus a gutshot to the nut straight (need K). Board is Tc-Js-Qc - very coordinated with a flush draw and straight possibilities. FiveTwo checked to me. I should c-bet for value and protection against draws. Standard sizing around half-pot to 2/3 pot makes sense here.

Claude’s 1/3-ish c-bet on T♣ J♠ Q♣ is going to be made with a very high frequency, and our 22 has extremely poor equity and realization OOP. We’re basically drawing to the last two deuces (~8.4% by river) plus a thin runner-runner club flush (~4.2%), still well short of the ~27% pot odds, and we don’t have good turn barreling prospects or relevant blockers to turn this into a profitable check-raise bluff. Best is to conserve stack and fold.
KQo is a strong hand from UTG. Standard opening raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate. This hand plays well and I've been successfully taking down pots with similar raises.

AhQc is a strong hand (AQo) for 6-max and plays very well in position vs an UTG opener. Claude has opened to 250 frequently, so 3-betting for value and isolation is the right plan — I want to take the initiative, deny cheap equity to the players behind, and play the flop in position if called. With ~67bbs I can size for standard value and fold equity; I’ll size to ~3.4x the open to 850. If Claude calls I’ll play postflop aggressively in position; if he 4-bets big I’ll fold (or reassess versus a small 4-bet). Calling would be too passive and folding is too weak, so a 3-bet to 850 maximizes expected value here.

I am holding King-Eight offsuit in the Cutoff. There has been an open from UTG (Claude) to 250 and a 3-bet from Middle Position (Minni) to 850. King-Eight offsuit is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet, especially when there is still action behind me and an original raiser yet to act. This is a standard fold.

Facing an UTG open and a MP 3-bet, K8o is too dominated to continue profitably. A cold call on the button also invites squeezes from the blinds and leaves us in tough multiway spots versus strong ranges, while a cold 4-bet bluff with poor blockers and no playability is unnecessarily high variance. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot/hand.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude followed by a 3-bet to 850 from Minni in middle position. My hand is Td5c, a weak offsuit connector with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 800 into 1250 pot for ~2.5:1), and postflop prospects are grim against likely strong ranges. Big blind still to act, but no value in continuing. Easy fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

I hold 2d 8s in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action involves a UTG open from Claude and a 3-bet to 850 from Minni. Facing a 3-bet with 8-2 offsuit offers poor equity and playability, especially out of position against two players showing significant strength. The cost to call is 750 chips, which is not justified by the pot odds or implied odds. The standard and only reasonable play here is to fold.

KQo facing a 3-bet from Minni. I need to call 600 into a pot of 1250, getting about 2:1 odds. KQo is a solid hand but tricky OOP against a 3-bet. 4-betting seems too aggressive with KQo - if I get 5-bet I'm in trouble. Folding is too tight given pot odds and my hand strength. Calling to see a flop is the balanced play here. Minni has shown some aggression but also folded to 3-bets, so this could be a wide range.

Flop is 9h Th 9c - paired board with a heart flush draw. I have KQ with two overcards and a gutshot (need J for straight). I called Minni's 3-bet so they have the initiative. Checking here is standard - I'm OOP against the 3-bettor with just overcards. If Minni c-bets, I can decide based on sizing. If checked back, I can see a free card. The paired board is somewhat favorable as it's less likely Minni connected with a 3-bet range.

Flop is 9h Th 9c and I hold AhQc — two overcards and the nut backdoor heart. I 3-bet preflop and Claude flat-called from UTG, so his continuing range is relatively strong (broadways, medium pairs, occasional 99/TT). He checked to me on the flop, which gives me an opportunity to apply pressure and take the pot or charge draws. I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 600). Size rationale: small enough to keep worse hands and floats guessing and to preserve pot control if called, but big enough to get folds from hands like Kx, Qx and other unpaired holdings and to charge backdoor draws. Plan after this bet: if he folds I collect; if he calls I proceed cautiously — bet for value on Ace/Queen turns, check-control on blanks and re-evaluate; if he raises me strongly I’ll assume a very strong made hand (sets/Ten) and fold to large aggression.

Facing a 600 bet into 1850 pot, I'm getting about 4:1 odds (need ~20% equity). I have KQ with two overcards (6 outs) plus a gutshot to the nuts with a J (4 outs) = ~10 outs, giving me roughly 20% equity on the turn. The paired board makes it less likely Minni connected with a 3-bet range (unlikely they have 99 or T9). Small c-bet sizing suggests possible thin value or probe. Math says call is breakeven to slightly +EV here.

The Qh is a great turn card for me! I now have top pair (Queens) with King kicker, plus my Kh gives me the nut flush draw. This dramatically improves my hand. I went from drawing to having a strong made hand with backup equity. Given Minni checked to me, I should bet for value. A bet around 60% pot (~1800) builds the pot with my strong hand while still giving Minni a chance to continue with worse queens, flush draws, or JJ. If they have a boat (99, TT) I'm in trouble but that's a small part of their 3-bet range.

I have top pair (Q) with the nut Ace kicker and a four‑heart draw (Ah + 9h Th Qh = one heart from the nut flush). Claude opened UTG and called my 3‑bet pre, then called my small flop c‑bet — his continuing range contains many Qx, some medium pairs (TT/99), and strong broadways. His turn sizing (1,800 into ~4,850) is relatively small and can represent thin value or a blocker/port of a balanced line, so I need to use pot‑odds/relative equity. Calling 1,800 gives ~27% pot odds (1800/(4850+1800)); with top pair + A‑kicker and a one‑card nut flush draw my equity vs his betting range is comfortably above that. Raising to 3,600 would bloat the pot and commit too much against sets/two‑pair combos that dominate me; folding is too weak. Plan: call 1,800, see the river, and then decide — if I complete the flush I will value‑bet; if the board pairs or he shows heavy river aggression I will respect full‑house/set lines and fold as necessary.

The river Qs gives me Queens full of nines (QQQ99) - an absolute monster! The board is 99QQ and I hold Qd. Minni has only 3500 left after calling my turn bet. They 3-bet pre and called my turn lead, so likely have a big pair (JJ-AA), AQ, or completed a flush. I need to extract maximum value. Betting around 2500 (38% pot) looks value-oriented enough that flushes and overpairs might hero call, while still building the pot significantly. If they have AQ we chop, but most of their range I'm crushing.

River: 9h Th 9c Qh Qs. My hand Ah Qc gives me Qc + Qh + Qs = three queens plus the pair of nines on board — a full house (Queens full of Nines). Very few hands beat me (only quads of nines are realistic; quads of queens are impossible since I hold Qc and two queens are on board). Claude’s line (UTG open, call of my 3-bet, check-call small flop bet, turn raise, small river bet) is exactly the sort of line a Qx / strong two-pair/full-house candidate would take, and his river sizing is small — he’s incentivizing a call. Pot is 9,150 and he bet 2,500; calling is +EV (you get ~21.5% pot odds), but with nearly the nut full house I should maximize value. I’ll raise to the min‑raise/all‑in (3,500) to extract extra value from worse full houses, Qx, 9x, two-pairs and other strong hands that will call the extra 1,000. The chance he has quads is tiny given his flop and turn play, so shove for value.

I have Queens full of nines (QQQ99). Minni min-raised me 1000 more. The only hand that beats me is 99 (quad nines), which is extremely unlikely in a 3-bet range. If Minni has AQ or KQ, we chop. Getting 12.65:1 pot odds, this is an easy call. Even if they have a flush or overpairs, I'm crushing. Folding a full house for a min-raise would be a massive mistake.

UTG with 9♥3♣ — a weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with very poor playability. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to limp; the min-open would be to 200. In 6-max UTG I should be opening only a much stronger, disciplined range. Limping hands like this often get dominated or put in tough multiway spots, and raising invites isolation and 3-bets from the frequent aggressive raisers at this table. With a healthy ~68bb stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct. Fold.

I am in middle position with 97 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially with four players left to act behind me. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

CO is a profitable steal spot with only the blinds left and both early positions already folded. T4s is near the bottom of an opening range, but suited gives enough playability in position, and raising applies immediate pressure to pick up the 150 in dead money. I’ll use the table’s common open size to keep ranges credible; if faced with a 3-bet I’ll generally just fold this hand.

Preflop on the button facing a standard 2.5x raise to 250 from the cutoff (FiveTwo). My hand Js4c is a complete trash hand: offsuit, non-connected, low cards with minimal playability even in position. FiveTwo has been an aggressive raiser in previous hands, often opening from late position, but this hand has no showdown value, poor implied odds, and folding avoids unnecessary risk to my deep 124BB stack. Multiway pots are possible with blinds behind, but no reason to speculate with this. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I hold AKo in the Small Blind, a premium hand. The player in the Cutoff (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. FiveTwo has been active, raising frequently, and history suggests he calls 3-bets pre-flop but often folds to aggression on the flop if he misses. To maximize value and define the range, as well as to discourage the Big Blind from entering, a 3-bet is the optimal strategy. Being out of position, I will size my 3-bet to roughly 3.6x the open raise, making it 900 total.

73 offsuit is a trash hand. Facing a 3-bet from Pro to 900, I need to call 800 more with one of the worst hands in poker. This is a trivial fold - no equity against a 3-betting range, no playability post-flop.

Pro’s small blind 3-bet to 900 versus my CO open is a range that’s generally strong and/or well-constructed (broadways, good suited connectors, and value). T4s is near the bottom of my steal range and performs poorly versus a typical SB 3-bet range (dominated when we make a ten, weak playability), and calling would create an awkward ~3–4 SPR spot where I’ll be forced to overfold postflop. A 4-bet bluff isn’t attractive with this hand and these stacks. Best EV is to cut losses and fold as planned versus resistance.

I am UTG with T4s. This is a very weak hand to open from the earliest position in a 6-max game. Given the aggressive nature of players like Claude and Elon who are still to act, there is a high likelihood of being 3-bet or finding myself in a difficult post-flop situation out of position. The standard and most disciplined play here is to fold.

94o is well below our opening range from middle position in a 6-max game. Limping is -EV (invites isolation and plays poorly postflop), and opening risks getting 3-bet or called by superior ranges with weak playability. With 73bb and no dead money beyond blinds, we preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Preflop in cutoff with two folds ahead (UTG and MP). Qs5d is a playable hand from CO in an unopened pot due to position. Standard stealing spot against blinds. Opponents behind have shown aggressive tendencies but Minni in BB is shorter at 67BB. Raise to 2.5x BB size of 250 to build pot or take blinds. Deep stack allows flexibility postflop.

I am on the Button with Qd7d. The Cutoff (Elon) has raised to 250. Elon has been quite active and aggressive recently (e.g., cold 4-betting Hand #20, active raising). While Q7s is a suited hand with some playability, it is generally below the standard calling range against a Cutoff open, even on the Button. It is easily dominated by better Queens (KQ, QJ, QT) and faces reverse implied odds. Furthermore, having just 3-bet the previous hand (Hand #28) with AK, aggressive action again might be met with resistance. Calling invites the blinds into the pot. Folding is the disciplined and standard decision here to preserve chips for better spots.
93o is a weak hand facing a cutoff raise. Poor hand to defend from SB - it's offsuit, not connected well, and will be difficult to play postflop out of position. Easy fold.

I’m in the big blind with T9o (9c Td) facing a CO open to 250. Pot is 400 and I only need to add 150 (stack ~67BB). T9o is a playable, connected hand with good post‑flop equity and playability — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity out of the BB rather than committing chips with a marginal 3‑bet. A min‑raise to 400 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity vs an aggressive opener, and a larger 3‑bet would be committing too much with a non‑premium hand. Plan: call to see the flop; proceed on top pair/strong draws or fold to heavy aggression on dry flops.

Flop is 8d 8c 9h; I hold T9o — a pair of nines with a 10 kicker. Heads‑up vs Elon's CO open, his range contains relatively few 8s/99s (sets/trips) but lots of overcards, broadways and medium pairs. My hand is ahead of a large portion of his continuing range (we beat lower pocket pairs and many broadways) and only loses to a small subset of hands (88, 99, and 9x with a better kicker). The board is dry and paired, so I want thin value and to charge any hands that will call (we also deny him a free card). I’ll bet about half the pot to extract value while keeping the pot manageable if he raises. If he raises big I’ll assume a very strong holding and fold or re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll re‑assess on the turn. Action: bet ~1/2 pot to get value and protect my hand.

Preflop raise with Qs5d from CO was a standard steal attempt into the blinds. Minni's check-call preflop from BB gives her a wide range including suited connectors, broadways, pairs. Flop 8d8c9h is a paired board that hits her calling range hard (pairs, straight draws like J10, 97, 76, sets). My hand is queen-high with only backdoor flush draw and no straight outs, zero showdown value, and poor implied odds even if I hit. Her 275 bet into 825 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard value/protection bet from her perspective. Calling risks stacking off light later or wasting chips; raising as a bluff is too thin with 5 players behind and my stack (121BB) not needing to bluff here. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots. Opponents' histories show aggression but this spot is clear.

Pocket tens is a clear value open UTG in 6-max. I want to build the pot versus dominated broadways/smaller pairs, while also reducing the chance we go multiway. Given the table’s common open sizing around 250, I’ll use 2.5x to keep my range consistent and avoid giving good implied-odds to the blinds with a min-raise size. If faced with a 3-bet, I’ll proceed based on sizing and opponent (often calling in position versus reasonable sizes, tightening versus very large 4-bet/stack-commit lines).

Ad9d is a strong suited ace hand with good post-flop playability, nut flush potential, and straight draws. FiveTwo has been aggressively raising from early position and folding to 3-bets or aggression in prior hands (e.g., Hand 28 vs Pro, Hand 12). Facing an UTG raise into a deep stack tournament spot (hand 30/150), a 3-bet is optimal for fold equity, isolation, and building the pot when called. Sizing to 850 (about 3.4x the open) balances value and pressure. Calling is too passive OOP, folding too tight.

I hold Ten of Hearts and Five of Clubs (T5o) in the Cutoff. The action has started with an UTG raise to 250 followed by a 3-bet from the Middle Position to 850. T5o is a very weak hand with no playability, especially facing a raise and a re-raise from early positions which indicate strong ranges. I have no equity to justify calling or bluffing here. The standard and only correct play is to fold.

I have QQ on the button - a premium hand. FiveTwo opened to 250, Elon 3-bet to 850. This is a perfect spot for a 4-bet. QQ is too strong to just call here and risk playing a multiway pot OOP. A 4-bet to around 2.2-2.5x the 3-bet size is standard. I'll make it 2300 to isolate and build the pot with my strong hand.

I’m facing a button 4-bet to 2300 after an UTG open and a 3-bet — that sequence is heavy on premium value hands. My J♥6♣ is a marginal, easily dominated holding with poor equity versus those ranges. The pot is 3550 and it costs me 2250 (≈22.5bb) to call; I’d need ~39% equity to break even on a call, which J6o does not have against a 4-bet/3-bet combo. I’d also be out of position postflop and still have ~48bb left if I call, so calling or attempting a squeeze would commit a large portion of my stack in a spot where I’m likely behind or playing multiway against strong hands. I’ll preserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot.

I am facing a significant amount of action before it even reaches me. FiveTwo opened, Elon 3-bet, and Claude has now 4-bet to 2300. My hand, Ace-Three offsuit (A3o), is very weak in this context. Even if I were to consider a bluff, 5-betting into a 4-bettor who has position on me is extremely risky and likely to be unprofitable. My hand is easily dominated by Claude's value range (JJ+, AK) and even his potential bluffs. The best move is to fold and preserve my stack.

We’re facing an UTG open (me) → MP 3-bet (Elon) → BTN 4-bet (Claude) to 2300. With TT and 70.5bb effective, a 5-bet jam has poor EV versus a likely value-heavy 4-bet range (QQ+/AK) because our fold equity is small and we’d often be flipping or crushed. Folding is too tight given we’re getting ~36–37% pot-odds (2050 to win ~5600 after calling) and TT retains ~39% raw equity versus QQ+/AK. Calling also keeps in any occasional 4-bet bluffs (A5s/KQo type) and avoids turning our hand into a low-fold-equity shove.

Plan: call and play with the low SPR; on low boards (no A/K/Q) we can often commit (check-jam versus c-bets). On A/K/Q-heavy flops we can proceed cautiously and be prepared to check-fold versus strong sizing/multiway action.

Ad9d is a strong speculative hand but facing a 4-bet from Claude on the button after my initial 3-bet isolation over FiveTwo's UTG open, and with FiveTwo flat-calling the large 4-bet sizing (now pot-committed with 5000 behind), this is a tough multiway OOP spot. Claude's range is likely premium (QQ+/AK) given the aggression over a 3-bet, while FiveTwo's call indicates strength too. Folding preserves my deep 11300 stack for superior positions later in the tournament (hand 30/150), as calling lacks fold equity postflop and shoving/raising risks too much equity against nuts. Better spots abound.

4-bet pot, heads-up and out of position with an overpair on a very low flop. With an SPR well under 1, I don’t gain much by donk-leading: it mostly gets called by QQ+ (where we’re in bad shape) and can fold out (or at least discourage) AK-high hands that would often continuation-bet if checked to. Checking my range here is standard in 4-bet pots OOP, keeps Claude’s bluffs/AK in, and sets up an efficient check-jam vs a c-bet given the shallow remaining stack.

I have QQ as an overpair on a 4c2c5h board - excellent spot. FiveTwo called my 4-bet preflop and checked to me. This low board is great for my hand. I should bet for value and protection against club draws and potential gutshots. FiveTwo has 5000 behind with 5600 in pot. I'll bet around 2800-3000 (roughly half pot) to extract value while keeping them in with worse hands.

4-bet pot, heads-up, and this flop (4c-2c-5h) is excellent for TT: no overcards and Claude’s small c-bet size is consistent with range-betting (AK/AQ as well as QQ+). With only 5,000 total and 2,800 to call, the SPR is tiny—calling just leaves ~2.2k behind and creates awkward turn decisions on A/K/Q/J runouts while giving two-overcard hands a free chance to realize equity.

By check-jamming to 5,000 we maximize value/protection versus AK/AQ (which will usually have the odds to continue anyway), simplify the hand, and avoid letting equity shift on later streets. We’re not relying on fold equity; we’re committing with a hand that’s near the top of our range on this texture.

FiveTwo is all-in (stack shows 0). Pot is 13400, I need to call only 2200 more. Getting 6:1 pot odds, I need ~14% equity. With QQ overpair on 4c2c5h, even against sets some % of the time, my equity is way higher than needed. FiveTwo's 4-bet calling range could include sets (22,44,55), club draws, or bluffs with AK/AQ. This is a mandatory call.

I am in UTG position holding 6s 7d, which is an unsuited connector. From early position, this hand is extremely weak and has negative expected value to play. With 4 players yet to act, including a deep stack in UTG+1 and aggressive players in the blinds, the risk of getting 3-bet or playing a multi-way pot out of position with a weak hand is too high. The standard and correct play is to fold.
2h4h is a very weak hand, especially from UTG+1. Suited connectors/gappers need to be stronger to open from early position. This is an easy fold.

Button with Qc7c — a suited Queen with decent postflop play and strong positional advantage. Pot is only 150 and calling 100 is cheap, but I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds (Flash SB, Elon BB) to steal the pot or play a heads-up pot in position. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB): big enough to gain fold equity and deter loose defends, small enough to keep the pot manageable if I get called. Plan: proceed aggressively postflop with flush/straight draws and any Q/top-pair; fold to a large, polarized 3-bet/shove from Elon (he’s shown willingness to make big preflop plays), consider calling smallish squeezes only with appropriate odds and implied equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 8h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. While 78 offsuit can occasionally be used as a 3-bet bluff, it generally performs poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the fact that I would have to play the entire hand out of position against the button opener, folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x raise) after SB folds. Holding 4d5s offsuit, a marginal speculative hand at best. Pot is 450, calling 200 more offers ~2.25:1 (31% equity break-even), and vs. a button opening range (~35-50% of hands), 45o has ~38-42% raw equity but plays poorly OOP with low SPR potential and vulnerable to overcards/c-bets. Postflop dynamics favor folding weak connectors OOP against an aggressive button like Minni (history of raises and bluffs). Preserve stack for better spots; disciplined fold maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Pocket 8s from UTG is a solid opening hand. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate. This hand plays well, can flop sets, and has good equity against overcards. I should raise for value and to define ranges.

UTG raised to 250 into a 400 pot. I'm UTG+1 with 9c7h — a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into 400 gives ~38% pot odds, but 9‑7o has well under that equity versus a standard UTG opening range and will often be dominated and very difficult to play postflop, especially with Button and the blinds still to act. Claude has opened frequently and looks aggressive from early position, so his range is likely strong. A light 3‑bet here is risky (I don't have good blockers and would be OOP vs the field), so the +EV line is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Jack-Four offsuit (J4o). Claude opened from UTG with a 2.5x raise. J4o is a very weak starting hand and does not play well against an UTG range, even with positional advantage. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop from SB facing an UTG raise to 250 from Claude, who plays aggressively but from early position likely has a strong range (top 10-15% hands like pairs, broadway, suited aces). Jc6c is a marginal suited connector/gapper with poor playability and equity against that range (~30-35% at best heads-up, less multiway if BB defends). Pot odds are 400:200 (2:1), but implied odds are mediocre due to hand's capped strength postflop. No reason to raise. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with deep stacks.

I hold [5h 2s] in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. My hand is statistically very weak (bottom 10%) and has poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against a strong UTG opening range. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, approx 3.6:1), my equity realization will be very low due to position and hand quality. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to preserve chips.

UTG with K♥4♥ is a marginal hand and should be folded here. In a five-handed game I’m first to act and face four players behind — many of whom have shown aggressive 3-bet/opening tendencies (Elon, Claude, Flash). K4s is frequently dominated by better Kx hands and doesn’t play well multiway or out of position, so opening risks getting 3‑bet or called into difficult spots. I have a healthy ~72 BB stack, so preserve chips for a better position/hand and fold.

I am in UTG+1 with Ace-Four offsuit. In a 5-handed game, this hand is at the bottom of a potential opening range from this position. Given that there are several active and aggressive players behind me (Elon on the button and Claude in the big blind), A4o is easily dominated and difficult to play post-flop if called or reraised. I will choose the conservative route and fold to maintain my stack for a better starting hand.

Folded to the button in a 5-handed game with 94o, a marginal stealing hand but positionally advantageous. The pot is 150, min-raise to 200, but a standard 2.5x raise to 250 provides good fold equity against the blinds. Pro (SB) and Claude (BB) have shown willingness to defend but also fold frequently to aggression. Post-flop position allows continuation if called. Deep stacks support taking this spot to accumulate blinds early without high risk. Folding trash hands too often misses dead money.

I hold Queen-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button (Elon). Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Although the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) might suggest raw equity is close, my realized equity will be poor due to the positional disadvantage and the likelihood of being dominated. Defending here is a losing play in the long run. The standard and prudent action is to fold.

I have 8d5d in the BB facing a button open to 250. Need to call 150 into a 400 pot (getting ~2.67:1). Suited hands with some connectivity play well in defended pots. Against a likely wide button range, this is a reasonable defend. The suited diamonds give flush potential and the 8-5 has some straight possibilities.

I flopped middle pair (8s) with a weak kicker on Js Ah 8c. This is a check-call spot against a button opener. I have showdown value but shouldn't be donk betting - checking to the preflop aggressor is standard. If Elon bets, I'll likely call one street given my pair, but I'm not strong enough to build a big pot.

Preflop raise on the button with 94o was a standard stealing attempt against a folding field, building the pot to 550. Flop Js Ah 8c completely misses my range but is a good board for continuation betting as the preflop raiser in position. Claude's check from the BB suggests he doesn't have top pair Ax or better very often and may have weaker Ax, pairs below J, or draws. My hand has zero equity but excellent fold equity here; betting maintains aggression, denies equity to his floats/draws, and takes it down often. Half-pot sizing (around 275-350) balances value and bluffs effectively deepstacked. Position allows me to shut down post-turn if check-raised or called large. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and misses stealing opportunity against his likely weak range.

I have middle pair (8s) with a weak kicker on Js Ah 8c. Getting 900:350 (~2.57:1 odds). Elon's button c-bet is standard and includes lots of air/draws. My 8s have showdown value against his bluffs (KQ, KT, etc.) but I'm behind Ax, Jx, overpairs. Pot odds justify a call, but I'm folding to significant turn aggression without improvement.

I turned two pair (8s and 5s)! Elon checked the turn after betting the flop, which suggests he's slowing down with a medium-strength hand or giving up on air. With two pair I should bet for value - I beat all single pair hands including Ax and Jx. A ~50% pot bet extracts value from his medium hands while keeping the pot manageable if he has a monster.

Facing a turn lead of 625 into 1875 after calling my flop c-bet with 94o, which completely misses the Ah Js 8c 5s board. My preflop raise was a standard button steal, and flop bet had good fold equity on a dry board, but Claude's call indicates strength like Ax, Jx, 88, 99-TT, or draws (e.g., spade flush draw now live with 5s). His turn lead after checking flop suggests he improved (two pair, set, straight draw like 67/9T) or value with top pair. My hand has no equity, no blockers to his value range, and poor implied odds deepstacked. Calling gives up position and invites further aggression; raising as a bluff is too thin with reverse position and his likely capped calling range. Pot odds ~3:1 not enough for 0% equity hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in 5-handed tournament.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 6h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Playing such hands out of position is a recipe for losing chips over the long term. Standard professional play is to fold this hand immediately.

9c6d is a trash hand offsuit with poor connectivity and no high card strength. In UTG+1 position with 5 players left, this is a clear fold preflop. No need to speculate or bluff with such a weak holding early in the tournament phase (hand 34/150). Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with A6o, and action has folded to me. In a 5-handed game, this is a standard spot to raise to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. An Ace blocker is useful, and while the kicker is weak, position provides a significant advantage. The standard opening size at this table has been 2.5bb (250), which I will use to apply pressure to the blinds (Claude with a deep stack and Minni with a mid-stack). If I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold.
4s7h is a weak hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Out of position with a hand that has poor playability. Easy fold.

Facing a BTN open to 250 from the big blind with Q♠8♣ and 71BB effective, this is a defendable but marginal hand — not strong enough for a 3-bet for value and I don't want to take an OOP bluff-raise line with a mediocre hand. Calling costs 150 more to win a 400 pot (≈27% equity needed) and vs a likely wide BTN opener I have reasonable equity and implied odds to continue on many flops. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: look to fold to heavy, well-timed aggression on dry boards and continue with top pair, strong draws, or favorable textures.

Check. I missed this J♥4♥4♣ flop with Q♠8♣ — no pair, no heart draw and I'm out of position versus the preflop raiser. I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than bluffing OOP on a board that strongly favors the opener's range (many Jx and 4x combos). Plan: check-call small continuation bets (thin value/float) and fold to large, well-sized aggression; if Pro checks back I can take a free turn card or barreled bluff on a favourable runout. This is a pot-control, fold-to-large-bet line to preserve my stack and avoid marginal confrontations.

I have Ace-high (Ad6s) on a paired board of Jh 4h 4c. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, this texture hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (trips, pairs). My Ace-high has showdown value against missed broadway hands or lower disconnected cards. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (pairs, draws) or folds out hands I am already beating (K-high, Q-high). Checking back allows me to realize equity, keeps the pot small, and protects my checking range. I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I check. With Q♠8♣ on J♥4♥4♣3♠ I have no pair and no heart draw and I’m out of position to the BTN opener who checked back the flop. This is a dry turn card that doesn’t improve my hand and betting OOP here would bloat the pot with little fold equity against a range that contains many Jx/4x and some floats. Pot control is the right line: take a free river, fold to substantial aggression on later streets, and only continue to small river bets or when the runout gives me a real showdown or bluffing opportunity (e.g. I pair my Q/8 or a very favorable blank).

I hold Ace-high (Ad6s) on a board of Jh 4h 4c 3s. After I checked back the flop, Minni checked to me again on the turn. The 3s is a safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. My Ace-high still has showdown value against Minni's missed flush draws or lower high-card hands. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (like KQ, QT) and get called by hands that beat me (pairs, trips). By checking, I control the pot size and realize my equity, hoping to get to showdown cheaply or catch an Ace on the river.

I have Q♠8♣ on J♥4♥4♣3♠5♣ — no pair, no real draw. I defended preflop and took a pot‑control line (check on flop and turn). Pro’s checks on both flop and turn narrow his range toward hands with real pair value (Jx, 4x, small pocket pairs) or showdown/medium hands — a single‑street bluff OOP on the river is unlikely to fold enough of that range. The river 5♣ doesn’t help me (it actually helps some parts of his range), so a bet would be a thin, low‑EV bluff that needlessly risks chips. I’ll check and take showdown; if Pro fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold rather than chase low‑equity calls.

I hold Ad6s on a board of Jh 4h 4c 3s 5c. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn, and Minni (Big Blind) checks to me again on the river. My hand is a pair of Fours with an Ace kicker (A-J-5-4-4). The board is somewhat connected with possible straights (A-2 or 2-6) and the 5 pairing the board for anyone holding a 5. However, Minni's triple check line indicates significant weakness, likely missed flush draws (hearts) or unconnected high cards. My Ace-high has good showdown value against missed draws. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (King-high, Queen-high) and get called by any pair (3x, 5x, Jx) or better. There is little value in betting for value, and turning Ace-high into a bluff to fold out a weak pair like 22 or 3x is risky and unnecessary given the likelihood of winning at showdown. Therefore, I will check back to realize my equity.

UTG position with 2c4h, an extremely weak, offsuit, gapped hand with no playability. Folding is standard as it has zero equity against calling ranges and no fold equity from early position. Preserves stack for better spots in this multi-hand tournament.

I hold Ace-Jack suited (AcJc) in UTG+1 (effectively the Hijack in a 5-handed game after UTG folded). This is a strong premium hand that clearly warrants an opening raise to build the pot and take the initiative. The standard opening size at this table has been 2.5bb (250 chips), which provides a good balance of fold equity and value. I have 114bb, so I am deep enough to play this hand aggressively. I will raise to 250.

K9o on the button facing an EP open of 250. This is a playable hand with position. Calling is reasonable - we have the button and can outplay postflop. 3-betting is possible but K9o is marginal against UTG+1 range. Calling 250 to see a flop in position.

I have pocket 3s in the SB facing a 2.5x open to 250 with ~69bb effective. This is a classic set-mining spot — calling preserves my deep-stack implied odds and keeps the pot manageable out of position. 3-betting is unnecessary (I have no blockers and would be playing a small pair bloated OOP), and folding a pocket pair here is too nitty. I will call 200 more (250 total committed) to see the flop; plan to proceed cautiously on dry, face-carded boards and to extract maximum value if I hit a set.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6s 4d. Pro opened with a raise to 250 from early position, and both Claude and Minni have called. Although I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 850, roughly 5.6:1), 64 offsuit is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly in a multi-way pot. Being out of position against three other players makes it even more difficult to play post-flop. I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I have pocket 3s and completely missed this J‑8‑7 rainbow flop. This is a multiway pot (preflop raiser + caller) and I’m out of position — with a small pair I want to control the pot and preserve my set‑mining equity. Betting into two players is value‑negative (I’ll get called by better hands and can be raised off my equity), so I will check and see a cheap turn. Plan if faced with action: call a small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 280) to keep set‑mining alive; fold to a large c‑bet/raise (≈>1/2 pot ≈ 425) since I have no real redraws; if both check to the turn I’ll continue cautiously and only start extracting or defending if I improve to a set or pick up a clear read.

I hold AcJc (Top Pair, Top Kicker) on a 7s-8h-Jd board. The pot is 3-way with the Button and Small Blind. This board texture (7-8-J) hits flat-calling ranges quite hard (sets, two pair, straight draws like T9). In a multi-way pot, c-betting TPTK on wet boards is often too thin or opens me up to difficult raises. Checking allows me to control the pot size, keep my opponents' ranges wide (including bluffs), and evaluate the turn. I am strong enough to check-call a bet, but betting myself risks isolating myself against better hands or being forced to fold to a raise.

Flop is 7s8hJd. I have K9o - no pair but two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (need a T). Both opponents checked to me in position. This is a good spot for a position bet to take it down or build equity. Betting ~40% pot.

I have pocket threes (3♠3♦) and completely missed the J♢‑8♥‑7♠ rainbow flop. This is a classic set‑mining spot multiway and out of position — Claude’s bet of 350 into a 1,200 pot (~29% pot) is small. Calling preserves my deep‑stack implied odds (if I hit a set I can still get paid) and keeps the pot controlled; 3‑betting OOP with a small pair here is reckless and folding to such a small c‑bet surrenders too much equity. Pot math: after Claude’s bet the pot is ~1,550, so calling 350 is ~22.6% pot odds; my chance to make a set by the river is only ~8–9%, so this isn’t profitable purely as a raw draw, but implied odds and the cheap price make a call correct. Plan: call now to see the turn; fold to a large raise or significant pressure (I’ll let go if Pro/Claude raise big), and only start building the pot if I improve to a set or get a clear read. Total committed after calling will be 600 chips.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcJc) on a J-8-7 rainbow board. After checking the flop, the Button bet small (350 into 850) and the Small Blind called. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 350 to win a total pot of 1900) and my hand is well ahead of a wide range of one-pair hands and straight draws that my opponents likely hold. Raising here would likely only get action from better hands (sets, two pair) or strong draws, while folding out the bluffs or weaker hands I beat. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while out of position and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have pocket 3s and did not improve on the 7♠‑8♥‑J♦‑2♥ runout. This is a multiway pot and I’m out of position with deep stacks, so pot‑control is the priority. I only have 2 outs to hit a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35%). With the current pot at 1,900, the largest turn bet I could profitably call is roughly ≈90 chips (solve b/(1900+2b) ≤ 0.0435). Since a practical min bet is ≥100, calling any realistic bet is negative EV. I will check to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot; plan is to fold to meaningful turn bets/raises, and if everyone checks to the river I’ll check back and only extract or bluff if I improve or get a very clear read.

On the turn, the board is 7s 8h Jd 2h. I hold Ac Jc (Top Pair, Top Kicker). The pot is 1900. The 2h brings a backdoor flush draw but is otherwise a brick. The action checked to me after the Small Blind checked. On the flop, I check-called a bet from the Button (Claude), as did the Small Blind. 

Leading out (donking) here into two opponents after checking the flop is rarely optimal; it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands (sets, two pair). Checking allows the initial aggressor (Claude) to continue firing with his bluffs or worse value hands (like KJ, QJ, or draws). I am currently ahead of most one-pair hands and draws. My plan is to check and evaluate the action behind me. If Claude bets again, I will likely call to keep his range wide. If it checks through, I can value bet the river.

Turn is a blank 2h. I have K-high with a gutshot (need a T). Both Minni and Pro called my flop bet, indicating they likely have at least a pair. Continuing to barrel into two callers with just K-high is risky - I'll check back to control pot size and potentially hit my gutshot or bluff the river if checked to again.

I hold pocket threes (3♠3♦) and the river (7♠‑8♥‑J♦‑2♥‑6♦) is a complete miss — I never improved. This is a multiway pot (Pro/Claude called action earlier and both checked the turn), so many hands that saw the flop will beat me: any pair of J/8/7/6/2, two‑pair, sets, and straight combos (9‑10 or 4‑5) are all in their ranges. Betting here would be a thin bluff that won’t fold many better hands and would get called or raised by hands that dominate me; it’s not a good spot to manufacture fold equity or extract value. With 1,900 in the pot and no bet to call, the correct line is to control the pot and take my small showdown equity — check to showdown and conserve chips. Action: check (amount 0).

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcJc) on a board of 7s-8h-Jd-2h-6d. The river 6d is a coordination card that completes the T9 straight draw (7-8-9-T-J) and potentially 54. Minni's line of check-calling the flop and checking down is consistent with a draw like T9 that may have just hit, or a medium strength hand. Claude bet the flop small but checked back the turn, capping his range, though he could also have T9. Betting here is thin because worse hands (weak Jx, 8x) might fold on this scary runout, while straights and two-pairs will raise or call. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value without reopening the action to a raise, and potentially induces a bluff from Claude or allows him to check back a worse Jack. Given the multi-way pot and the completed straight draw, pot control is the prudent play.

River 6d completes some straights (9T has 6-7-8-9-T). I have K-high which loses to any pair. Both opponents called flop and checked turn/river - they likely have weak made hands like small pairs. Bluffing into two players who've shown interest is too risky. Checking and giving up is the best play here.

I am holding 7s 2c in the UTG position. This is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Opening from Under the Gun with this hand is extremely negative EV. I have a healthy stack of over 120 big blinds, so there is no desperation to play. The correct play is to fold immediately.
5-6 offsuit is a weak hand, especially from early position (UTG+1). With 4 players left to act behind me, this hand has poor playability and limited equity. Easy fold.

I open-raise to 300 (3x BB). On the button with J9o in a three-handed pot this is a clear open: I want to leverage position and steal the blinds or go heads-up with a playable hand. A 3x sizing reduces multiway callers versus a min-raise and gives good fold equity against the small blind while still keeping weaker hands in play. My stack (~64 BB) supports a standard raise here. If I get called, I’ll c-bet favorable flops (dry, high-card or J/T flops) and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards. If Elon (BB) 3-bets large I’ll usually fold J9o, since it’s often dominated; I’ll re-evaluate facing a small 3-bet based on sizing and stack-to-pot. Overall this raise maximizes my positional advantage and long-term EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine of Spades, a premium suited connector with excellent post-flop potential. Minni has opened from the Button for 300. In a 5-handed game, 9sTs is well ahead of a typical Button opening range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and discourage the Big Blind from entering. If called, my hand plays very well on most boards and my range remains polarized and strong.

78o is a weak offsuit hand with minimal playability. Facing a button open-raise to 300 (3x BB) and a significant small blind squeeze-raise to 1000 (over 3x the open), this range is very strong. Pot is 1400, calling 900 commits ~9% of my stack into a pot where I'll be OOP against two aggressive opponents (Minni on button, Flash SB). No fold equity if I shove given stacks (Flash 8150 effective). History shows Flash and Minni aggressive but winning with strong bets; no incentive to defend trash. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament.

I opened to 300 on the button with Js9d and the SB (Flash) 3-bet to 1000. Pot is 1400 and I must call 700 more — calling to a total of 1000 makes the pot 2100, so I need ~33% equity. J9o is marginal but has solid playability in position and can realize extra equity post‑flop. Flash has shown willingness to 3‑bet large earlier, so his range is likely wider than pure premiums. I have no strong blockers for an effective 4‑bet bluff and I’m not strong enough to 4‑bet for value, so a 4‑bet is a negative EV play here. Folding surrenders too much equity and position after already investing 300. I’ll call to see a flop, use position to control the pot, c‑bet on favorable dry flops, and proceed cautiously (folding to heavy action on very unfavorable or highly coordinated boards).

On this Qs-Jh-Qd board, I have missed with my Ten-Nine of Spades, though I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). This board texture is quite coordinated and likely hits a lot of Minni's 3-bet calling range, which includes hands like pocket pairs (TT, JJ), suited connectors (JTs), and broadway cards (KQs, AJs, AQ). As the out-of-position pre-flop aggressor, I will check to see how Minni reacts. If Minni checks back, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or a favorable turn. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the price to continue with my gutshot.

Pot is 2100. I hold J♠9♦ on Q♠ J♥ Q♦ — second pair (jacks) with a weak kicker on a paired board. Flash 3‑bet preflop, so his range contains lots of Qx, JJ, AQ/AK and other hands that now often have trips/full houses or dominate my pair. Betting here would build a pot when I’m frequently behind and would mainly get called by better jacks or slow‑played monsters; my fold equity is low. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking. Plan: use position to evaluate the turn — call small/medium turn bets for pot control, fold to large aggression, and only pursue value on the river if the card improves me or he shows clear weakness.

On this turn of 5d, which is a total brick, the situation hasn't changed much from the flop. I still have Ten-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Minni's check on the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a Queen, or she's playing a medium-strength hand like a Jack or a pocket pair cautiously. By checking again, I am trying to realize my equity and see a free river card. If Minni checks back, I get to see the river for free. If she bets, I'll evaluate the price to see if my 4-out gutshot is worth chasing based on the pot odds and her sizing.

I have second pair (Jacks) with a weak 9 kicker on Q♠ J♥ Q♦ 5♦. Flash 3‑bet preflop so his range contains a lot of Qx, QQ, JJ and strong broadways — many of which now beat or tie me (trips/full houses and better Jx combos). He checked both the flop and turn, which could be either weakness or a slowplay, so betting here would bloat the pot when I’m frequently behind and get value mostly from better hands. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking. Plan: call small/medium bets for pot control, fold to large aggression, and re‑evaluate on the river — if the river improves me (J or 9) or he shows clear weakness I can extract thin value; otherwise avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On the river, the King of Clubs completes my gutshot straight (9-10-J-Q-K). However, the board is paired (Queens) and any Ace in Minni's range (like Ace-Ten) now makes a higher straight (Broadway). Given that both of us checked the flop and the turn, it is possible she is slow-playing a monster (like a Queen for trips or a full house) or has a marginal hand like a Jack or a pocket pair. By checking, I can induce a bluff from her missed draws or a value bet from her Jacks and Kings, which I can then comfortably call. If I bet, I risk her folding hands I beat or being raised by the few hands that beat me (AT, QX, or a boat). Checking is the safest way to realize my equity and potentially extract value from a bet she might make thinking I've missed my 3-bet range.

Check. I have second pair (J♠) with a weak 9 kicker on Q♠ J♥ Q♦ 5♦ K♣ — a very vulnerable hand against the player who 3‑bet me preflop. Flash checked flop and turn and now checks river, a line consistent with slow‑played strong hands (QQ, Qx, JJ) or medium hands opting for a showdown. Betting here would mainly get called by hands that beat me or fold out only the weakest Jx combos — I’ll seldom extract meaningful thin value and risk bloating the pot against better hands. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; plan is to call a small river stab but fold to a large bet/raise.
6s5h is a weak offsuit connector in UTG position. This is not a hand to open from early position - it plays poorly and will be dominated by most calling/3-betting ranges. Easy fold.

In a 5-handed spot UTG+1 is effectively an early/mid position — ranges should be wider than full-ring but still selective. J♣7♣ is a playable, suited hand with decent postflop play and blocker value to broadways. I prefer to open rather than limp: raising gains fold equity against the button and the blinds and keeps initiative. I'll open to a standard 2.5x BB (250) — bigger than the absolute minimum so it's a credible steal and reduces multiway limps. I can comfortably commit 250 from my ~53.8 BB stack; if I face a sizable 3-bet (e.g. ~800+), I'll fold OOP since J7s does poorly vs strong 3-bet ranges. If called, I'll proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting favorable club/paired/low-connectivity boards and controlling pot size otherwise.

I am on the button with A2o. Minni, the short stack, has raised to 2.5bb from UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in this 5-handed game). While A2o has some value on the button, it is easily dominated by Minni's opening range and the players in the blinds have yet to act. Given my healthy stack and the marginal nature of the hand against an early position raise, folding is the most prudent play to avoid high-variance situations with a weak kicker.
