Preflop in SB with 5c2c, a suited but very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a raiser's range from early position (Minni UTG+1). Out of position postflop, calling 200 into 400 pot offers bad implied odds and high risk of domination. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament with many hands remaining. No history indicates Minni is overly loose here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s Th, facing a 2.5x open raise from Minni in early position (UTG+1). The pot odds are offering me about 3.6:1 (need to call 150 to win 550), requiring roughly 27% equity. While T7o might have raw equity near that threshold against a wide opening range, it suffers significantly from poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position. I will likely be dominated if I hit a Ten, and the hand makes very few strong draws. Given my stack depth and the unplayable nature of the hand, defending here is negative EV. Folding is the clear decision.

Fold. UTG in a 5-handed game I need a fairly tight, playable open-raising range — K6 offsuit (Kc6h) is weak and easily dominated. Calling 100 into a 150 pot (making it multiway) leaves me with poor postflop prospects and vulnerable kicker situations against likely stronger Kx/Ax holdings and frequent 3-bets from the big stacks (Claude 21k). With a healthy 55bb stack I should preserve chips for better spots rather than limp or open with this hand. Long-term EV is to fold here.
I am holding Jd 3s in UTG+1. This is a very weak starting hand and does not justify a raise or a call from this position. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket eights is a strong premium hand preflop, particularly on the button with position on all remaining players. UTG and UTG+1 have folded, indicating weakness ahead. Raising standardizes action, builds the pot, and thins the field against blind calling ranges which 88 dominates. A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate given common sizing in prior hands and deep stacks. Avoid limping or calling to maintain initiative and value.

I hold Pocket Queens [Qh, Qc] in the Small Blind, a premium hand. Elon has raised to 250 from the Button, a position from which players typically open with a wide range. I want to build the pot for value while I likely have the best hand and isolate the opener, preventing the Big Blind from seeing a cheap flop. Calling would be passive and invite multi-way action which devalues high pairs. A standard 3-bet size from out of position is roughly 3.5x to 4x the open. Raising to 900 (3.6x) extracts value from Elon's wide range and defines the hand early.

I have JJ in the BB - a premium hand. Elon opened from BTN and Pro 3-bet to 900 from SB. This is a classic spot to 4-bet for value with pocket Jacks. JJ plays well heads-up and poorly multiway. By 4-betting, I can isolate Pro and define his range. If he 5-bets all-in, I can reassess, but most hands in his 3-bet range (AK, AQ, TT, 99) I'm ahead of or flipping against. I'll 4-bet to around 2400 for value.

Pocket 88 is a strong hand but faces significant action: my button open to 250, SB Pro 3-bets to 900 (strong range from blinds defending vs late steal), BB Claude 4-bets to 2400 (very premium range: likely QQ+, AK). Equity realization postflop in a bloated pot with low SPR (~1.4 after call) risks domination and poor implied odds without a set. Shoving ~10k risks stack vs better (AA/KK crush 88 for ~18% equity) with uncertain fold equity vs aggressive opponents shown in history (e.g., Claude's big 4-bets and calls). Calling leads to committed spot multiway OOP for them but position for me, still -EV long-term. Folding preserves deep 100BB stack early (hand 38/150) for superior spots, avoiding cooler potential.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qc) in the Small Blind. After 3-betting to 900, the Big Blind (Claude) cold 4-bet to 2400. This is an extremely strong line, typically representing a range of AA, KK, AK, and occasionally QQ or JJ. Against a tight value range of [QQ+, AK], I have approximately 40% equity. The pot odds are favorable: I need to call 1500 to win a total pot of 5050, requiring about 29.7% equity. Since my equity against his likely range exceeds the required odds, folding is incorrect. 5-bet jamming would likely only get called by better hands (AA, KK) or coin flips (AK), maximizing my losses against the top of his range. Calling allows me to see a flop at a reasonable price, keeping his range wider and avoiding punting my stack directly into Aces or Kings pre-flop.

I flopped top set on a board of [9s, Qs, 3d]. This is a monster hand, effectively the nuts at this point given the pre-flop action (Claude cold 4-bet, making 99/33 unlikely). The pot is 5050 and I have 7850 behind (SPR ~1.5). Claude's range is heavily weighted toward AA, KK, and AK. He will likely c-bet this texture with his overpairs and flush draws (AsKs). I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to allow him to commit chips with his strong range. Leading out might fold out his AK (non-spades) or make AA/KK play cautiously. My goal is to get all the money in, and checking is the best way to induce a bet.

I have JJ on Qs9s3d - one overcard and a flush draw possible. Pro called my 4-bet preflop, so his range includes AK, AQ, QQ+, maybe TT. He checked to me. I should c-bet for value and protection - I beat TT, AK, and need to charge flush draws. Betting around 60% pot makes sense here.

I have flopped top set (QQ) on a board of Qs-9s-3d, which is virtually the nuts given the pre-flop action. Claude's cold 4-bet pre-flop and subsequent large continuation bet of 3,000 into a 5,050 pot indicates an extremely strong range, heavily weighted towards AA, KK, and AsKs. I am crushing AA and KK and am a massive favorite against flush draws. With an SPR of roughly 1.3 starting the post-flop play, and significantly lower now facing the bet, my goal is to get all the money in immediately. Calling risks a scare card (like a spade, Ace, or King) appearing on the turn, which could either kill the action by scaring him off or improve his hand to a flush or higher set (though unlikely). Raising all-in now maximizes value from his overpairs which will find it very hard to fold given the pot odds and strength of their hand.

Pro called my 4-bet preflop (range: QQ+, AK, maybe AQ), then check-raised all-in on Qs9s3d. This line screams extreme strength - likely QQ (flopped set), AA, or KK. Even AKss semi-bluffs are reduced since I hold Js blocking spade draws. I need 28% equity to call (7250 into 25550), but against Pro's realistic range of {QQ, AA, KK} I have only ~8% equity. The overcard on board hits his 3-bet/call 4-bet range perfectly. This is a disciplined fold - JJ is good but not good enough against this line.

I am UTG with Qs 4s. While the hand is suited, it is very weak for an opening position. In a 5-handed game, standard opening ranges should be tighter from first position, especially with aggressive players like Claude and Pro behind who have shown a willingness to 3-bet and 4-bet recently. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

97o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and no straight or flush potential. In UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game, the range should be tight. Folding avoids difficult post-flop situations out of position with a speculative hand. Stack is deep at 101x BB, no need to play marginal hands.

I hold [8c 4d] on the Button. This is an unsuited, disconnected hand with very low playability (trash hand). Although I am in position and have a large stack, opening this hand is generally losing strategy, especially with active blinds still to act. The Small Blind (Claude) has a large stack and just lost a major pot to me in the previous hand, so he might be prone to playing back aggressively. The Big Blind has ~54 BBs. There is no incentive to get involved with such weak holdings. I will fold.

Holding A5s in the SB with action folded to me. This is a clear raise spot heads up against the BB. A5 suited plays well with its potential for straights, flushes, and high card strength. Standard raise to 250-300 to put pressure on Minni in the BB.

Claude opened to 250 from the small blind; pot is 350 and I need 150 to call. Calling 150 into an effective pot of 500 requires ~30% equity, which is a cheap price to see a flop with Q♦8♠. Claude has been opening a lot of pots to 250, so his range is likely wide — Q8o is marginal but defendable from the big blind. Raising to 400 would inflate the pot and put me out of position with a mediocre hand; folding is too nitty for such a small price. I'll call to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the flop — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards or to a large 3-bet preflop.

I flopped top two pair (Aces and Fives) on As-5d-Qh. This is an excellent hand. The board is fairly dry with no flush draws. I need to bet for value - Minni can call with Ax, Qx, or draws. Betting around 60-70% pot to build the pot and extract value.

I have Q♦8♠ on A♠-5♦-Q♥ — a single pair of queens with a weak kicker and an Ace on board. Claude opened from the small blind (he opens a lot) and now c-bet 350 into an 850 pot; calling 350 requires only ~29% equity, so the price to continue is cheap. Against a wide SB c-betting range I’m ahead of many hands (Kx, Qx, lower pairs, bluffs) but behind any A‑x and hands with Qx+ better kickers. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair would bloat the pot and expose me to 3-bets or tough decisions on later streets, while folding is too tight given the pot odds. I’ll call 350 to keep the pot controlled and see the turn — plan is to fold to heavy aggression if an Ace or other scary card comes and I don’t improve, call or single-barrel small bets on safe cards, and extract value if I improve or he checks behind.

The turn Qc paired the board, which significantly changes my hand strength. I flopped two pair (Aces and Fives), but now anyone with a Queen has trips and beats me. My hand is effectively Aces and Fives with a Queen kicker. Minni called my flop bet which could indicate Ax, Qx, or a draw. If they have any Qx they now have me crushed. Given the board pairing and the possibility Minni holds a Queen, I should check to control pot size and reassess based on their action. If they check back, I can potentially value bet river; if they bet, I can decide whether to call based on sizing.

I have Q♦8♠ on A♠-5♦-Q♥-Q♣ — that’s trips (three queens), a very strong hand on this relatively dry board. Claude opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn, which is consistent with either a medium-strength holding (A‑x, smaller pairs, KQ) or a pot‑control line with a vulnerable top pair. Very few hands beat me on the turn (pocket 5s or pocket AAs for a full house are possible but uncommon); many of his hands are worse and will pay a value bet. Betting also denies a free river that could pair the A or 5 and turn a vulnerable trips into a losing hand later. I’ll make a value bet about half the pot to get called by Ax, smaller pairs, and any lone Q, while keeping the size reasonable if he elects to raise. Pot ~1200 — bet 600.

I have A5 with board As 5d Qh Qc, giving me two pair (Aces and Fives). The turn Queen is concerning - any Qx now beats me with trips. Minni bet 600 into 1200 (half pot). They called preflop and flop, so Qx is definitely in their range (Q2-QJ). However, they could also have Ax or be bluffing. Getting 3:1 odds (600 to win 1800), I only need to be good ~25% of the time. My hand beats all Ax and bluffs. Folding is too tight, raising only gets called by better. Calling is correct to see the river.

River: As 5d Qh Qc Jc. I have Ad5s giving me two pair (Aces and Queens, using my Ad with the board). Minni checked to me after betting turn. They likely would've bet again with Qx for value, so their check suggests Ax, Jx, or weaker. I should value bet small to extract from Ax that chops/loses, Jx (Queens and Jacks), and worse. A small bet around 600 gets called by worse while keeping the pot controlled if raised.

I have Q♦8♠ and the river A♠-5♦-Q♥-Q♣-J♣ gives me trips (three queens). Trips is a very strong but vulnerable hand here — the only hands that beat me are full houses (AA, 55, AQ, Q5 combos). Claude’s line (SB open, c-bet flop, check on the turn when the Q paired, call my 600 turn bet, then a small 650 river bet) looks like a wide/capped range with a lot of single-pair A-x, medium pairs or bluffs — most full houses would more often raise the turn or size up for value. Pot odds are attractive: calling 650 into ~3,700 (post-bet) requires only ~17.5% equity. Raising risks folding out worse hands and bloating a pot where a full house could already be waiting; folding would be too nitty. I’ll call the 650 to realize showdown value from Ax and other weaker holdings while keeping the pot controlled.

UTG position with 9h2c offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands. No playability, no high cards, no suited or connected potential. Standard fold preflop to preserve stack. Effective stacks are deep, but no reason to open with trash from early position. Opponents have shown aggression but irrelevant here.

Current hand is Q5 suited in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed play). Elon (UTG) has folded. While I have a deep stack (183 BB), Q5s is generally below the standard opening threshold for this position, which typically bottoms out around Q8s or Q9s. With an aggressive player (Claude) on the Button who has shown willingness to 3-bet and play big pots, opening a weak suited Queen risks getting isolated or dominated. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here to preserve chips for better spots.

QJo on the button with UTG and UTG+1 folded. This is a premium stealing spot - I have position against the blinds with a solid Broadway hand. Standard raise to 2.5x to apply pressure and potentially take down the blinds.

Fold. 3h8s is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that will be dominated by a Button opening range. Claude opened to 250 (a standard sizing) and I am out of position for the whole hand; the BB still acts behind me and can isolate or 3-bet. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires roughly ~33% equity to break even — 3-8 offsuit has far less equity versus a reasonable open range. A 3-bet bluff from the small blind here is thin with this holding and likely to run into stronger hands or a 4-bet. With a healthy ~73 BB stack I can preserve chips and wait for better spots. Fold and conserve stack for spots with more playability or position.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s 6s. The button, Claude, has raised to 250. Minni folded the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, and the current pot is 400. My pot odds are roughly 2.6 to 1 (150 to win 400). 96s is a playable suited hand that can flop well in a heads-up pot against a standard button opening range. Given the stack depths and the price, calling to see a flop is the most profitable and standard play here.

I have flopped trips (three-of-a-kind) with 9s 6s on a 9c 9d Js board. I am out of position against Claude, who was the pre-flop aggressor from the button. Checking is the most effective play here to allow Claude to continuation bet with his wider button range, including air, overcards, or J-x hands. This maximizes the potential to extract value later in the hand or induce a bluff.

I flopped top pair with Jacks and a Queen kicker on a 9-9-J board. Flash checked to me. This is a strong hand that I should bet for value. A bet of around 2/3 pot will extract value from worse hands like lower pairs, draws, or weaker jacks while also protecting against potential overcards.

I have flopped trips with 9s 6s on a 9c 9d Js board. While my kicker (6) is relatively weak, it is unlikely Claude has a better 9 given his button opening range. By calling his continuation bet rather than raising, I allow him to continue bluffing with air or value-betting a Jack on future streets. Raising now would likely fold out his weaker hands and only get called by hands that have me beat or have significant equity. I will check-call to keep his range wide and maximize potential value.

I have trips (Nines) with a 6 kicker on a board of 9c-9d-Js-Ah. The Ace is a significant card that likely hits Claude's button opening range. By checking again, I am playing my hand deceptively and allowing Claude to continue betting with his bluffs or value betting with any Ace-x hands. Given that I am out of position, checking keeps the pot manageable and maximizes the chance of inducing further action from weaker hands that might fold to a lead.

I have top pair Jacks with Queen kicker on a 99JA board. The Ace on the turn is a concerning card - it could have hit Flash's BB calling range (Ax hands are common). Flash checked again, which could be a trap with an Ace or a 9. My hand has showdown value but betting and getting check-raised would be painful. I'll check back for pot control and reassess on the river.

I have trip nines (9s 6s) on a board of 9c-9d-Js-Ah-4h. The turn went check-check after the Ace landed, which often indicates that the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) is pot-controlling with a marginal hand like a Jack or a weak Ace, or has given up on a bluff. If I check again on the river, I risk the hand going check-check and missing out on value from those Jx or Ax hands. By leading with a half-pot bet of 625, I represent a thin value bet or a missed draw, increasing the likelihood that Claude calls with his showdown-value hands. While my kicker is weak, it is mathematically more likely he has Jx, Ax, or a pocket pair than a superior Nine.

Flash's small river bet (1/3 pot) after check-calling flop and checking turn is a strange line. This sizing often indicates a blocking bet with medium strength or thin value. With top pair Jack and Queen kicker, I'm ahead of most Jx hands and pocket pairs. Getting 4:1 odds, I only need to win 20% of the time. Folding is too weak, raising only gets called by better. Standard call.

I am in UTG position with [9d Qh] (Queen-Nine offsuit). In a 5-handed game, UTG is still the earliest position, requiring a tighter opening range. Q9o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by calling ranges (AQ, KQ, QJ, A9, etc.) and plays poorly out of position. Despite having a large stack, opening this hand is generally negative EV. The standard play is to fold.
K4o from UTG+1 is a weak hand in early position. Not strong enough to open-raise - folding is the standard play here.

I’m on the button with Tc4s — a marginal hand but a good spot to open-steal with both UTG players folded. I’ll raise to 2.5x the BB (250): it gives decent fold equity against the blinds and is large enough to discourage loose 3-bets, while still small relative to my ~73BB stack. Plan: if both blinds fold I take the pot; if I get a single caller I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet good flops, check/control dry or dangerous boards); if I face a significant 3-bet I’ll fold this dominated holding.

I am holding 2d 7d in the small blind. While it is suited, it is statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni, on the button, has raised to 2.5x the big blind. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a very low-equity hand, and raising as a bluff is risky given Minni's button range and the presence of the big blind yet to act. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 250 (2.5x), with pot 400 and needing to call 150 more into an effective pot of 550 post-call (27% pot odds). K6d is a suited king, which plays well multiway or heads-up against a wide button opening range (Minni has been aggressive from late position in history). Equity is decent (~40-45% vs typical BTN range), good postflop playability with flush/straight potential. Folding is too tight in position; 3-betting is unnecessary as it's not premium strength and risks bloating pot OOP. Calling is standard defense to realize equity cheaply.

Flopped top pair kings with a weak six kicker on a paired board (TTKss) heads-up OOP vs aggressive button raiser Minni, whose range is wide (Ax, broadway, suited connectors, pairs). My Kd blocks strong kings like AK/KQ. Equity strong vs her missed draws/bluffs (~60%+ vs typical range without 10/K), showdown value exists, but vulnerable to any 10, sets, or better kicker. Leading ~2/3 pot (350) extracts value from worse Kx/Ax/Tx while protecting against spade flush draws and denying equity to floaters/OESD (e.g., QJ). Checks behind risk giving free cards to overcards/draws; check-raise too aggressive for thin value spot. Deep stacks allow bet sizing without overcommitting. History shows Minni calls light stations sometimes (e.g., Hand 5,39), likely pays off.

Flop: Th Ts Ks. My hand Tc4s = top trips (three tens) in position, heads-up vs Elon who called preflop and now made a small 350 probe into a 900 pot. Most of his continuing range is Kx, medium pairs and floats; the hands that currently beat me are limited (pocket kings/set of kings, or the single remaining Ten + a strong kicker or a Td+K full house combo). I block some tens (I hold Tc) and I have a very strong hand that I want to extract value from while denying free cards to backdoor draws. Raising for value/protection is the correct line — the min-raise to 700 is large enough to charge draws and weaker pairs (Kx, Qx, mid pairs) while keeping worse hands in to call. Plan: if he calls I continue extracting on a blank turn; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (but trips are strong enough to continue against most oversized raises). Action: raise to 700 (total).

Facing a raise to 700 after my flop lead of 350 into 1250 on a paired TTKss board OOP with top pair weak kicker K6d. Minni's button raise preflop was wide, but her flop reraise polarizes her range to strong value (sets TT/KK unlikely, KT/AT/KQ better Kx), flush draws (spades), or bluffs (missed floats like QJ/AQ). My Kd blocks some Kx but 6d kicker is vulnerable to any turn 6/T/A/K or spade flush. Equity vs her raising range drops to ~35-45%, but OOP with deep stacks (9550 effective), calling commits to bloated pot (~1950 post-call) facing further aggression on scary turn/river cards. Raising as bluff risks stacks with dominated hand and reverse polarized range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; my lead extracted value from worse initially, and her raise indicates strength we can't profitably realize equity against multi-street. History shows Minni aggressive but capable of value-raising (e.g., Hand 16,27). Discipline > curiosity.
Pocket 7s in UTG is a solid opening hand. With 127 BBs effective, I should open raise to build a pot and aim to set mine postflop. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I hold Th4d — a weak, unsuited hand that is largely dominated by a typical UTG opening range. Calling 250 to play out of position against an early-position raiser gives me poor equity and likely puts me in tough multiway pots. A light 3-bet would be too risky here against an UTG range and I have plenty of chips to wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve the stack.

I am on the Button with Queen-Jack offsuit (QJo), which is a strong enough hand to continue against a 2.5x open from the UTG player (Claude) in a 5-handed game. Claude has been active and aggressive, but QJo plays well in position. Calling is the preferred option over raising (3-betting) here as it allows me to see a flop with a decent hand while keeping the pot manageable and maintaining position throughout the hand. 3-betting might force Claude to fold hands I dominate or leave me vulnerable to a 4-bet from his stronger range.

T2o is a complete trash hand with poor playability, low equity against a UTG raiser like Claude who is aggressive and a button caller in Flash. Facing a raise to 250 and a call, getting correct pot odds but range is dominated. Folding from SB preserves stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o facing a raise from UTG (Claude) and a flat call from the Button (Flash). Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win 650, roughly 5.3:1), my hand has very poor playability and suffers from significant reverse implied odds. I am out of position against two opponents, one of whom (UTG) likely has a strong range that dominates mine (AQ, KQ, QJs). Q6o rarely flops well enough to continue profitably in a multi-way pot. Therefore, preserving chips is the better option than defending a marginal hand out of position.

I have pocket 7s on Ah 6h Kc - two overcards to my pair plus a flush draw on board. This is a bad flop for my hand. Flash called from the button and likely has many Ax and Kx hands in their range that connect here. My 77 has marginal showdown value but no reason to bet for value since I'm only getting called by better hands. Checking to control pot size.

I have Queen-Jack offsuit on a board of Ace-high and King-high (Ah-6h-Kc). This board heavily favors Claude's UTG opening range, which likely includes many Aces and Kings. Although Claude checked, betting here is risky as he might be checking for pot control with a marginal hand or trapping with a monster. I have a gutshot straight draw (to a Ten) and very little showdown value. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially realize my equity if a Ten falls, without risking a check-raise on a board that I am unlikely to have hit hard.

Turn 5d doesn't change much. My 77 still has marginal showdown value but I'm behind any Ax or Kx. Flash checked behind on flop suggesting weakness or a draw. Betting here would only get called by better hands. Check to control pot and potentially win at showdown vs missed draws.

On a board of Ace-high and King-high, Claude's second check suggests he may not have a strong Ace or King, but Q-high still has no showdown value. I have a gutshot straight draw to a Ten. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, preserving my stack while giving me a chance to hit my straight. Since Claude opened from UTG, he could still be trapping or planning to call with a marginal pair, so checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity.

River Qd brings a third overcard. Board is AhKcQd with no flush completing. Flash checked flop and turn, showing weakness. My 77 has very marginal showdown value against this board. Betting gets called only by better hands. Check and hope to beat missed heart draws or complete air.

I have improved to a pair of Queens on the river with [Qc, Jh] on a board of [Ah, 6h, Kc, 5d, Qd]. Claude, who opened from UTG, has checked every street. While my pair of Queens is a decent hand, there are still an Ace and a King on the board, both of which are heavily represented in an UTG opening range. By checking back, I can realize my showdown value against hands like JJ, TT, or missed heart draws that Claude might have played passively. Betting is unlikely to get calls from worse hands and risks a check-raise from a trapping Ace or King.

UTG with 9♠2♠: a very weak, non‑connected suited hand with poor playability out of position. I can fold, call 100 or min‑raise to 200 — none of which are attractive. Calling 100 gets me into multiway pots out of position with a hand that is often dominated; raising opens me up to 3‑bets from several aggressive, deeper stacks at the table (we've seen frequent strong opens and re‑raises). I have ~80bb and can wait for a much better spot to attack. Fold and preserve stack equity.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit in UTG+1 in a 5-max game. This hand is well below the threshold for a standard opening range from any position except perhaps the small blind in a steal situation. Given my healthy stack and the lack of any compelling reason to bluff with such a weak hand from early position, the optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pre-flop on the button with 5c8d, an offsuit gapped hand with low cards and no high card strength. Despite button position allowing a wide stealing range, this hand has poor playability post-flop, low equity against calling ranges, and is dominated by better hands in any confrontation. Pot is small at 150, blinds are deep-stacked, but folding preserves stack for better spots. No value in calling or raising this trash hand. Prioritize position and hand strength for long-term stack maximization.

In a Blind vs Blind situation with no ante, the pot odds and incentives to enter the pot are lower than in an ante game. I hold T9o, which is a marginal unsuited connector. While it has some playability, I am out of position against the second-largest stack (Claude) who has shown capability for aggression. My stack is healthy (chip leader), and I should avoid playing marginal hands out of position deep-stacked against competent opponents. Standard charts for SB vs BB with no ante suggest folding T9o is the prudent play to minimize variance and avoid difficult post-flop spots.

I am holding J5o in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and equity. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand from the earliest position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop, UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game with 95BB stack. Holding 3s2d, a very weak suited hand with poor equity, low playability, and dominated by better suited connectors or wheel hands. No prior action beyond blinds and UTG fold. Facing a call of 100 into 150 pot offers negligible odds. Folding is standard to preserve stack and avoid difficult postflop spots out of position.

I am on the Button with 3c 2h (32 offsuit), which is effectively the weakest hand in Hold'em. Action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, attempting to steal with the absolute bottom of my range is unnecessary given my healthy stack (181 BBs) and the early stage of the tournament. The risk of getting called or 3-bet outweighs the small pot of 1.5 BBs. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket Aces in the small blind with everyone folding to me. This is the best starting hand in poker. I need to raise for value. Going 3x to 300 to build the pot while hopefully getting action from the big blind.

Heads-up vs the small blind who opened to 300. I have K♣8♣ — a playable hand with suitedness and decent high-card value in a heads-up pot. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total committed) which gives ~33% pot‑odds; against a likely wide SB opening range K8s has enough equity and good postflop playability. With ~79BB effective I prefer to see a flop in position rather than thinly 3-bet; calling keeps the pot controllable and lets me realize equity or apply pressure on favorable boards. I will call to see the flop.

I have pocket Aces on QhJhTd - a very draw-heavy board with two hearts and straight possibilities. I have an overpair but K9 makes a straight and there are many draws out there. I need to bet for value and protection. Betting around 2/3 pot to charge draws and get value from Qx, Jx hands.

Board Qh Jh Td. I hold Kc8c — no pair, only a gutshot to a 9 (4 clean outs) and backdoor club possibilities. Claude opened from the SB and now bets 400 into the ~600 pot (about a 2/3-pot c‑bet). The pot after his bet is 1000; calling 400 requires ~28.6% equity to break even (400/(1000+400)). My realistic equity to the river vs a c‑betting SB range (Qx, Jx, Tx, many broadways, some two‑pair/straights) is well below that — roughly in the high teens to low twenties at best (4 outs ≈17% to the river, plus only small backdoor equity). Raising as a semi‑bluff is attractive sometimes in position but here the sizing and villain’s range make a bluff thin: I don’t block enough of his strong combos and would be committing chips with very little real equity. With ~79BB effective and many better spots to pick up chips, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

Q℃ 2♥ is a trash hand from UTG position in a 5-handed tournament. Offsuit, gapped, low playability postflop, and dominated by any queen or pair. No action yet, but raising or limping would be a mistake with this holding. Folding is standard to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

I am in UTG+1 (HJ) with King-Three offsuit (K3o). This is a statistically weak hand with very poor playability and significant reverse implied odds if I hit a King. With three active players left to act behind me, including aggressive opponents like Claude, opening this hand is not profitable. I have a leading stack and should preserve it for better spots. The correct play is a standard fold.

AQo on the button is a premium hand. With two players already folded, I have excellent position and should open raise. Standard raise to 2.5x BB to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop or isolate against the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠5♥ facing a button open to 250. 95o is a very weak, unplayable hand out of position — calling 200 (2 BB) is cheap but will leave me OOP vs the raiser and the big blind, likely facing continuation bets and difficult folds. With about 76 BB effective I can wait for a much better spot; 3-betting is out of the question. Fold and preserve the stack for a higher EV opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 7s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Claude has raised from the Button, a position where players often open with a wide range. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more into a pot of 400), 47 offsuit plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are still many hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.

I am in UTG position (first to act) with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). The blinds are 50/100. Even in a 5-handed game, K9o is a marginal hand to open from the earliest position. It is frequently dominated by calling ranges (KQ, KJ, AK) and plays poorly post-flop out of position due to reverse implied odds. Although I have the chip lead with over 180 BBs, disciplined folding of weak offsuit broadways in early position is standard strategy to preserve the stack for better spots. Therefore, the correct action is to fold.
9c7h offsuit in UTG+1 is a weak hand that should be folded from early position. No reason to open or call with this hand - it plays poorly and has limited potential.

I have T6o on the button — a marginal hand but with maximum positional advantage and both UTG players folded. Limping/calling 100 invites the blinds to see a cheap flop and often leads to difficult multiway spots; opening a wide range from the button is standard. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or play heads-up postflop in position. If either blind 3-bets I will generally fold this hand (unless the 3-bet is tiny or the opponent is extremely loose); if called I’ll c-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously, folding to significant resistance when I miss. Total chips committed (including current BB) = 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3BB (300). Q5o is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against an active button open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a hand that is easily dominated or difficult to realize equity with. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB), with Kd4h offsuit. Pot is 450, to call 200 more, requiring about 31% equity to justify a call. K4o is king-high with a poor kicker, offsuit, and I'll be out of position postflop. Minni's button range is wide but strong enough to make this a marginal spot at best. From hand history, Minni is active on the button but capable of strong hands and value betting. Defending this hand OOP risks tough spots postflop with poor playability. Stack is 94BB deep, no need to get involved with a weak hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots.
Ts6s in UTG is too weak to open. It's a marginal suited hand with poor connectivity, and UTG requires a tighter range. Easy fold.

UTG+1 with K♣3♥ — a weak, offsuit King in early position. The pot is just the blinds (150) and it would cost 100 to see a flop; calling or opening here risks playing out of position and often being dominated by other Kx hands. Table dynamics show frequent raises and aggressive post-flop action from deep stacks behind me, so neither a speculative call nor an early-position steal attempt (raise to 200+/250) is profitable. Preserve the ~77bb stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the Button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While I have position, this hand is significantly below a standard opening range even in a 5-handed game. Elon (SB) and Pro (BB) are both capable of defending or 3-betting. Given the weak nature of the hand, there is no reason to commit chips when there are 100 hands left in the tournament.

Preflop, unopened pot after folds from UTG, UTG+1, and Button. In SB with AsTc (ATo offsuit), a strong hand for raising in this spot to steal the dead blinds (pot 150) or build pot OOP with good equity and blockers. Standard open-raise size around 2.5x BB (250 total). Pro BB deep but history shows mixed calling ranges; this pressures BB effectively. Limping weak, folding too tight for position and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with J6o facing a 2.5x raise from the Small Blind (Elon). While I have position post-flop, J6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds (often dominated by better Jacks). Pot odds are 150 to call into a pot of 350 (30%), and my raw equity against a standard SB opening range is roughly 37%. However, realizing that equity with J6o is difficult. It falls outside the standard defending range against a 2.5x open. Folding is the prudent play to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

UTG with T9o (off-suit) in a 5-handed game is a marginal/early-position hand. The pot is already 150 and I have to call 100 with four players left to act — likely to face raises from aggressive players behind (we’ve seen frequent preflop 3-bets and steals). Calling invites multiway pots where T9o performs poorly and raising for a steal from UTG is too loose; with deep stacks I should conserve chips for better spots. Fold and wait for a stronger hand or a better position.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit in middle position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and offers no significant strategic value or drawing potential. Given my position and the hand's strength, folding is the standard and correct play.

Q℃ 3♥ is a trash hand offsuit, lacking connectivity, suitedness, or domination potential. Position on button is nice but doesn't justify playing this weak holding voluntarily preflop. Blinds are deep-stacked (Pro 18k SB, Claude 12.8k BB), likely to defend or raise. From hand history, players are aggressive with opens, so limping risks multiway pot with dominated hand or getting squeezed. Raising is spewy. Best to fold and wait for premium spots to maximize stack long-term in tournament.

In a Small Blind vs Big Blind situation, Queen-Eight suited (Q8s) is a strong hand, well above the median hand strength. With the action folded to me, raising is the preferred play to take the initiative, define ranges, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Being out of position post-flop, raising to 3bb (300) discourages a call with marginal holdings and asserts dominance. Limping is an option but allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free or raise me off my equity. Given my healthy stack size, applying pressure is the optimal strategy.

J5 suited in BB facing a min-raise from SB. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). J5s has some playability with flush potential and can flop well. Against a SB steal attempt, this is a reasonable defend. Calling to see a flop.

I hold middle pair (Queens) on a wet board (Kc 6c Qd) out of position. While I have showdown value, the King hits the Big Blind's defending range fairly well. Betting risks getting raised or called by better hands (Kx, better Qx) while folding out worse hands that I beat. Checking allows me to control the pot size, keep the opponent's range wide, and induce bluffs or call a reasonable bet with my marginal made hand. It is a standard pot control line for second pair out of position.

Missed this flop completely with J5d on 6c Kc Qd. No pair, no meaningful draw - just backdoor diamonds and a weak backdoor straight. Pro checked to me, so I get a free card. No reason to bluff here with zero equity when I can see a turn for free.

On the turn, the board develops to [6c, Kc, Qd, Js], which is extremely coordinated. The Jack completes potential straights for T9 and AT, and creates more two-pair combinations (QJ, KJ). Since I hold second pair (Queens) and am out of position, my hand has marginal showdown value but cannot confidently value bet for three streets, especially on this texture. The opponent checked back the flop, signaling a capped range or a draw, but the turn card interacts heavily with that range. Betting likely folds out worse hands while isolating me against better ones (Kx, two pair, straights). Checking is the best option to control the pot size, realize equity, and potentially induce bluffs or catch a cheap showdown.

Turned a pair of jacks - third pair on this KQJ6 board. Pro has checked flop and turn, showing weakness. I should bet small for thin value and to charge club draws. My jack is vulnerable but likely ahead of Pro's checking range. Betting around 1/3 pot.

The opponent bet 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot) on the turn after checking back the flop. The board is [6c Kc Qd Js]. I hold second pair (Queens). The Jack on the turn is a scare card that completes straights (AT, T9) and improves hands like QJ or KJ. However, the opponent's small bet size and flop check suggest he likely does not have a nutted hand, or he is making a thin value/protection bet with a hand like Jx or a weak K. I am getting 4:1 pot odds, requiring only 20% equity to call. My hand beats all bluffs (club draws, straight draws) and weak pairs. Folding for this price with second pair in a blind battle is exploitable. Raising would isolate me against better hands. Calling is the most prudent play to see a river and keep the pot manageable.

I hold second pair (Queens) on a very coordinated board [6c, Kc, Qd, Js, 2c]. The river 2c completes the flush draw, making the board even scarier with three clubs and four-to-a-straight possibilities (AT, T9). Being out of position with a marginal made hand, I cannot value bet as worse hands will fold and better hands (Kx, flushes, straights, better Qx) will call or raise. Leading as a bluff is ill-advised given I have some showdown value. The optimal play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent checks back, I hope to win at showdown against a missed straight draw or weaker pair. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the dangerous board texture.

River brought the 2c completing the club flush. Pro has checked all streets except calling my small turn bet. My pair of jacks is third pair on KQJ62 - way too weak to value bet. If I bet, Pro only calls with better hands (Kx, Qx, flush) and folds worse. Checking back for showdown value is correct here.
K2off is a very weak hand to play from UTG, even in a 5-handed game. It lacks post-flop playability and is easily dominated by better Kings. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips.

7h4d is a complete trash hand offsuit with no connectivity or high cards. In UTG+1 position (early position) with 5 players left, this is an automatic fold regardless of the action. Facing a to-call of 100 into a 150 pot gives poor implied odds even if limped, but we don't speculate with garbage. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV.

I am on the Button with King-Jack suited (Kc Jc), which is a premium hand for this position in an unopened pot. With two players left to act in the blinds (Claude and Minni), my goal is to raise for value, seize the initiative, and potentially steal the blinds. Previous history shows standard opening raises at this table are around 2.5bb (250). Given my deep stack and position, raising to 250 is the standard and optimal play to build the pot while retaining the advantage of position.
93 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise, this hand has poor playability and no good equity. Easy fold from the small blind.

8♥2♣ is one of the weakest starting hands. Pro opened to 250 from the button (2.5x); I’m out of position and would need to call another 150 into a 400 pot. That call gives me ~27% pot odds (150/(400+150)), but 8‑2 offsuit has far less equity versus a standard button raising range and will be dominated or face difficult post‑flop decisions. A tiny 3‑bet to 400 is unlikely to fold enough of his range and would be a poor use of my deep stack here. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

UTG in 5-handed 50/100 NLHE with Kd2h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, dominated by any king caller, and no strong equity. Standard play is to fold trash from early position to avoid difficult postflop spots OOP. Pot 150 (blinds), to call 100 (1.5bb) not worth it with this hand. Preserves stack for better spots, aligning with tournament goal of maximizing stack over 150 hands.

In a 5-handed game, UTG+1 acts as the Cutoff. Q9s is a standard open from the Cutoff, although it is near the bottom of the range. As the chip leader with a deep stack (187 BBs), I can apply pressure to the shorter stacks in the blinds. While Q9s is vulnerable to domination (e.g., KQ, AQ), the suited connectivity gives it enough playability to justify a steal attempt. If faced with significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold. I will make a standard open raise to 2.5bb.
AKo on the button is a premium hand. Facing a UTG+1 open to 250, I should 3-bet for value. I have position and AKo plays great against Pro's opening range. Standard 3-bet to around 800.

Facing a button 3-bet to 800 after an UTG+1 open, I’m in the small blind with Kc9c. To continue I must call 750 more (800 total) out of a 7625 stack and then play out of position against at least the button — and likely the original raiser if he stays in. K9s is often dominated by typical 3‑bet ranges (AK/AQ/KQ/strong Ks, medium pairs) and has poor multiway OOP playability. A light 4‑bet would be too thin here and a call is spewy with limited fold or value equity. Preserve chips for a better spot — fold.

I am holding Ten of Spades and Deuce of Diamonds in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. There has been an open raise from Pro and a 3-bet from Claude to 800. My hand has no equity against their ranges and playing out of position against two active players with this holding is a recipe for disaster. Folding is the only sensible move.

I opened with Q9s from the Cutoff, which is a standard steal attempt, but Claude 3-bet to 800 from the Button. While the pot odds (calling 550 to win a pot that will be 1750, requiring ~31% equity) might suggest a call is mathematically close against a wide Button range, the positional disadvantage is the deciding factor. Playing Q9s out of position in a bloated 3-bet pot is difficult; it suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated by KQ, AQ, QJ) and it is hard to realize equity. As the chip leader with a comfortable stack, I don't need to take marginal, high-variance spots OOP. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with 9c7c, a suited one-gapper. While I have the chip lead, the player to my direct left (Claude) just 3-bet me in the previous hand. 97s is a marginal opening hand from early position that plays poorly against 3-bets and is often dominated if called. Given the recent aggression from my left, tightening my opening range from UTG is the prudent adjustment to avoid bleeding chips in difficult spots out of position. Therefore, I will fold.
4c6h offsuit is a very weak hand in UTG+1 position. With 4 players left to act behind me, this hand has poor playability and is a clear fold.

Button with Kd7d — marginal but playable, especially with position and decent stack depth. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds, so I’ll open to 3x the BB (300) to maximize fold equity and avoid giving cheap odds to the small/big blinds. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (anything large and polarizing), but if I get a single caller I’ll C-bet favorable textures (K-high, diamond/flush/straight draw boards) and use position to realize equity; control the pot if checked to or multiway. This sizing keeps postflop play manageable while leveraging position to steal the blinds and build the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Q6o. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While Q6o has some high card value, it is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button open which could be wide but still dominates my range. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in the BB facing a button raise to 300 after folds from UTG, UTG+1, and SB. Pot is 450, calling 200 more into it offers good odds (~4.25:1 effective). A2 suited clubs is a strong defending hand here due to ace blocker, suited connectivity, and playability. Button ranges are wide (likely 35-50% of hands based on Minni's history of frequent opens and folding to 3-bets/4-bets). Equity vs random button hand is around 42-45%, and we block AA/AK. Folding is too tight; flat OOP risks domination postflop. 3-betting exploits fold equity (Minni folds to aggression often, e.g., Hand 20, 25, 46), builds pot with strong blocker/hand, and gives good SPR if called (~10:1 post-3bet). Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard, polarizes our range, and pressures Minni's 73BB stack without overcommitting our 94BB. If shoved on, we have ~35% equity vs her calling range.

I opened to 300 on the button with Kd7d and got a 3-bet from the big blind to 900. 4-betting as a bluff here would be too thin — K7s is dominated by large portions of a BB 3-bet range and I don't want to take a polarized 4-bet spot. Folding is defensible, but with a ~73BB stack and a small absolute price to see the flop (600 more, total 900), calling is the better long-term play. Call keeps weaker hands in, preserves position postflop, and gives me playability (backdoor diamond flush and straight potential) against what is likely a somewhat wide 3-bet range from Elon. Plan postflop: play cautiously — use position to c-bet small on favorable textures, control the pot when checked to, and fold to significant resistance on dry boards where I'm likely behind. If Elon 4-bets again I will fold most of this holding.

Flop completely misses my A2c: ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (no gutshot or open-ender), no flush draw. Pure air, minimal showdown value. Board Jd9sTd is highly coordinated, favoring villain's button 3-bet calling range (suited connectors like JTs, T9s, 98s, QJs; overpairs JJ+/TT/99; broadway like KQ/AQ). My 3-bet pre polarizes range to value (AJs, AQs, KQs, pairs) and bluffs (suited Ax, connectors), many of which connect here. Donk-betting OOP with nothing is poor: low fold equity vs her range, vulnerable to check-raises from sets/straights or calls from TP/D/FD. Checking preserves stack, controls pot, allows free card if she checks weak/missed draws/floats. Can fold to bet easily. Multiway potential avoided as heads-up. Long-term ICM/tourney play favors discipline over spew.

Flop: Jd 9s Td. Holding Kd7d I have the nut diamond draw plus a King overcard — roughly 12 clean outs (9 diamonds + 3 kings), ~50%+ to improve to river. Heads-up and in position vs Elon (who 3‑bet preflop and checked the flop), this is a strong semi‑bluffing spot. I want to apply pressure to fold out single-pair hands and charge his equity while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. Bet ~½ pot to get fold equity and the right price to see turn cards. Plan: if he makes a large raise I’ll fold (hand has poor showdown value); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — barrel when I improve (flush/Pair) and control or give up on blanks against heavy resistance.

Facing a half-pot donk bet on a highly coordinated Jd9sTd flop (open-enders, flush draws, pairs connect well with villain's button calling range vs my 3-bet: JTs, T9s, QJs, 98s, overpairs, broadways). A2c is pure air: ace-high no showdown, no straight draw (needs Q8 or runner-runner nonsense), backdoor flush at best. Pot odds ~4:1 require 20% equity, but realized equity is far lower vs her value-heavy donk range. Calling bloats pot OOP with reverse implied odds if I brick turn/river; induces check-raises from monsters. Raising as bluff has poor equity vs her range (often 2-pair+, sets, strong draws that continue). Folding preserves 94BB stack for better spots; discipline key in tourney with 100 hands left. Minni's history shows she donks for value/protection here.
AKs (Ace-King suited) is a premium hand. From UTG, I should raise to build the pot and narrow the field. Standard raise to 2.5-3x BB.

I have 6♦3♠ in UTG+1 facing an UTG open to 250. This is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability — especially out of position against an early-position raiser. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires roughly ~38% equity to break even, which 6‑3o doesn't have versus a standard UTG range, and any multiway action only reduces my equity further. A 3‑bet bluff here would be loose and high-risk given Claude's UTG open and the remaining players (button, SB, BB) who can still enter. Stack depth means I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve chips for stronger opportunities.

I am on the button with 5d 4h, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from UTG to 2.5bb. Against an early position raise, 54 offsuit is an easy fold as it has very poor equity and playability, even with the advantage of position.

Hand is 3h5c offsuit, extremely weak with low, disconnected cards. Facing a standard UTG raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from Claude, who plays a likely strong range from early position. In SB, OOP postflop with poor playability, speculative value, or equity realization. Pot odds of calling 200 into 400 (3:1) are insufficient given the dead money is minimal and postflop disadvantage is huge. Easy fold to preserve stack in a tournament setting aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

The opponent Claude opened from UTG to 2.5bb. UTG opening ranges are typically strong (top 15-20% of hands). While I am getting reasonable pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 400, needing ~27% equity), K3o is a very poor hand to defend. It has poor playability, suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated by better Kings), and I would be playing out of position against a strong range. Standard strategy dictates folding K3o to an early position raise.

UTG with 4♣3♣ in a 5-handed game is a marginal, speculative hand. With deep stacks (~85BB) and several aggressive players behind who frequently open/3-bet, raising here is thin and calling 100 into a 150 pot commits me to a likely multiway pot where this hand’s equity is poor. The immediate pot odds (100 into a 150 pot → ~40% break-even) don’t justify getting involved out of position against multiple opponents. I’ll preserve my stack and fold, waiting for a better spot to apply pressure or play postflop with position.

I am in UTG+1 with 3d 5h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With several players left to act behind me, including the blinds, there is no reason to play this hand from early position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop on the button with Ac6c in an unopened pot after UTG and UTG+1 folds. A6s is a strong suited ace with good playability, position, and equity against blind calling ranges. Standard to raise for value, isolation, and to build pot in position. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is a balanced size that pressures the blinds while keeping pot manageable. Opponents like Claude are aggressive but this hand plays well postflop. Folding or limping is suboptimal.

I am in the Small Blind with AQo, a premium hand that plays well as a 3-bet against a Button opener (Elon) who has been active and has a wide range. Calling would allow the Big Blind to enter with a discount, creating a multi-way pot where I am out of position. By 3-betting, I aim to isolate the Button, take the initiative, and define ranges. Since I am out of position, a larger 3-bet size (around 3.5x to 4x the open) is appropriate to discourage calls and generate fold equity or extract value from dominated hands like AJ, AT, KQ.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro (SB) after Elon opened from button. A9o is a marginal hand here - we're often dominated by AK, AQ, AJ, AT when called/raised. Being OOP against the 3-bettor makes this even worse. While we have an ace blocker, calling 1000 chips with A9o out of position against a 3-bet is -EV long term. Folding is the disciplined play.

Preflop on the button with Ac6c after opening to 300. Pro 3-bets to 1100 from SB after folds and Claude folds BB. Pot is 1500, calling 800 more (total 1100 committed). A6s suited has excellent playability, straight/flush potential, and dominates weaker aces or suited hands Pro might 3-bet wide from SB OOP. Position is crucial; I can play postflop profitably OOP for Pro. Equity vs typical SB 3-bet range (AA-JJ, AK, AQ, some bluffs/broadways) is around 35-40%, and pot odds are ~2.9:1 requiring only ~26% equity. Folding wastes position and hand strength. 4-betting risks bloating pot OOP postflop or getting called by better; better to call and control postflop with initiative. Stacks deep (82bb effective vs Pro), realize equity in position over jamming/folding.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with an SPR of roughly 2.75. The board comes 8s Js 3h, which connects reasonably well with the Button's calling range (sets, Jx, 8x, flush draws). While I have range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor (holding AA, KK, QQ, AJ), my specific hand (AQo) is currently Ace-high with two overcards. C-betting here is risky because the opponent has shown aggression on flops previously (raising c-bets) and this board texture invites action from draws and pairs. If I bet and get raised, I am forced to fold substantial equity. Checking allows me to pot control, realize my equity (seeing a turn for free if he checks back), and potentially induce bluffs or call a small bet. It protects my checking range which includes strong hands trapping.

Flop 8s Js 3h gives me Ac6c a strong nut club flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity standalone), ace-high blocker, and excellent playability in position. Pro's check OOP after 3-betting preflop suggests possible weakness (missed high cards, air, or traps with sets like 88/JJ/33), but their SB 3-bet range (JJ+, AK/AQ/ATs+, suited broadways) is strong yet this board doesn't hit it super hard except sets/paired J. I have range advantage as button open-caller with initiative now. Pot 2300, deep stacks (~74bb eff). Betting ~60% pot (1400) realizes equity profitably, gets value from worse Ax/draws/pairs Pro checks, denies equity to their floats/OOP draws (spade draws), and builds pot when ahead. Checking risks free cards to improve their equity/draws or giving up initiative. Position allows controlling pot if check-raised. Balanced aggression continues preflop story.

I 3-bet pre-flop with AQo from the Small Blind and was called by the Button (Elon). The flop comes 8s-Js-3h. I checked to the aggressor. Elon bets 1400 into a pot of roughly 2300 (approx. 60% pot). This board texture interacts well with a calling range that includes Jx, sets (88, 33), and flush draws. My hand is Ace-high with two overcards and no flush draw. Given the large bet size, I am getting poor immediate odds to call, and implied odds are limited given the stack depth (SPR ~1.2 on turn). I do not block flush draws (don't hold a spade), so his semi-bluffing range is wide and retains good equity, while his value range has me crushed. AQo is near the bottom of my range here; I have better hands to defend (overpairs, AJ). Folding is the prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

Holding 6c4c UTG in a 5-handed game is generally a fold. While suited connectors have some value, 64s is too weak to open from early position as it is easily dominated by better hands and I will likely have to play out of position against the button or the blinds. Folding to preserve my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here.

Preflop, UTG+1 position (early), with 32o - a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, offsuit, and dominated by any calling range. Deep stack (97BB), but folding avoids spew and difficult post-flop decisions. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (Jc 2h), which is a very weak hand (bottom 20% of hands). In a 5-handed game, while the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, J2o is too weak to justify an open raise. It has poor playability post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the standard and correct play here to conserve chips for better opportunities.
Folded to me in the small blind with Ad6h. This is a standard spot to raise and try to take the blinds or isolate against the big blind. A6o plays well heads-up. I'll raise to 250.

Claude (SB) opened to 250; I’m in the big blind with K♠4♦. Calling costs me 150 more (250 total in the pot), which is a small price relative to my ~84BB stack and the 350 pot (I’m getting ~30% break-even equity). K4o is a marginal holding and can be dominated, but vs a likely wide SB opening range it still has playable equity and I have positional advantage postflop. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin 3-bet bluff or fold away too many hands; calling lets me see a flop, control pot size, and use position to fold to aggression if I miss. I will call the 150 (250 total).

Flop is 8s7c9d - very connected and drawy board. I have Ad6h which is just ace-high with no pair and no draw on this texture. This board heavily favors the big blind's calling range - they'll have many pairs (77, 88, 99, 87, 97, etc.) and straight draws (T6, JT, 56, T, J). C-betting here with no equity when called is burning money. Best to check and potentially give up.

Claude opened from the SB preflop and then checked the flop — his range still contains lots of 7/8/9 pairs and straight/draw hands that connect very well with 7‑8‑9. I hold K♠4♦ and have essentially missed: one overcard and a weak backdoor spade are my only prospects. Betting here would have very low fold equity and will mostly get called by better hands; with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin bluff. Check to control the pot, keep weaker hands and bluffs in my range, and use position to see the turn. Plan: fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw; consider a delayed bluff on a turn that helps my range if he shows further weakness.

Turn pairs the board with 8c. I have Ad6h - just ace high with no pair, no draw on this 8-7-9-8 board. After both checking the flop, Minni likely doesn't have a huge hand either. However, betting here with ace high risks getting check-raised or called by any pair. I have some showdown value if checked through and could win at showdown vs complete air. Checking keeps the pot small and avoids bloating it when I'm likely behind if called.

I have K♠4♦ on 8♠ 7♣ 9♦ 8♣. I completely missed — no pair and no realistic flush/straight draw (with one spade in hand and only one on board a flush is impossible by river). I only have ~6 clean outs (three Ks, three 4s) ≈13% to improve on the river, so my equity is very low. Claude raised preflop then checked the flop and turn, which can be either a medium-strength pot-control line or a slowplay with 8x/sets; either way I have little fold equity and would be bloating the pot by betting. With deep stacks and a 500 pot, I’ll check to control the pot, preserve chips, and see the river. Plan: fold to any meaningful turn/river aggression (or strongly re-evaluate vs a tiny bet), and only look to bluff or value-bet if the river gives a clear advantage (e.g., I pair a King) or Claude shows extreme weakness.

River Ts gives me a 6-high straight (6-7-8-9-T)! The board runs 8s7c9d8cTs and my 6h completes the straight. After checking through flop and turn, Minni likely has a marginal hand - a pair or weak two pair. I should bet for value targeting 8x, 9x, Tx hands, or even trips. Betting around 60-70% pot should extract value while not scaring off weaker holdings.

Fold. I completely missed the river with K♠4♦ on 8♠ 7♣ 9♦ 8♣ T♠ — I have no pair and no realistic showdown equity. Claude opened from the SB, then checked the flop and turn; that line is consistent with medium‑strength value hands (T, 9) or single‑card 8x trips that are now very happy to take a small river value stab. He bet 350 into the ~500 pot (pot is now 850), so calling 350 requires about 41% equity (350/(500+350) ≈ 41%). I have essentially zero chance to be ahead and block few/no bluff combos, so a call is mathematically unjustified and a raise would be an overly thin bluff. Preserve chips and fold to this river aggression.

65o is a trash hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. Offsuit low connectors have poor playability multiway and little showdown value. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. No position advantage and opponents have deep stacks for potential 3-bets. History shows frequent raises but no need to open wide here.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with Tc 6c. This is a weak suited gapper (T6s). With 172 BBs, I have plenty of chips, but this hand is below the standard opening range for the Cutoff, especially with three players left to act including the Button. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or called by better hands that dominate me. Discretion is the better part of valor here; I will fold.

I have 2c3d on the button - one of the worst hands in poker. While button is a great stealing position, 23o is too weak to profitably open even against just the blinds. Better to wait for a better spot.

Heads-up preflop from the small blind with 4♥8♠ — a weak, unconnected offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see the flop (total committed = 100), giving ~25% pot odds to call. Raising to 200 to try and steal is possible, but with this hand I don't want to bloat the pot OOP or face a 3-bet where I must fold. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop cheaply; plan is to fold to meaningful aggression if I completely miss, and continue only with a pair, strong draw, or a favorable board texture.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, the Small Blind (Minni) has just completed the bet to 100. I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6s and have the option to check and see the flop for free. Given that my hand is mediocre and there is no need to bloat the pot or bluff at this stage against a completed blind, checking is the most standard and efficient play to realize my equity.

I missed the flop completely with 8♠4♥ on Jc-3s-Kd: no pair, no realistic backdoor flush or straight, and I’m out of position to the big blind who defends a wide range. The pot is only 200 and betting here would bloat a pot I have almost no equity in. Standard play is to check and concede the initiative — plan to fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I pick up a very favorable turn card or the opponent shows clear weakness by checking back. Preserving my 8,225 stack and waiting for a better spot is the best long-term line.

I am in the Big Blind and checked the option pre-flop. The flop is Jc 3s Kd. I have 8h 6s, which is essentially air on this board (no pair, no draw). The Small Blind (Minni) has checked. In a limped pot like this, there is no reason to bet into a board that favors the pre-flop completion range or the Big Blind range significantly, and I have zero equity. Checking back to see a free turn is the standard play.

I have nothing (8♠4♥) on J♣-3♠-K♦-J♠ — no pair, no real draw, and I’m out of position. The pot is small (≈200) and Flash defended a wide range preflop but has been passive (checked flop). A bet here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and real risk of being called or check-raised by any J, K, or pocket pair. I want to preserve my 8,225 stack in a marginal spot, so I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Flash checks back I can reassess the river and only continue with a pair, a J, or a very favorable runout.

I am in a limped pot from the Big Blind with 8-high and no draws on a board of Jc-3s-Kd-Js. The Small Blind has checked twice. While I could attempt a bluff to take down this small pot of 200 chips, there is little incentive to risk chips with zero equity when my opponent could easily be trapping with a Jack or holding a better high card like an Ace. Checking behind allows me to see a free river and potentially realize some unlikely showdown value or simply fold if the opponent bets the next street.

The river (Jc-3s-Kd-Js-Ks) is a very bad runout for my 8♠4♥ — I have absolutely nothing. The board now contains both a pair of Jacks and a pair of Kings, which means any J or K in an opponent’s hand (and some other holdings like pocket 3s) converts to a full house. My opponent has been passive (check/call line through the hand) and tends to call down weak to medium-strength holdings, so my fold equity on a river bluff is low. The pot is small (200) relative to both stacks, and there's no reason to risk chips with zero equity. I’ll check and conserve my stack; if I face a river bet I’ll fold to any meaningful sizing.

The board has completed with two pairs (Kings and Jacks) and a Trey. My hand is 8-high, meaning my final hand is the two pair on the board with an Eight kicker (KK-JJ-8). In a limped pot where the Small Blind has checked every street, it is possible they are holding a better kicker (9, 10, Queen, or Ace) or even a 3, though my 8 beats a 3. There is no reason to bluff into a small 200-chip pot when the opponent could easily call with any Ace or better high card that naturally beats me. Checking behind is the most prudent play to see the showdown for free.

I am in UTG position with a weak hand, 9c 7h (97 offsuit). In a 5-handed game, opening with 97o from early position is not profitable. The hand has poor equity, is easily dominated, and plays poorly out of position post-flop. With a healthy stack of 17,250, there is no need to get involved with trash hands. The correct play is to fold immediately.
T8o in UTG+1 is too weak to open from early position. Even at a 5-handed table, this hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to profitably raise. Standard fold.

I have A♠ K♣ on the button — a premium hand and excellent spot with two players left to act (SB and BB). Limping/calling is too passive; raising preserves fold equity, narrows the field and builds a pot I can play post‑flop with position. With stacks around 80–115 BB a standard 2.5–3xBB sizing is appropriate: big enough to get folds from weak blinds but not oversized to constantly invite big 3‑bets. I will raise to 300 total. If I face a 3‑bet I will assess size and opponent (call or 4‑bet for value depending on sizing and who 3‑bets).

I am in the Small Blind holding 3c 6d. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. 3-6 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability, especially out of position. Given that I have over 100 big blinds, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand against a button open. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in BB with T7s suited connectors vs button raise to 300 after two folds. Button range is wide including broadway, pairs, suited hands. T7s has good playability with straight and flush potential, solid equity (~35-40% vs wide button open) and implied odds given deep stacks (96BB). Pot odds excellent: calling 200 into 450 (pot becomes 650, ~31% needed). Defending BB wide standard in tournament. Flat call to realize equity OOP, avoid bloating pot OOP with 3bet. Folding too tight, 3bet too thin/polarized here.

Flop Jh 3h 3d completely whiffs my Tc7c. No pair, no flush draw (clubs, board has hearts), no straight draw (T7 doesn't connect with J33; possible backdoor but irrelevant). Ten-high has zero showdown value OOP. First to act in BB vs button raiser who will c-bet frequently with wide range including Jx, 33, Ax, broadway, draws. No fold equity to donk bet air on paired board; checking allows pot control and easy fold to likely c-bet. Deep stacks but no implied odds with nothing. Defending preflop was fine for playability, but this texture folds out clean.

Heads-up on a paired, relatively dry flop J♥ 3♥ 3♦. I raised pre and Elon (BB) simply called, so his range is wide (Jx, pocket pairs, broadways, some suited connectors). With A♠K♣ I have two overcards (no heart draw) and good fold equity as the preflop aggressor — a continuation bet is the +EV line to fold out weaker broadways and unpaired hands and to define his range. I'll bet ~50% pot (325 into 650). Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I still have 6 outs (3 Aces + 3 Kings) and will reassess on the turn — size up for value on an A/K, pot-control or float on blanks, and fold to a strong check-raise that looks like a set.

Facing a 325 bet into 975 pot (pot odds ~3.0:1, needing ~25% equity) on Jh3h3d flop with T7cc. Hand is complete air: no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (T7 doesn't connect), ten-high with zero showdown value OOP vs button c-bet range. Button wide preflop (~25-40% open), c-bets frequently here with Jx, Ax, KQ, sets, flush draws, air. My equity crushed vs value (<20%), marginal vs bluffs but no fold equity for raise OOP on paired board where villain can call/raise light. Defended pre wide for playability, but this dry paired flop misses hard. Easy fold to realize no equity, preserve stack for better spots (94BB deep). No implied odds without hand.
K6o from UTG is too weak to open. UTG requires a tight range and K6 offsuit doesn't make the cut - it plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated by better kings. Easy fold.

5♥2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands — unsuited, unconnected and dominated by almost every calling or raising range. I’m in early position (UTG+1) with multiple players to act behind, including aggressive raisers and a big blind who defends often. Calling 100 to limp is poor: it puts me out of position into multiway pots with very little equity and invites raises. Opening is equally unjustified from this spot with trash. With ~87 BB I have plenty of fold equity later — no need to risk chips here. I’ll fold and wait for a much better spot.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button provides a positional advantage for stealing the blinds, 7-2o has almost no post-flop equity and is not worth the risk of a raise or even a limp. With a comfortable stack of over 115 big blinds, I will wait for a better opportunity and simply fold.

Hand #57, pre-flop in SB with 3h9c, a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high cards. Everyone folded to me, pot 150, to call 50. While SB steal raises can be profitable against passive BB, 39o has terrible post-flop playability OOP, low equity, and no showdown value. Folding preserves stack in a multi-handed tournament, avoiding risky OOP spots with trash. Deep stack allows waiting for better spots. Prioritizing tight-aggressive play to maximize long-term EV.

UTG in a 5‑handed game I need a reasonably strong opening range. 2♦3♥ offsuit is one of the weakest hands — poor equity, easily dominated and not profitable multiway. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, so my break‑even equity is 100/(150+100)=40% — well above what 2‑3o can expect even versus a single hand. The table has regular open/3‑bet action, so calling or min‑raising here frequently puts me in big pots in a bad equity spot. With ~86 BB I can and should wait for better spots. Fold and preserve my stack for a playable hand.

I am in UTG+1 (middle position 5-handed) with Queen-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for a standard opening range from this position. With no action before me except for a fold from Minni, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folded to me on the button in a 5-handed game with K9o, which plays well post-flop in position and has good stealing potential. Standard button raise size of 2.5x the big blind to build the pot and take it down frequently pre-flop against the blinds. Opponents have shown aggression in the past, but position favors us here, and our stack depth supports this play to maximize stack over many hands.

I hold 7h 2c in the Small Blind, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Button (Elon) has raised to 250. Although I have a large stack, calling 200 more chips into a pot of 400 offers pot odds of 33%, while my hand's equity against a button opening range is significantly lower (around 25-28%). Furthermore, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. There is no strategic merit in defending or bluffing with this specific holding in this spot. The disciplined and correct play is to fold.

K3o in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 2.67:1 odds (150 to win 400). Against a typical button opening range, K3o has decent equity, and the price is attractive. I'll defend my big blind here.

I flopped bottom pair with K3o on Q93 two-diamond board. I have showdown value but this is a marginal hand. Standard play is to check to the preflop aggressor. If Elon bets small, I can consider calling with my pair. If he bets big, I can fold. No reason to lead out with bottom pair.

Heads-up on the flop after my button raise was called by Claude in the BB. Board is Qd 9c 3d, giving me second pair (9s) with King kicker, which is a strong but vulnerable hand on a drawy board with two diamonds. Claude checked, which could be weakness or trapping, but as pre-flop aggressor in position, a continuation bet is standard to protect against overcards, diamond draws, and straight draws while extracting value from worse hands like A-high, weaker pairs, or pure draws. Opponent has shown aggression when strong in history, but check suggests possible float or weak range. Betting ~60% pot maintains pressure and builds the pot with our decent showdown value. Deep stacks allow flexibility post-flop.

I have bottom pair (3s) with a king kicker on Q93 two-diamond board. Elon bet 350 into 500, giving me 2.4:1 odds (350 to win 850). With bottom pair and an overcard, I have some showdown value against his c-bet range which includes many air hands. However, I'm behind his value hands (Qx, 9x, overpairs). This is marginal but the pot odds are decent enough to peel one more street and reevaluate on the turn.

Turn brought a blank 4h. Elon checked behind, which suggests he may have been c-betting the flop with air or a weak hand. With bottom pair (3s) and king kicker, I have some showdown value. No reason to bet here - I'd only get called by better hands. Check and see a river.

Heads-up pot on turn after my c-bet was called on a Q-9-3 rainbow-ish flop (two diamonds). I hold middle pair with TKO (K9 on 9c-3d-Qd board), which is strong against his calling range of Ax, flush draws, straight draws, or weaker Qx.
