Turn 4h is a brick that doesn't complete the flush draw and adds minimal new draws (gutshots like 5-2 unlikely). Claude's check-check suggests possible weakness, a draw, or a trap, but his history shows mixed aggression—passive here favors continuation betting for thin value from Ax/floats/weaker pairs and protection vs overcards or missed draws. Position allows control; deep stacks (88bb effective) support building the pot while denying equity. Pot 1250, betting ~2/3 pot (800) sizes well for value, gets calls from worse, and sets up river flexibility. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or inducing bluffs, suboptimal with showdown value.

I have bottom pair (3s) with king kicker on Q-9-3-4 board. Elon bet flop and now fires 800 on turn into ~1250 pot. Pot odds are about 2.5:1 (need ~28% equity). However, his continued aggression strongly suggests he has a real hand - likely Qx, 9x, or better. Bottom pair is way behind his value range, and I have no draws. The turn 4h didn't help me at all. Calling would put me in a difficult spot on the river facing another bet. Time to fold and save chips.

Holding 4d 6c in the UTG position (under-the-gun) in a 5-handed game is an easy fold. This hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop, and acting first against four other opponents with such a weak range is not profitable. Given my healthy stack of over 115 big blinds, there is no reason to pursue this hand.

UTG+1 position with 2d6h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, poor connectivity, and dominated by any raising range. Early position requires a tight range to avoid difficult postflop situations out of position. Pot is small at 150, to call 100 (likely a limp or small bet ahead? Wait, Flash folded, blinds posted, perhaps Pro limped? But irrelevant), but folding is optimal to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stack (100BB) allows patience.

I am on the Button with Ten-Five suited (T5s). The action has folded to me. While the Button position allows for a wide opening range in a 5-handed game, T5s is generally too weak to open profitably, especially considering the Big Blind (Minni) has shown a tendency to defend their blind in previous hands. The hand has poor playability post-flop if called, often being dominated. Therefore, folding is the standard and prudent play here.

Heads-up against the BB with J2o. Everyone folded to me in SB. This is a weak hand but in a steal situation I can raise to take it down. J2o has some playability with the Jack. A standard raise to 250 should get folds often enough to be profitable, and I can fold to any 3-bet.

Call 150 more (250 total committed). 4c3c is a speculative, suited connector with good implied odds and I’ll have position post-flop against Claude’s SB opening range. The pot (350) gives me ~3.3:1 immediate odds to call 150 to see a flop, and with ~85bb stacks there’s plenty of post-flop play to realize equity or fold to heavy aggression. A minimum 3-bet to 400 is an option as a rare bluff, but with this hand I prefer to defend and see a flop in position.

Flop is 7d 5c 9s - completely missed with J2o. This low connected board favors Minni's BB calling range (pocket pairs, suited connectors like 67s, 68s, 86s). I have zero equity - just jack high with no draws. C-betting here is burning chips. Better to check and fold to any aggression.

Check. I’m holding 4♣3♣ on 7♦5♣9♠ — a small, speculative suited connector with only backdoor club potential and a modest straight redraw (the 6s are my main straight outs). Claude was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, so there’s no free value to extract and I don’t want to build a pot with a marginal hand. Checking lets me realize equity (roughly ~38% to pair or make a straight/better by the river) and see a free turn. Plan: if Claude bets a small/medium size on the turn I’ll call to see if I improve; I’ll fold to large, committed sizing unless I’ve improved. If I hit a 6 (made straight) or pair a 3/4 I’ll convert to value; for now it’s best to check and re-evaluate on the next street.

Turn Ks is a good scare card for my range as the preflop aggressor. However, I still have complete air (J-high) with no draws. Minni checked flop showing weakness, but betting here commits me to potentially barreling river with nothing. If Minni has any pair (which they likely do given the call preflop), they're not folding. The risk/reward of bluffing with zero equity isn't worth it. Check and potentially give up or see a free showdown.

Check. I have 4♣3♣ on 7♦5♣9♠K♠ — no pair and essentially only a one-card straight draw (any 6) plus the three remaining 3s and three remaining 4s. That’s 10 outs on the river (10/46 ≈ 21.7%), and a flush is no longer possible with one card to come. Claude was the preflop raiser but checked both flop and turn, which looks like weakness or pot control rather than a value line. Betting here is a pure bluff that risks bloating the pot against hands that will call or raise (pocket pairs, Kx, 9x), and with ~84bb stacks there’s no need to force the issue. I’ll check to realize equity for cheap and keep the pot small; plan to call a small/medium river bet if odds are favorable (or bluff-catch marginally), but fold to large river aggression unless I’ve improved.

River Qh completes the board 7d5c9sKsQh. I have J-high with complete air. Both players checked through flop and turn, showing mutual weakness. Minni just checked river to me - they're giving me a free showdown. Bluffing here into a small 500 pot with J-high is risky; Minni likely has at least a small pair or ace-high that could call. The passive action throughout suggests neither of us connected strongly. Better to just check and lose the small pot rather than invest more chips bluffing.

I missed my draws with 4♣3♣ — no pair and no flush with the river Q♥. Claude was the preflop raiser but has played very passively (checked flop and turn and then checked the river), which suggests pot control or a medium-strength hand (Kx/Qx/9x/7x or a pocket pair). A river bet here would be a pure bluff with little fold equity against a player who has been check-calling; many of Claude's likely hands will call a small bet. The pot is only 500 and our stack is deep enough that there's no reason to risk chips on a thin bluff that is unlikely to fold better showdown hands. Checking preserves our stack and takes the cheapest line — if Claude suddenly bets the river I can re-evaluate (fold to a larger size, consider a call only to very small bets). For now I will check and take it to showdown.

Preflop UTG in 5-handed game with As8s, a suited Ace with good playability, flush and straight potential. Standard opening range includes this hand due to suited broadway potential and deep stacks. Opponents have shown aggression but also frequent folds to raises. Raising to 250 (2.5BB) builds the pot, takes initiative, and has good fold equity against blinds while denying equity to speculative hands. Folding is too tight; limping is weak and invites multiway pots.

I hold [9s 4d] in UTG+1. The UTG player (Elon) has opened to 250 (2.5BB). My hand is unsuited, disconnected, and very weak (trash). Facing an early position raise, I am significantly behind their range. I have no pot odds or playability to justify a call, and bluffing here is ill-advised given the strength of a UTG open and players left to act. The correct and only play is to fold.
42 suited is a very weak hand. Even on the button with position, calling an UTG raise with this holding is unprofitable. The hand has poor high card value and limited straight potential. Easy fold.

I have 6s6h in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250. With stacks around ~80–85 BB this is a standard set-mining spot: calling 200 more (250 total committed) into a 400-pot gives excellent pot odds and very strong implied odds if I hit a set. A 3-bet to isolate is an option, but with a small pair I prefer to keep the pot manageable — 3-betting here risks a 4-bet or bloating the pot and reduces my implied odds. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy, committed aggression if I miss, and extract maximum value if I hit a set or improve on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Seven offsuit. Elon opened for 250 from UTG, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me only 150 more to see the flop. This gives me excellent pot odds of 4:1 (requiring about 20% equity). While K7o is not a premium hand and plays poorly multi-way, the price is too good to pass up, and I will be closing the pre-flop action. I'll call and see if I can flop a King or a strong draw.

I flopped a full house (6s6h on 6d-4h-4s) — sixes full of fours is extremely strong. The only realistic hand that currently beats me is pocket 4s (quads), which is rare, so my priority is extracting value from overpairs/top pairs/one-pair hands and any weaker full houses. With two opponents and deep stacks I should lead for value rather than slow-play; a ~2/3-pot bet (500 into 750) is large enough to build the pot and charge calls but small enough to keep worse hands in the pot. If I face a sizable check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (that line often indicates quads), but I’m willing to continue given how strong this hand is.

On a flop of 6d-4h-4s, I hold King-Seven offsuit. Minni, who called from the small blind pre-flop, has led out for 500 into a pot of 750. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor from UTG, is still left to act behind me. This lead typically indicates either a made hand like a 4 or a pocket pair, or a very aggressive bluff. Given that I have only King high with no draws and am sandwiched between a bettor and the original raiser, there is no reason to continue in this hand. My equity is low, and I am likely behind at least one, if not both, opponents.

Facing a donk bet of 500 (66% pot) from SB Minni on a paired 4s board (6d4h4s). My As8s gives me the nut spade flush draw (9 outs), ace-high, and backdoor straight potential. As preflop UTG raiser, my range is perceived as strong (overpairs, broadways), giving excellent fold equity against Minni's likely weak donk range: pocket pairs below 66, weak 4x, draws, or floats trying to take it down. Calling is suboptimal as it invites check-raises or free cards; folding is too tight with 35%+ equity and position. Semi-bluff raise extracts value from worse (weaker draws/pairs), protects against overcards, and builds the pot when I hit. Sizing to 1500 (3x the bet) balances value, protection, and fold equity while leaving room for further action given deep stacks.

Board 6d-4h-4s with 6s6h — I have a near‑nut full house (sixes full of fours). The only hand that beats me is pocket 4s (quads). I led small for value (500) to keep worse hands in; Elon’s large check‑raise to 1500 polarizes his range to quads or a very strong/blocked value hand but also includes many hands I beat. With virtually no realistic draws and a very strong made hand, I want to build the pot and extract value from worse made hands rather than folding or flatting and giving him a free turn. I will raise to the minimum allowed (2500 total) to charge calls, keep worse hands in, and set up for further value on later streets while still leaving flexibility if Elon 4‑bets (which would strongly indicate quads).

Facing a 3-bet jam nearly from Minni after her donk lead and my continuation raise on a paired flop. My As8s holds the nut spade flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity realized to river against her likely range of 4x, sets, or bluffs). Current pot odds are excellent: 1000 to call into 4750 (~22% pot), far better than break-even for the draw. Folding discards too much equity and fold equity already invested. Raising all-in (to ~9800 total) has some fold equity against bluffs but low against her strong range, as her 5675 remaining stack gets favorable odds (~30% needed) to call off with any 4x or better, building a massive pot OOP where I am crushed at showdown if no improvement. Calling preserves stack flexibility, controls the pot size, realizes equity in position on turn (I act after her), and allows check-fold to overbet/shove if turn bricks or value bet if it hits spade/backdoor. Her SB limp-call preflop then aggressive donk-3bet suggests capped range (trips unlikely, quads rare) polarized to value 4x/sets or thin bluffs/draws she continues with, many of which I dominate post-flush. Over long tournament, balancing aggression with disciplined equity realization maximizes EV.

I have the near‑nut full house (6s6h on 6d‑4h‑4s). Only realistic hands that beat me on the turn are pocket 4s (quads) or pocket 99 (the 9 on the turn gives 99 a boat) — roughly 1 combo of 44 and ~3 combos of 99 given the board. Elon’s flop check‑raise then call is consistent with a strong but not-nut range (overpairs, 99, maybe a slow‑played set/quads), but 44 is relatively unlikely because he flat‑called my 3‑bet. The vast majority of his range is still behind my hand, so I should extract value and deny a free river card. I’ll bet for value but not polarize the pot so much that marginal hands fold or that I immediately commit to a pot I can’t fold in the face of a huge raise. Betting 3000 (~50%–55% of the current pot) charges overpairs and single‑pair hands, keeps worse full houses in, and lets me re‑evaluate if Elon responds with a big raise (which would narrow him to the few combos that beat me). Plan: bet 3000 now, call/reevaluate to river based on his action — fold to an obviously polarized shove if the sizing indicates quads/999s, otherwise extract more value on the river.

Turn board 9h brings a heart flush draw possibility and pairs 9s, but my As8s remains the nut spade flush draw with 10 clean outs (~21.7% equity raw). Pot odds to call 3000 into 8750 + 3000 = 11750 require ~25.5%, marginally tight but favorable against Minni's SB limp-call range polarized to 4x trips (A4s/K4s/54s/34s etc., which I dominate post-flush), 64 two pair, or sets (44 quads/66 trips), with few spades in her range blocking. Her aggressive donk-3bet-shove line caps her somewhat (unlikely premium overpairs/sets always), includes bluffs/merely draws she turns into value bets. Raising offers minimal fold equity given her commitment (~2675 behind) and commits my 7300 stack OOP on river if called, while pure equity call EV near-zero but +EV with position on river for value extraction if hit. Folding discards too much equity after pot-committing flop call; consistent with prior semi-bluff/equity realization strategy in deep-stack tournament to maximize EV over 150 hands. History shows Minni calls light but folds to heavy aggression often; here her strength warrants disciplined call over spew.

I have 6s6h on 6d-4h-4s-9h-Ad — sixes full of fours (66644). That’s an extremely strong hand but the river Ace actually creates several realistic boats/quads that now beat me: pocket 44 (quads), pocket 99 (turned a full house on the 9), and crucially pocket AA (which turns into AAA44 on the river). Elon’s line (UTG open, flop check‑raise, turn call) is consistent with one of those heavy hands. Betting now risks getting raised or called by the relatively small number of combos that beat me; checking lets him have to take the initiative and reveals more about his holdings. I’ll check river to either take it down uncontested if he checks behind or to get a clear signal — I’ll call smaller, non‑polarized bets for thin value and fold to an obviously polarized shove that represents quads/999/AA.

River Ad pairs the top card, dramatically improving my hand from missed nut spade flush draw to two pair, aces and fours (As-Ad-4s-4h-9h), a very strong hand on this board. Minni's line (SB limp-call, flop donk on paired board, 3-bet my c-bet, turn bet) is consistent with trips 4x (remaining 4c/4d + kicker, ~limited combos), two pair (64/94), sets (66/99), or aggressive bluffs/draws semi-ing. However, only trips 4s or better (66/99 sets, rare 44 quads) beat me, and her river check after aggression suggests a capped range: unlikely nuts (would often bet for value), more likely medium strength like overpairs (JJ-QQ/TT beat pair 4s but lose to AA44), weaker two pair, or air wanting showdown. Pure bluffs unlikely to check without rep strength. With pot 11750, her 2675 stack, and my 4300, betting 2675 all-in offers great SPR, gets value from her ~50%+ range that calls (overpairs, board pair floats, weaker trips? No trips beat but call anyway), while fold equity vs pure air preserves stack. Checking risks cheap showdown where she wins with better ~30% time but realizes equity OOP; betting maximizes EV in tournament by extracting from worse and denying free rivers (though last street). History shows Minni calls light (e.g., Hand 27 river shove/call, Hand 5 river call); balanced aggression post-draw whiff but range advantage now wins long-term stack growth.

Board: 6d-4h-4s-9h-Ad; my hand 6s6h = sixes full of fours (66644). The only realistic holdings that beat me are pocket 44 (quads), pocket 99 (turned a bigger full house on the 9), or pocket AA (rivered a bigger full house). That's roughly 1 combo of 44 + ~3 combos of 99 + 3 combos of AA = ~7 combos. Elon’s line (UTG open, flop check‑raise, turn call) is strong, but his tiny river sizing (2675 into a 14425 pot — I need ~15.6% equity to justify a call) reads more like a blocking/thin value bet than a polarized shove. Against his likely range (many overpairs, single pairs and slower full houses), my hand is far ahead. Given the pot odds and the very small number of combos that beat me, calling the 2675 (effectively all‑in) is +EV — extract value from worse hands and only fold to the extremely small number of nut hands which I’m unlikely to face often.

I hold King-Four offsuit (K4o) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a statistically weak hand that does not perform well post-flop and is easily dominated. In a 5-handed game, while ranges are wider, K4o is still well below the threshold for a profitable opening raise. Additionally, the Big Blind is short-stacked, increasing the risk of a shove if I open light. The standard and prudent play is to fold.
7d6d is a suited connector - playable but marginal from UTG+1 with 4 players behind. Early position makes this hand difficult to profitably open. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

Button with A♠5♥ — a playable Ace with position and an ace-blocker. Only the SB and a very short-stacked BB (Elon ~15BB) to act behind me, so this is a clear open-raise spot to seize the initiative and pick up the blinds or isolate the short stack. I’ll open to 250 (≈2.5x BB) — a standard, familiar sizing that gives fold equity vs the SB but keeps contestable price vs the short BB. Plan: if Elon shoves (~15BB) I’ll call — his shove range is wide at this depth and A5 has decent showdown equity. If Flash (SB) 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range (likely fold to a large isolation 3-bet without improvement). Postflop I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously on Ace-high boards where I’m likely dominated.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s4h, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the button. Given the poor equity of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Preflop in BB with Kc2c vs Minni's button raise to 250 after folds. My stack is ~15BB (1525), short-stacked. Pot 400, to call 150 more (~1.5BB). K2s suited has some equity vs wide button range (~35-40%) and pot odds are good (~3:1), but OOP postflop playability is poor with low kicker and short stack. Shoving 1525 risks entire stack for 400 pot; fold equity uncertain vs aggressive Minni who calls/3bets often from history, and K2s dominated by better Ks in calling range. Standard short-stack BB defense vs BTN open shoves K8s+/Q9s+/etc., not K2s. Calling OOP leaves awkward SPR postflop. Best to fold trash hand and wait for better spot.
K2o is a weak hand from UTG position. With 4 players left to act behind me and several big stacks, this is a clear fold. Not worth opening with this marginal holding.

UTG+1 with 9♣5♠ — a weak, unsuited hand in early position. Pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to see the flop. Table has been showing lots of opens/raises (~250) and I’ll be out of position vs the button and big stack in the blinds. Limping or raising here is suboptimal: calling invites multiway spots with poor equity and raising is too light from this seat (vulnerable to 3-bets or a shove from the short SB). Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 5-handed game. With both players before me folding, I have a standard opening raise. I am aware that Elon in the Small Blind is short-stacked (less than 15 BBs), and my raise might induce a shove from him, which I am likely to call given the strength of my hand and the pot odds.

Short stack of 1475 (~15BB) in SB with QcTc, a strong suited connector. Facing isolation raise from deep-stacked button after two folds (UTG and UTG+1). Button range is wide (likely 35-50% of hands). QTs has excellent equity (~45%+) vs this range due to straight/flush potential and domination of weaker Qx/Tx. Calling OOP postflop is awkward with short stack, especially with BB to act who may squeeze. Folding is too tight for this spot. 3-bet shove maximizes fold equity from BB, extracts value from button's wide calling range (AJ+, KQo+, suited connectors, pairs), and avoids postflop SPR issues. Optimal play to build stack in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with [Ts 3d], a very weak hand. The action involves a raise from the Button (Flash) to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Elon) to 1475. Facing a 3-bet with 10-3 offsuit is an immediate fold. I have no equity to justify calling, especially with the original raiser still left to act behind me. The pot odds are insufficient for such a trash hand against a 3-bet range.

I hold KQo on the button, a strong hand in short-handed play. I opened for 2.5x the big blind, and Elon in the Small Blind has 3-bet to 1475, leaving himself with only 50 chips. This is essentially an all-in shove. Given Elon's extremely short stack (starting the hand with roughly 15 BBs), his range is very wide, including many hands that KQo dominates or flips against (like AJ, AT, KJ, or lower pairs). The pot odds are excellent; I need to call 1225 more to win a total pot that will be approximately 3050, requiring about 40% equity. KQo has more than enough equity against a desperate short-stack range. I will call the raise and proceed to the flop, where the remaining 50 chips will likely go in regardless.

Remaining stack of 50 into 3050 pot (~1.6% pot size) on flop of 9s Js Jd with Qc Tc, giving an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 4x8, 4xK for 9-T-J-Q-K or 8-9-T-J-Q) and queen overcard blocker. ~32% equity on turn/river vs typical calling range (pairs, broadway, suited Ax). Pre-flop, button called 3-bet shove with wide range (AJ+, KQ+, suited connectors, mid pairs). Flop is draw-heavy (spade flush draw, str8 draws), but shoving 50 as semi-bluff maximizes EV: high equity if called, plus fold equity vs marginal hands like weak Ax, small pairs without draw (e.g., 88-77, AQo). Checking risks facing a bet where I have to call anyway OOP, or getting check-behind but denying free realization of equity. Tiny bet size has minimal risk, builds pot for tournament stack growth. Optimal play to get all-in with ~30%+ equity.

I have King-Queen high on a board of 9s-Js-Jd. Pre-flop, Elon 3-bet to leave himself with only 50 chips, which he has now put into the pot. The pot is 3100, and I only need to call 50 more to see the rest of the hand. With pot odds of 62:1, folding is mathematically impossible, as I have significant equity with two overcards and the potential to win against any of Elon's desperate shoves that I currently beat or have high card value against.

In a 5-max game with deep stacks (≈172 BB) 8♠6♠ is a speculative but playable suited connector UTG — worth opening rather than limping/passively folding. I want initiative and fold equity, and a standard 3x BB open (300 total) fits the table tendencies (other players commonly raise ~2.5–3x). Plan: take the lead postflop — c-bet when the flop hits our range (spades, connected/low boards) and use position/pressure; play cautiously if facing heavy resistance. If I face a large 3-bet (e.g. >~3x my open, ≳900 total) I’ll fold; vs a short-stack shove (Elon ~31 BB) I’ll evaluate pot odds and his tendencies and lean to calling if he’s shoving wide.

I am holding 7 of diamonds and 2 of spades, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from Minni in the UTG position, there is no strategic reason to play this hand, especially out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

2d3d is a speculative hand at best, low suited connectors with poor equity against an UTG raising range from Minni, which is likely strong (high pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.). Even on the button with position, calling 300 into a 450 pot commits ~10% of my stack (3150), but SB and BB still act and can squeeze or call with stronger hands. Playability is limited preflop as it rarely flops strong; better to fold and preserve stack for premium spots in a tournament with 150 hands total.

Opponent Minni opens to 300 (3bb) from UTG in a 5-handed game (equivalent to Hijack). With 170bb effective stacks, [Qh, Kd] is a strong enough hand to defend from the Small Blind, as it has good equity (~50-55%) against a standard 5-handed opening range. However, 3-betting from the Small Blind creates a bloated pot out of position against a range that likely contains dominating hands like AK/AQ or pocket pairs. A flat call allows me to realize equity with a strong broadway hand while keeping the pot manageable, despite the positional disadvantage. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 to win roughly 700+), and folding would be too tight given the short-handed table dynamics.

I have 2c5d in the BB - one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni and a call from Pro, I need to call 200 into a 700 pot (3.5:1 odds). While the pot odds are decent, 2-5 offsuit is too weak to play profitably, especially out of position against two opponents. I'll be in bad spots postflop almost always. Fold.

I hold [Qh, Kd] on a flop of [Js, 9h, 5c]. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Ten makes a straight). As the pre-flop caller out of position against an UTG raiser, standard strategy dictates checking to the aggressor. The board texture connects reasonably well with an UTG opening range (sets, AJ, KJ, QQ+), so leading out (donk betting) would accomplish little. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's subsequent action.

I opened UTG with 8♠6♠ and Pro (SB) just checked the flop Js‑9h‑5c — a good spot to continue as the preflop aggressor. My hand has no pair but it has real semi‑bluff equity: four outs to a 7 for a made straight (≈8.5% on the turn, ≈16.5% by the river) and backdoor spade potential (≈21.3% to hit a spade on the turn, ≈4.2% to complete two spades by river). The board tends to hit my opening range (broadways, A‑X, Jx) so a c‑bet can often take down the pot and charges weaker pairs/draws. I’ll make a medium-sized c‑bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the price reasonable to see a turn if called. If I face a large raise I will fold this turn‑card‑dependent semi‑bluff; if called I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue aggressively with a 7, a spade, or a favorable free card.

Opponent bets 350 into a pot of 700 (50% pot). I hold [Qh, Kd] on a [Js, 9h, 5c] board. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Ten). I need roughly 25% equity to call (350 / 1400). My 4 gutshot outs alone give about 16% by the river, and the 6 overcard outs add significant equity, making my total equity likely around 30-35% against a standard range. Folding is too tight given the price and draw strength. Raising would isolate me against better hands. Calling is the correct play to realize equity.

The turn brings the Ace of Diamonds [Ad], making the board [Js, 9h, 5c, Ad]. This card heavily favors the UTG opener's range (which includes AK, AQ, AJ) rather than my Small Blind flat-calling range. While I still hold a double gutshot straight draw (a Ten makes a straight), leading out (donk betting) into the aggressor on a scare card that hits their range is strategically unsound. I will check to see if Minni continues their aggression. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing and implied odds for my draw.

Check. The Ad on the turn kills my backdoor spade equity and leaves me only four 7-outs (~8.7% to hit the river). I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop, but Pro’s call on the flop and his check on the turn indicate he has at least some showdown value (Jx, 9x, small pair or an Ace/AX occasionally). With very low improvement equity and a 1,400 pot, a double-barrel would be a thin bluff that will often be called by better hands; checking controls the pot and preserves my ability to realize equity or take a free river. Plan: check the turn; if Pro bets a small amount I may call to see the river, fold to large pressure, and if the river brings a 7 I will proceed aggressively for value.

I hold the absolute nuts (Broadway Straight, A-K-Q-J-T) on a rainbow board. Minni opened UTG, bet the flop, but checked back the turn (Ace). The turn check usually caps her range to marginal made hands (like KK, QQ, weak Aces, or 2nd pair) or sets she decided to slowplay. Given she checked the turn, she is likely to check back the river with her marginal hands. Therefore, checking is suboptimal as I would miss value. I need to bet to extract chips from hands like Ax, two pair, or sets. A massive bet might fold out her one-pair hands, so a value bet of roughly 40-45% of the pot is appropriate to induce a call from a wider range. If she has AK, we chop, but I must play to maximize value against inferior holdings.

Fold. I opened and c‑bet the Js‑9h‑5c flop; Pro called the flop and both of us checked the Ad turn. The river Ts is a blank for my 8♠6♠ — no pair, no straight (I missed my 7-outs) and no flush. Pro's small river bet (600 into ~2,000) is a thin value size paid off by pairs like Jx/Tx/9x or Ax, hands he was happy to call the flop with. Calling 600 to win ~2,600 (~23% break‑even equity) requires meaningful showdown equity I simply don't have. A raise would be a very thin, high‑variance bluff against a caller of my flop bet and is unnecessary with deep stacks and many hands left. Preserve chips and fold.

I am dealt 7h 6h in the UTG position in a 5-handed game. Suited connectors like 76s have good playability and are strong enough to open from any position in a short-handed game. Opening with a raise allows me to take the lead in the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately. Given the stack sizes, specifically Elon who is short-stacked at 3,150, I need to be aware of a potential shove, but 250 is a standard opening size that maintains pot control while being aggressive.

2h4c is a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Flash's UTG raise to 250 suggests a strong range, and calling from UTG+1 out of position with 31BB effective stack is -EV. No reason to speculate; preserve stack for premium hands.

I am holding 8c 4s on the Button, facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG. 84 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability. Facing an Early Position open, which typically represents a stronger range, I cannot profitably call or raise. The correct and standard play is to fold.

2d7d is a weak suited hand. Facing a UTG raise, this is an easy fold from the small blind. The hand has poor playability and will rarely flop well enough to continue profitably against an early position raise.

Holding QQ in the big blind facing a single UTG open to 250 in a 5-handed game. QQ is a premium hand and with deep stacks I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener, and get value while charging speculative hands. A standard 3-bet sizing of ~3.5–4x the open works well here — I’ll raise to 1000 total. This sizing (1) folds out many weaker A/Kx and small pairs, (2) prices out multiway callers, and (3) keeps us flexible postflop with plenty of stack behind. If he calls I plan to c-bet for value/protection; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate against his range (tighten up vs a big 4-bet, but continue vs smaller or polarized 4-bets).

I opened to 250 from UTG with 7h6h, and Minni in the Big Blind has 3-bet to 1000. This is a 4x raise, which is quite strong, but I have a stack of nearly 95 big blinds and, most importantly, I will have post-flop position. Suited connectors like 76s have high playability in 3-bet pots when deep-stacked. The price to call is 750 to win a total pot of 2050, giving me roughly 2.7:1 direct odds. Given my position and the potential implied odds if I hit a disguised monster (straight or flush), calling is a viable and standard play in a high-stakes tournament setting to maximize potential value from an aggressive opponent.

I have QQ on a very dry 2♠ 9♥ 7♣ flop — an overpair to the board. I was the preflop 3-bettor and I act first here (out of position), so a value/protection continuation bet is standard: this board hits few of my opponent’s likely calling hands but gets called by worse (AQ/Ax, KQ, medium pairs like TT/88, some 9x) and denies equity to any backdoor combos. Pot is 2050; I’ll size for about half-pot to extract value and keep weaker hands and floats paying to see a turn. Bet 1000. Plan: take it down if he folds; if called, re-evaluate on the turn for further value or pot control; if he raises big I’ll re-assess against his range and fold to extreme polarized pressure.

On this flop of 2s 9h 7c, I have hit middle pair with 7h 6h. The pot is 3050 and Minni has bet 1000, giving me roughly 3:1 odds to call. Minni's 3-bet range pre-flop from the big blind certainly contains strong overpairs like JJ-AA and top pairs like A9s, but it also contains many high overcards like AK, AQ, and AJ that I am currently ahead of. Given that I have position and a made hand with some backdoor straight and flush potential, folding would be too tight. Raising is risky against a range that could contain overpairs. Calling is the most balanced approach, allowing me to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot manageable.

I hold QQ — a clean overpair — on 2♠‑9♥‑7♣‑2♣. I was the preflop 3‑bettor and fired the flop; I’m out of position to the original raiser who flat‑called both pre and the flop. The turn pairs the board which introduces some risk (deuce combos / sets), but those are relatively uncommon given his line. My hand still beats the majority of his calling range (worse pairs, KQ/Ax, etc.), so I want to extract value while keeping the pot controllable OOP. A standard half‑pot turn bet (~2,000) charges worse pairs and any backdoor draws without overcommitting. Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river for further value; if he check‑raises big/polarized I’ll assume sets/trips and fold or strongly re‑evaluate rather than auto‑shoving.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second deuce (2s 9h 7c 2c). Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, continues with a 2000-chip bet into a 4050-chip pot. My pair of sevens (middle pair) is extremely vulnerable here. Minni's range for 3-betting from the big blind and double-barreling includes strong overpairs like TT, JJ, QQ, KK, and AA, as well as top-pair hands like A9. I am only beating total bluffs or overcards like AK/AQ that have chosen to fire twice. Given the pot size and the likelihood of another large bet on the river, calling 2000 with a marginal middle pair is too risky. I will fold to preserve my remaining 5700 chips for a better spot.

UTG position in 5-handed play with J3o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and no strong blockers or suited/connectors. Standard tight play requires folding to avoid difficult post-flop spots and preserve my 31BB stack for better opportunities. No history suggests loosening up here.

I am in the Hijack (UTG+1) with a strong broadway hand, King-Queen offsuit. In a 5-handed game, this is a clear opening hand. The UTG player has folded. I have a deep stack (179 BB). Raising allows me to build the pot with a good hand and potentially take down the blinds immediately or play in position against the blinds. A standard open size in this tournament has been 2.5x the big blind.

I have Th7h on the button - a suited connector with good playability in position. Pro raised to 250 from UTG+1. With position and a hand that can make straights and flushes, this is a reasonable spot to flat call and see a flop. My position will help me navigate postflop. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting turns this into a bluff against an early position raiser.

5♣2♦ is a very weak, unsuited hand with negligible equity. Pro opened from UTG+1 and the button called, so this will be a multiway pot and I’ll be out of position postflop against at least one competent opponent. Calling 200 (to 250 total) to play a marginal, often-losing spot is +EV negative long-term, and a 3-bet bluff (min raise to 400) is ill-advised facing an early-position opener and a caller. Stack is deep enough to wait for a profitable opportunity — I fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ah6d. The action has been a raise to 250 from Pro (UTG+1) and a call from Claude (Button). The pot is currently 650 and it costs me 150 more to call. I am getting very good pot odds (roughly 4.3 to 1) to see a flop. While my hand is mediocre and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too attractive to fold Ace-high in a multi-way pot. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

I have top pair with an Ace on a flop of Ac-8c-Tc. However, my kicker (6) is very weak. In a multi-way pot against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and another caller (Claude), I should proceed with caution. Betting into two opponents here often only gets called or raised by better hands. Checking allows me to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts and keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate the strength of my hand relative to their actions.

I raised pre-flop with KdQs and got two callers. The flop comes 8c As Tc, which is an Ace-high board with a flush draw. This texture connects well with the calling ranges of both the Big Blind and the Button (who often flat with Ax, broadways, or pocket pairs). In a multi-way pot, c-betting this board with just King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) is risky because it is likely at least one opponent has an Ace or a strong draw. Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating, while better hands will call or raise. Checking allows me to realize the equity of my gutshot draw for free and potentially see a turn card without bloating the pot.

I flopped middle pair (tens) with 7 kicker on 8c As Tc. Both opponents checked to me. I have decent showdown value and position. The board has flush draw potential with two clubs. Betting for value and protection makes sense - I can get called by worse tens, flush draws, and smaller pairs. A bet around 350-400 (45-50% pot) is appropriate.

I have top pair with an Ace on a flop of As-Tc-8c. While my kicker (6) is weak, folding top pair to a single half-pot bet in this situation would be overly tight. Claude's bet of 400 into a 800 pot (making it 1200) offers me 4:1 pot odds. I need to be cautious because the board is quite coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible, and I am out of position. However, my hand is strong enough to warrant seeing a turn card. I will call the 400 and re-evaluate based on the turn card and subsequent action from Pro and Claude.

I hold King-Queen offsuit on a flop of 8c As Tc. I checked the flop, Claude bet 400, and Flash called 400. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) but no made pair. The fact that Flash called the bet in front of me significantly reduces the likelihood that a bluff is happening and increases the chance that someone has a strong Ace or a flush draw. My pot odds are roughly 5:1, requiring about 16% equity. While a gutshot has roughly 16% equity to hit by the river, many of my outs (clubs) are tainted, and I have zero showdown value currently. Playing a gutshot multi-way out of position on a wet board is losing poker. Folding is the correct decision.

On the turn, the 6c has improved my hand to two pair (Aces and Sixes). However, this card also completes a possible flush. Being out of position against Claude, I should check to see how he reacts to this board texture. If Claude bets, I will have to carefully consider his range, which includes many clubs given his flop bet. Checking allows me to control the pot size while holding a strong but vulnerable hand on a coordinated board.

The 6c completes the flush draw. Flash called my flop bet and now checked the turn - could be trapping with a flush, or could have Ax/weak pair checking for pot control. My middle pair with no club is very vulnerable now. Betting only gets called by better hands (flushes, Ax, better tens). Check back for pot control and showdown value.

The river completes a four-flush board with the 9 of clubs. I hold two pair (Aces and Sixes) but do not have a club in my hand. While Claude's check on the turn suggests he might not have had a made flush at that point, any single club in his range now beats me. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, as most non-club hands will fold to a lead on this texture. Checking is the best option to control the pot and attempt to reach a cheap showdown.

River brought the 9c, completing a 4-club board. My Th7h (middle pair, no club) is essentially worthless - any single club beats me. Flash has been passive throughout, calling flop and checking turn/river. Betting achieves nothing - I only get called by hands that beat me (any club = flush). Check back for a free showdown in case Flash has air or a worse non-flush hand.

I am in UTG position at a 5-handed table with Ten of Diamonds and Four of Diamonds (T4s). This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low rank. Opening from early position with such a hand is generally a losing strategy as it often gets dominated or faces 3-bets from the players yet to act. Given my healthy stack size, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
T7o is a weak offsuit hand in early position (UTG+1). This is not a profitable open from this position - it's too weak and will be dominated often. Easy fold.

I'm on the button with Js6c, stacks are deep (18600) and two blinds to act (Flash SB 7000, Elon BB 3050). This is a classic spot to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds with position — J6o is marginal but playable in position. A standard 2.5–3xBB open keeps the raise meaningful without bloating the pot unnecessarily and gives me fold equity against the blinds. If I face a shove/large 3-bet from the short BB (Elon) I can fold and preserve my deep stack; if I get called I'll play postflop in position. I will raise to a 3x BB size to attempt to take the pot now or see a flop heads-up with positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni, who has a large stack and has been playing aggressively, raised from the Button. While a Button raise can often be wide, 8-3 offsuit does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet, especially while out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 70BB stack.

AQo is a premium hand in the BB facing a button min-raise from Minni, who raises frequently from late position with a wide range based on hand history (e.g., Hand 5, 16, 29). Button ranges are typically broad, and AQo has excellent equity against them (dominates Ax weaker, suited connectors, etc.). With 30BB stack, 3-betting for value and fold equity is optimal OOP heads-up. Raising to 900 (3x her raise size) balances protection, builds pot, and pressures her wide range. Calling risks multiway if Flash wakes up, but he's folded SB; still, 3-bet isolates. Folding is too tight. Postflop OOP playability is good with top pair potential.

I opened to 300 on the button with Js6c and Elon (short BB ~22.5bb) 3‑bet to 900. J6o is a marginal, often‑dominated holding versus a 3‑bet range — and Elon's 900 size out of a short stack is effectively a committal/polarized play. Calling 600 to see a flop invites an easy shove or tough postflop decisions for very little equity; 4‑betting with J6o would be reckless. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

5h8h from UTG is too weak to open. Suited connectors play better in position and this one is low. With 4 players behind me including some aggressive opponents, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding J♥3♠ UTG+1 in a 5-handed game is a marginal spot. J3o is a weak, poorly connected hand that will be dominated by most calling and 3‑betting ranges; it has very limited postflop playability out of position against the SB, BB and Button. Limping (calling 100) invites multiway pots that are negative for this hand, and open‑raising (my usual 2.5x to 250) would be an over-commitment with little equity or fold equity against the active players behind (Elon and Flash show aggression). With a healthy 183 BB stack I can wait for better, more playable spots. Fold and preserve stack for a stronger opportunity.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. The action has folded to me. While A4o is a marginal hand, in a five-handed game from the button, it is a standard opening candidate to attack the blinds, especially since the small blind (Elon) is relatively short-stacked and the big blind (Pro) has a massive stack and might be playing conservatively or wide. Raising puts pressure on the blinds to fold. A standard 2.5x open (250) is appropriate here to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early.

Flash raised to 250 from the button, which is a wide range including many hands worse than QJs suited. QJs is a strong suited Broadway hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. Facing a min-raise effectively (2.5x), calling 200 more into a 400 pot offers excellent implied odds and direct pot odds (3:1). Out of position but SB vs button is manageable, especially if BB folds often. Folding is too tight given equity vs button range (~40-45% equity). 3-betting OOP with non-premium hand risks playing a bloated pot OOP. Plan to call, see flop, and play aggressively if it hits or as a semi-bluff if draws.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc9d facing a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 600 chips, and I need to call 150 more to see the flop, giving me pot odds of 5:1 (requiring ~16.7% equity). Although K9o is not a premium hand and being out of position multi-way is a disadvantage, the extremely favorable odds make this a mandatory defense. My hand has sufficient equity against a wide Button opening range and a capped Small Blind calling range to justify the small investment to close the action.

Flop is Ks 4s 3h, a dry board with top pair potential for villain ranges and some low straight draws. My QJo has queen-high with no pair, no straight draw (misses entirely), and only a weak backdoor spade flush draw (Qs with two spades on board) plus backdoor straight draws at best. No strong equity against Flash's wide button opening range (includes many Kx, Ax, broadways, suited connectors that hit or semi-bluff well here) or Pro's wide BB calling range. OOP first to act after calling pre, standard play is to check-fold to likely c-bets from Flash, preserving stack for better spots. Donk betting air OOP vs pre-flop raiser is -EV, invites raises. Folding to one bet still leaves good stack depth (3250 into 750 pot). Prior hand history shows Flash cbets flops aggressively from button.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker on a K-4-3 board with a flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) is on the Button, this board texture (High card King) favors their range significantly, as they hold all the strong King combos (AK, KQ, KJ) and overpairs (AA). Donk betting here would likely only get called by better hands or fold out bluffs I want to keep in. The standard line is to check to the raiser, intending to call a continuation bet (check-call) to keep their bluffing range wide and control the pot size out of position. The Small Blind has already checked.

I am on the button and was the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Four. The flop comes King-Four-Trey with a flush draw. I have hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker. While I could continue betting to represent the King, checking back is a solid pot-control move in a three-way pot. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable, and by checking, I avoid bloating the pot while keeping my range wide. If an opponent bets on the turn, I can re-evaluate based on the card and the bet sizing.

Turn pairs the 3d on a K-4-3 board, which is still dry but now favors made hands like Kx, sets, two-pair (K3 unlikely), or 52 straights. QJo has pure queen-high no pair, no straight, no flush draw (one spade blocker on two-spade board, but irrelevant). Equity realization OOP multiway is poor vs button raise range (Kx, broadways, Ax, suited connectors that connect better) and BB defense range (similar wide). Flop checked through, indicating possible missed draws or weak hands, but turn likely prompts bets from any king or improved hands. No fold equity for thin value or bluff OOP first with air in 3-handed pot; check-fold preserves stack for superior spots. History shows opponents bet aggressively when strong (e.g., Flash cbets buttons, Pro defends wide but folds to heat). Pot odds irrelevant since no strong hand.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a 9 kicker on a board of 4s 3h Ks 3d. The pre-flop raiser (Flash) checked back the flop, which caps their range and suggests they do not have a strong King. The Small Blind has also shown weakness by checking twice. With the board pairing on the turn and a flush draw present, I should take the lead to extract value from lower pocket pairs and draws, and to deny equity to random overcards. A bet of roughly half the pot targets these capped ranges while not over-committing with a weak kicker.

On the turn, the board has paired the three, and I have bottom pair (fours) with an Ace kicker. After everyone checked the flop, Pro's lead for 350 into 750 on the turn is a common sizing for either a King trying to get value or a semi-bluff/steal attempt. Given that I have two pair (4s and 3s) and the price is relatively cheap (350 to win a total pot of 1100), I have enough showdown value to justify a call. Raising doesn't accomplish much as it likely folds out hands I beat and gets called by hands that beat me (like a King or a 3). Elon still has to act behind me, but the call is the most standard pot-control play here.

Facing a 350 bet into 1450 pot after checking turn first OOP. QsJd is pure queen-high bluff-catcher with no pair, no straight draw (board K-4-3-3 rainbow-ish with spades), and only insignificant backdoor spade potential (one spade). Pro's turn bet sizing after multiway flop check-through (dry K-high board) indicates value range: Kx (hit top pair), sets (KK/44/33 unlikely but possible), two-pair (K3 rare), or straight (52s suited connectors in BB wide range). Flash's button call of small bet behind after checking flop suggests improved strength or float turning into value/draw. My equity <20% vs combined ranges (blocked QJ but irrelevant without improvement). Raising air OOP has low fold equity vs two players (Pro deep 17k, Flash 6k), risks multiway bloating OOP. Pot odds ~4:1 but implied odds poor with no hand. Stack 3250 deep enough (30BB+), preserve for better spots hand 67/150. History: opponents bet aggressively with value (e.g., Flash cbets, Pro turns up heat). Standard fold.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) on a board of [4s 3h Ks 3d Qc]. My actual hand strength is Two Pair (Kings and Threes) with a Queen kicker. My opponent (Flash) raised pre-flop from the Button, checked back the flop, and called my bet on the turn. This line typically indicates a capped range consisting of medium pocket pairs, weak Kings, or draws that missed. The river Queen is unlikely to have improved Flash unless he held exactly KQ (unlikely to check flop) or a Queen-high float. Betting here has thin value as worse hands (like 88-JJ) likely fold, and I chop with most other Kings due to the board's Queen kicker. Checking is optimal to induce bluffs from missed spade draws or to get to showdown cheaply against hands I beat or chop with.

On the river, the board is 4s-3h-Ks-3d-Qc. I have two pair, Fours and Threes, with an Ace kicker. Pro, who led the turn for a small sizing, has now checked the river. While my hand is relatively weak in the absolute sense, in this specific sequence (checked flop, small turn bet, river check), it has significant showdown value. I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, but I stand a good chance of winning the pot if I check back and reach showdown against missed draws or smaller pocket pairs. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

4h6h is a very weak hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. I’m first to act with plenty of deep stacks behind (and a short stack on the button who can shove), and the table has shown a lot of preflop aggression and large opens — I’m likely to face a raise or squeeze and will be dominated or have poor equity postflop. Limping UTG is passively giving the initiative to aggressive players and invites squeezes; opening with such a hand is risking a lot of chips with poor playability. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in early position (UTG+1) with 9d 5d. While suited, this hand is too weak to open-raise from this position, especially with several aggressive and larger stacks still to act behind me. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folds to me on the button with Qh8d, a speculative hand with good position. Standard stealing spot in late position against blinds. Pot is 150, blinds 50/100, my stack is 32.5BB which is comfortable for a raise. Raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to steal blinds or play post-flop profitably from position. Q8o has decent playability if called. Opponents deep but history shows frequent folds/steals succeed.

I hold 8s7s in the Small Blind, a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop. Facing a standard 2.5x raise from Elon on the Button, I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 into a pot of 400). Elon is somewhat short-stacked (30BB), making a 3-bet risky as it might induce a shove that forces me to fold my equity. Calling allows me to realize my hand's potential relatively cheaply, even though I will be out of position. I opt to flat call to see a flop.

J6o in the BB facing a raise and a call. Getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to call into 600), but J6o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly multiway. Easily dominated by better Jx hands and tough to extract value when I hit. Fold is the disciplined play.

I missed the flop completely on a board of Ks Jc 2h, which favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Button) significantly more than my Small Blind calling range. I have no pair and no immediate draws, only a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position with virtually no equity, the standard line is to check to the raiser. If he bets, I will fold.

Preflop, raised Qh8d from button as a standard steal, got called by Pro in SB who has a wide defending range including Ax, suited connectors, small-medium pairs. Flop 2h Jc Ks is a good c-bet board for my range: it hits strong hands like KJ, KT, KQ, AJs, pairs, while Pro's range often misses (weak Aces, low suited cards, small pairs below J). Pro checks, indicating possible weakness. I have position for postflop control. Qh8d has Queen-high with backdoor heart flush draw and gutshot straight draw potential (needs T for QJT9 or something, but blockers to Q). C-bet small, 1/3 pot (200 into 600), to get value from weak Ax/pairs that missed, charge any backdoor draws, and high fold equity. Stack 3000 (30BB) comfortable, opponents deep. This balances range, denies equity, and builds pot when ahead.

I hold 8s7s on a board of Ks-Jc-2h. I have no pair and no immediate draws, only a very weak backdoor flush draw. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Elon), who has now c-bet 200 (1/3 pot). Even though the price is good (requiring 20% equity), my hand has virtually zero equity against a King or Jack, and very little playability on future streets out of position. Continuing here would be losing chips. The correct play is to fold.

I am UTG with 7s 8h. In a 5-handed game, 78 offsuit is a weak hand to open with, especially from the earliest position. My stack of 6400 (64 BB) is relatively healthy but I am the second shortest at the table. Given the position and the weakness of the hand, folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop, UTG+1 position after UTG fold. Holding 5d6s, a weak suited connector. Early position with 5 active players, multiway pots likely, and I'm out of position postflop. Stack of 3600 (36BB) is decent but hand lacks strength for opening or calling the raise (to call 100 into 150 pot). Standard play is to fold marginal hands from early position to avoid difficult spots. Opponents have deep stacks, increasing variance. Prioritizing stack preservation and selective aggression.

I am on the Button with J6s. The action has folded to me. We are 5-handed. While button opening ranges are wide, J6s is near the bottom or outside of a standard opening range (top 40-50%). Additionally, Claude in the Small Blind has previously 3-bet me when I opened the Button (Hand #50), suggesting he defends his blinds aggressively. Given the marginal nature of the hand and the potential for aggression from the blinds, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips and avoid a difficult post-flop spot with a weak hand.
Folded to me in the SB with K8o. This is a decent hand to raise with heads up against the BB. K-high has reasonable equity and I should take the initiative. Standard raise to 250-300.

Call (150 more, total 250). 6♦3♦ is a weak but playable suited connector with backdoor flush/straight potential and good postflop playability heads-up. Claude opened to 2.5bb from the SB, so his range is fairly wide — calling is cheap versus my deep stack and gives me good pot odds to see a flop. 3-betting this hand would be a thin bluff with little fold equity (min-raise to 400) and would inflate the pot out of position with a marginal holding. Plan: see the flop and proceed only with meaningful equity (made hands or strong draws); check/fold to heavy aggression if I miss.

Flopped top two pair (Kings and Eights) on Kc 2h 8c. This is a very strong hand. Board has a club flush draw that I need to charge. Standard c-bet around 65-75% pot for value and protection. Pot is 500, betting 350.

Pot is 850 and Claude has bet 350 — I need ~29.2% equity to justify a call (350 / (850+350)). With 6♦3♦ on K♣ 2♥ 8♣ I have no pair and only remote backdoor outs: ~24.2% to hit a pair by the river and ~5.1% to make a backdoor diamond flush, so my total raw chance to improve is right around the breakeven point (~29%). However, most of that equity is to make a weak pair that will often still be behind Claude’s likely continuing range (Kx, pocket pairs, sets, and two-club combos given the two clubs on board). I’m out of position, raising would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity (min-raise to 700), and calling sets me up to face further aggression on turn/river with a marginal hand. Given the board texture, opponent profile and our plan to only proceed with meaningful equity or strong draws, folding and preserving a deep stack for better spots is the best play.

UTG position with 5d7h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity multiway, and no strong blockers. Standard fold from early position to avoid playing speculative hands out of position. Stack of 3600 (36BB) allows waiting for premium spots in a tournament with 80 hands left. Pot is blinds only (150), to call 100 is BB but as first to act, no reason to enter with this trash.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game) with King-Seven offsuit. This hand is statistically weak and easily dominated by many hands in the opponents' calling or raising ranges (like KQ, KJ, KT, A7, pairs). Although I have a deep stack, opening with K7o from this position is loose and likely negative EV in the long run. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand pre-flop.
87 suited on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a premium steal spot with a playable speculative hand. Standard open raise to 250 to either take the blinds or play in position.

Call 250. J♥T♣ is a defendable hand from the small blind versus a standard button open to 250. It only costs me 200 more into a 400 pot (≈2:1 pot odds / ~33% equity required) and JTo has reasonable equity vs a typical button opening range and good post‑flop playability. I don’t want to 3‑bet light to 400 out of position — that’s a thin reraise that can create difficult OOP decisions and invite a squeeze from the big blind (Flash, 6300). With a deep 18k stack I’ll call to see a flop, fold to a large 3‑bet, and then play the flop based on texture: continue aggressively with a pair or strong draw, check/ pot‑control or fold if I miss and face significant pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. I am being offered 5:1 pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 750). While the price is tempting, Q3o is a very weak hand to play out of position in a multi-way pot. A Queen-high hand with a poor kicker is frequently dominated by the Button's raising range or the Small Blind's calling range, leading to difficult post-flop decisions and potential losses. I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Flop A♦ 3♦ 2♠ vs Claude's button open. I hold J♥T♣ and completely missed — no pair and no diamond draw. Claude was the preflop raiser so his range is heavy on A-x, broadways and pocket pairs; he has also shown aggression in past hands. Out of position with virtually no equity, my default is pot control: check and see what he does. If he fires a very small c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈200) I can consider a float to take the turn, but I will fold to medium/large pressure (≥~1/2 pot). If he checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate and can barreI on a favourable card or continue to pot control. Checking keeps the pot small and avoids bluffing into the preflop raiser's range on an Ace‑high board.

I completely missed this A-3-2 board with 87cc. However, this is a great c-bet spot - ace-high boards favor the preflop raiser's range, I have position, and Minni checked showing weakness. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should get folds often enough to be profitable.

Flop A♦ 3♦ 2♠ — I checked to control the pot and Claude fires a very small c‑bet (200 into 800 ≈25%). I hold J♥T♣ and completely missed with no diamond. Calling 200 is the correct, cheap option: I’m getting ~16.7% pot odds (200 to win ~1000), so the price to float and try to take the turn is excellent. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with no pair or robust equity — that just creates difficult OOP decisions and can be punished. Plan: call to see the turn and attempt a float / bluff on a favourable card or continue if I pick up equity; fold to medium/large turn pressure (≈≥1/2 pot) since I currently have no showdown value.

I just picked up top pair (J♥) on the turn (board A♦ 3♦ 2♠ J♦). I’m out of position vs Claude (button) who opened preflop and c‑bet a very small 200 into 800 on the flop — a line consistent with a wide range (A‑x, broadways, small pairs and bluffs). The turn gives me a vulnerable top pair: good enough for value vs worse Jx/Kx/medium pairs, but beat by any A‑x and also risky because there are three diamonds on board (only a two‑diamond hand in his pocket makes a made flush, which is less likely but possible). With a 1000 pot and a healthy stack, I want to control the pot size OOP and avoid getting raised off my hand by an Ace or a set. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his calling range, lets me call a reasonable river bet, and avoids bloating the pot where Claude’s aggression can punish me. Plan: check here, call small/medium river bets for value where appropriate, and fold to large river pressure that polarizes to better hands.

I have complete air (8c7c) on a 3d-2s-Ad-Jd board with three diamonds. I c-bet the flop and got called, indicating Minni likely has something (ace, diamond draw, or a float). The turn Jd completes many flush draws and I have zero equity - no pair, no draws. Double-barreling with no equity into a calling station who's now on a three-diamond board is burning money. Check and give up - I can't profitably bluff here.

I hold JhTc on A♦3♦2♠ J♦ T♠ — that’s two pair (Jacks and Tens). Claude opened from the button and fired a very small c‑bet on the flop (200) and then checked the turn. That line is wide and often contains Ax, broadways, small pairs and some diamond draws; his turn check keeps that range uncapped and not heavily polarized. I’m behind only to A‑x and any two‑diamond combo that makes the flush; I beat most of his missed broadways, single pair Jx/T‑type hands and smaller pocket pairs. Checking to showdown gives up thin value; a small value bet will charge worse pairs and non‑diamond J/T combos while keeping the bet size manageable if I face a raise.

Plan: make a small/medium value bet to extract from worse hands and charge any missed draws. Bet ~40% pot (400 into 1000) — big enough to get calls from Jx/Tx/pocket pairs and to fold pure air, but not so large that I’m committed facing a sizable raise (against a big raise I’ll fold, since that line is likely an A or a flush).

I have complete air (8-high) on this board. Minni called my flop c-bet and now leads river for 400 into 1000. While I'm getting 3.5:1 odds, I can only beat a pure bluff. Minni's line suggests at minimum a pair or completed diamond draw. With zero showdown value, folding is correct despite the odds.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with 5h6h. While 56s is a hand with good implied odds due to its straight and flush potential, opening from the earliest position is risky. We are playing deep-stacked (182 BB effective), which normally favors suited connectors, but I am out of position against four other players. Additionally, the Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (35 BB) and may decide to shove pre-flop, forcing me to fold and lose my opening bet. Given the positional disadvantage and the potential for aggression behind me, folding is the disciplined and standard decision.
9d6d suited from UTG+1 is too weak to open. While it has some straight and flush potential, this hand plays poorly from early position. Standard fold here.

I have J♠6♠ on the button with only the blinds to act. This hand is worth opening because of position, suitedness and the ability to steal. Standard open sizes here have been ~250; with a deep 186bb stack I’ll make a routine button open to apply pressure while keeping the sizing familiar to the table. If I get a large 3‑bet or a shove (especially from Elon with ~35bb) I’ll fold — J6s is poorly positioned against a polarized 3‑bet range — but if I see a call I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting favorable flops and exercising pot control when out of line. Raise to 250 to steal/see a flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 8c. Minni, on the button, has raised to 250. While 98o has some connectivity, playing it out of position against a large-stacked, aggressive opponent like Minni is generally unprofitable. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack (3500 chips) and could easily shove over my call, putting me in a difficult spot. Given the tournament context and my current stack size, folding is the most disciplined and safe play here.

J2o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against Minni's button raising range, which is wide but still favors folding trash from BB facing a raise after two folds. Pot odds are 150 to call into 400 (27.3% needed), but postflop dynamics are bad; better to preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.
32 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. From UTG with 4 players behind, this is an easy fold.

UTG+1 with Qc6s is a marginal, easily-dominated hand. I'm out of position to act against the button, blinds and big stack (Pro) — and the table has shown active preflop raising and postflop aggression. Calling 100 keeps me in a likely multiway pot where Q6o has poor equity and reversing to a raise (min to ~200 or a standard 250+) with this holding is unnecessary risk. With a healthy stack (18750) and ~halfway through the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the right play. Fold and avoid marginal, dominated situations.

I am on the button with 6h 8d. While I have positional advantage, 8-6 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability against a deep-stacked Big Blind and a medium-stacked Small Blind. Since Claude and Minni have already folded, I could attempt a steal, but given the tournament situation and my current stack, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Preflop in small blind with 9d7c, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against any BB defense range. Everyone folded behind, but completing the SB for 50 into 150 pot (needing to call 50 more) offers poor implied odds and postflop prospects. My stack is 34.5BB effective against BB's deep stack, but no value in playing this speculative hand out of position. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 78 hands left.

UTG with A♠J♠ in a 5-handed game is a clear open-raise spot — the hand plays very well postflop and has strong equity against callers. The table’s standard preflop sizing has been ~2.5x BB (250), so I’ll open to that size to build the pot, take fold equity, and avoid giving a cheap multiway flop. I want to isolate the shorter stack on the button and keep the pot playable versus the deeper blinds; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on the opponent and sizing (call or 4-bet as appropriate).

I am holding King-Five offsuit in UTG+1. Minni, who is the chip leader, has opened from UTG to 2.5x. K5o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by Minni's opening range (which typically includes better Kings like AK, KQ, KJ, and KT). Calling out of position against the chip leader with such a weak hand is a losing play in the long run. My stack size is healthy (62.5 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks or attempt a high-variance 3-bet bluff here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Qd2d is a marginal suited hand at best, with poor high card strength and limited straight potential. Facing a UTG raise from Minni, who has a strong range from early position, my equity is low even in position on the button. Calling 250 into a 400 pot offers insufficient odds for this speculative hand, which plays poorly postflop against likely strong holdings. Folding preserves my 34.5BB stack for better opportunities in this tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 7s (an offsuit connector). Minni raised to 2.5bb from UTG. Against a UTG opening range, 78o is too weak to call, especially out of position. Even though the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my equity realization will be poor due to position and the potential for the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall. The disciplined play is to fold.

Pocket sixes in the BB facing a UTG open to 250. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds to call 150 more. With deep stacks (Minni has 18500, I have 13150), set mining is profitable. 66 plays well as a set-mine hand rather than 3-betting. Easy call.

Missed my set with 66 on 2c7cTc board. Three clubs out there means a flush is possible. I have a small underpair with no club for a draw. Checking to see what Minni does is the right play - I have some showdown value but can't bet for value here and don't want to bluff into a wet board where they likely c-bet.

I raised UTG to 250 preflop with A♠J♠ and we see a single caller (Claude). Flop 2♣-7♣-T♣ is a bit hostile for our spades — there are three clubs on board so anyone holding two clubs has an immediate flush, and single-club hands have decent equity/backswing. That said, as the preflop aggressor I should c-bet often here: I have two overcards (A and J) with decent showdown value against missed parts of Claude’s calling range and plenty of hands to fold out (weak broadways, small pairs, floats). I don’t want to bloat the pot given the club texture and deep stacks, so I choose a modest sizing to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. A bet of ~1/3 pot (~200) hits that balance — it folds many hands, gets value from worse unpaired hands, and lets me fold to heavy aggression. Plan: bet 200; if Claude raises big I’ll give up (likely he has a Ten or two clubs), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously unless I improve.

I have 66 with no club on a monotone club board (2c7cTc). My underpair has minimal equity here - Minni could easily have a flush, flush draw, or top pair. With only 2 outs to a set that might not even be good if a 4th club comes, the 200 to call isn't worth it despite the decent pot odds. Clean fold.

I am sitting UTG in a 5-handed game with 5c 7c. While this is a suited connector (one-gapper), it is generally too weak to open from early position, especially with an aggressive big stack like Minni in the blinds and a short stack like Elon behind me who might shove. My goal is to maximize my stack over the 150 hands, and preserving chips with marginal hands in poor position is key. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5d7h is a very weak offsuit hand with a gap, unsuitable for playing from UTG+1 early position. Early position requires a tight range to avoid difficult postflop situations out of position. No pot odds justify limping, and raising would be a bluff with no equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Opponents have deep stacks, but hand selection is key.

I am on the Button with QJo, which is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds when folded to. My opponents in the blinds are deep-stacked, but playing in position gives me a significant advantage. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if called.

K8s in the SB facing a button raise of 250. Getting 2:1 odds to call 200 more. K8s has decent playability - can flop top pair, straights, or backdoor flush draws. Button raises are typically wide, so my hand has reasonable equity. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

Fold. I’m facing a button open to 250 with the small blind calling, making this a 3‑way pot; I still need to call 150 into a 600 pot (150/(600+150) ≈ 20% equity required). 4♣7♦ offsuit has very poor multiway equity and poor post‑flop playability out of position to both opponents. With deep stacks I can wait for a much better spot — 3‑betting here is risky and thinly disguised as a bluff against two players, and calling hands like this will be dominated too often. Preserve the stack and fold.

Missed the flop completely with K8o on A42 two-heart board. No pair, no draw. Ace-high boards favor the preflop raiser. Standard check and likely fold to a bet - I have no reason to put money in with king-high and no draw.

I hold a Jack-high flush draw (Jh on a 4h As 2h board). The Ace connects fairly well with the Small Blind's flat-calling range. Betting here has little fold equity against an Ace, and I risk getting check-raised, which would put me in a difficult spot with a drawing hand. Checking back is the superior option to control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and disguise the strength of my hand. I will see the turn card and re-evaluate.

Pro checked back on the flop which could indicate weakness or pot control with a medium hand. Turn 9c doesn't help me - still have K-high with no draw. No reason to bluff here as Pro could easily be trapping with an ace or have a pocket pair. I'll check and reevaluate on the river. If we both check down, K-high might have some showdown value.

I hold a flush draw (Jh) on a board of 4h As 2h 9c. After checking back the flop, checking the turn is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity 100% of the time by seeing a free river card. Betting risks a check-raise which would force me to fold my draw, and I likely have little fold equity against an Ace or a pair of nines. I will take the free card and re-evaluate on the river.

River Js completes no obvious draws. Pro checked flop and turn, showing weakness or pot control. With K-high I have almost no showdown value - I lose to any pair, any ace, any jack. Bluffing here is risky since Pro could be trapping with a weak ace or have connected with the jack. Given Pro's passive line, they might just be checking down with a small pair or weak ace. Checking is the safest option - if Pro has complete air, K-high might win at showdown.

I arrived at the river with a pair of Jacks (Second Pair) after the board ran out 4h As 2h 9c Js. The action has been checked down on the flop and turn. My hand has showdown value, beating missed hearts, smaller pairs (9x, 4x, 2x, pocket pairs < JJ), and high card hands. However, it loses to any Ace, which is well within the Small Blind's flat-calling range. Betting here is thin; worse hands like a 9 or 4 are unlikely to call given the Ace and Jack on board, and better hands (Ax) will never fold. Therefore, the optimal strategy is to check back and take the showdown.

UTG in a 5-handed game with K4s, a speculative suited hand but with a weak kicker and low second card. Hand strength is marginal for opening from early position, where ranges should be tighter to avoid difficult post-flop spots OOP. Opponents show aggression in history (frequent raises from various positions), increasing risk of 3-bets or multiway pots. Stacks are deep enough (34BB effective), but folding preserves stack for better spots later in the tournament to maximize long-term EV. No value in limping UTG as it plays poorly multiway. Raising risks variance with a non-premium hand. Clear fold.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively early position in a 5-handed game) with Ten-Seven suited (Ts7s). This is a speculative hand that is generally considered too weak to open-raise from early position due to the likelihood of being dominated or facing 3-bets from the button or blinds. With a healthy stack of 186 BBs, I do not need to take marginal spots. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

On the button with J5 suited diamonds. Two players folded, only blinds remain. This is a good spot to steal with a suited broadway card. J5s has playability with flush potential. Standard button raise to 2.5x.

I’m in the small blind with A♥7♦ facing a button open to 250.
