Calling (200 more, total 250) is the best line: it’s a cheap defend with an ace blocker and reasonable equity versus a BTN opening range. 3-betting out of position with a weak kicker bloats the pot unnecessarily and the BB (Flash) can still come along and complicate isolation. Plan: see the flop and play for pot control — continue aggressively only with an ace, two pair, strong draw or good board texture; fold to heavy preflop 3-bets or blatant postflop overbets that put me to a tough decision.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 5s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call the remaining 150 chips, 25o performs very poorly multi-way and I will be out of position throughout the hand. Given the stack sizes and the tournament progress, it is better to fold and wait for a more playable hand than to commit chips to a pot where I have very little equity.

Heads-up vs Claude on a Q♦-9♠-5♣ flop. I hold A♥7♦ — an ace-high with an ace blocker and little showdown value but enough to continue as per my preflop plan to ‘continue with an ace’. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot, but a small c-bet takes the initiative, folds out lots of unpaired junk and pocket pairs that miss, and gets value from worse broadway/ace-x hands. I’ll bet about 1/3 pot to control the size (200). If Claude raises big I’ll fold (per my plan to avoid tough OOP decisions); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed only with real improvement or clear odds.

I flopped bottom pair with Jd5d on Qd9s5c. Minni's small donk bet of 200 into 600 is suspicious - could be a weak queen, a draw, or a probe. I have decent equity with my pair plus backdoor flush draw in diamonds. Getting 4:1 odds to call makes this an easy continue. Will reassess on turn.

Turn (Q♦-9♠-5♣-4♠): the 4♠ changes very little. Claude called my flop c-bet, so his range contains Qx, 9x, 5x, small-to-medium pocket pairs and some floats — many of which still beat my A♥7♦ or will call a turn bet. I have no pair and no spade backdoor, so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. Per my preflop/ flop plan I only barrel further with real improvement or clear equity. Checking for pot control is the safest +EV line: it lets me realize my equity, induces bluffs from floats, and preserves chips against strong hands. If Claude overbets or jams I’ll fold; I can call smaller bets on the turn to see the river. Action: check.

Turn brought 4s, creating a spade flush draw on board. Minni's check after donk-betting flop is interesting - likely weakness or a draw. My bottom pair with jack kicker is marginal. Betting here doesn't accomplish much - better hands won't fold, and worse hands that call have draws or better pairs. Pot control is correct with this showdown value hand.

I have A♥7♦ — only ace-high on Q♦‑9♠‑5♣‑4♠‑3♦. Claude called my small flop bet and checked the turn, so his range is heavy on made hands that beat me (Qx, 9x, 5x, small/medium pairs) plus some floats. Betting the river has very thin value (only worse A‑x or rare bluffs) and will mainly get called by better hands or induce a raise I don’t want to face out of position. With deep stacks and little fold equity, pot control and showing down is the +EV line. I’ll check and go to showdown; if Claude now leads a large overbet I’ll fold, and I can call only small river bets that look like bluffs.

River 3d completes no obvious draws. I have bottom pair with jack kicker on Qd9s5c4s3d. Minni checked again showing weakness. My hand has showdown value but is too weak to bet for value - only better hands call. Check back and see if my pair of fives is good.

I am in UTG position with 3d 5h. This is a very weak hand (low unsuited one-gapper) and is not suitable for opening from early position in a 5-handed game. My stack is healthy, and there is no reason to get involved with trash cards from a disadvantageous position. The standard and correct play is to fold.
T8o is a weak hand in UTG+1 position with 4 players behind me. Not strong enough to open from early position - standard fold.

Button with Kc3h — marginal hand but excellent position. With UTG/UTG+1 folded I should open to steal the blinds and take advantage of my positional advantage rather than limp. I size to 250 (≈2.5x BB): big enough to deny a cheap flop and create fold equity against Flash (SB, ~61BB) while keeping the pot controllable against Elon (BB, ~33BB). If Flash shows heavy 3-bet aggression I’ll fold — he can have a strong/isolating range. If Elon (shorter stack) jams I’ll re-evaluate based on his tendencies and pot odds, but the primary plan is to open and play postflop in position, c-betting favorable boards and folding to strong resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 4c, a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button. While the price to call is only 200 more chips into a 400 pot, 24o is one of the worst starting hands and plays poorly out of position. With Elon still to act in the Big Blind and the risk of being dominated or outplayed post-flop, folding is the most disciplined and correct move to preserve my stack.

8c4d is a complete trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards with poor playability postflop. In BB facing a button raise to 250 (150 to call into 400 pot), the pot odds are roughly 2.7:1, requiring ~27% equity, but 84o has very low equity vs. a button opening range (often 40-50% hands). Even if Minni is wide, this hand rarely improves enough to win at showdown and is hard to play profitably OOP. With 33BB stack, better to preserve chips for stronger hands rather than defend marginal spots. Standard fold.

Pocket 9s in UTG - solid opening hand. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot while maintaining position control. This hand plays well postflop if we hit a set and can fold to significant pressure on overcards.

8♥4♣ is a junk hand — dominated by almost every UTG opening range and has very poor post‑flop playability. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38% equity; 8‑4o is nowhere near that versus an UTG raise. There are still multiple players to act (button, SB, BB), so the chance of getting isolated is low and a light 3‑bet would be thin and unnecessary. Preserve a healthy stack in early/mid tournament and fold.

I am on the button with 8s6d, which is a weak speculative hand. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. Given Claude's stack size and position, his range is likely strong. Calling or raising with 8-high offsuit would be -EV, especially since I'd still have the blinds to act behind me. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play in this spot.

AKo is a premium hand, especially with a short stack of 33BB. Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 into a 400 pot, calling OOP risks multiway pots and positional disadvantage. Folding is too weak. A 3-bet is standard to isolate, apply pressure, and leverage fold equity. Sizing to 750 (3x the raise) is appropriate for pot control and stack commitment. BB (Pro) has a large stack but may fold wide. History shows Claude aggressive but AK dominates much of his calling range like AQ, KQ, suited broadways.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 8d, a very weak offsuit hand. The action involves a UTG raise to 250 followed by a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 750. Facing significant strength from two players and requiring 650 chips to call into a pot of 1100, my pot odds are roughly 37%, which is far worse than my equity against their ranges. This is a trivial fold.

Elon 3-bet to 750 from SB with only 2600 total stack (1850 behind). Getting 2.2:1 pot odds to call 500. Pocket 9s plays well here - I'm set mining with good implied odds since Elon is short and committed. Folding 99 to this sizing is too tight, and 4-betting just gets us into a flip or dominated. Flat call to see a flop.

Heads-up on the flop with AKo after my 3-bet which Claude called. The board Tc-7h-7s is dry and paired, favoring my 3-bet range of overpairs (QQ+, AK, AQ) which often hold ace-high or better here. Claude's defending range vs 3-bet includes JJ+, AK, some suited broadways, but he rarely has 77/TT. Ace-high has solid showdown value and blocks AA/AK. Equity is good vs his range (~45-55% assuming typical calls). As first to act OOP with 26BB effective stack (~1.6 pot-sized bets left), a c-bet maintains aggression, denies equity to his floats/draws (spade draw, gutshots like 89/J8), and pot controls. Sizing 2/3 pot (1050-1100) balances value from overpairs with bluffs (AK is semi-bluff with fold equity). Checking risks giving free cards to draws and OOP passivity. Shoving too polarized/overplays; smaller bet best for stack preservation/maximization.

Board: T77. I have 99 - an overpair to the 7s but under the T. Elon has only 1500 behind after this 1100 bet, essentially committing himself. Pot is 2700, calling 1100 gives me ~2.5:1. His 3-bet range from SB is typically JJ+, AK, AQs. On this paired board, I beat AK/AQ bluffs and lose to overpairs JJ+/TT. Given his short stack and pot commitment, calling to evaluate turn is reasonable - folding 99 here is too tight given pot odds and his range includes air/overcards.

On the turn, the board is Tc-7h-7s-3h, pairing the sevens with a completed heart flush draw (two hearts) and a low card. I hold AhKd, ace-high with the nut heart blocker, no pair or straight draw. Effective stack is 1500 into a 3800 pot (~40% pot shove). As the preflop 3-bettor and flop c-bettor OOP, my range is perceived as QQ+, AK/AQ, some bluffs. Claude's calling range preflop and flop bet includes QQ-JJ (overpairs), AQ+/AJs, sets (unlikely on paired board), pocket pairs below 77, or draws. The turn doesn't hit his overpairs hard but completes heart draws (which I block with Ah) and adds gutshots. Checking OOP risks giving free cards to draws or check-raises, and with short stack, pot control is less relevant. Shoving applies max pressure, gets value from AJs-QJs (worse ace-highs/draws), AQ/AJs, and overpairs JJ-66 that might fold but shouldn't. He calls ~25% of the time profitably for us due to blockers and range advantage; equity realization ~35-45% vs his range (GTO sims support thin shove here). History shows Claude calls stations but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hands 9,10,20). Aggression maximizes EV in tournament with 73/150 hands left.

Elon is all-in (stack shows 0) with this 1500 turn bet. Pot is 5300, calling 1500 gives me ~4.5:1 pot odds. I only need ~18% equity to call profitably. His 3-bet range is JJ+/AK/AQ - I lose to overpairs but beat AK/AQ. Given the excellent pot odds, this is a mandatory call even against a strong range.

I am dealt 5s 2s Under the Gun in a 4-handed game. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak in terms of high-card value and connectivity. Opening from UTG with such a bottom-tier hand is generally unprofitable, especially as the shortest stack at the table (61 BBs). I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

With 4 players remaining, King-Nine offsuit on the button is a reasonable hand to open-raise to steal the blinds. The UTG player folded. I have a deep stack (185BB) and position. Raising to 2.5x (250) puts pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind, who will likely fold weaker hands. If called, I can play post-flop in position. Folding is too passive for 4-handed play on the button.
Q7o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with poor playability and no real equity advantage. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I'm in the big blind with Kc9c facing a button open to 250. K9s is a defendable hand here — good playability (top-pair potential, backdoor club/straight equity) and useful blockers. Calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~2.7:1 immediate pot odds and, with ~18.5k effective stacks, plenty of implied odds to continue when I hit. A 3-bet bluff is possible, but out of position versus the button with a marginal hand invites tough decisions and likely cold-calls or 4-bets from Pro. I’ll call to 250, keep the pot manageable, and proceed postflop: continue aggressively if I hit a K, a club draw, or strong equity; fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss.

Check. With Kc9c I missed the flop — I only have king-high and a very remote backdoor club runner-runner. The flop T♣ 7♠ 7♥ strongly favors the preflop raiser’s c-bet range (tens, pocket pairs, value hands), and I’m out of position, so leading as a bluff is expensive and often wrong. I’ll keep the pot small, realize equity, and avoid tough OOP bluffs: check and fold to heavy raise/aggression, but call small-to-medium continuation bets to see a turn since I have two overcards and a tiny backdoor club possibility. If I pair a K or hit a club on the turn I’ll consider betting/raising; otherwise continue pot-control play.

I am in position on the Button with King-high on a paired board (Tc 7s 7h). The pot is small (550). While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, this board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range moderately well (Tens, Sevens, pocket pairs). My hand has some showdown value but cannot comfortably call a check-raise, nor does it get value from worse hands. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or hit a pair. There is little benefit to betting as I likely only fold out hands I am already beating (worse high cards) and get called by better.

I have Kc9c on T♣ 7♠ 7♥ 2♣. The turn gave me a four‑to‑a‑club flush (9 club outs ≈19.6% on the river) plus 3 kings and 3 nines as additional improvement outs (15 unique outs ≈33% to improve to at least a pair/flush, though paired 9s are often weak). The preflop raiser (Pro) checked both flop and turn, which often signals weakness or a slow‑play — many missed hands and medium pairs will check back. With the pot at 550 and my stack large, I prefer to apply pressure now with a constructive half‑pot bet to pick up the pot when he’s missed and to charge any draws; if he folds I win the pot outright, if he calls I still have ~20% to make the flush and other river outs. If he raises, I will respect that strength and fold (I’m OOP with a drawy hand). Plan for river: lead/value bet if a club or a clean K hits, check back and re‑evaluate if I miss. Betting 300 buys immediate fold equity while keeping the commitment small relative to stacks.

I hold King-Nine offsuit on a board of [Tc 7s 7h 2c]. After checking back the flop, the Big Blind leads into me for 300 (approx. 55% pot) on the turn. This lead often indicates a made hand like a Ten, a Seven, or a pocket pair trying to extract value or protect against overcards after I showed weakness on the flop. While there is a chance the opponent is bluffing with a draw (e.g., clubs or straight draws), my King-high has very little equity against their value range and cannot comfortably call down or raise as a bluff. Conserving chips for a better spot is prudent here.

I am in UTG (first to act in 4-handed play) with King-Queen suited (Qh Kh), a premium broadway hand. In a short-handed game, this is a mandatory open-raise for value. My stack is deep (182bb), allowing me to play comfortably post-flop. A standard opening size of 2.5bb (250 chips) is consistent with my previous actions and balances my range, putting pressure on the blinds while building a pot with a strong holding.

K5o on the button facing an UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a UTG opening range. K5o is often dominated by better Kx hands and struggles to realize equity. While I have position, the hand is too weak to call or 3-bet profitably against a UTG range. Folding is the disciplined play.

Pro opened UTG to 250 — a standard, fairly strong open. QTs suited is too good to fold from the small blind against a single raiser, but I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a tiny raise that won’t fold many worse hands. With deep stacks this hand plays very well postflop (top pair, tens, spade backdoors, straight draws), so I’ll defend by calling the 200. Plan: see a flop and play based on texture — be aggressive when I hit (c‑bet/top pair/draws) and control pot size / fold to heavy aggression when I miss. If I face a large 3‑bet preflop I’ll give up (too much dead money OOP); if it’s a small isolation 3‑bet I can consider calling depending on size.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand. Pro opened from UTG and Minni called from the small blind. As the shortest stack at the table with 60 big blinds, I want to 3-bet for value and to reduce the field. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing that builds a pot for my hand while putting pressure on the initial raiser and the caller.

I hold KhQh, a premium suited broadway hand, in position against the 3-bettor (Flash). Flash started the hand with ~51bb and has 3-bet to 1000 (20% of their stack), creating a low SPR scenario post-flop. The price to call is 750 into a pot of 1500, offering 33% pot odds. KQs has sufficient equity against a reasonable 3-bet range (TT+, AJ+, KQ) to continue. 4-betting would likely only get action from better hands or force a fold from worse, whereas calling keeps Flash's range wider and utilizes my position. Folding is incorrect given the hand strength and odds.

I called the SB initially to see a flop vs a single UTG open (consistent with my plan). Flash’s sizable 3‑bet to 1,000 and Pro’s call turns this into a multiway pot with me out of position. QsTs is fine vs one raiser heads‑up, but multiway OOP against a 3‑bettor greatly reduces playability and fold equity. Pot math: I must call 750 to win ~3,000 (≈25% break‑even equity), and I’m often dominated or in uncomfortable spots facing a short‑stack shove or heavy action. With deep stacks I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a cleaner, positionally favorable spot. Therefore I’ll fold here.

I 3-bet pre-flop and the flop came Queen-high with a pair of deuces. This board favors my 3-betting range which includes AA, KK, QQ, and AQ. Although I missed with AK, I have a significant range advantage over Pro, who capped their range by calling the 3-bet pre-flop. A small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 of the pot is effective here to fold out Pro's weaker holdings (like AJ, ATs, or potentially small pocket pairs) while maintaining the initiative. If I'm called, I still have two overcards as outs and can re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and Pro's action.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (KhQh) on a paired board of 2h-Qc-2s. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop from the Big Blind with a relatively short stack, has continued with a small bet of 700 into a pot of 2250 (roughly 1/3 pot). My hand is very strong in this spot, beating pairs like JJ, TT, and missed high cards like AK, while only losing to AA, KK, AQ, and QQ. Raising here would likely fold out the hands I beat (bluffs or underpairs) and isolate me against the hands that crush me. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or valuing worse hands on later streets. The price is excellent, and I am in position.

The Ten of Diamonds on the turn does not improve my Ace-King. My flop continuation bet was called, which suggests Pro likely has a piece of this board, most likely a Queen or a pocket pair. Given my remaining stack of 4,400 and a pot of 3,650, the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) is very low (~1.2). If I bet now, I am essentially committing my entire stack. Since I am out of position and the board has become more coordinated for Pro's calling range (hands like JJ or JTs now have more equity), checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see Pro's action; if they check behind, I might have the best hand or get a free river. If they bet, I will have to decide whether to fold or make a high-variance shove based on the bet sizing.

The opponent (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop, c-bet the flop, and has now checked the turn. The board is [2h, Qc, 2s, Td]. I hold [Qh, Kh] for top pair, top kicker. Flash's check on the turn creates a 'pot control' spot. The pot is 3650 and Flash has 4400 behind (SPR ~1.2). If I bet, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (AA, KK, AQ, TT) or forced to fold out hands I beat (AK, JJ). By checking back, I avoid being check-raised, control the pot size, and induce bluffs on the river from missed high cards like AK. The Ten on the turn improves TT to a full house and gives KJ a straight, so caution is warranted. Checking allows me to realize equity and re-evaluate on the river.

The river 3s is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. My Ace-King has failed to improve to a pair. After Pro called my continuation bet on the flop and then checked back the turn, their range likely consists of marginal showdown hands like JJ, TT, 99, 88, or perhaps a weak Queen. While a bluff might occasionally fold out those pocket pairs, it is risky given that Pro has already invested significantly in the pot. By checking, I am hoping to reach a cheap showdown where I might occasionally win against other missed overcards (like AJ or suited connectors) or split against another AK. Given my stack size, preserving my remaining 4,400 chips is more important than a high-risk bluff in this spot.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (KhQh) on a board of 2h-Qc-2s-Td-3s. The opponent (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, but then checked the turn and checked the river. This line often indicates a hand like JJ, AK, or AJ that is exercising pot control or giving up, though a slow-played monster (AA, KK, TT) is possible but less likely given the double check. If I bet, worse hands like JJ or AK will likely fold, while better hands like AA, KK, AQ, or TT will call or raise. There is very thin value in betting. Checking back allows me to win the pot against weaker hands without exposing myself to a check-raise or losing value to a trap. It is the safest and most EV-neutral play to realize my equity.
As7s is a playable hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Suited aces have good equity with nut flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

Holding A♣A♦ on the button is the single best preflop hand — I want to build the pot and isolate the UTG opener while still keeping worse hands (Ax, broadways, medium pairs) and the big blind’s calling range in. Claude opened to 250 into a 50/100 structure; pot is ~400 and to-call is 250. A standard 3‑bet sizing that achieves those goals and preserves post‑flop maneuverability is to about 1.0–1.5k. I’ll raise to 1200 total: big enough to discourage light SB squeezes and fold out some speculative hands, but small enough that Pro (BB) and Claude will call with hands worse than mine and produce value. Plan postflop is to c‑bet most boards for value and protection, and to re-evaluate aggressively only if I face a big 4‑bet or heavy action — I have 18,700 behind so I can still exert pressure later.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 2h, an extremely weak hand. There has been an UTG raise to 250 from Claude and a significant 3-bet to 1,200 from Minni on the Button. Given my relatively short stack of 4,350, committing 1,150 chips (more than 25% of my stack) out of position with a marginal suited hand against a strong 3-betting range would be mathematically incorrect and highly risky. Folding is the only viable option.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o) in the Big Blind. The action involves an UTG open from Claude to 250 and a significant 3-bet from Minni on the Button to 1200. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly against both ranges, especially out of position. I am facing a large bet and have no equity or blocker reasons to continue. This is a standard fold.

Minni 3-bet to 1200 from the button. As7s is a suited ace with nut flush potential. Getting about 1.7:1 pot odds (950 to win 1600). Against a button 3-bet range, I have reasonable equity with implied odds if I hit my flush. However, I'm OOP and As7s isn't strong enough to 4-bet. The hand plays okay to a call given stack depths and flush potential, but folding is also reasonable. I'll call and look to hit a flush or strong ace high flop.

Completely whiffed this flop - I have ace high with no draw (my spades don't connect with the diamond board). Against Minni's 3-bet range, they likely have better hands that connect with Qd6dQc. No point betting into the 3-bettor with air. Check and evaluate - may need to give up if faced with aggression.

Preflop I 3‑bet to 1,200 and Claude just called — heads‑up pot is 2,550. Flop Q♦ 6♦ Q♣ is a paired queen board with two diamonds. Holding A♣ A♦ I have a very strong overpair and the Ad is a useful blocker to nut‑diamond combos. This is a spot to continuation‑bet for value and protection: I want to charge diamond draws and worse broadways/mid pairs that will call, while making it expensive for hands to see free cards. I size to ~60% pot (1,500) — big enough to deny equity to many draws and get value from hands like A‑x, KQ, JJ/TT, and some suited connectors, but not so big that I only get called by the tiny fraction of Claude’s range that already has trips/sets. If he raises hard I’ll re‑evaluate (those raises will usually indicate trips/sets/quads) but with these stacks I’m willing to continue with AA against his 3‑bet‑call range.

I have ace-high with no draw on Qd6dQc. Minni 3-bet preflop and is now betting 1500 into a ~2400 pot. I have zero equity here - no queen, no diamond draw (my spades are useless), just ace high. Against a 3-bettor's range that's betting this board, I'm drawing nearly dead. Easy fold.

UTG with 3♦2♠ in a 4-handed game is effectively the worst opening hand — very low equity, poorly coordinated and offsuit. I’m out of position to act versus the Button, SB and BB; calling 100 is just limping OOP with a junk hand and inviting multiway action, while raising to 200 will often get me isolated or 3‑bet by Pro (SB) or Claude (BB) who both have deep stacks and can punish a bluff. With a 20k stack and plenty of hands left, there’s no reason to gamble chips with this holding. Fold and wait for a better spot to attack.

I am on the button with 8d 6s. This is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, the blinds (Pro and Claude) have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to play back. With about 43 big blinds, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with a hand that has poor post-flop playability and low showdown value. Folding is the most prudent play here.

Current hand is 81/150. I am in the Small Blind with J5o. The action has folded to me. My stack is 20050. The Big Blind (Claude) has 15400. J5o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to complete, realizing equity with this hand OOP is difficult and likely negative EV. Raising is also unattractive as a pure bluff with no equity backup against a deep-stacked opponent. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips.

I am dealt 6c 2h in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em and lacks any significant potential for straights, flushes, or high-pair value. Given my relatively short stack of 4350 chips compared to the table leaders, I should play conservatively and wait for better starting hands to maximize my survival and growth potential. There is no strategic benefit to opening or calling with such a weak hand from this position.

I am on the Button with 7s8s, a strong suited connector. The UTG player has folded. This is a classic spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has excellent playability and implied odds. Folding is too passive for this position and hand strength. Limping is weak. I will raise to 2.5bb (250), which is a standard sizing for this table.
2c 4s is one of the weakest hands in poker. Out of position against a button raise, this hand has very poor playability and almost no equity. Easy fold.

Facing a button open to 250 while I'm in the big blind with Jc2d. This is a very weak, easily-dominated offsuit Jx with poor playability OOP. I already have 100 in the pot; calling 150 for a pot of ~400 gives ~27% pot odds, but even if I see a flop I'll be out of position and likely face C-bets and tough decisions versus the button's opening range. A 3-bet bluff to the minimum (400) isn't attractive — I have no meaningful blockers and don't want to bloat the pot with such a poor holding. With deep stacks I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot in position. Fold and take the 100 BB posted as a small loss.

I am in UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with Queen-Five offsuit (Qh 5c). This is a statistically weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially when out of position against three potential opponents. Despite my deep stack (202 BB), opening with Q5o is loose and unprofitable in the long run. The standard and correct play is to fold.

ATo on the button is a strong opening hand. Pro folded UTG, leaving only the blinds. This is a standard button raise spot - I have position and a solid ace-high hand. Raising to 250 (2.5x) is standard.

I’m in the small blind with 9♥8♣ (offsuit) facing a button open to 250. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call. 9‑8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position — especially versus the button’s opening range and with the big blind (shorter stack) still to act. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~25% equity to break even, and my realized equity will be lower in multiway / OOP spots. 3‑betting to 400 to isolate would bloat a pot out of position with a hand that doesn’t fare well postflop. With a healthy stack there’s no reason to gamble here; fold and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) in the Big Blind, which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Claude has opened for 250 from the Button, a position from which players often raise with a wide range of hands. With a stack of 4250 (roughly 42.5 BB), I need to build the pot immediately to maximize value and protect my hand. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here. I will raise to 850, which is a standard sizing from the blinds, to entice a call or a 4-bet shove from Claude's stronger range while putting pressure on his speculative holdings.

Flash 3-bet to 850 from BB with only 3500 total stack (35BB). ATo is a solid hand that plays well in position. Calling 600 more keeps the pot manageable and I have position. Flash has only 2650 behind after this bet, so SPR will be very low postflop. I can call and re-evaluate on the flop - if I hit, I can easily get stacks in. 4-betting here commits me with a hand that's often flipping or dominated vs a tight 3-bet range.

I have an overpair (Kings) on a relatively safe board of 4d-Jd-7h. The current pot is 1750 and my remaining stack is 3500, giving a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of 2.0. This is a value-betting situation where I want to charge Claude for any diamond draws or pocket pairs/Jack-x hands he might be holding. A bet of 875 (exactly half the pot) is ideal as it builds the pot and sets up a pot-sized shove on the turn if he calls, making the remaining play mathematically straightforward.

Missed the flop completely with ATo - just A-high on 4d Jd 7h. Flash 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting 875 into 1750. With only ~1750 behind after this bet, Flash is essentially pot-committed. I have backdoor diamonds and a gutshot to broadway, but these are very weak draws. Against a 3-bet range this flop hits well (JJ, QQ, KK, AA, AJ, KJ). Continuing with just A-high against a committed opponent is burning chips.
T5o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, and UTG requires a tight opening range. No reason to open this trash.

Call 100. 2♦5♦ is a weak hand but it’s suited and has some wheel/flush potential — nothing to raise with out of position versus two players who can easily call or shove. I’m on the button so calling 100 is a small investment to see a flop with positional advantage against the small blind (short stack) and the big blind. A min-raise here would commit more chips and often get called or 3-bet by better hands; calling keeps the pot small, preserves stack depth for future hands, and lets me continue only with a diamond, a pair, or a strong draw. If I face significant preflop aggression (a reraise/shove) I’ll fold and conserve chips.

I am in the small blind with 3h 2s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although the button limped and I only have to call 50 more chips to see a flop (offering 5:1 pot odds), the hand has almost no post-flop equity and I would be forced to play out of position against much larger stacks. Preserving my 52 BB stack for better spots is the most prudent strategy here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). The player on the Button (Minni) has limped for 100, and the Small Blind folded. I have already posted the 100 big blind, so there is no additional cost to see the flop. My hand is not strong enough to raise for value or isolation out of position. Therefore, checking is the standard and most prudent play to realize my equity for free.

I hold [Tc 8h] on a [8c Ac 6c] board. I have middle pair (Eights) and a flush draw (Ten-high flush draw, specifically to the 4th nut flush since the Ace is on the board). The pot is small (250) and single-raised/limped. Being out of position against the button (Minni) who limped, my hand has sufficient showdown value and draw equity to continue, but not enough strength to lead out (donk bet) on this texture. Leading might fold out worse hands that I beat or get called/raised by Ax or better flush draws. Checking allows me to pot control and evaluate the opponent's action. If Minni bets, I have a comfortable check-call given my pair and flush draw.

Check. I have 2♦5♦ with no clubs and no pair on a monotone A♣8♣6♣ flop — essentially air. The opponent in the BB checked, which could be missed cards, Ace-x, or a made two‑club flush. Given their tendency to defend wide and the deep stacks, a c‑bet here will often be called by Ace-x, clubs, or small pairs and can be shoved/raised by a made flush — I have almost no fold equity and very little equity myself. Pot is small (250) relative to stacks, so I should control the pot and see a free turn. Plan: check and fold to meaningful pressure unless the turn gives me real improvement (pair of 2 or 5, runner-runner straight/runner club situations). If Pro checks back I can take another look on the turn or bluff a safe card; if they lead out I’ll fold unless the sizing gives correct pot odds for a speculative call.

On the turn, the board is [8c Ac 6c 4h]. The 4h is a relatively blank card unless the opponent holds 57 for a straight or two pair like A4/84/64. The opponent checked back the flop, which often indicates a weak hand, a draw, or a pot-controlling marginal hand like a weak Ace. I hold middle pair (Eights) and a Ten-high flush draw. While I have equity, my hand is not strong enough to value bet confidently across three streets, and betting might fold out weaker hands (bluffs/air) that I currently beat while isolating myself against better hands (Ax). Checking allows me to control the pot size out of position and potentially induce bluffs or get to a cheap showdown. If the opponent bets, I have an easy call given my pair and flush draw outs.

Check. I have 2♦5♦ on A♣8♣6♣4♥ — no pair, no club backdoor and essentially no showdown value or fold equity against a caller-heavy big blind. The pot is tiny (250) relative to our 19,800 stack and the opponent’s tendencies to just check/call, so there's no reason to build the pot or bluff into a likely caller on the turn. I’ll check and see a free river; if Pro bets a meaningful amount on the turn I’ll fold (only consider calling a very tiny probe for pot odds). If we get to the river I’ll re-evaluate and only continue if I improve or the board/line gives a credible bluff opportunity.

The river brings the 5c, putting four clubs on the board (Ac 8c 6c 5c). I hold the Tc, giving me a Ten-high flush. On this board, I beat any flush made with the 9c, 7c, 3c, or 2c (straight flushes are possible but extremely unlikely given the specific combos needed). I lose to the Kc, Qc, or Jc. Given Minni's line (limp pre-flop, check back flop, check back turn), her range is weighted towards weak made hands, missed draws, or perhaps a trap, though a trap is less likely with checking twice. Betting here is thin because worse hands (straights, sets, two pair) will likely fold on a 4-flush board, and I only get called by better flushes or the few worse flushes (9c/7c/3c/2c). Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff, although bluffs are rare on 4-flush boards. In a small limped pot, pot control with a non-nut flush is the prudent play.

I made one pair (5♣ paired my 5♦ on the river), but the board is highly dangerous: 8♣ A♣ 6♣ 4♥ 5♣ — four clubs on board means any club in the opponent’s hand now makes a flush and beats my pair. Pro checked the flop and turn (passive line) but his defending range preflop and postflop includes many club combos and A‑x hands that also beat me. The pot is tiny (250) vs deep stacks, so there's almost no thin value to extract and bluffing has low EV against a calling opponent. Best line is to check and take showdown; if Pro fires a meaningful river bet I’ll fold to large pressure and only entertain calling a very small probe if my read says he’s likely bluffing.

4-handed, UTG with A♦6♥ — marginal but playable. In a short-handed format you should open a wider range and A6o has decent blocker value to A‑x hands. The button (Flash) is the shortest stack (≈52bb) so raising now both gains fold equity and can isolate him; most players at this table open to ~250–300 so I’ll size to 3x the BB to 300 to take the initiative and keep the field small. Postflop plan: continuation bet dry boards, proceed cautiously facing a big 3‑bet (A6o is often dominated) but call an all‑in shove from the short stack since the cost is small relative to my stack and his shove range can be wide. Overall raising to 300 is the best exploitative, pressure play here.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit. Minni opened from under-the-gun with a raise to 300. Although I am the shortest stack at the table with 52 big blinds, I am not in a desperate position yet. K5o is a weak hand that is frequently dominated by an early position opening range (which likely includes stronger Kings like AK, KQ, KJ, and KT). Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a marginal hand, and 3-betting as a bluff is not ideal with this specific holding. The best course of action to preserve my stack for better opportunities is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2c, a very weak suited hand (one-gapper at the bottom of the deck). The action started with Minni raising to 300 from UTG. Given that Minni is the second-largest stack and has opened from early position, their range is likely strong. Although I am the chip leader, playing 6-2 suited from out of position against a strong range is a losing proposition due to reverse implied odds and the difficulty of realizing equity. The price to call (250) is not compelling enough to justify the positional disadvantage and weak holding. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

Ts7s is a suited connector in the BB facing a min-raise from UTG. Getting good pot odds (200 to win 450). While I'm out of position, suited connectors have decent implied odds if I hit a straight or flush draw. Calling is the standard play here.

Flopped middle pair with Ts7s on Tc Js 5c. I have a decent but vulnerable hand - middle pair with weak kicker. Minni opened UTG so their range includes many hands that beat me (JJ, TT, AJ, KJ, QJ). The board has a flush draw in clubs. Standard play is to check and see what Minni does - no reason to donk bet with a marginal made hand.

I opened preflop and as the aggressor I should take the initiative on this fairly dry flop. With A♦6♥ I have no pair but I hold the Ace blocker and an overcard to T/J; the board has two clubs (a draw I don’t have) but is otherwise not very coordinated. A half‑pot continuation bet applies pressure to many of Claude’s calling hands (weak broadways, small pairs, floats), protects my equity, and keeps the pot manageable given my marginal hand. I’ll bet ~50% of the pot (325). Plan: fold to a large raise, call small resistance only vs obvious semi‑bluffs and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I pair my Ace I’ll extract value, if I’m called and the turn is blank I’ll pot‑control.

I have middle pair (tens) with a weak kicker on Tc Js 5c. Minni's small bet of 325 into 650 (1/3 pot) could be value from a jack or better, or a c-bet with draws/air. Getting 3:1 odds (325 to win 975), I need about 25% equity. Middle pair has decent showdown value and folding to such a small bet would be too tight. Call and reassess on turn.

Turned trips! I have Ts7s and the board now shows Tc Js 5c Th - giving me trip tens. This is a strong hand. Minni has shown aggression pre-flop and on the flop. I should bet for value here rather than slow-play. A half-pot bet of around 650 looks like a blocking bet with a marginal hand, which could induce a raise from Minni if they have a jack or better, or get called by weaker holdings.

Claude called my 325 flop C‑bet and now bets 650 on the paired‑Ten turn (pot ≈1300 before his bet, ≈1950 after). Calling 650 to win ~2600 means I need ≈25% equity. With A♦6♥ I block many AT/AJ combos and have reasonable equity vs a calling range of Jx, medium pairs and missed club draws — while Claude’s small sizing is consistent with both value (Tx/trips, Jx) and bluffs. Raising would be thin (poor fold equity and I’d be bloating the pot with marginal showdown value) and folding surrenders good pot odds. I’ll call 650 to see the river and re‑evaluate: fold to a large river shove if I don’t improve, but extract value or apply pressure if I pair up or villain shows weakness.

I have a full house - tens full of sevens (Ts7s with board Tc Js 5c Th 7c). This is a monster hand. The river completed the club flush, which is great for me as Minni might have been drawing to it and will now pay me off. Minni called my turn bet showing interest, likely with a jack, trips, or a draw. I need to bet for value - checking risks missing value if they check back. Betting around 65% pot (1700) should get calls from flushes, trips, and strong jacks.

I opened and c‑bet the flop with A♦6♥, then called Claude’s 650 turn bet. I didn’t improve — I only have Ace‑high — and now Claude raises to 1,700 into a 4,300 pot. Calling 1,700 to win ~6,000 requires ~28% equity. Claude’s line (check‑call flop, bet turn when the board paired tens, now a river raise after the backdoor club) is weighted heavily toward value: Jx (two‑pair), any Ten (trips/full), and two‑club combos that just made a flush. I block a few Ace combos but not the club/current value combos, and I’m a clear underdog to his value range; his sizing is consistent with thin value rather than a polar bluff. Raising as a bluff is too risky (poor blockers, likely to be called or shoved), while a call needs villain to be bluffing a large portion of the time. I estimate my equity well below the ~28% needed, so the +EV play is to fold and preserve a healthy stack for later spots.

I am in the UTG position with 8s2s. While suited, this is an extremely weak hand to open from early position even in a 4-handed game. My stack is currently 52 big blinds, which is healthy enough to wait for better spots rather than taking an unnecessary risk with a bottom-tier starting hand. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

I am on the Button with Jc9c, a strong suited gapper that plays well in position. The action has folded to me. Raising is the standard play here to steal the blinds or build a pot with position and initiative. I will open to 2.5bb (250), consistent with standard tournament sizing and my previous play.

A7o in the SB facing a button open of 250. This is a playable hand to defend - decent ace that can make top pair. Against a button steal, calling is standard. 3-betting would be too aggressive with this hand quality. I'll call and play postflop in position relative to BB.

I'm on the big blind with pocket 4s (4h4c) facing a button open to 250 and the small blind called — a three-way pot. With a deep stack (~184 BB) this is a classic set‑mining spot: calling 150 more (to 250 total) gives decent immediate pot odds (~4:1) and huge implied odds if I hit a set. I don't want to 3‑bet OOP with a small pair (risking a 4‑bet or bloating the pot with a weak hand), so I'll call to see the flop. Postflop plan: if I flop a set or strong texture I'll extract value; if I miss and face heavy aggression I'll fold or proceed cautiously depending on read and action.

Flop missed me completely - I have A-high with no pair, no flush draw. Board is 3c5c Jh with two clubs. I'm out of position against the preflop raiser (Pro) and Minni. Betting into two opponents with just ace high is a bad play. I should check and see what develops - if it checks through I might improve, and if someone bets I can fold without losing more.

I called preflop as a set‑mining play. On the 3c-5c-Jh flop I missed my set — I only have two outs — and it's a three‑way pot with two clubs on board and an overcard (J) that favors the opener's range. With deep stacks but very low draw equity here, I should avoid bloating the pot out of position. Check to control the pot, see if the raiser gives up or fires a small c‑bet; fold to meaningful aggression and only continue if I improve or get an extremely cheap price to the turn.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a decent kicker and a Jack-high Flush Draw on a 3c-5c-Jh board. This is a very strong hand with excellent equity. In a multi-way pot, checking back is a valid option to pot control and conceal strength, but betting is preferable to extract value from worse pairs (5x, 3x, 66-TT), weaker Jacks, and to charge other draws (straight draws, lower flush draws, or single high clubs). Betting also prevents giving free cards on a dynamic board where overcards could scare me or kill my action. A bet size of roughly 40% of the pot (300 into 750) strikes a balance between getting value and keeping the opponents' ranges wide enough to continue with worse hands.
I have A-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw on a 3c5c Jh board. Pro's c-bet into two players is strong. Calling 300 with just ace-high and no equity is throwing chips away. Easy fold.

I called preflop with pocket 4s intending to set‑mine and missed on the 3♣-5♣-J♥ flop. Pro (button) fires a relatively small c‑bet (300), and Claude just folded so it’s heads‑up. With a small pair out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot or 3‑bet light — raising is thin and folding to this small sizing is too nitty. Calling 300 gives me ~22% immediate pot odds to see the turn, preserves fold equity vs bigger turn aggression, and lets me realize equity or fold if the action gets heavy. Plan: call now, re‑evaluate on the turn and fold to significant barrels unless I improve to a set or get a very cheap price to continue.

I called the flop as a set‑mine and missed — I only have pocket 4s (4♥4♣) with two outs to hit a set on the river (~4%). The turn (8♥) doesn’t help me and Pro checked to me, which can be weakness or pot control. Out of position with a vulnerable small pair I don’t want to bloat the pot or turn a thin bluff into a costly mistake: a bet will get called by many hands that currently beat me (Jx, 8x, overpairs, some clubs) and won’t fold enough better hands. I’ll control the pot and see the river for free — check now and reassess on the river (only continue aggressively if I hit a set or get a very cheap price).

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a mediocre kicker (9) and a Jack-high flush draw on a board of [3c, 5c, Jh, 8h]. The turn card (8h) makes the board wetter, introducing a second flush draw and completing some straights (6-7). While I likely have the best hand, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would be a difficult spot given my equity but marginal made hand status. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my equity (including the flush draw) for free, and induce bluffs or value bet safely on the river. I avoid getting blown off my hand and keep Minni's range wide.

I check. I started this hand as a pure set‑mine with 4♣4♥ and never improved: on the final board 3♣-5♣-J♥-8♥-2♥ I still only have a small pair. Pro’s line (button open, a standard flop c‑bet of 300, then checking the turn and river) is consistent with a wide c‑betting range that includes Jx, 8x, small/mid pairs, club draws and occasional bluffs. The river 2♥ actually increases the number of hands that beat me (pairs, Jx/8x/2x and two‑heart combos that now make a flush) and I only block one heart (4♥) — I don’t credibly represent a completed flush. There are very few worse hands that would call a river bet, and many hands that will call or raise, so a bluff has low expected value and a value bet is impossible. With deep stacks and a large pot, the lowest‑variance, most +EV line is to check and take showdown (and fold to a sizable river raise). Betting here risks unnecessary chips with almost no fold equity and little chance to get value.

I hold Top Pair (Jacks) with a 9 kicker on a board of [3c 5c Jh 8h 2h]. The river 2h completes a potential backdoor flush (3 hearts on board). Minni called a flop bet and then the action went check-check on the turn. Minni checks to me on the river. While I likely have the best hand against missed club draws or lower pairs (8x, 5x), the board texture is somewhat wet with the third heart arriving. Betting for thin value is thin because better Jacks (QJ, KJ, AJ) or flushes/two-pairs certainly beat me, and worse hands (like 8x) may not call on this scary runout. Additionally, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would be a difficult spot. Checking back realizes my equity and secures the pot if I'm ahead, which is the standard line with a medium-strength top pair on this texture.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Ace-Three offsuit (Ac 3h). While ranges are wider 4-handed, A3o is a marginal hand to open from first position, especially with deep stacks (50+ BBs) all around. It plays poorly post-flop out of position and suffers from reverse implied odds against better Aces. As the chip leader, I do not need to take high-variance, marginal spots. Folding is the standard and disciplined decision here.
KTs on the button is a strong hand to open. Only blinds left to act, and I have position throughout the hand. Standard button raise to 250 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds.

Pocket eights in the small blind vs a button open to 250 — a good hand to defend and often worth isolating. Claude is opening frequently from the button; I’d rather play 88 heads‑up than out of position in a multiway pot. A standard 3‑bet to ~900 (~3.5x the open) gives fold equity vs his wide open range, charges speculative calls from the big blind, and keeps the pot manageable given deep stacks (I ~179BB, Claude ~155BB, Flash ~51BB). Min‑raising to 400 is too small and invites multiway action; folding is too tight. I’ll 3‑bet to isolate and play postflop with a clear range advantage.

I am in the big blind holding Queen-Trey offsuit. Claude (Button) opened to 250, and Minni (Small Blind) three-bet to 900. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well in a three-bet pot, especially out of position against two larger stacks. Calling would be a waste of chips, and a four-bet bluff is not advisable with such a poor hand. Therefore, I will fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the SB with KTs on the button. I have position and decent pot odds (~1.9:1). KTs plays well postflop - can make flushes, straights, and top pairs. Folding is too tight given my position and the pot odds. 4-betting would be overplaying this hand. Calling to see a flop in position is the right play.

I 3‑bet preflop to isolate Claude and he called, so his calling range is wide and contains many Qx, A/Kx, and small pairs. On Q♣‑2♥‑5♠ my 8♠8♥ is a medium pocket pair — it has showdown value but is crushed by most queens and any higher pocket pair. The board is relatively dry but actually favors the caller (more Q combinations in his range), and I’m out of position to the button with deep stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot and face a check‑raise or big turn shove with just a vulnerable middle pair. I’ll exercise pot control: check and see how Claude responds. If he bets a reasonable size I can call to realize equity; if he fires a large polarizing bet I can fold and preserve my stack.

Minni 3-bet preflop but checked this Qc 2h 5s flop. This shows weakness - with a strong Q or overpair, they'd likely c-bet. I have K-high with a gutshot to the straight (need J). This is a good spot to take a stab with a small bet to represent the Q or put pressure on hands like 77-JJ or AK that missed. Betting around 1/3 pot as a probe bet.

I checked the flop as planned to control the pot with my vulnerable pocket eights. Claude fires a small c‑bet (650 into a 2550 pot) — a cheap probe that represents a wide continuation range from the button. Calling is the prudent play: I get ~20% pot odds to see a turn, my 88 has good showdown value versus many of his continuing hands (small pairs, floats and bluffs), and I avoid bloating a pot out of position with a hand that can be easily dominated by Qx or bigger pairs. I’ll call the 650, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large, polarized aggression and continue versus small/medium bets.

The 8♣ on the turn gives me trips (8♠8♥ + 8♣). Claude called my 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — his continuing range is wide but contains lots of Qx, Ax/Kx, and smaller pairs that will gladly call a value bet. This is a clear spot to build the pot and extract value rather than meekly checking behind. I'll bet for value and protection: sizing to about 3/4 pot (2400 into 3200) is large enough to charge top pair and many pairs/draws while not so large that I fold out most of Claude’s calling hands. If he raises heavily I’ll re‑evaluate (but with trips I’m happy to commit a lot), and if he calls I’ll look to extract more on the river when appropriate.

Minni 3-bet preflop, check-called my flop probe, and now leads out 2400 on the turn. This line looks like they're finally betting a made hand - possibly a Q they was slowplaying, or 88 just hit a set. I have K-high with only a gutshot (need J). The pot odds are about 2.3:1 (~30% equity needed), but K-high has very little equity against a range that 3-bets pre and now leads turn for near half-pot. Even if I hit a K, I could easily be dominated by AK or lose to QQ/AQ. Folding is correct - no need to burn chips chasing with minimal equity.
92 suited from UTG is way too weak to open. Even suited, this hand has very little playability and will often be dominated. Easy fold.

I'm on the button with 2♦6♦ (suited), stacks are deep (me ~196bb, SB Flash ~50bb, BB Pro ~210bb). With UTG folded this is a good spot to open a wide range and use position/fold equity. 2♦6♦ is marginal but playable in position — suitedness gives flush/straight potential and I can play postflop aggressively or for position control. I will raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure on the SB and try to isolate the BB. Size is large enough to get folds and preserve equity if faced with a small defense, but small enough relative to my stack to avoid overcommitting with a speculative hand. If I face a significant 3-bet or an all-in shove from Flash, I'll fold preflop (this hand has poor equity vs a strong 3-bet). Postflop I'll c-bet on favorable boards and exercise pot control with marginal holdings, looking to realize my position and flush/straight outs.

I am holding King-Jack suited in the small blind, which is a very strong hand in a four-handed game. Minni opened for 300 from the button. With a stack of about 50 big blinds, I should look to take the initiative rather than just calling out of position. A 3-bet allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or set up a favorable stack-to-pot ratio. I will raise to 1,050, which is a standard sizing for a 3-bet from the small blind against a button open.

I hold 8s 4d in the Big Blind. The action involves a Button raise from Minni and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Flash). My hand is extremely weak (disconnected, unsuited, low ranks) and performs poorly against a 3-betting range, especially from a short-stacked player in the Small Blind who is committing a significant portion of their stack. The pot odds do not justify calling with trash. This is a standard fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 2♦6♦ and Flash (SB) has 3‑bet to 1050. Calling 750 into a 1450 pot would require ~33% equity (pot ~2250 after a call). 2♦6♦ suited has very poor equity against a normal SB 3‑bet range (pocket pairs, broadways, Axs) and is frequently dominated. A 4‑bet bluff here is thin — Flash has ~40bb behind and can repush or call with hands that crush my equity. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold this marginal hand to the 3‑bet and wait for a better spot in position.

UTG with a medium pocket pair (8♢8♡) and a very deep stack — this is a clear open-raise spot. Limping from UTG invites multiway action and makes set-mining and fold equity harder; I want the initiative and to either take the pot down preflop or go heads-up where 88 performs well. The table’s standard open sizing has been ~250, so I’ll use that size: big enough to charge callers and deny free cards, small enough to keep implied odds and avoid committing too much versus likely 3-bets from big stacks. Plan: see a flop heads-up or one caller and play for sets/value; fold or proceed cautiously if faced with a large 3-bet out of position.

I am holding Ace-Queen of hearts on the button, which is a premium hand. Minni opened for 2.5x from under-the-gun. With 55 big blinds, I want to 3-bet here to take initiative, isolate the opener, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 850 (roughly 3.4x her open) is a standard sizing from position that puts pressure on both the opener and the blinds.

I am holding 7d Tc in the Small Blind. The action facing me is an UTG raise to 250 followed by a Button 3-bet to 850. My hand is very weak (unsuited connector/gapper) and performs poorly in multi-way or 3-bet pots, especially when out of position. Facing significant aggression from two players, including a strong range from UTG and a re-raise from the Button, I have no equity to justify a call. Conserving chips is the only logical play here.
Q4o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. I'm out of position against Flash who 3-bet over Minni's open. Q4o has poor playability postflop and is likely dominated by Flash's range. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 250 with 8♦8♥ looking to play for sets or heads-up value. Button (Flash) 3‑bet to 850; pot is 1,250 and it costs me 600 more to call (total 850 committed). With my huge stack vs his shorter 4,650, the effective stack after a call will be ~3,800 — deep enough that set-mining and postflop play are profitable. I need ~32% equity to break even on the call (600/(1250+600)), and 88 has that versus a typical button 3‑bet range (and much better implied odds if I flop a set). A small 4‑bet from me here would be thin and a large 4‑bet or folding surrenders too much equity; calling keeps the pot manageable and gives the best chance to realize equity OOP. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to extreme aggression on dry high-card boards unless I improve, and extract maximum value if I hit a set.

Check. With 8♦8♥ on 5♥-3♦-9♥ I have an underpair OOP to a button 3‑bettor. The flop is fairly dry and my hand has little fold equity — betting will mainly fold hands I already beat rarely and will be called by overcards/overpairs that dominate me. I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply (set‑mining still the plan). If Flash barrels a small c‑bet I will call to see a turn; if he jams or posts a very large overbet I will fold unless I’ve improved. Checking keeps the pot manageable and preserves future extraction if I hit a set.

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and Queen) on a board of 5-3-9 with two hearts. Having 3-bet pre-flop, I maintain the range advantage on this board. I am betting approximately 40% of the pot to maintain initiative, fold out her weaker hands that missed this flop, and build a pot for when I hit my draw. With about 4650 chips remaining, this sizing keeps my stack-to-pot ratio manageable while putting pressure on the opponent.

I opened UTG with 8♦8♥ and called Flash’s button 3‑bet preflop. Flop is 5♥-3♦-9♥ and Flash fires a small 750 into a 2600 pot. That sizing gives me ~22% pot‑odds to call (750 to win ~3350) and the small bet polarizes less — his range likely contains many overcards, floats and medium pairs that I have reasonable equity against. I’m an underpair with only a backdoor heart; betting OOP here has very little fold equity and only bloats the pot when I’m behind. With deep implied odds to hit a set and a clear plan (call to see a turn, fold to extreme turn shove if I haven’t improved, and extract value/raise if I hit a set), the correct, lowest-variance play is to call. Total committed after calling = 850 (preflop) + 750 = 1600.

I’m OOP with an underpair (8♦8♥) on 5♥-3♦-9♥-7♠. I called the button’s 3‑bet preflop and his small flop bet — I’m set‑mining and keeping the pot manageable. He checked the turn to me, so I didn’t improve (no straight, only one heart; two hearts on board but no flush). Betting OOP here has very little fold equity against a button’s range (many overcards/medium pairs will simply call), and it just bloats the pot when I’m almost certainly behind. Flash is shorter (≈3.9k) so a turn bet could induce a shove that I don’t want to call with only an underpair. The low‑variance, correct line is pot control: check and see the river. If the river pairs my hand (an 8 -> set) I’ll extract; if Flash bets small I can call to realize equity; if he overbets/ships and I haven’t improved I’ll fold.

The turn 7s is largely a blank card for the ranges involved. I currently hold the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and Queen). The pot is 3350 and I have 3900 remaining, giving a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of about 1.16. If I bet now, I am essentially committing my entire stack. By checking back, I take a free card to realize my equity and avoid getting check-raised all-in before the river. This allows me to potentially hit my flush or top pair and value shove the river, or evaluate a bluffing opportunity if the river is a good scare card and I miss my draw.

I never improved — I’m still an underpair (88) to a 9-high board. Flash’s line (button 3-bet preflop, small flop c-bet, check turn) looks like a lot of missed overcards/float hands or medium pairs; betting OOP on the river has very little fold equity and is thin for value (few worse hands will call). Lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and take showdown cheaply. Plan if he fires: call small, but fold to a large overbet/shove since I’d be beat a large portion of the time.

I have reached the river with Ace-high after missing my nut flush draw. The board is 5-3-9-7-2 with no straights possible other than 46 or 68 (unlikely for Minni's UTG calling range). While Ace-high loses to all of Minni's likely made hands (pocket pairs 44-88, TT, JJ, or a 9), it does beat other missed draws like King-high hearts. Minni's check on the river, following a check on the turn, suggests she either has a medium-strength hand she wants to take to showdown or she has also missed a draw. Given that she called a 3-bet pre-flop and a bet on the flop, she likely has some showdown value. Bluffing into this pot would require a large commitment of my remaining stack (shoving approximately 1.1x pot), which is extremely risky against a range that has already committed 1,600 chips. I will check back to preserve my 39-big-blind stack and hope my Ace-high is good or simply concede the pot.

I am holding J3o UTG in a 4-handed game. With a stack of 3900 chips (39 BB), I am the shortest stack at the table, but not in immediate desperation. J3o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the correct strategy here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with K8o in a 4-handed game. This falls within a standard opening range for the button, primarily to steal the blinds. Although my hand is marginal, my position allows me to navigate post-flop effectively if called. I am deep-stacked (approx. 200 BBs), as is the Big Blind (Minni), so I must be cautious of 3-bets or aggressive defense, but applying pressure here is profitable in the long run. I will raise to 2.5x BB. If faced with significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold.

Ad7s in the SB facing a button open of 250. Suited ace is a reasonable defending hand here - we have decent equity against a wide button range. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). Calling is standard; 3-betting is an option but flatting keeps the pot manageable with a marginal holding.

Fold. 2♠8♥ is a very poor, dominated hand and I’m out of position facing a button open with the small blind already calling — this will be a multiway pot with very low playability. The extra 150 to call offers weak pot odds versus Pro’s opening range, and there’s no reason to 3-bet or limp with garbage. I’ll preserve my ~21k stack and wait for a better spot later in position.

Flopped bottom pair (7s) with Ace kicker on Jd7h9h. This is a wet, coordinated board with straight draws (T8) and flush draws. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. As the preflop caller out of position, checking to the preflop aggressor is standard here. I'll see what Pro does - if they check back I can potentially win at showdown, if they bet I'll have to evaluate sizing.

I raised pre-flop with K8o on the button and got called by the Small Blind. The flop comes J-7-9 with two hearts. This board texture hits the Small Blind's flat-calling range very well (sets, two pair, top pair, straight draws, flush draws). While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and an overcard, my equity is marginal and my fold equity is low against a range that connects so well with this board. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by a better hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and potentially hit my straight or a King while keeping the pot small.

Pro checked back the flop, showing weakness. I have bottom pair with ace kicker on Jd7h9h5c. The 5c is a blank. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value - I'd mostly get called by better. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me realize equity cheaply. If Pro bets, I can consider a call depending on sizing.

I hold [8c Ks] on a board of [Jd 7h 9h 5c]. I have a double gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 for 5-6-7-8-9 or a 10 for 7-8-9-T-J), giving me 8 outs to a straight. I also have King-high which has some minimal showdown value but is likely behind. After the flop checked through, the 5c on the turn doesn't change the texture significantly for ranges that contain Jacks or 9s. Claude checking twice suggests weakness or a draw, but betting risks a check-raise which would force me to fold my significant equity. Checking back guarantees I see the river to realize my equity (hitting the straight) and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand. I will check.

River Kc completes the board Jd7h9h5cKc. I have bottom pair (7s) with ace kicker. Pro has checked flop and turn showing weakness - likely has a weak hand or missed draw. The K is a scare card but my pair of 7s has showdown value against Pro's capped range. Betting would turn my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Checking to showdown is optimal.

I have improved to Top Pair on the river with an 8 kicker. The board contains possible straights (T8, 68) and my opponent has checked to me on all three streets. Given my checks on the flop and turn, my hand looks weak, and while I likely have the best hand against a range of Jx or 9x, a bet risks reopening the action against a slow-played straight or better King. Checking back secures the pot against his weaker range and avoids value-owning myself against better hands.

I am in UTG position (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with Q7 offsuit. Even though the table is short-handed, Q7o is a below-average hand that plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. With a deep stack, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings out of position, especially with active players behind and a short stack in the Big Blind who might shove. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

9h3s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. While I'm on the button with position, this hand lacks connectivity, suitedness, and high card value. Even with only 2 players left to act in the blinds, 93o is below my opening range. Folding is the correct play.

I’m on the small blind with 7♦8♠ and only the big blind left to act (Flash, 3,800). This is a playable, connected but offsuit hand — good to see flops heads-up but not worth inflating the pot out of position. A min-raise to 200 would cost me 150 to win the current 150, requiring >50% fold equity to be +EV versus a short, often wide-defending big blind; that’s optimistic. I’ll complete to 100 (call the 50) to keep pot control, see a flop and re-evaluate. If Flash responds with a large 3‑bet or an all‑in I will fold this marginal hand; if he just calls I’ll play postflop for position/initiative accordingly.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Minni (Small Blind) has just completed the blind, and there have been no raises. Since I am already in for the full big blind amount and my hand is quite weak, the most prudent play is to check and take a free flop rather than trying to bluff or raise into the chip leader.

I hold 7♦8♠ on 7♠ J♠ K♣ — a weak pair (pair of 7s) with an 8 kicker. I have only two clean outs to trips and no meaningful straight or immediate flush draw (only a very remote backdoor spade possibility). Flash (BB, 3,800) checked preflop and is likely defending a wide range that contains lots of Kx/Jx and overcards that currently dominate me. Betting here gains little value (better hands will keep calling or raise, worse hands that I beat will often fold) and needlessly bloats the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and re-evaluate. Plan: check — call a reasonable small c-bet to extract vs worse, but fold to a large raise; if Flash checks back I get a free card and can proceed cautiously on the turn (bet if I improve, otherwise keep pot control).

I have Queen-high with no direct draw on a board of King-Jack-Seven. Since the Small Blind (Minni) checked, and I am in the Big Blind, checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn card. There is no value in betting air into the chip leader in a small pot.

Check. I have 7♦8♠ on Kc Js 7s 3d — a marginal made hand (pair of 7s, 8 kicker). The turn (3♦) doesn’t help my draws — the backdoor spade draw is dead and I only have 5 outs (two remaining 7s + three 8s) to improve on the river, ~5/46 ≈ 10.9%. Flash checked the flop, so his range is wide but contains many Kx/Jx and overcards that currently dominate me. Betting here would bloat the pot with a weak hand and will be called by better hands or fold out only the very weakest holdings. With position and a small pot (200) I’ll control the pot and see the river for free. If Flash leads into me on the turn I’ll call modest probes to realize my showdown value against bluffs but will fold to a large raise or shove (his effective stack is 3,800, so a big bet would put me to a bad decision). If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river and only invest more if I improve or get a clear read.

On a board of Seven-Jack-King-Trey, I have improved to a pair of Threes. While this is bottom pair on a very high board, the pot was limped pre-flop and checked through the flop and turn by the Small Blind. This suggests a very weak range for my opponent. I have some showdown value now and there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff or bet for thin value against the chip leader. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially win a small pot at showdown.

Board is 7s Js Kc 3d Kd; I hold 7d8s — just a pair of sevens. Flash checked the flop and turn and has now checked the river as well; that passive line can include a lot of Kx and Jx that currently beat me (the river paired the kings, giving any K in his hand trips). Betting here would fold only complete air and get called by better hands, while the pot is tiny (200). No reason to inflate or bluff a marginal hand out of position — take the showdown. I’ll check and show; I’ll fold to a sizable river shove but may call a very small probe if it looks like a block bet.

I have a pair of Threes on a board that just paired Kings. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked on every single street (flop, turn, and now the river by the Small Blind). In this situation, my pair of Threes has significant showdown value against a range that likely consists of high-card air or missed draws (like spades). There is no value in betting, as I won't get called by worse, and I don't want to risk a check-raise from the chip leader. Checking back to realize my showdown value is the most optimal play.

K3o from UTG in a 4-handed game is too weak to open. Even though we're short-handed, this is still a clear fold from early position. K3o lacks the connectivity and strength needed to profitably open here.

I'm on the button with 4♣5♣ — a suited connector with great position and deep stacks (~210bb). With only the blinds to act behind me I should take the initiative and open a wide button range rather than limp/call and give the big blind free or cheap flops. Standard exploitative sizing is 2.5x the BB to 250: it applies pressure to the small blind (37.5bb) and isolates or gets heads-up where my hand plays well postflop. If I face a small 3-bet I can continue or call in position; if I face a large shove from the short-stack I can fold. Overall raising to 250 maximizes fold equity and allows me to use position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the button, and even though button ranges are wide, this hand does not play well out of position. With a stack of 3,750 chips (37.5 BB), I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid being bled out, especially when I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the most prudent play here.

I hold 9h 3s in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Minni). The pot odds are favorable (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, roughly 27%), but 93o is one of the worst hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity, especially out of position against a deep-stacked opponent. Defending this hand is expected to be negative EV. Therefore, the disciplined action is to fold.

3♠6♣ is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability out of position. There are three players left to act (including the button and blinds) who will frequently raise or exploit a limp; calling 100 to limp OOP is unnecessary and open-raising is overly risky with such junk. Preserve the deep stack and fold preflop.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While my stack of 3,750 chips (37.5 BB) is relatively healthy, T7o is a weak hand to open with, even from late position in a 4-handed game. My opponents, particularly Claude and Pro, have shown willingness to defend or re-raise. Given the marginal nature of the hand and the potential for getting into a difficult spot post-flop, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 4d, a weak suited two-gapper. While the stacks are deep, playing this hand out of position against a competent Big Blind (Claude) is generally -EV. Claude has demonstrated a willingness to defend his Big Blind frequently against raises (e.g., Hand 90, 48, 18). Since my hand has low playability and I would be acting first post-flop, attempting to steal or limp is not worth the risk. I choose to fold and forfeit my small blind.

I am holding pocket sixes (6c 6s) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. With a stack of 37.5 big blinds, pocket pairs are definitely worth an opening raise. Opening here allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or set up for a set-mine. I will open for a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand (4s 2s). The UTG player (Flash), who has a stack of about 35 big blinds, has opened with a raise to 250. Despite being suited, 4-high is too weak to profitably call a raise, especially against an Under-the-Gun opening range which is typically strong. I have a large stack and no incentive to get involved in a pot with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.
T3o is a weak hand out of position against an UTG raise. Flash is short-stacked which means their raising range is likely decent. Calling 200 more with this hand OOP is -EV. Easy fold.

I’m in the big blind with Q2o facing an UTG open to 250 from a 35bb, aggressive player. Q2o is a poor hand here: it’s often dominated by an UTG raising range and has very weak postflop playability and reverse implied odds. Calling 150 to chase marginal equity (pot odds ~3.7:1, ~21–22%) or making a light 3-bet into a short stack that can shove is low EV. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold this marginal holding.

Current hand is 95/150 with 4 players remaining. I am in the UTG (Cutoff) position with a deep stack of 21,150 (211 BBs). My hand is K5 offsuit. While K5o has a blocker to King-high hands, it is generally too weak to open from the Cutoff, especially with three players left to act. The risk of being dominated by better Kings or Aces is high, and the hand plays poorly post-flop if called. With a comfortable stack, there is no need to force action with marginal holdings. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

On the button with J4o after UTG fold. This is a marginal hand but I have position. Standard button steal opportunity - raising to 2.5x to try to take down the blinds. If I get 3-bet, easy fold. Minni is active with a big stack but Flash is short-stacked and may not want to defend light.

I'm in the small blind with 3♦4♦ against a standard button open to 250. Calling costs me 200 more into a 400 pot (~33% break-even equity). 3♦4♦ has decent equity vs a wide button range and gains implied odds if the BB (Flash) also defends, so a call to see a flop is +EV. I don't want to 3-bet with such a weak, OOP speculative hand — raising invites 3-bets and puts me in tough spots. Plan: call to 250 total, proceed on flops with diamond/straight potential or a pair, and fold to large, committed aggression on dry high-card boards.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. I am being offered 4:1 pot odds (150 to call into a 600 pot) to close the pre-flop action.
