While I could consider a squeeze, my stack size of 38BB is healthy enough to see a flop without overcommitting pre-flop. KQo plays well against Button opening ranges and Small Blind flatting ranges. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

I called preflop with 3♦4♦ to see a flop and I’ve hit only a weak backdoor package: a gutshot to a 5 (4 outs, ~16.5% to hit by river) and a very slim backdoor diamond flush (would need both turn+river diamonds). This is a multiway pot (Button + BB), I’m out of position and the board (6♥7♦J♠) heavily favors the raiser’s range (many Jx/overcards). Against two opponents my fold equity is low, so a continuation-semi-bluff OOP is unlikely to get enough folds to justify building the pot. With a deep stack I want to control the pot and realize equity cheaply — check and see the turn. Plan: call small bets when the price is right to chase the 5 or a diamond, but fold to large, committed aggression since I have no pair and limited redraws.

I have King-high on a board of 6-7-J. I have no direct draw and only two overcards to the board. In a three-way pot with the original pre-flop raiser (Claude) yet to act, checking is the most standard and prudent play. I want to see how Claude reacts to the board and if Minni shows any interest. My hand has some marginal showdown value but cannot withstand much pressure on this texture.

Flopped top pair (Jacks) with weak kicker on 6h7dJs board. Both opponents checked to me. I should bet for value and protection - there are straight draws (89, 58) on this board. Standard c-bet around 50% pot to charge draws and get value from worse pairs.

Fold. Claude's 400 into a 1,150 pot requires ~25.8% equity to make a call profitable. With 3♦4♦ I only have a 4‑out gutshot to a 5 (~16.5% to hit by the river) plus a tiny runner‑runner diamond flush (~4.2%), and while I have ~24% chance to pair by the river those paired hands are weak and are frequently dominated by Claude's c‑betting range (Jx, 6x/7x, overcards). This is a multiway pot (BB still present) and I'm out of position, so my fold equity and ability to realize equity are poor. Calling 400 here would commit chips to a low‑equity draw; better to preserve my 20k+ stack and wait for a clearer spot. This follows my earlier plan to check/see turns but fold to sizable, committed aggression on a dry, high‑card flop.

I have King-high with no draws on a Jack-high board (6-7-J). Claude, the pre-flop raiser, has bet 400 into a 750 pot (over 50% pot). In a multi-way pot, this bet usually shows more strength. I have two overcards, but no backdoor equity or straight draws. Calling 400 to win 1150 (roughly 26% pot odds) is not justified by my raw equity, and I am out of position for the remainder of the hand. I will fold to preserve my stack.
T3o is a very weak hand from UTG position. With three players behind me, I should fold this and wait for better opportunities.

4-handed, on the button with Qd6h. This is a marginal hand but in position against only the blinds it’s a good spot to try to steal. Pot is 150 and to call is 100; folding is too tight and calling is passive (invites the small blind to see a cheap flop and creates multiway pots). I’ll open to a standard 2.5x-ish size (250) to apply pressure to the shortish SB (3600) and the BB, while keeping the sizing normal for this table. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet/shove (Q6o is easily dominated vs 3-bet ranges); if called, use positional advantage to c-bet on dry/favorable flops and play for pot control when missed, value-bet when I pair the Q or improve.

I am holding Ace-Six of Spades in the Small Blind. In a 4-handed game, this is a very strong hand, especially with the nut flush potential. Minni is opening from the button, which typically indicates a wide range. My stack is currently 3600 (36 BBs), making me the shortest at the table. To maximize my fold equity and prevent the Big Blind from entering the pot cheaply or squeezing, I will 3-bet to 850. This sizing is consistent with my previous aggressive plays (like Hands #88 and #89) and puts pressure on the button to either fold or commit more chips out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Td Qh. The Button (Minni) opened to 250, and the Small Blind (Flash), who is short-stacked with only 2800 chips, 3-bet to 850. This 3-bet represents over 30% of Flash's stack, indicating a very strong range (likely 99+, AQ+) which dominates my QTo. Additionally, the original raiser (Minni) is still left to act behind me and could 4-bet or call, leaving me squeezed. Calling 750 would require about 38% equity, which I do not have against a strong 3-betting range, and playing a dominated hand out of position in a 3-way pot is -EV. Therefore, the best action is to fold.

I opened to 250 on the button with Qd6h and Flash in the SB 3-bets to 850. That leaves me facing a 600 call into a ~1200 pot. Q6o is a marginal hand that is routinely dominated by a standard SB 3-bet range (TT+, broadway pairs, AK/AQ and suited broadways), and its post‑flop playability is poor despite positional advantage. Calling 600 only gets ~33% pot odds and I have bad reverse implied odds when I do hit a Queen. A 4‑bet (min to 1450 or a shove) would be thin and unnecessarily committing large chips with a dominated hand. I’ll fold, preserve my healthy stack, and wait for a better, cleaner spot in position.

Pocket queens UTG in a 4-handed game is a premium hand — I want value and to protect vs overcards and wide button shoves. Flash on the button is short (≈40BB) and likely to shove or call light; Pro and Claude are deep and can 3‑bet. Open‑raising builds the pot and isolates the short stack while giving me room to react to 3‑bets. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300 total): big enough to get value/isolate, but not so large it commits me unnecessarily before seeing a 3‑bet. Plan: call or fold/4‑bet depending on opponent sizing — call most single raised pots and call/4‑bet shove against large 3‑bets as appropriate.

I am currently the short stack with 4,000 chips (40 BB). I have a strong hand in the Ace of Spades and Eight of Spades on the button. Minni opened from UTG (effectively the cutoff in a 4-handed game) to 300. In previous hands, I have successfully 3-bet Minni to take down the pot pre-flop (Hand #96). Given my position and the strength of my suited Ace, I will 3-bet to 900. This applies pressure to the blinds and puts the decision back on Minni. If she calls, I have position and a hand that flops well. If she folds, I add valuable chips to my stack without further risk.

I am in the Small Blind with [8c, Ad]. Minni (UTG) opened to 300, and Flash (Button) 3-bet to 900. Flash is the short stack with only 3,100 total, meaning this 3-bet commits nearly 30% of their stack, indicating a strong range (likely TT+, AQ+). I am out of position and sandwiched between the 3-bettor and the original raiser. A8o is easily dominated by the ranges involved here. Cold calling is unprofitable due to the likelihood of domination and the short SPR. 4-betting is too loose given the strength shown. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

Q9o facing a UTG raise and a button 3-bet is a clear fold. This hand doesn't play well against a 3-bet range, and Flash's short stack (3100) means they're committed - if I call and Minni folds, Flash will likely shove most flops. Q9o is dominated by many hands in their ranges. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 300 and Flash (button) 3‑bet to 900. Flash is short (~31 BB) and Pro/Claude folded. QQ is a premium that plays well post‑flop against a short, wide 3‑betting range — I want to keep worse hands and shoves in while avoiding unnecessarily bloating the pot with a sizing war. A flat call isolates the short stack, preserves post‑flop maneuverability, and gives correct pot odds to call a potential shove. I’ll call the remaining 600 (900 total committed) and evaluate on the flop; if Flash jams I’ll call for stack given QQ’s strong equity versus their likely ranges.

I have QQ on a 7h Td 6d board after calling Flash’s 3‑bet preflop. Pot is ~1,950 and Flash is short (~3,100). This is a clean overpair but there are two diamonds and some straight/connector possibilities in a short‑stack 3‑betting range. I want to charge worse pairs, tens and diamond/straight draws while keeping the option to call a shove. A ~2/3‑pot bet (~1,300) achieves that: it extracts value from calling hands and charges draws, still allows Flash to jam (which I’ll call with QQ vs his likely wide short‑stack range). If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

On a board of 7h-Td-6d, Minni has led out (donk bet) for 1,300 into a pot of 1,950. This action, coming from the pre-flop caller against the 3-better (me), typically indicates significant strength—either a set (TT, 77, 66), a strong top pair, or a massive draw that wants to realize equity. While I have an overcard (Ace) and an inside straight draw (needing a 9), my hand is currently only Ace-high. With only 3,100 chips remaining, calling 1,300 would commit nearly half my stack on a very coordinated board where I am likely behind. Shoving is also unattractive as it is unlikely to generate enough fold equity against a range that just donk-bet. I will preserve my 31 big blinds and look for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

I am holding Ace-King of Spades (AKs), a premium starting hand. I am currently the shortest stack at the table with 3100 chips (31 BB). In a 4-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. My goal is to maximize value or potentially double up to get back into contention with the larger stacks. A standard open-raise is the best way to build the pot. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with the table's opening sizes and represents significant strength from under-the-gun.

I hold 2s5d on the Button. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em (unsuited, low, disconnected). The player in UTG (Flash) has opened with a raise to 300. Given the strength of a UTG opening range and the poor quality of my hand, there is no justification for calling or raising. I will fold.

64o is a very weak hand. Flash raised from UTG which typically indicates strength. Even with decent pot odds (250 to win 450), this hand plays poorly postflop and has minimal equity against an UTG range. Easy fold.

I have A♦T♠ in the big blind facing an early-position raise to 300 from Flash (stack ~28bb). AdTs is a strong defender here — ace-blocker, good straight/flush potential and plays well postflop. Calling 200 (300 total committed including my BB) gives me pot odds of ~31% (200 into a 650 pot) and I expect my hand to have better than that vs a standard UTG open range. I don’t want to 3-bet big and risk an isolated shove from a shorter stack or bloat the pot unnecessarily; calling keeps my options, lets me realize equity, and lets me apply pressure on favorable flops. Plan: see the flop, continue with top pair/strong draws, or control/fold vs heavy action on dry, unfavorable boards.

Flop: 4h 2h 4d — I hold Ad Ts: top pair with a decent kicker but the board is paired, so sets (22,44) or even quads are a real concern. Villain (Flash) was the UTG preflop raiser and is relatively short/aggressive (~28bb), so his range contains a lot of Ax, broadways and medium pairs plus some small pairs that will call a c‑bet. Heads‑up top‑pair A is strong enough to extract value and to deny free cards to many turn outs, so I want to charge worse aces, Kx/Qx and small pairs while keeping control of the pot size (I don’t need to commit my huge stack here). I’ll lead for ~1/2 pot to get value and protect; if he jams, I’ll re‑evaluate given his stack and shove sizing, but this sizing keeps weaker calls in and makes draws pay to continue.

I hold Ace-King of Spades on a board of 4h-2h-4d. After my pre-flop raise, Minni has led into me with a half-pot donk bet. This board texture is very dry, and a lead like this often represents a marginal hand (like a 2), a flush draw, or total air attempting to take the pot away. My Ace-high is likely still the best hand, and I have significant equity even if I am slightly behind. Given my stack size (28BB), I want to be careful but I cannot fold here. Calling allows me to keep her bluffs in her range and see a turn card without over-committing my remaining chips on a board where I only have high cards.

I need to correct my earlier note — I don’t actually have top pair. I hold A♦T♠ with a flop of 4♥ 2♥ 4♦, so I was only showing Ace-high when I bet the flop. I bet the flop (350) to take down the pot and charge worse Ax/broadway hands; Flash (UTG, ~2,450) called, so his range likely contains Ax, broadways, small pairs and possibly a slow-played 4. The turn K♦ is a dangerous card — it gives Kx hands real two‑pair/top‑pair value that now beat my Ace‑high and it brings a second diamond to the board (but a river diamond cannot make a five‑card diamond flush for me). Flash checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control with a made hand or a trap with a set. With no pair myself and limited fold equity against this opponent, I don’t want to bloat the pot and face a shove I’m flipping or behind to. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my showdown equity, and re‑evaluate on the river (call small bets, fold to large shoves unless the sizing/line strongly indicates a bluff).

On the turn, the King of Diamonds (Kd) is an excellent card for my hand, giving me Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a paired board. After Minni led into me on the flop and I called, her check on the turn suggests she may be slowing down with a marginal hand, a flush draw, or potentially trapping with a 4. Given my relatively short stack (24.5 BB), I need to extract value from her draws (hearts) and middle pairs while the board is relatively safe. Betting approximately half the pot (675) allows me to build the pot for a potential river shove while still leaving me room to navigate if she check-raises or if a scare card hits the river.

I misread my holding earlier — I only have A♦T♠ (Ace‑high), not top pair. Spot: board 4♥ 2♥ 4♦ K♦. Action: Flash (UTG) opened preflop, called my 350 flop c‑bet, then checked the turn and now raised to 675 into a 2,025 pot. Calling 675 would require ~25% equity (675 / (2025+675) = 25%). Given his line (EP open, call on a paired flop, then check‑raise on the turn) he is weighted toward real value: Kx, sets/paired hands or sticky Ax/broadway hands — hands that beat my lone ace. My actual equity with just A‑high vs that range is well below the 25% needed. Raising as a bluff with no pair and only an ace‑block is thin and unnecessarily risky (he still has ~1,100 behind and can shove). The prudent play is to fold, preserve a large stack, and wait for a cleaner spot to invest chips.

I am in UTG position (effectively Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with KTo. While this is a borderline opening hand in a 4-handed game, it is susceptible to domination by hands like KQ, KJ, AK, AQ, AJ, and AT. Recent play has shown some aggression with 3-bets from the short-stacked Flash and activity from Minni. Opening KTo risks getting 3-bet or playing a dominated hand out of position. With a healthy stack and chip lead, there is no need to force marginal spots. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play here.

J3s on the button with only blinds to act. This is a decent steal opportunity with position. While J3s isn't premium, button is the best position to open light. Standard 2.5x raise to put pressure on the blinds.

4h8h is a weak, low suited hand with very limited post‑flop playability. I’m in the small blind facing a button open to 250 — calling 200 commits me out of position to the raiser and the big blind and often creates a multiway pot where my equity is poor. 3‑betting as a bluff here is too thin (Claude’s BTN range and Flash’s short stack can create awkward, high‑variance spots). With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude raised from the Button, which is a standard steal position. However, my hand lacks connectivity and high-card value. Although the cost to call is only 150 more, 94o is not a hand worth defending with, especially as the shortest stack at the table. I should preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

K9o from UTG is a marginal hand. With 3 opponents behind, I could fold but it's playable. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to try to take down the blinds or play in position post-flop with some showdown value.

I’m on the button with Qh Ts (Q‑10 offsuit) facing an UTG open to 250. QTo is marginal vs an UTG raising range but plays well in position — broadway and straight potential — and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a light 3‑bet out of position against players who can 4‑bet or shove (SB is short ~36bb, BB and UTG are deep). Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me use position postflop, and the pot odds are reasonable (250 into a 400 pot ≈ 2.6:1). Plan: call to see a flop and proceed based on texture (bet/call when I hit or have strong equity; fold to heavy aggression when I miss).

I am holding J4 offsuit in the Small Blind. Claude raised from UTG and Minni called on the Button. J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against two opponents, especially an UTG raiser. With a stack of 36.5 BB, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid bleeding chips. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o facing a raise from Claude (UTG/CO in 4-handed) and a call from Minni (Button). While the pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a total pot of 800, requiring ~18.75% equity), K9o plays poorly multi-way and out of position. It suffers from reverse implied odds, often being dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, or AK if I hit top pair. My ability to realize equity (equity realization) will be low, likely below the threshold needed to make this profitable despite the good price. Folding avoids a high-variance, likely negative EV spot.

Missed flop completely with K9o on Qc 2s 6c. I have no pair, no draw (Kc gives backdoor club draw at best). Minni called preflop so likely has connected with this Q-high board or has a pocket pair. C-betting into this dry board with air is risky - better to check and potentially see a free card or give up.

I called preflop to use position with Q♠T♥. The flop Q♣ 2♠ 6♣ gives me top pair with a decent but vulnerable 10-kicker. Claude (UTG open) checked — that can be missed broadways, underpairs, or the occasional slowplay, but overall the board is pretty dry. I want to extract value from worse Qx and small pairs and deny free cards (and some backdoor club equity). Betting also takes the initiative in position. I choose a half‑pot size to get value while keeping worse hands and draws in — if Claude check‑raises big it’s usually stronger Qs/overpairs/sets and I’ll proceed very cautiously (likely fold to a large check‑raise; consider a call only to a small raise depending on sizing). If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and continue to control the pot.

Completely missed this flop with K9o - no pair, just king high. Minni bet 325 into 650 pot after I checked. I have backdoor clubs but that's not enough to continue. Calling 325 to chase a K or 9 is -EV when Minni likely has a Q, pocket pair, or a better draw. Disciplined fold here.

UTG with T♠7♥ is a marginal, non‑suited, one‑card‑inside hand and plays poorly out of position. Calling 100 to see a flop gives poor fold equity and will often put me multiway OOP vs SB/BB (and the button could shove or call), while raising to 200 is thin and invites 3‑bets from deeper stacks. Pot odds to call are ~40% (100 into a 150 pot) — not enough vs likely defending/3‑bet ranges and dominated Tx hands. With a healthy stack I'll wait for a better spot in position or with a stronger holding. Fold.

Being on the button with only two opponents left to act, I have a wide opening range. Jack-Six suited is strong enough to open for a standard 2.5x raise to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately or play a pot in position.

I hold 3d 7s in the Small Blind. Flash (Button) has raised to 250. My hand is 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. I am out of position against the raiser, and despite the reasonable pot odds, my equity against a standard Button opening range is poor (around 28-30%). Furthermore, realizing that equity out of position with such a disconnected hand is difficult. Flash has a shorter stack (34bb), so he might be more committed to the pot if I were to 3-bet, making a bluff ineffective. This is a standard fold.
52o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even getting 2.67:1 pot odds, I'm out of position against a button raise with a hand that has very little playability. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with 2c 5h, which is an extremely weak hand. With a stack of 3800 chips (38 BBs), I have plenty of time to wait for a better starting hand rather than entering the pot with junk, especially from early position against deep-stacked opponents.

I am on the Button with [9h 5d], a very weak off-suit hand (9-high, 4-gapper). The player Under the Gun (Flash) has folded. Although I am in position, attempting to steal the blinds against deep-stacked opponents (Claude and Minni) with such poor holdings is unnecessary and -EV. The standard play for 95o is a fold. I will wait for a better spot.

K8o in the small blind with only Minni (BB) left. This is a decent hand to raise with in a heads-up spot. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to apply pressure and try to win the blinds or play in position postflop.

I have 7♦4♠ — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor equity and playability. Claude opened to 250 from the SB; I must put in 150 more to call. Even though the call is small relative to my 21.2k stack, this hand is frequently dominated by the SB's opening range and will be very hard to play out of position on the flop. A shove/3-bet or even a min-raise is unjustified with such a trash hand. I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot rather than committing chips with almost no chance to improve.

I am in UTG position with 7c 6d, an unsuited one-gapper. This is a very weak hand to open from early position, as it plays poorly post-flop and dominates very few hands. With active players behind and a short stack in the big blind who might shove, simply folding is the correct and standard play.
Q3o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a decent stealing spot. Standard 2.5x open to 250 to take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

Standard spot vs a button open to 250. 6s8s is a playable, speculative suited connector but out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3-bet. Calling the extra 200 (250 total committed) gets good pot odds and keeps options open — I can realize equity multiway or fold to heavy action. If the BB folds I’ll still be OOP vs the button but can see a cheap flop and play a well-disguised hand; if I hit the flop I can extract value, otherwise I can fold to large aggression. Call 250.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting favorable pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 600), 43o realizes its equity very poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. Given my relatively short stack of 3700 chips compared to the table leaders, it is more prudent to preserve my chips for a better starting hand rather than entering a pot with trash and being forced to fold on most flops.

Flop is J♦ T♦ K♦ — a monotone, very wet diamond board. I hold 8♠6♠: no diamonds, no pair, no straight draw and effectively zero showdown equity vs a button raiser’s realistic range. Claude (button) is the preflop raiser and has shown a propensity to continuation-bet/raise — his range will contain lots of diamonds and broadway combos that have me crushed (any diamond = flush, K/J/T pairs, Q9 straights, etc.). With no backdoor equity and out of position, bluffing here is low-expected-value because he’ll often call or already have a made hand. Best line is to control the pot: check and give up to a c-bet. If he checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn; if he bets I’ll fold to normal-sized bets (only consider a very cheap peel in rare cases).

I have Qc3s on a Jd-Td-Kd board. I have an open-ended straight draw (need A or 9 for the nut straight), but the board is monotone diamonds. Without a diamond, I'm in trouble if Minni has any diamond for a flush. Betting here is risky - if I get raised, I'm in a bad spot. Best to check behind and take a free card. If a non-diamond A or 9 comes, I'll have the nuts.

I have 8♠6♠ on J♦ T♦ K♦ 9♣ — no diamonds, no pair, only a one-card (7) river straight draw (4 outs ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7%). Claude was the preflop raiser and typically c-bets, but he checked the flop which either shows weakness or a trap — either way I have virtually no showdown equity and no fold equity doing a clean bluff here. Betting the turn bloats the pot out of position against a range that contains many made hands (K/J/T pairs, diamond flushes, Broadway combos) that will call or have me crushed. With deep stacks and a small pot (600) the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to control the pot: check and give up to a reasonable bet. If Claude leads into me I’ll fold most normal-sized bets (only consider a very cheap peel in rare sizing spots); if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river where I can stab a favorable brick or fold to aggression.

The 9c completes my nut straight (K-Q-J-T-9). I now have the best possible non-flush hand. With three diamonds on board, a flush is possible, but I need to extract value. Minni checked, so I should bet for value. If they have a flush they'll raise and I can fold. Betting around 60% pot for value.

Board J♦ T♦ K♦ 9♣ with 8♠6♠: I have no diamonds, no pair, and only a one-card straight draw (a 7 on the river — 4 outs ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7% equity). Pot was 1000, Claude bets 400 — calling 400 to win 1400 gives pot odds ≈ 28.6%, far greater than my actual equity. Claude was the preflop raiser and checked the flop (could be a trap or a hand that plays well here — lots of diamonds, K/J/T pairs, broadway combos). Out of position, with virtually no fold equity and a small but nontrivial bet, a call is a clear negative EV and a raise would be reckless. I should preserve my stack for better spots. Fold.

Q3 suited in UTG is too weak to open. While suited, the hand lacks connectivity and the queen with a 3 kicker plays poorly postflop. With 3 players behind me and a healthy 144BB stack, I should stick to stronger hands from early position.

Button with Jc6h — marginal but in position and a great spot to steal. Pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise 200. Flash (SB) is relatively short (~36BB) and Pro (BB) is deep; a standard button open will pick up the blinds a lot and costs very little relative to my ~210BB stack. I’ll raise to a normal open (250) to take the initiative and fold out weaker blind defense. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/all-in (J6o has poor equity vs strong 3-betting ranges); if called, c-bet on dry/favorable flops and exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression with only second pair or backdoor draws.

I am holding pocket eights in the small blind with a stack of 36.5 big blinds. The button (Minni) has opened for 250. In a 4-handed game, pocket eights is a very strong hand, especially against a button open range. Based on previous hands (#88, #96), Minni has shown a tendency to fold to my 3-bets. I will 3-bet to 850 to take control of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a strong enough hand to play post-flop, though being out of position is a disadvantage.

I am in the Big Blind holding J8o. The Button opened to 250 and the Small Blind (who is short-stacked with ~28BB start but now committed ~30% of their stack) 3-bet to 850. Facing a 3-bet, J8o is far too weak to continue. The SB's range is likely strong given the stack dynamics, and I would be out of position against the Button if they call. The price is not good enough to defend with this hand strength. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I opened to 250 on the button with Jc6h and Flash (SB, ~2850) 3-bet to 850. The pot is 1200 and I must call 600, which requires ~33% equity to be profitable immediately — J6o is routinely dominated by a typical SB 3-betting range (pairs, Axs, broadways) and has poor playability if I miss. Flash is short enough to shove behind, which would put me in a terrible marginal all‑in decision for ~2600 total. 4-betting or calling with J6o is (-)EV here; preserving my ~20775 stack and waiting for a better spot is the correct play. I fold.

4-handed UTG with K3s and a deep 207bb stack — this is a hand with enough playability (suited king, backdoor flush/straight potential) to open rather than limp. Limping is too passive here; I want the initiative and fold equity against the shorter button (Flash ~40bb) and the blinds. I’ll open to ~3bb (300) — a reasonably standard size that discourages light squeezes and builds a pot I can play postflop. If I get one caller I’ll C-bet favorable textures (K/queen/broadway-heavy boards) and play cautiously multiway or on coordinated/rainbow boards. If faced with a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold K3s (only consider calling very small 3-bets from obvious light 3-bettors), since it is often dominated.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni, the UTG player, has raised to 3x the big blind (300). With a stack of 4050, I have approximately 40 big blinds. A8o is a decent hand, but it performs poorly against a tight UTG opening range. However, at a 4-handed table, opening ranges are wider. Minni has been active. Calling is an option to see a flop in position, but raising or folding are also viable. Given my stack size and position, I will call to maintain pot control and evaluate the flop, as a 3-bet might commit too much of my stack if I'm forced to fold to a 4-bet.

I hold Pocket Queens (QQ) in the Small Blind, a premium hand. Minni (UTG) opened to 300 and Flash (BTN) flat-called. Flash is working with a short stack (~37 BB), while Minni and I are deep (~200 BB). Flat-calling here is dangerous as it invites the BB to overcall and leaves me playing a multi-way pot out of position with a hand vulnerable to Aces and Kings on the flop. A 'squeeze' raise is the standard and optimal play here to build value, isolate a single opponent, or take down the pot pre-flop. Given I am out of position and there is a caller, I need to size up. A standard squeeze size is around 4x-5x the opening bet. A raise to 1400 (4.6x) puts significant pressure on Flash to shove or fold and charges Minni a premium to see a flop. If Flash shoves, I am committed to call.

Holding T5 suited facing a 3-bet to 1400 from Pro in the SB. This is a weak speculative hand that doesn't play well against a 3-betting range, especially out of position in a multiway pot that could go 3-way. The pot odds (~1.6:1) aren't good enough to justify calling with this holding. Easy fold.

I opened UTG to 300 with K♠3♠ but Pro (SB) 3‑bet to 1,400 — I must call 1,100. Calling requires roughly 34% equity (1,100 into a ~3,200 pot) and K3s has far less versus a standard SB 3‑bet range (lots of Aces, Kings and pairs). The hand is frequently dominated and is very difficult to play out of position, with the button already in the pot. A 4‑bet shove or small squeeze is not justified — K3s is not a proper 4‑bet bluff candidate here. I’m deep-stacked and should preserve chips for better spots. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold K3s to sizeable 3‑bets, I’ll fold and wait for a cleaner opportunity.

Pro has executed a large squeeze from the Small Blind, raising to 1400 over an UTG open and my call on the button. This action typically represents a very strong range (JJ+, AQs+). My Ace-Eight offsuit is significantly behind this range and likely dominated by hands like AJ, AQ, or AK. Calling 1100 more would leave me with 2350 chips in a 3200 chip pot, creating a very difficult situation on most flops where I would be forced to commit my entire stack with a marginal holding. Folding preserves my 34.5 big blind stack for a more favorable spot.

Holding King-Eight of hearts in UTG (4-handed), I have a hand that is well within the standard opening range for this position. Given my stack size of 37.5 big blinds, a standard raise is the most effective play to take initiative. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good playability and nut potential.

I am on the Button with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) facing a raise to 250 (2.5bb) from Flash who is Under the Gun (UTG) in a 4-handed game. Flash has 35bb. While A9o has some value 4-handed, it is easily dominated by a UTG opening range which likely includes better Aces (AT+) and pairs. Playing a dominated hand against a short-stacked aggressor can lead to difficult post-flop situations or bleeding chips. Given I am the chip leader, I prefer to avoid marginal spots with reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

K7o in SB facing UTG raise from short-stacked Flash. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Even against a potentially wider range from a short stack, K7o doesn't have enough playability to profitably continue here. Easy fold.

Flash opened UTG to 250; I'm in the big blind with T♦8♦. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 150 more (bringing my total in this street to 250). I will call. Rationale: T8s is a very playable defending hand from the BB — suited and connected with good multi-street playability and decent equity vs an open. Calling here gives ~27% break-even equity (150 into a 400 pot) and keeps the pot manageable out of position while preserving post‑flop maneuverability. Flash is only ~35bb, so implied odds are somewhat limited (which argues against a light 3‑bet), and a minimum 3‑bet to 400 is marginal with T8s vs an UTG opener and could commit me to awkward spots if Flash shoves. Plan after the call: see the flop and proceed based on texture — continue with top pair, strong diamonds or combo draws; fold to heavy aggression on dry boards when I miss; look to extract value or apply pressure on favorable runouts.

Flop: A♦ J♠ 5♦, my hand Td8d. I have a strong four‑card diamond draw (9 outs, ~35% to hit by the river) but no pair, and I’m out of position heads‑up vs the UTG opener (Flash, ~35bb). Checking hands initiative to him and allows easy c‑bets; betting is the correct semi‑bluff — it creates fold equity vs Jx/Kx/Qx and charges worse draws while building the pot when I hit. I’ll make a constructive half‑pot sized bet (~300): big enough to force some folds and deny cheap cards, but not so large that a short‑stack shove commits me automatically. If he calls I still have ~35% to improve and can continue on favorable turns; if he jams I’ll re‑evaluate based on his shove frequency (fold to an isolated tight shove, call only if I believe he’s shoving wide).

On a board of Ad-Js-5d, I have completely missed with my Kh-8h. Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 550 pot. This lead often indicates a hand that has connected with the board (like a Jack, an Ace, or a diamond draw) or a defensive bet. Given that I have no pair, no significant draw, and am currently the shortest stack at the table, there is no reason to float or bluff-raise here. I will fold and preserve my 32.5 big blinds for a more favorable situation.

I am in UTG position with King-Three offsuit (K3o). In a 4-handed game, while ranges are wider, K3o is still too weak to open profitably from the earliest position. It has poor playability post-flop, dominated by many hands that would call a raise. Folding is the standard and correct action here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

On the button with 48o after UTG folded. Weak hand but excellent stealing position with only blinds to act. Flash is short-stacked (34BB), making this a good spot to apply pressure. Standard button open to 250.

QQ in the small blind vs a standard BTN open is a premium — this is a clear 3-bet spot for value and to keep the BB from coming along cheaply. I want a size that isolates Claude and creates fold equity while leaving room to play postflop given my deep stack relative to the raise. Raising to 900 total (~3.5x the open) accomplishes that: it charges speculative hands, discourages cold-calls, and keeps worse pairs/broadways paying to continue. If I get called we'll proceed to c-bet and evaluate; if the short-stacked BB jams I can call; if Claude 4-bets big I will reassess but QQ is often good enough to continue. Overall the +EV play is an aggressive 3-bet to 900.

I am holding a mid-pair (77) in the Big Blind with a stack of 34BB (3,400 chips). The action started with a Button open from Claude to 2.5x and a 3-bet from Minni in the Small Blind to 9x. In a 4-handed game, 77 is a strong hand, but playing it out of position with a shallow stack-to-pot ratio after calling would be difficult, especially since most flops will contain at least one overcard. Minni has shown high aggression throughout the tournament, 3-betting frequently. Given my stack size, shoving (4-betting all-in) is the most effective move to maximize fold equity and ensure I realize the full equity of my pair if called. Folding is too tight for this stack depth 4-handed, and calling puts me in a 'fit-or-fold' spot on the flop.

I opened 48o from the button as a steal attempt. Minni 3-bet to 900 and Flash 4-bet all-in to 3400. My hand is complete trash - 48 offsuit has terrible equity against 3-bet and 4-bet ranges. This is a clear fold.

I 3‑bet to 900 with QQ to isolate Claude; Flash in the BB then put in a 4‑bet to 3,400 (leaving ~100 behind), so I need 2,500 more to call (total commit = 3,400). This is effectively a short‑stack shove and he will be jamming a very wide range (lots of Ax, broadways and small/medium pairs). Calling 2,500 into the ~4,550 pot (pre‑call) gives me ~1.82:1 pot odds (need ≈35% equity) — QQ has far better equity vs a realistic shove range, and a fold would be too passive. A reraise is pointless because he’s essentially all‑in; calling for value is the +EV play here.

Recap: I 3‑bet QQ to isolate Claude preflop; Flash responded with a near‑all‑in 4‑bet to 3,400 (leaving ~100 behind) and I called. Claude folded, pot is 7,050 and Flash is effectively all‑in for a very small remaining amount. Flop: A♢ 3♣ 8♡ — an Ace is a dangerous card for QQ against Ax, but Flash cannot fold any further and cannot be bluffed off his holding. 

Why check: Betting here cannot make Flash fold (he's effectively committed) and there are no other players in the hand to punish or extract extra value from. With the Ace on board my hand is vulnerable to Ax combinations, so there's no benefit to over‑committing or turning this into a bigger side decision — the correct line is pot control and take the free cards to showdown. Therefore I will check and see the turn/river (or go to immediate showdown if Flash is fully all‑in).

I am in an extremely shallow stack-to-pot situation (SPR of 0.014) with only 100 chips remaining and a pot of 7050. While the flop of A-8-3 is unfavorable for my pocket sevens, the 100 chips are negligible and I must commit them to the pot to maximize any remaining equity or potential to double up. Checking provides no real strategic advantage as I will be forced to call any bet on a later street anyway, and betting now technically realizes my equity immediately against Minni's range, which will call with 100% frequency given the odds.

Preflop I 3‑bet QQ and called Flash’s 4‑bet; he was left with a tiny remainder. I checked the A‑3‑8 rainbow flop to keep the pot under control since an Ace hits his likely 4‑betting range. Now he fires his last ~100. He’s effectively committed and can’t be folded, so raising accomplishes nothing and folding would be absurd given the price. Calling 100 into a ~7,150 pot is trivial (need ≈1.4% equity) to see the next card/unwrap showdown. QQ is vulnerable to Ax but still ahead of a lot of his wide 4‑bet/shove combinations, so I call to realize my equity and extract value where possible.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot with deep stacks. 3♦6♠ is a very weak hand, but button position is powerful — opening here to steal the blinds is standard. I want a raise size that gives decent fold equity without committing too many chips if faced with a 3-bet, so I'll open to ~2.5x the BB (250). Plan: fold to a sizeable 3-bet since this hand has poor postflop equity; if called I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot size.

In a 3-handed game, Minni on the Button raises to 2.5bb. I hold Q6s in the Small Blind. While Q6s has some playability due to being suited, playing out of position against the Button raiser with a marginal hand that is often dominated (by better Queens or Ace-highs) is generally a losing strategy. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to come along with a very wide range, putting me in the worst position in a multi-way pot. A 3-bet bluff is an option but Q6s is not the best candidate for it. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips for better spots.
9c4d is a weak hand even 3-handed. Facing a min-raise from button, this hand has poor playability and equity. Not worth defending with an offsuit gapper that will be dominated postflop. Easy fold.

Playing 3-handed on the Button with King-Four offsuit (K4o). While the Button position allows for a wide opening range, K4o is at the very bottom of the playable spectrum and is often a fold, especially against a Big Blind (Minni) who covers me or has a similar deep stack and has shown aggression. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or calling a defend and playing a weak hand post-flop against a capable opponent. Prudence dictates folding this marginal hand to preserve chips for better spots.

Holding 2h8h in the SB with only the BB left to act. This is a weak suited hand, but heads-up against the BB, I can complete cheaply for 50 more chips to see a flop. Folding is too tight, and raising to steal with this hand quality against Minni's large stack is risky. I'll limp and see a flop.

Claude limped from the small blind — a passive, wide-range action. I hold A♣Q♦ in the big blind, which is a strong hand heads-up and should be played aggressively to isolate the limp and take the initiative. With deep stacks (me ~24.6k, him ~13.8k) I want to build value against worse aces/broadways and deny good pot odds to speculative connectors and small pairs. I’ll raise to 800 total: big enough to fold out lots of junk and extract value from dominated hands, but small relative to our stacks so we keep postflop maneuverability. Plan: take the lead postflop — c-bet on ace/high and draw-heavy boards, control pot on dry misses, and re-evaluate if Claude shows significant aggression (treat a large 3-bet as a polarized range and respond accordingly).

I limped 2h8h from SB and Minni raised to 800. I need to call 700 into a 900 pot. This is a weak hand - 82 suited has poor equity against a standard isolation range. The pot odds require ~44% equity which I don't have. Folding is correct here.
3-handed on the button with J5 suited. This is a standard open from the button - suited jack has reasonable playability and I have position. Raising to 2.5x is standard.

I'm on the small blind with AA in a 3-handed pot and Claude opened to 250 from the button. This is a textbook 3-bet spot: short-handed BTN opens wide and I want to build the pot and isolate with the best starting hand. The to-call is only 200 and the min-raise would be 400, but I prefer a larger sizing to get value and fold equity. I will 3-bet to ~4x the open — raise to 1000 total. This sizing extracts value from worse hands, discourages speculative multiway pots, and still keeps weaker calls in. If Pro (BB) or Claude 4-bets I’ll re-evaluate (I'm prepared to commit more vs a shove or continue for value), but with AA my default plan is to get chips in when reasonably priced rather than folding out now.

I am facing a Button open to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. I hold 9dTd in the Big Blind. While 9Ts is a strong suited connector with good playability deep-stacked (approx 200BB deep), cold-calling a 3-bet here is risky. The 3-bet size is large (10bb), offering poor immediate pot odds (approx 1.5:1). Additionally, the original raiser (Claude) is still to act behind me and could 4-bet, forcing me to fold and lose the investment. Even if Claude just calls, I am sandwiched in a 3-bet pot. Given the action and position, the disciplined play is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni after my button open with J5s. While I have position and the hand has some playability, J5s is too weak to profitably continue against a 3-bet. Getting about 1.8:1 on a call, but I'll often be dominated or in bad shape postflop. Clean fold.

3-handed on the button with Ah5s and deep stacks (~250 BB). This is a strong opening spot — A5o has good playability and the ace blocker reduces opponents’ premium Ace combinations. Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me, so I want to take the initiative and use fold equity to steal the blinds/isolate. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable: raise to 300 total. Postflop plan: c-bet dry, uncoordinated boards and any A-high; control the pot or check-call against heavy resistance; re-evaluate vs a sizable 3-bet (fold to very large 3-bets vs tight ranges, consider calling smaller 3-bets because of blocker and deep stacks).

I am in the Small Blind with [4s Qd] (Queen-Four offsuit). Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard open (3BB). Q4o is a very weak hand, dominated by most of the Button's raising range. Playing this hand out of position is difficult and likely to result in chip loss. Although I am getting reasonable pot odds, the poor playability and reverse implied odds make this a clear fold. I will preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding T2o in the BB facing a button min-raise to 300. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (need ~31% equity), T2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly postflop. Even against a wide button opening range, I'll frequently be dominated when I hit and have difficulty extracting value. With 39 hands left and a comfortable stack, folding marginal spots is fine.

I am on the Button with 3h-2h, the lowest suited connector. While being in position usually encourages opening a wider range, this hand is extremely weak. The Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has been showing aggression recently, making a steal attempt less likely to succeed without resistance. Even if called, I will often be dominated or struggling to realize equity. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.
ATo is a strong hand heads-up against the BB after the button folds. I should raise for value here. Standard sizing to around 3x BB.

Claude opened to 300. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (pot odds ≈ 2:1, need ~33% equity). My hand J♥3♠ is very weak — offsuit, poorly connected, and dominated by most of Claude’s opening range. If I call I’ll be out of position post‑flop against an opponent who has shown frequent aggression; that makes realizing equity costly. 3‑betting as a bluff with J3o is thin and unnecessary with deep stacks and better spots coming. Preserve my stack and fold this marginal holding.
K5s on the button in 3-handed play is a standard open. I have position and a suited king which plays well. Standard raise to 250.

Deep stacks (≈250BB) and three-handed — 8♦9♦ is a playable suited connector with strong implied odds and good postflop playability. Claude’s open to 250 (2.5x) is standard; this hand is not strong enough to 3‑bet for value, and a minimal raise will often be called or 4‑bet by the BB, putting me in a bigger, uncomfortable OOP pot. Calling 200 keeps the pot reasonable, preserves stack depth, and allows me to realize equity — I’ll continue aggressively with diamonds/straight draws or strong pairs and fold to heavy action when I completely miss. Default: call and see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 6d facing a Button raise to 2.5bb and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me 150 to call, giving me excellent pot odds of 5:1 (16.7%). My hand is suited, which plays well in multi-way pots due to flush potential. Given the deep stacks (over 100bb effective) and the great price closing the action, calling is clearly profitable. I will proceed to the flop and look to hit a flush draw or two pair+.

Flop Td Ac Kd with 8♦9♦ — I have a 4‑flush (9 outs) but no pair. That’s roughly a 19.6% chance to hit on the turn and ~35% by the river. With both an Ace and King on board Claude (the raiser) and Pro (the caller) are weighted toward top‑pair/strong Ax or Kx hands; some of their ranges also contain diamond blockers (e.g. A♦) which can reduce the value of my outs. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloated the pot with a vulnerable draw that will often be called or raised by better hands. I’ll exercise pot control and realize equity — check and see the turn for free. Plan: if a diamond (or a very favorable card) hits I’ll take an aggressive line; if I face a small turn bet I’ll call with correct pot odds but fold to large raises.

I am in the Big Blind with a flush draw (Jd 6d on Td Ac Kd) and a gutshot straight draw (Queen makes the broadway straight). The pot is 750. The Small Blind has checked. The board texture heavily favors the Button (pre-flop raiser), as it contains three high cards. It is standard strategy to check my entire range here to the aggressor. I have plenty of equity to continue against a bet, but no incentive to lead out into a range advantage. I will check and evaluate based on the Button's action.

Flopped second pair with K5s on Td Ac Kd. Both opponents checked to me. I have middle pair which has showdown value but needs protection from diamond draws. Good spot for a value bet around 1/3-1/2 pot to get value from worse hands and charge draws.

Board is Td Ac Kd; I hold 8♦9♦ — a four-card diamond flush with 9 outs. Hitting by the river is ~35% (≈19.6% on the turn alone). Pot is 1,100 and Claude’s C‑bet is 350, so calling 350 to win ~1,450 (current pot + my call) requires ~24% equity to break even. Even after accounting for some blocked outs (Claude/Pro can hold A♦ or K♦), my raw equity comfortably exceeds the break‑even threshold and the call is cheap relative to our deep stack. 

Raising as a semi‑bluff is unattractive: I’m out of position, it’s three‑handed, and a raise will inflate the pot against players who often have Ax/Kx or T and can 3‑bet/4‑bet. I want to realize equity and preserve stack depth. Plan: call the 350 to see the turn; if a diamond hits I will take an aggressive line (bet/raise depending on action). If I miss and face a large turn bet, I’ll fold — but I’ll call small/medium turn bets with the correct odds and reconsider on the river.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd6d on a TdAcKd board. This gives me a flush draw (two diamonds on board, two in hand) and a gutshot straight draw (need a Queen). The pot is 1450 and it costs 350 to call, offering me roughly 5:1 odds (19.4%). Given my strong draw with approximately 12 outs (9 diamonds + 3 non-diamond Queens) and the multi-way action ensuring a good price, calling is the clear positive EV play. Raising would isolate me against stronger hands and likely higher draws, while folding is incorrect given the pot odds.

Turn brought the A♦ giving me a made diamond flush (board: T♦ A♣ K♦ A♦ + my 9♦8♦ → A-K-T-9-8 ♦). This is a strong, valueable hand but vulnerable to the very small subset of full houses/quads (AA/KK/TT) and to any opponent holding two higher diamonds (Q♦/J♦ combos). Both opponents called Claude’s flop bet, so their ranges include lots of A/K/T holdings and some pocket pairs — many of which will pay a reasonable bet on the turn. I want to extract value and protect against giving a free river that could pair and make a full house, so I’ll bet for value rather than check and allow a free card or a check-raise. A bet of roughly 2/3 pot (~1200) is large enough to get calls from Kx/Tx/Ax and weaker diamond combos but small enough relative to our deep stacks to keep worse hands in. If I face a big, committed raise I’ll respect it (full house/quads likely) and fold; if I get called I’ll reevaluate on the river.

I have turned a strong Flush (Jd 6d on Td Ac Kd Ad), which is the second-nut flush (only losing to a flush with the Qd). However, the board pairing the Ace significantly changes the texture, introducing a real threat of Full Houses (AK, AT, TT). Minni's lead (donk bet) of 1200 into a 1800 pot on this card strongly suggests Trip Aces or a Full House. Raising would likely only isolate me against Full Houses or the Nut Flush (Qd), causing worse hands like Trip Aces to fold or be put in a tough spot. Calling is the optimal line to keep Minni's range wide (including trips and bluffs) and to control the pot size given the paired board risk. I am in position relative to the bettor and close the action on the bet unless the button raises.

Turn brought Ad, pairing the aces on board. Minni leads for 1200, Pro calls. My K5 is now just a King kicker hand - anyone with an Ace has trips and crushes me. The board is also 3 diamonds, so flush draws are possible. With a bet AND a call in front of me on this Ace-heavy board, at least one opponent likely has an Ace. My hand has very little showdown value against this action. Fold is the disciplined play.

I have 9♦8♦ and made the diamond flush on the turn (A♦,K♦,T♦ + my 9♦/8♦). On the turn I bet 1,200 and Pro (BB) called; Claude folded, so it’s heads‑up. The river is 7♦ which (a) gives me the same flush (best five: A♦ K♦ T♦ 9♦ 8♦) and (b) makes life more complicated — any single diamond in Villain’s hand now also completes a flush, and some river/turn combos (pocket Ks/TT/AA or A+K/A+T) produce full houses/quads that beat me.

Given Pro’s line (calling the flop c‑bet and calling my 1,200 turn raise) his range is weighted toward Kx/Tx/Ax and some diamond combos. A fairly small/medium value bet on the river will get called by Kx, Tx and by some worse diamond holdings while folding pure air. I don’t want to check behind and miss value from those hands; I also don’t want to shove and fold out only worse hands or bloat the pot vs a potential full house.

Plan: make a thin value bet (≈2/5–1/2 pot) to charge worse pairs and weaker/medium diamond hands. If Pro raises big, it’s usually indicative of a full house/quads or a superior flush — I’ll fold to large, committed raises. If he calls, I’m happy to go to showdown. Bet 2,000 into the 4,200 pot to extract value while preserving stack flexibility.

I hold the Jack of Diamonds and Six of Diamonds on a board of Td Ac Kd Ad 7d. This gives me a Jack-high flush (A-K-J-T-7). The board contains four diamonds and a pair of Aces. The opponent, Minni, called in the Small Blind pre-flop, check-called the flop, and then led out (donk bet) on the turn when the Ace paired. This line is heavily indicative of an Ace-X hand (Trips) or a Full House (AK, AT, A7, TT, 77). The river bet of 2000 into a ~6000 pot signifies further strength. On a four-flush paired board, betting usually represents a Full House or the Nut Flush (Qd). My Jack-high flush loses to any Full House (which are abundant given the Ace pair) and the Queen of Diamonds flush. While I beat lower flushes and trips, it is unlikely an opponent would value bet those hands into a four-flush board with a paired Ace. Given the pot odds of ~3:1, I likely do not have the requisite ~25% equity against a range weighted towards Boats and Nut Flushes. I fold.

A6o on the button in a three‑handed game is a clear open‑raise spot. I'm the largest stack and should be applying pressure — raising takes the initiative, narrows opponents' calling ranges, and gets value/blind steals. Table norm is ~250 preflop, so I’ll raise to 250 (≈2.5x BB): big enough to deny good odds to the big blind and keep postflop options in position, but not overcommitting. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet; call or re‑evaluate vs a small 3‑bet and use position to play postflop.

I hold 9c 3h in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand with poor playability (unconnected, unsuited, low ranks). Facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Minni), I am out of position and the pot odds do not justify calling with trash. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better spots.

J7o is marginal but I'm getting good pot odds (150 to win 400, ~2.7:1) against a button raise. With only 3 players, Minni's button raising range should be wide. Defending the big blind here is reasonable given the odds.

Completely whiffed this flop with J7o on 5-T-4. I have jack high with no draws and no real equity. Standard check here - no reason to bluff into the preflop raiser on this board. Will fold to any significant bet.

I opened to 250 preflop and Claude (BB) just checked the dry flop 5h Ts 4d. Holding Ac6d I have ace‑high (and block some A‑x combinations) and I’m in position — a large chunk of his calling range is missed on this board. I want to apply pressure and take the pot down or get called by worse (small pairs, worse A‑highs, floats) while keeping the pot manageable given my marginal hand. I’ll make a small‑to‑medium continuation bet of ~200 (~35% of the current pot). If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll use position to re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll likely fold.

Completely missed this flop with J7o. I have jack high, no pair, no real draws. Minni bet 450 into ~550, about 82% pot. Getting 2.2:1 odds but with zero equity - no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. This is a clear fold. Can't continue with nothing here.

The current game state is 3-handed with blinds at 50/100. I am on the Button with King-Six offsuit (K6o). While K6o is a borderline opening hand in 3-handed play (often within the top 40-50% range), there are strategic reasons to fold here. First, the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader (27,775 chips) and has demonstrated aggressive post-flop play in recent hands (e.g., Hand #113, #114). Opening a marginal hand like K6o invites a defense from the Big Blind, leading to a difficult post-flop spot with a weak kicker, potentially dominated by better Kings or facing aggression that forces a fold. Second, my recent tight image might garner respect, but K6o has poor playability if called. Preserving my stack (19,400) for stronger holdings or better stealing opportunities is a prudent low-variance strategy at this stage of the tournament.

Heads up vs BB with J4o after Pro folded. This is a marginal hand but it's only 50 more to complete. J-high has some showdown value. Raising to steal is an option but J4o is weak - I'll complete and see a cheap flop.

Claude (SB) just limp-called. I have 9h5h — a low suited connector/one-gapper with some playability but no preflop value to build a pot out of position. The pot is only 200 and I can see the flop for free, so I prefer pot control and realize equity rather than thin‑value/feint-raising. A min-raise to 200 will rarely fold his limp range and will put me in tricky spots OOP; with deep stacks I can extract more when I hit. Plan: check, see a flop; call small bets with hearts, pair or strong draws, fold to large pressure when totally missed, and exploit hands I connect with.

Flopped nothing - just J-high on Q63 rainbow board. Minni checked behind pre-flop and now it's checked to me. I could stab small to try to take it down, but with no equity and Minni potentially trapping with a Q or any pair, checking is safer. I'll look to see a free turn and potentially bluff or improve.

I have 9h5h on 6c 3h Qd — a very weak hand with only backdoor potential (a backdoor heart flush needs both turn+river: ~4% chance) and ~24% chance to pair one of my cards by the river, so overall showdown/improvement equity is low-to-moderate (~25–28%). Pot is tiny (200) and stacks are deep, and Claude limp-called preflop and checked the flop — his range is wide and checking suggests he’s not strongly committed. Betting here would bloat a pot I’m out of position in and will rarely fold his limp/call range; raising would put me in awkward spots with marginal equity. I prefer pot control and to realize equity: check and see a turn card for free. Plan is to call small c-bets that price me correctly but fold to large pressure or raises. If checked to on the turn I’ll re-evaluate based on the card.

Turn brought 8c, creating a club flush draw. I have J-high with no clubs - zero equity. Minni checked flop which could mean weak hand or slowplay. With jack-high I have no showdown value. Betting would be a pure bluff into a player who could easily have a queen or pair. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me see a free river - if I miss again I can fold to any bet.

I have 9h5h on 6c 3h Qd 8c — no pair and the turn kills any backdoor flush (flush now impossible). My realistic outs are three 9s + three 5s (6 outs) and four 7s to complete a 5-6-7-8-9 straight → ~10 outs or ~22% to improve. The pot is tiny (200) and Claude limp‑called preflop then checked flop and turn, indicating a wide, non‑committed range. Betting OOP with this marginal hand just bloats a pot I’d usually be behind in; raising would create tough decisions on later streets. Plan: control the pot and check the turn — see the river for free, call small value bets with correct odds but fold to substantial aggression.

River 9d completes the board 6c3hQd8c9d. I have J-high with no pair. Minni has checked every street showing weakness. I could bluff but it's a tiny 200 pot and Minni likely calls with any pair (6, 8, Q, 9). My J-high might actually beat some of Minni's range (T-high or worse). Checking is optimal - if Minni had anything decent they would have bet at some point.

I paired my 9 on the river (9h5h on 6c 3h Qd 8c 9d) for second pair. Pot is tiny (200) and Claude limp‑called preflop then checked flop and turn — a wide, passive range that will include many worse pairs and missed hands. Checking surrenders thin value; a large bet bloats the pot OOP against hands (Qx) that beat me. I’ll bet small — half pot (100) — to extract value from worse pairs (6x/8x/3x, lower pocket pairs, some single‑pair hands and showdown calls) while keeping risk controlled. If Claude raises substantially I’ll assume he’s ahead and fold to avoid getting badly priced; if he calls, I’m getting value from weaker holdings.

Minni min-bet 100 into 200 on the river after checking every previous street. This is typically a thin value bet with a weak made hand (6x, 8x, 9x, or a Q). I have J-high which beats almost nothing. Pot odds are 3:1 needing 25% equity, but Minni is rarely bluffing here after this passive line. Easy fold.
3-handed on the button with K4o. This is a standard open-raise spot. K-high plays well in position against the blinds. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to steal or play in position.

I'm in the small blind with 3♣9♥ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). To continue I must call 200 which would make the pot 600 — I need ~33% equity to justify a call. 3-9 offsuit is a very weak, poorly performing hand against a button's opening range (and even worse multiway if the big blind calls), so it has well under the required equity and is frequently dominated. A tiny raise to 400 isn't a credible squeeze with these cards (no blockers, poor playability), and getting OOP postflop against one or both opponents on a marginal holding is a recipe for losing chips. With deep stacks and a lot of hands left, preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4c, a very weak off-suit hand. The Button (Claude) raised to 2.5bb. The Small Blind folded. I am getting pot odds of roughly 27% (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550). While my raw equity against a wide button range is technically around 30%, 84o has extremely poor playability and I will be out of position. It is very difficult to realize equity with this hand. Defending here would likely result in a net chip loss due to the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. Folding is the standard and correct play.

2♣6♠ is one of the worst starting hands — virtually no playability, poor equity and high reverse implied odds. There are only the blinds in a 3‑handed pot (pot 150) so calling 100 to see a flop is a marginal, losing proposition: I’ll often miss and be dominated when I hit, and raising to try to ‘steal’ is thin because both SB and BB have shown willingness to defend and re‑raise. My stack is healthy; no need to gamble chips with a junk hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a real spot with position and fold equity.

Action is Blind vs Blind (BvB) as the button folded. I hold Queen-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a top ~30% hand and well ahead of a random Big Blind range. While being out of position suggests caution, open-limping can be passive and raising allows me to take down the pot immediately (winning the 1 BB from Claude and 0.5 BB posted by me) or seize the initiative post-flop. Given standard BvB strategy, raising to 2.5x or 3x is typical to apply pressure. I will raise to 250 to deny equity to random weak hands and build a pot with a respectable holding.

4d7d is a weak suited hand. Facing a min-raise from the SB, I'm getting 2.3:1 odds but this hand plays poorly postflop against a raising range. With only 3 players left in the tournament, I should be selective. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the Button with 2d 4s, which is an extremely weak hand (bottom range). Despite having the positional advantage, opening with 4-high offsuit is not profitable, especially against a large stack in the Big Blind (Minni) who can defend widely. With 194 BBs, I have plenty of chips and no need to force action with garbage hands. The standard and correct play is to fold.
Button folded, I'm in the SB with A9o against just the BB. This is a clear raising spot - A9 is well above average for heads-up and I should be raising most aces here. Standard raise to 250.

This is effectively heads-up (Pro folded) with Claude opening the small blind to 250. I’m in the big blind with 4♥3♥ — a low suited connector that plays well heads‑up because of implied odds and good multi‑street play when it hits. I’m out of position (BB acts first postflop), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3‑bet; nor is this hand strong enough to fold for such a cheap price. The call is cheap: I need to put in 150 more into a 350 pot (≈30% immediate pot odds), so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: call to 250, proceed only with strong equity on the flop (pairs, two‑pair, sets, or good draws), and fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable flops or face a large 3‑bet preflop.

Missed flop completely with A9 high. Board of Kc Jh 6s hits Minni's BB calling range well (Kx, Jx hands). C-betting as a bluff here is risky since this texture connects with their range. Check and potentially catch an ace or fold to aggression.

I check. With 4♥3♥ on K♣ J♥ 6♠ I have no pair and only a weak backdoor heart/straight possibility — very little equity vs a preflop-raising SB range that contains many Kx/Jx/overcards. Claude checked the flop, which could be weakness or a trap, but from out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with a bluff that has poor backup. Checking controls the pot and preserves stack to continue only if a meaningful equity-improving card hits. Plan: fold to a medium/large bet; consider calling a very small probe (roughly ≤1/3 pot) to realize equity cheaply; if he checks behind I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look to continue only on a heart or a card that pairs my hand or gives a real draw.

Turn brought 5c, no improvement to my A9 high. Minni checked behind on the flop, suggesting they don't have a strong K or J. My ace high has some showdown value. Betting as a bluff risks getting check-raised or called by better hands. Check to control pot size and potentially see a free showdown or river card.

I check. With 4♥3♥ on K♣ J♥ 6♠ 5♣ I have no pair and my backdoor heart draw is dead after the turn. My realistic river outs are 2s/7s for a straight (8 outs) plus three 3s and three 4s to make a small pair (6 outs) — ~14/46 ≈ 30% raw — and many of those improvements will still lose to Kx/Jx/two pair. Claude was the preflop raiser but checked the flop and turn, showing either weakness or pot control; bluffing the turn for folds is thin and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. The pot is small (500) relative to stacks, so the correct course is pot control and taking a free river to realize equity. If I improve on the river I can bet for value; if I miss I’ll fold to a meaningful bet but may call a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) to see showdown.

River paired the 6s. Minni has checked all three streets, showing significant weakness - they likely don't have K, J, or a pocket pair they're confident about. My ace high might actually be good here against missed draws or smaller high cards. Betting as a bluff risks getting hero-called by weak pairs or better high cards. Check to showdown and hope A-high takes it.

Board Kc Jh 6s 5c 6c; I hold 4h3h — no pair, no straight or flush. Claude was the preflop raiser but played very passively (checked flop and turn), which usually indicates pot control with a made Kx/Jx or a medium pair that won’t fold to a river bluff. The pot is small (500) relative to stacks and my hand has essentially zero showdown value, so betting to try to “steal” is low EV (many hands in his range will call). I’ll check and take the free showdown; fold to any meaningful bet if he acts, and only consider calling an extremely tiny probe (≤ ~1/3 pot).
7s4h on the button in a 3-handed game. Even with wider opening ranges 3-handed, 74o is too weak to open. Both opponents have much larger stacks (27k and 19k vs my 12.9k). Folding is standard here.

Heads-up from the small blind with J♣7♦ after the button (Claude) folded. This is a marginal but playable hand heads-up and I’ll have postflop position if I continue. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to see a flop (about 3:1 immediate pot odds), so calling is a cheap way to realize equity without bloating the pot. Raising to 200 risks a 3-bet from an opponent who has shown aggression in this game, and J7o doesn’t fare well facing that pressure. I’ll call to see the flop (total committed = 100). Postflop plan: play straightforward — seek value with top pair or better, call small bets with reasonable draws/backdoors, and fold to large aggression when I have little equity.

In a 3-handed game, Minni (SB) has limped. I hold AJo in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand relative to a limping range, which often consists of marginal hands or traps. While raising is a valid option to extract value and deny equity, checking is a superior strategic choice here for several reasons. First, it strengthens my checking range, ensuring I am not only checking with weak hands that can be easily bluffed. Second, AJo plays well post-flop in position; I can comfortably call bets on Ace or Jack high boards or induce bluffs from Minni's weak holdings. Third, checking controls the pot size, avoiding a scenario where I get limp-raised or play a bloated pot with a hand that is typically one-pair strength. Given Minni's history of aggressive post-flop play, keeping the pot manageable and using my position is prudent.

Heads‑up with position vs the Big Blind. I have J♣7♦ on 5♦5♣8♣ — no pair but I'm last to act and the flop is relatively dry. Pro checked, which signals weakness (many missed broadways/small pairs will check behind). A standard half‑pot continuation bet both wins the pot now and folds out a lot of unpaired hands while not committing too much with nothing. Size: ~50% pot (100 chips) gives good fold equity but keeps the pot manageable given deep stacks. Plan: if Villain folds I take it down; if called I will pot‑control on the turn and only continue if I improve (pair, two pair, trips) or pick up a clear read. If Villain raises, I’ll fold — I have very little showdown equity vs value raises on this board.

In a limped blind-vs-blind pot, the range of the Small Blind is very wide. On a paired board of 5-5-8, my Ace-high (AJo) is a significant portion of the time the best hand, beating missed draws and lower unrelated cards. Minni's lead of half-pot (100) is a standard size that could represent a wide variety of holdings, including draws, small pairs, or simply a stab to take the pot. Folding is far too tight given the pot odds (getting 3:1) and my relative hand strength. Raising would isolate me against trips or better pairs and potentially fold out the bluffs I beat. Calling allows me to keep the pot small, use my position, and re-evaluate on the turn. I have clean outs to improve if behind and sufficient showdown value to call one street.

I check. I have J♣7♦ and didn’t improve on the turn (board 5♦-5♣-8♣-9♣). I only have a 4‑card club draw (need one club on the river, ~9/46 ≈ 19.6%) and no pair, so my equity is modest. I was the aggressor on the flop and Pro’s call shows some willingness to continue (likely an 8/9, a small pair, or a club draw), so betting here with just a draw is thin — my fold equity is limited and a raise would force me to fold. Checking controls the pot (current = 400), lets me realize equity to the river, and keeps options open: if Villain bets I can fold to large pressure or call small bets only when the price justifies my draw/improvement chances; if Villain checks back I get to see the river and can re-evaluate with the extra card.

I hold Ah Jd on a board of 5d 5c 8c 9c. After calling a flop bet, Minni checks to me on the turn. The 9c brings a third club, completing possible flushes and straight draws. My hand is Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (need a 7). Minni's check could indicate pot control with a marginal made hand or giving up on a bluff. Betting would turn my hand into a bluff, but I lack club blockers and might get called by any pair or flush draw. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, seeing a river where I could improve to top pair or a straight, or win at showdown if my Ace-high is currently good against a busted draw.

River completed Q♠ and my J♣7♦ never improved — I have J‑high with a missed club draw. The pot is 400 and it’s checked to me, so the smallest bet I can make is 100. A bluff of 100 only breaks even if Villain folds >100/(400+100)=20% of the time. Pro called my flop bet and then checked the turn, which narrows his range toward hands that will call a small river bluff (5x, 8x, 9x, Qx, some club combos). Given that likelihood and that this opponent is capable of calling down with medium strength, I don’t expect ≥20% fold frequency — so a bluff is -EV. With a very strong stack and no need to gamble here, the best play is to check and see showdown (or induce a bluff). I avoid risking chips with a pure bluff that’s unlikely to work.

In a limped blind-vs-blind pot, the river card is the Queen of Spades, making the final board 5d 5c 8c 9c Qs. Minni checks to me. My hand is Ace-high (Ah Jd). Minni's line of betting the flop and checking down the turn and river suggests a range of marginal made hands (like a weak pair of 8s or 9s), busted straight draws, or potentially a weak flush that is pot-controlling. Ace-high has showdown value against the busted draws (e.g., T7, J7, 64) and worse high cards. Betting would turn my hand into a bluff, but it is unlikely to fold out any pair or flush in a small pot where ranges are wide. Since I beat Minni's bluffs and likely lose to her value range regardless of a bet, taking the free showdown to realize my equity is the best play.

I’m first to act preflop in a three‑handed pot with 3♦6♥ — a very weak, unsuited low hand with poor playability and frequent domination. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to break even and will likely become a multi‑way pot or face a post‑flop raise from opponents who have shown they defend and 3‑bet often. Raising as a bluff is too risky here because both SB (Pro) and BB (Claude) have histories of aggression and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a hand that rarely improves. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving chips for better spots is the +EV play.

I am in the Small Blind with K4s. The Button (Minni) has folded. This is a standard Blind vs Blind scenario. In Hand #117, facing the same opponent (Claude) in the Big Blind, I raised with Q9o and he folded. K4s is a reasonable hand to steal with due to the King blocker and suited potential. Claude has shown a mix of defending and folding, but aggression here risks little (250 chips) to win the dead money (150 chips) and takes initiative. I will raise to 250 to steal the pot or play in position with the betting lead if the flop comes favorable.

8Ts suited connector in BB facing min-raise from SB. Getting 350:150 pot odds (2.3:1). This is a clear defend - suited connectors play well postflop and I'm getting excellent odds. No need to 3-bet, just call and see a flop.

The flop comes [Jd Ac Tc], which is an incredibly wet board that interacts very favorably with the Big Blind's calling range (lots of Ax, KQ, QJ, QT, JT, and two-pair combinations). Although I have a very strong draw with the King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needs a Queen), I am out of position and at a range disadvantage. Betting here risks getting raised, which would put me in a difficult spot with my draw. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and see a turn card. I plan to call if my opponent bets, given the strength of my draw.

I flopped middle pair (tens) with a gutshot straight draw (Q gives me the nut straight). Pro raised preflop and checked this Jd Ac Tc flop. Their check could indicate weakness or they're trapping with a big ace/set. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards and draws. I should bet for value and protection against hands like KQ, small pairs, or flush draws. Betting around 60% pot to get value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable.

I hold Kc4c on a board of Jd Ac Tc. I checked the flop, and the opponent bet 300 into a pot of 500. I have a very strong draw: a King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight (a Queen makes the straight). This gives me approximately 12 outs (9 clubs + 3 non-club Queens). My equity is very high, likely around 40-50% even against a strong range containing Top Pair. Raising is an option to generate fold equity, but given the board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range (lots of Ax and two-pair combos), getting 3-bet would be difficult. Calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity for a good price (getting nearly 4:1 odds on a call, needing ~27% equity). I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have improved to a pair of Kings on the turn while retaining the Nut Flush Draw (holding Kc on an Ac-high board). The board [Jd Ac Tc Ks] is extremely wet; any Queen makes a straight, and my opponent's flop bet suggests they connected with the board (Ax, two pair, or a straight draw).
