Leading out OOP is risky as I likely only get called by better hands (Two Pair, Straights, Sets) and fold out bluffs. Checking is the optimal line to control the pot size and realize my high equity. I plan to call a reasonable bet given my pair and strong flush draw.

The King on the turn completes a Broadway straight for any Queen. Board is now Jd-Ac-Tc-Ks, meaning anyone with a Q has the nut straight. My pair of tens is now a very weak holding - I'm behind any Ace, King, Jack, Queen, or two pair. Pro checked, so I should check back for pot control. Betting accomplishes nothing - worse hands fold and better hands call/raise.

The board shows [Jd Ac Tc Ks Qs], which forms a Ten-to-Ace straight (Broadway). There are no flush possibilities as the board contains mixed suits (2 clubs, 2 spades, 1 diamond). Since the board itself is the nut straight, every active player currently holds the best possible hand. This is a guaranteed split pot. I cannot improve, nor can I lose. The standard and most efficient play is to check to get to the showdown and chop the pot. There is no value in betting unless I believe the opponent might mistakenly fold the nuts, which is highly unlikely.

The river completed a Broadway straight on the board (A-K-Q-J-T). No flush is possible - only 2 clubs, 2 spades, 1 diamond on board. The board plays for everyone, meaning this is a guaranteed chop. My pair of tens is irrelevant. Checking is optimal since betting accomplishes nothing - Pro will never fold when they have the nuts on the board.

I hold Pocket Tens [Ts Td] on the Button in a 3-handed game. This is a premium starting hand. With effective stacks being quite deep (over 100 BBs), I want to build the pot and extract value from weaker hands in the blinds. A standard open-raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) is appropriate to take the initiative and deny free equity to the big blind.
92o is a trash hand with no playability. Facing a button raise from Pro, I'm out of position with one of the worst hands in poker. Easy fold.

Facing a standard 2.5x button open to 250 from the button while I’m in the big blind with A♦4♦ and a deep stack. Pot is 400 and I must call 150 more (total commit 250). A4s is a good defend heads-up: it blocks many ace combinations, has nut-flush potential and enough equity vs a wide opening range to justify a call (150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% break-even equity). I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand via a tiny 3-bet; calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity and apply pressure when I hit or pick up strong draws. Plan: call preflop, evaluate flop — continue aggressively with top pair/strong draws or c-bet favorable textures, and fold to heavy resistance on dry boards without improvement.

Check. I have A♦4♦ on K♦ 3♥ 5♥ — only ace‑high and a very weak backdoor diamond possibility (there is only one diamond on the board, so I need runner‑runner to make a flush). The preflop button opened, so his range contains a lot of Kx and broadways that connect with this flop; he will c‑bet this texture very frequently. I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand that is often behind to a king or any made two‑pair/sets he might have.

Checking accomplishes pot control and keeps my range wide. If the button bets a small/medium size I can call and float (I block many A‑x bluffs with the Ace), see a free turn, and then decide based on whether I pick up an Ace, a diamond, or meaningful showdown equity. If he barrels big (large sizing or overbet) I will fold — I have no pair and too many Kx/strong hands are in his range to justify calling big. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn card and plan to barreL only if I improve (A/4/diamond) or if the turn action signals weakness.

I hold Pocket Tens on a [Kd 3h 5h] flop. The King is an overcard that connects well with the Big Blind defending range. While I am ahead of draws and lower pairs, I am behind any King. Betting risks getting called by better (Kx) or folding out worse hands that have very little equity. Checking back allows me to pot control, induces bluffs on later streets, and avoids facing a check-raise which would put me in a difficult spot. I will take the free card to see the turn.

Turn (Kd 3h 5h 7d). I hold A♦4♦ — now the nut-diamond draw (A♦) plus a couple of extra outs to pair the Ace. BTN opened wide preflop and checked back the flop, so his range is broad and contains many hands that will fold to a turn semi‑bluff. Pot is 550 and I’m out of position, so I want to take the initiative without overcommitting. Betting about half pot (275) achieves two things: (1) generates fold equity against Kx/broadway-type hands that checked back, and (2) builds the pot for when I hit — I have 9 diamond outs + 3 non-diamond Aces = 12 clean outs on the river ≈ 12/46 ≈ 26% to improve, plus some current showdown equity (A blocker). A 275 half-pot bet needs ~33% folds to be profitable as a pure bluff; combined with my ~26% improvement chance and blocker/showdown equity this is a +EV semi-bluff. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I go to the river and value-bet a hit or fold to heavy action when I miss; if he check-raises big I’ll respect the strength (sets/two-pair/Kx) and fold, but I’ll call small raises that offer correct pot odds for the draw.

On the Turn [Kd 3h 5h 7d], I face a lead of 275 into 550 from the Big Blind after checking back the Flop. My hand (Pocket Tens) is effectively a bluff-catcher here, as I lose to any King. However, the opponent's range contains many draws (hearts, diamonds now, straight draws like 64) and weaker pairs (7x, 5x, 3x) that might probe the turn after I showed weakness. The bet sizing is small (half pot), offering me good odds (3:1) to call. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (Kx) or strong draws, so calling is the optimal play to keep their bluffing range wide and realize my equity.

I semi-bluffed the turn with A♦4♦ to represent diamonds/strong hands and generate fold equity; Pro’s call on the turn shows he’s willing to continue with at least some pair or decent showdown hands (Kx, small pairs, or stubborn broadways). The river (2♥) bricks my diamond outs and I’m left with only Ace-high. Betting now would be thin — I’d mostly be getting called by better hands that beat me and only rarely folding out hands that I beat. With no real showdown-improvement and limited fold equity after his turn call, the best play is pot control: check and take the showdown. If he bets later, I’ll evaluate sizing, but I won’t bluff into a caller-heavy range.

I hold Pocket Tens on a board of [Kd 3h 5h 7d 2h]. The river 2h completes the heart flush draw. My opponent Minni checks to me. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to any King, any Flush, or random two-pair/straight combinations. I checked back the flop and called a small bet on the turn, representing a marginal made hand or a draw. Betting now serves little purpose: worse hands (like missed diamond draws or small pairs) will likely fold, and better hands (Kings, Flushes, Straights) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity against missed bluffs or weaker made hands without risking more chips in a spot where I am unlikely to extract thin value.

3-handed on the button with Q3o. This is a marginal hand but in position with only 3 players remaining, I should be opening fairly wide from the button to steal blinds. Standard button open to 2.5x BB.

6♣2♥ is one of the weakest hands — offsuit, unconnected, and with almost no playability. Claude opened to 250 from the button; calling 200 more leaves me out of position to two players (button and big blind) in a multiway pot with very poor equity. A 3-bet bluff here is thin and unnecessary given deep stacks and better spots to apply pressure. I’ll conserve my stack and fold.

I hold KJs in the Big Blind facing a Button raise from Claude. This is a strong hand that plays well post-flop (suited broadway). I have excellent pot odds to call (need to put in 150 into a pot that will be 550, requiring ~27% equity). While a 3-bet is an option for value, calling is a lower-variance standard play to keep the opponent's range wide and control the pot size while out of position. I will call to see a flop.

I have flopped the nut straight (K-J on a Q-T-9 board) with a backdoor flush draw. This is a very strong hand, currently the nuts. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Claude), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This board texture interacts very well with a Button opening range (sets, two pairs, top pairs like AQ/KQ), making it highly likely Claude will continuation bet. Checking gives me the opportunity to check-raise, which is the best way to build a large pot and extract value from strong second-best hands or draws. Leading out (donk betting) might scare off weaker hands or deny Claude the chance to bluff.

Flopped top pair with Q3 on Qh-Tc-9d. This is a very coordinated board with straight draws (KJ, J8) and possible two pair combos. Pro checked to me. I should bet for value and protection - my top pair is vulnerable to many turn cards. Betting around half pot to charge draws and get value from worse hands like Tx, 9x, or smaller pairs.

I have flopped the nut straight (K-J on Q-T-9) on a very coordinated board. The board texture connects heavily with a Button opening range, meaning the opponent likely has a piece (sets, two pair, top pair, or draws). Checking and calling is passive and risks a scare card (board pairing or flush card) killing the action or giving the opponent a better hand on the turn. A check-raise is the best play to extract maximum value immediately from strong second-best hands (like sets of 10s or 9s, or KQ/AQ) and to build a pot large enough to play for stacks. I will raise to 975, which is roughly 3.5x his bet, to charge draws and get value.

Pro check-raised me on a very coordinated Qh-Tc-9d board. My Q3 is just top pair with a terrible kicker. This board smashes check-raising ranges: sets (TT, 99, QQ), two pair (QT, Q9, T9), straight draws (KJ, J8), and made straights (KJ). Getting 2.5:1 but I'll face more aggression on later streets with a marginal holding. Q3 is too weak to continue - folding is the disciplined play here.

I'm on the button with Qc3s — a weak but playable hand in 3‑handed play. With pot 150 and only 100 to call, I prefer to apply pressure and take advantage of position rather than limp/call. I will open-raise to 250 (a standard ~2.5x BB/table sizing) to maximize fold equity versus the blinds and to represent a wider button range. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold this marginal holding; if called I will use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot size.

I am in the Small Blind holding 5s Th (Ten-Five offsuit). The Button (Minni) has raised to 250. T5o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated. I will be out of position post-flop against the aggressor. The pot odds do not justify calling with this trash hand, and it is not a suitable candidate for a 3-bet bluff. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

KJs is a strong hand in the BB facing a button min-raise. 3-handed, Minni's button opening range should be wide. KJs plays well as a 3-bet for value and to take initiative. I'll 3-bet to around 800 to put pressure and define their hand.

I opened to 250 on the button with Qc3s to apply pressure and steal the blinds. Claude’s 3-bet to 800 is a sizable isolation raise that represents a much stronger value-heavy range (pairs, broadways, strong suited holdings) and heavily dominates my hand. Q3o has poor equity and playability against that range — a call of 550 is cheap relative to my stack but will put me in marginal postflop spots; a 4-bet would be spewy. As the chip leader late in the tournament I’ll preserve my stack and fold this marginal holding.

I hold 2d 6h on the Button, which is an extremely weak hand (bottom 5% of hands). Although I have the best position, the risk of entering the pot with such poor equity outweighs the potential reward of stealing the blinds, especially with active opponents in the blinds (Claude in SB and a deep-stacked Minni in BB). My stack is healthy at ~196 BB, so there is no pressure to force action with garbage cards. The standard and most +EV play is to fold.

J8 suited in the SB heads up against BB after button folded. Good spot to raise for value/initiative rather than just completing. J8s has decent playability postflop with flush potential and connected cards. Standard raise to 250.

Facing an SB open to 250 from the small blind with 5h Td in the big blind. I’m getting a cheap price to defend (call 150 more for a total commitment of 250) — roughly ~30% pot odds — and the hand has playable equity (can make top pair, two pair, and occasional straights). I don’t want to isolate out of position by 3-betting as a bluff vs a competent opening range, and raising to 400 is too thin with this holding. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to large turn/river aggression on dry A/K/Q-heavy boards if I miss, and proceed (value/float) when I connect with a Ten or strong draw. If Claude 3-bets large preflop, re-evaluate and likely fold.

Flopped middle pair with top kicker (8s with J kicker) on a 2c 7s 8s board. Board has a spade flush draw. I should c-bet for value and to charge draws. Betting around 1/3 pot is reasonable - gets value from worse hands and charges the flush draw.

Flop: 2c 7s 8s. I hold Td5h — I have no pair and no spade draw, but Claude opened from the SB and made a small c-bet (175 into a 675 pot). Calling 175 is cheap — I already committed 250 preflop, so calling makes my total commitment 425. The call costs 175 to win a pot that will be ~1025 after I call, so I only need ≈17% equity to break even (175/1025 ≈ 17%). Claude’s small sizing indicates a wide continuation-bet range; being last to act (I have position) lets me float and see a turn card where I can either take the pot away, fold to meaningful aggression, or continue if I pair my Ten or pick up a legitimate draw. Raising here is a thin bluff with no backup equity and folding is too nitpicky given the price. Plan: call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn and fold to large turn/river aggression unless I improve.

Turn brought the Js giving me two pair (Jacks and Eights) - a very strong hand. However, the board now has 3 spades creating a flush possible. I need to bet for value and protection against the flush draw. Minni could have a flush draw, a weaker pair, or even the made flush already. Betting around 2/3 pot seems right - extract value from draws and worse made hands while not overcommitting if they have a flush.

I have T♦5♥ on 2♣ 7♠ 8♠ J♠. Pot is 1400 and Claude leads for 550; calling 550 to win 1950 requires ≈28% equity. My realistic outs are 4 nines to make the wheel straight (4/46 ≈ 8.7%). There are three remaining tens (≈6.5%), but tens are dirty outs — they often still lose to any Jx, two pair, sets or a made spade flush. Naively counting all 7 outs gives only ~15% equity, well below the ~28% break-even. The J♠ on the turn helps Claude’s value range massively (made J’s and now many spade flushes); his line (small flop bet then larger turn bet) looks like real strength rather than air. Raising as a bluff here is too thin with almost no backup equity and poor fold equity. My earlier plan was to fold to large turn aggression unless I improved — this is that spot. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better, +EV spot rather than paying 550 with very poor equity.

On the button 3-handed with Q6o. This is a standard open from the button position - not a strong hand but playable when folded to me. Standard raise to 250 to steal blinds or play in position postflop.

I’m in the small blind with Q♥T♥ facing a button open to 250. Pot is 400 and I need to commit 200 more (total 250) — a small price relative to my 27,275 stack. QTs is a playable, suited broadway with good equity and postflop playability, but from the SB I’ll be out of position versus the button (and possibly the big blind). Raising here would bloat the pot OOP and likely get me facing resistance from the big blind or a stronger 3-bet; calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: call to see the flop, evaluate texture and opponents’ reactions, and proceed (c-bet/raise/float) only if the flop and actions justify it; fold/re-evaluate to any large 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 4d. Claude raised to 250 from the Button, and Minni called in the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600, and I need to call 150 to see a flop. This gives me pot odds of 150/750 = 20% (5 to 1). Although Q4s is a marginal hand and I will be out of position against two opponents, the price is too good to fold a suited queen which has potential to make strong flush hands. A 3-bet squeeze is too risky with this specific holding against a deep-stacked SB caller. Therefore, closing the action with a call is the most positive EV play.

Board is 4♠ K♦ J♣; I hold Q♥T♥. I have an open‑ended straight draw to a 9 or A (8 outs — ~31.5% to hit by river) and some equity from pairing Q/T, but no flush (board has no hearts). This is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position, so leading as a semi‑bluff has limited fold equity and risks bloating a pot I’ll have to play OOP. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my draw cheaply. Plan: call a standard c‑bet (or a modest bet ~1/3–1/2 pot) to see the turn, fold to extreme aggression that prices out my equity, and if both check through consider betting a favorable turn (or barreling when I improve).

I hold Qd4d on a flop of 4s Kd Jc. I have bottom pair and a backdoor flush draw. The board texture (Broadways) connects well with the Button's (Claude) raising range and the Small Blind's (Minni) calling range. Minni has checked. Acting in the Big Blind, leading out (donk betting) into two players, one of whom is the pre-flop raiser, is not advisable with a weak pair on this texture. My goal is to pot control and see a cheap showdown or improve on the turn. Checking is the standard play to let the pre-flop aggressor act.

Flop is K-J-4 rainbow. I have Q6o - complete air with just queen high. Both opponents checked to me showing weakness. Standard c-bet spot to take down the pot with position. If I get called or raised, I can give up. Betting ~45% pot.

Call 350. With Q♥T♥ on K♦ J♣ 4♠ I have an open‑ended straight draw to A or 9 (8 outs, ~31.5% to hit by the river) and some extra equity from pairing Q/T. Claude’s flop bet is small (350 into a 1,100 pot) so the price is right — calling 350 to win ~1,450 gives ~24% pot odds, which is lower than my raw chance to improve, making a call +EV. Raising OOP in a three‑way pot with just a draw is suboptimal: limited fold equity and it bloats a pot I’ll have to play out of position. Plan: call to realize the draw cheaply, fold to large turn aggression that prices me out, and if I hit (pair/straight) or the turn gives a clear advantage I’ll apply pressure or extract value.

On the flop [4s Kd Jc], I hold bottom pair (4s) with a backdoor flush draw. The action went: Minni checked, I checked, Claude bet 350 (half pot), and Minni called. While the pot odds are attractive (approx 19%), the fact that Minni called in the Small Blind significantly weakens my relative position. It is highly probable that at least one of my opponents holds a King or a Jack, leaving me drawing very thin (likely 5 outs or fewer, which may not even be clean). Overcalling with bottom pair, weak kicker in a multi-way pot on a broadway-heavy board is a losing play. I fold to preserve chips.

Board: 4♠ K♦ J♣ 5♦. I hold Q♥T♥. On the flop I called Claude’s small c-bet; on the turn I’m first to act OOP in a heads‑up spot. I still have an open‑ended straight draw (A or 9 — 8 outs), which is ~8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river, plus modest pair equity if Q or T comes. Betting the turn here would be a pure semi‑bluff with limited fold equity — Claude’s button raising range and his small flop c‑bet mean he’ll call many Kx/broadway hands, and if he calls I’m left with only ~17% to improve. If I bet and get raised I’m OOP with just a draw, which is a bad spot. With a healthy stack lead (26,725 vs his 12,475) there’s no need to bloat the pot; pot control and realizing equity cheaply is the right play. Plan: check the turn. On the river I’ll adapt — value bet big if I make the straight (A or 9), consider a small thin value bet if I pair Q/T depending on the action, and fold to large river sizing that gives me worse than ~17% pot odds. (For reference, with the current pot of 1,450 a pure draw call breaks even at roughly a 387‑chip river bet.)

Minni called my flop c-bet, indicating some hand strength - likely a K, J, or some draw. The 5d turn is a blank that doesn't change much. I have complete air with Q-high and no draws. Firing a second barrel here against a caller is risky and likely burning chips. Better to check back, control pot size, and see a free river.

Board: 4♠ K♦ J♣ 5♦ 5♠. My hand Q♥T♥ — I missed the straight and have only Q‑high (no pair). Claude opened the button preflop, made a small flop c‑bet (350 into ~750) and then checked the turn. That line maps to a lot of single‑pair Kx/Jx and broadway hands that beat me; the river pairing the 5s makes a bluff less credible and actually helps some parts of his range. Betting OOP here as a bluff has very limited fold equity (K and J will call small bets) and risks getting check‑raised into a terrible spot with almost no showdown equity. With a large stack and no need to gamble, pot control and taking the free showdown is the correct play. I’ll check and only continue if he bluffs — fold to reasonable bets, call tiny river bluffs only if sizing/timing strongly indicate a bluff.

River pairs the 5s. Minni has checked all three postflop streets. I have Q-high - complete air that can't win at showdown. However, bluffing here is unlikely to work since Minni called my flop c-bet, showing they have something. They likely have a weak King, Jack, or small pair. Betting would just burn chips against a player who's shown a willingness to call. Check back and accept the loss.

Button in a 3-handed pot with Q♣9♥ — a playable hand with position. I want to take the initiative, build the pot with positional advantage, and put pressure on the blinds' marginal ranges. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) achieves that: it's big enough to fold out some hands, small relative to our 27,450 stack, and leaves room to fold if I face a large 3-bet. Plan: raise to 300; if called use position to c-bet favorable flops, fold to heavy aggression from likely stronger ranges.

Current hand 126/150. Blinds 50/100. Minni opens to 300 (3x) from the Button. I am in the Small Blind with 5s4s. While suited connectors have potential, playing 5-high out of position against a 3x open is generally a losing proposition, especially with the Big Blind still to act who might squeeze or call, leading to a multi-way pot where I am sandwiched or OOP. The 3x sizing makes the price to call unattractive (need ~36% equity, realizing equity OOP is hard). Conserving stack for better spots is prudent.

K8o in BB facing a button min-raise. With 3 players left, button opening ranges are wide. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (call 200 to win 450). K8o is playable against a wide button range - I'll defend and see a flop in position.

Flopped top two pair (Kings and Eights) on Jd 8h Kd. This is a very strong hand. Board has two diamonds creating flush draw potential. I should bet for value to charge draws and get called by Kx, Jx, and straight draws. Betting around 60-65% pot is appropriate.

I opened to 300 from the button; Claude (BB) called. Flop J♦‑8♥‑K♦ and Claude makes a small c‑bet of 400 into a 1,050 pot (pot after the bet 1,450). I hold Q♣9♥: no pair but a gutshot to a T (4 outs) and some showdown equity vs light c‑bets. Calling 400 into 1,450 gives ~27.6% pot‑odds. A pure turn/river chance to hit the straight is ≈16.5%, but against Claude’s small c‑bet range I expect additional showdown equity (he’s likely firing Kx, Jx, diamond draws and some thin air). Raising as a bluff here is attractive only if I trust Claude folds a lot — but his range contains many K/J and diamond hands that will call or 3‑bet, and I don’t have enough fold equity or raw equity to justify inflating the pot out of position on later streets. With position and stack depth, the +EV line is to call, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the turn: if a T hits I can extract value; if a diamond/king pairs the board or he fires a big donk on the turn I can fold. Plan: call 400, see the turn, proceed based on improvement and opponent reaction.

Still have top two pair (Kings and Eights) on a Jd 8h Kd 7s board. The 7s is relatively safe - doesn't complete the flush draw. However, T9 now has a straight. Need to continue betting for value and protection against diamond draws and remaining straight draws. Betting around 70% pot to build value and charge draws.

Situation: heads-up postflop — I opened preflop, Claude called. Board J♦‑8♥‑K♦‑7♠. Claude barreled the turn for 1,000 into the pot (pot now 2,450); I must call 1,000. Hand: Q♣9♥ — no pair, but a gutshot to a Broadway straight (T) plus pair outs. Outs on the river: 4 tens + 3 queens + 3 nines = ~10 clean cards ≈ 21.7% to improve. Calling 1,000 to win ~3,450 requires ≈29% equity, so purely on raw outs this is slightly short. However Claude’s line (small flop c‑bet then a turn barrel) contains a mix of Kx/Jx, missed diamond draws and some air; I carry non‑zero showdown equity against his bluffs/A‑highs and I keep positional leverage by just calling. A raise here as a bluff is low EV — he can call with many K/J combos and I risk bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Folding is too tight given the price (1,000 ≈ 3.7% of my 26,750 stack) and the potential to extract value or fold cheaply on the river. Plan: call 1,000, see the river. If I hit Q/9/T I’ll value‑extract; if I miss and he fires a large river bet I’ll fold most of the time; if he checks I can consider a small probe bet or check behind to show down.

River King gives me a full house - Kings full of Eights (KKK88). This is a monster. Minni called flop and turn, likely holding Kx (now trips), Jx, diamond draws (missed), or T9 straight. All these hands except the unlikely 88 for quads are crushed by my full house. I need to value bet to extract from trips and straights. Betting around 65% pot for max value while still getting called by worse.

Fold. Claude’s line (call pre‑flop, small flop c‑bet, larger turn barrel and now a meaningful river bet) is weighted heavily toward value — Kx that now makes trips, Jx/8x/7x that make two pair, or other made hands — with some missed diamond bluffs mixed in. The math: calling 2,300 into the ~8,050 total pot requires ≈28.6% equity. With Q♣9♥ I have only Q‑high on this paired K board and my showdown equity versus Claude’s continuing range is well below that threshold, so a call is -EV. A bluff‑raise is too thin — it will fold only some airs and will get called or crushed by the many hands that beat me, unnecessarily risking chips. I’ll preserve my stack and fold to the river bet.

I am on the Button with pocket Sixes (6s 6d) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to deny equity to the blinds. My stack is deep (193BB), as are my opponents'. A standard raise to 2.5BB (250 chips) is consistent with the table flow and sufficient to put pressure on the SB and BB while maintaining pot control. If I hit a set on the flop, I have massive implied odds. If I face a 3-bet, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, aggression is the correct play.

K7 suited is a reasonable hand to defend from the SB against a button open. We have suited king potential with flush possibilities. Calling 200 to see a flop is standard here - hand isn't strong enough to 3-bet for value but too good to fold against a likely wide button range.

I have A♣4♦ in the big blind facing Pro's raise to 250 and Claude's call. With three players this will be a multiway pot and my A4o is easily dominated by the button's raising range (A5+, broadways, pairs). A small 3‑bet to the min (400) would have limited fold equity and likely get called, leaving me out of position postflop. Pot odds to call are favorable (150 to win ~600 ≈ 4:1) and stacks are deep, so the lowest-variance + highest-EV play is to call and see a flop. Plan: call 150 more (250 total committed). Postflop I will continue when I pair my Ace or pick up strong draws; otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to large aggression. As chip leader I can preserve chips and pick better spots later rather than 3‑bet bluff with a marginal offsuit Ace.

Flopped the nut flush draw with Kh7h on Jh Qs 4h. I have 9 outs to the nut flush. This is a great semi-bluffing spot - I can bet to potentially take down the pot now or build it when I hit. A bet of around half pot should work well here to get value/fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if I miss.

Call 400 (650 total committed). I have A♣4♦ — a made pair of 4s with a useful Ace blocker, but it’s a marginal holding in a 3‑way pot where the button opened and is likely to have many J/Q/overpair/broadway combos that dominate me. Claude’s small blind check‑call preflop and relatively small flop bet (400 into the current pot) looks like a standard c‑bet or a probe rather than a big value shove. Calling is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line here: the pot odds (~3:1) justify seeing the next card and keeping the pot manageable since I’m out of position to the raiser. Plan: call and reevaluate on the turn — fold to significant aggression (large check‑raise or pot‑size raise) because this is a vulnerable, second‑pair hand; call smaller bets or check back safe turns and only get aggressive if I improve (pair an A or pick up a strong draw). As chip leader I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise here.

Pre-flop, I raised with pocket Sixes on the Button, which is standard. The flop comes Jh Qs 4h, which is a very coordinated board hitting the blinds' calling ranges hard (Queens, Jacks, Flush draws, Straight draws). Claude leads out for 400 into two players, showing strength, and Minni calls. With two high cards on the board and multi-way action involving a bet and a call, my pocket Sixes are effectively a bluff catcher with very low equity and almost no potential to improve (only 2 outs to a set). The pot odds are not compelling enough to float against two opponents on a texture this wet. Folding is the clear discipline play here.

Turn 7d gave me bottom pair to go with my nut flush draw. I now have 9 flush outs plus potential two-pair outs. Minni called my flop bet showing some interest. With 12+ outs and a semi-bluff opportunity, I should continue betting. A bet around 2/3 pot keeps pressure on while building the pot for when I hit. If Minni has a jack or weaker queen, they're in a tough spot.

Claude is betting 950 into a 2,500 pot on the turn. I hold A♣4♦ — a made pair of 4s with a useful Ace blocker. I had 650 total committed (250 preflop + 400 on the flop), so calling 950 brings my total commitment to 1,600. Calling 950 to win ~3,450 (post-call pot) gives ~27–28% break‑even equity; vs Claude’s likely c‑bet range (Jx/Qx, some overpairs, smaller pairs and occasional semi‑bluffs) my second pair has reasonable equity and blocks some Ace‑high bluffs. Raising here is thin — it bloats the pot with a marginal hand and will often get called or 3‑bet by better hands. As the chip leader I prefer the lower‑variance, +EV line: call now, realize equity, and fold to large river aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or get a very cheap river. Therefore I will call the 950.

River 5c is a brick - my flush draw missed. I'm left with just a pair of 7s (bottom pair). Minni called both my flop and turn bets, showing real interest in this pot. They likely have at least a jack or maybe a queen. My pair of 7s has very little showdown value against their calling range. Betting here would be a pure bluff into someone who's shown willingness to call down. Better to check and potentially check-fold or get a cheap showdown if Minni checks back.

Check. I hold A♣4♦ — second pair with an Ace blocker. Claude led the flop and barreled the turn (I called both), then checked the river. That line is consistent with either a marginal made hand (Jx/Qx), a pot-control line with a stronger but vulnerable hand, or a missed draw. Very few worse hands will call a river bet here, while many better hands (J/Q/overpairs/sets) will call or continue; betting risks bloating the pot with a hand that is often behind and has little fold equity. As chip leader I prefer the lower-variance line: take the free showdown, preserve stack, and only invest more when I have clear value or a bluff with strong blockers. Therefore I check to showdown.

Premium hand - pocket Aces on the button. This is the best starting hand in poker. I need to raise for value to build the pot. Standard raise to 3x BB should work well here to get action from the blinds while building a pot.

Claude opened to 300 from the button (a standard 3x raise). I’m in the small blind with T3o suited to clubs — a very marginal speculative holding that will play poorly out of position against a button open and likely the big blind as well. Calling 250 more to see a flop often creates a multiway, out-of-position pot where my hand has poor equity and I’ll face difficult decisions on many runouts. A small 3-bet (min to 500) as a bluff is unattractive too — T3s lacks the right blockers and I’d prefer to use stronger hands to apply pressure. I’m the largest stack and can wait for a better spot to pick on them, so I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for higher-equity situations.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 4s. The Button (Claude) raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450. Pot odds are approximately 30.7%. Against a typical wide Button opening range (approx 50%), Q4o has about 38% raw equity. However, being out of position with a hand that has poor playability (weak kicker, off-suit, large gap) makes realizing this equity difficult. I will likely face bets on many flops where I miss or hit marginal pairs. Standard defensive ranges from the BB against a 3x open usually exclude the lowest off-suit Queens. Therefore, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

On the button with 3s4c. Pot = 150, 100 to call, min-raise to 200. This is a weak offsuit hand, but in position I want to leverage fold equity and the ability to play postflop. Limping is too passive (invites isolation) and folding surrenders the button. I'll open-raise to 250 (slightly above min) — a small, table-consistent size that pressures the blinds while keeping the investment small relative to my 24,100 stack. If called I'll continuation-bet favorable low/unpaired boards and use position to control the pot; if I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold and preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s Tc, a very weak off-suit hand. Minni has raised from the Button to 250. Although the price to call is relatively cheap (200 chips into a pot of 400), playing T2o out of position against a Button raiser is a losing play in the long run. The hand has poor equity and playability. There is no strategic merit in defending or 3-betting this hand. The correct decision is to fold.
74o is a very weak hand to defend from the BB against a button raise. While I'm getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), this hand plays poorly postflop and rarely makes strong holdings. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand (9h 5s). While raising from the button to steal blinds is a common strategy, 9-5 offsuit is too weak to be part of a standard opening range, even for a wide steal. It has poor playability post-flop if called. With 189 big blinds, I am comfortable and do not need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. Folding is the disciplined and correct play here.

Heads-up against the BB with Q8o after button folded. This is a decent hand to raise and apply pressure. Q8o plays reasonably well and I can take down the pot preflop or have position-neutral play against the BB. Standard raise to 250.

Claude opened to 250 from the small blind. I’m in the big blind with 6♥7♠ — a low connector but offsuit and therefore marginal. The pot is 350 and it costs me 150 to call, so I’m getting ~30% pot-equity odds. At three-handed Claude’s SB open is likely fairly wide, and with deep stacks I have reasonable implied odds to defend one street with a hand that can flop straights/paired hands. I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff: 6-7o has poor playability OOP, a small 3-bet to 400 buys little fold equity and risks creating a bigger pot out of position. Plan: call to 250 to see the flop, fold to a large 3-bet preflop, and postflop play cautiously — continue only with a pair/strong draw or vs a small continuation bet where a float makes sense, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

Flopped middle pair (8s) with Q kicker on K84 two-club board. I have a decent made hand that needs protection. Standard c-bet for value against worse pairs and to charge draws. Betting around 60% pot.

Heads-up vs Claude (SB) on Kc 8c 4s with 6h7s. I have no pair, no club, and only backdoor straight possibilities — essentially no realizable equity on this flop. Calling 300 into the ~800 pot (would make ~1100) requires ~27% equity; against a SB open/c-bet range that includes many Kx, clubs and broadways my hand’s equity is well under that. I’m out of position with poor turn/river playability and no showdown value, and there’s no reason to gift chips with a weak float. Per my preflop plan (only continue with a pair/strong draw or a clear float spot) this is not a spot to defend — fold and wait for a better situation.

3-handed on the button with Q2o. This is a marginal hand, but in a 3-handed game from the button, I should be opening fairly wide to steal blinds. Q-high has some showdown value. Standard 2.5x raise to 250 - easy fold if 3-bet.

I'm Minni (SB) with As2h. Claude (button) opened to 250; pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (250 total committed including my 50 SB). My stack is deep (23,950) so the 200 call is cheap relative to stack size. Claude has been opening a lot of 250 raises from the button in these hands, so his range is likely wide and A2o has reasonable equity vs that range. Calling gives me ~33% break-even equity (200/(400+200)) and good implied odds to see a flop; if the BB (Pro) folds I play heads-up with an Ace, if he defends I can proceed cautiously OOP. 3-betting with A2o OOP is unattractive (dominated by many Ax, and I don't want to inflate the pot out of position), so I'll defend by calling and evaluate the flop.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7s) in the Big Blind facing a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 600, and I need to call 150 more, offering me 4:1 pot odds. My hand has significant equity against typical opening and flatting ranges, making a fold incorrect. While a squeeze (3-bet) is an option to deny equity and capitalize on the dead money, playing a medium pair out of position in a bloated pot if called is difficult. Calling allows me to close the action cheaply, set-mine (trying to hit a 7), or navigate low boards. Given the deep stack depth (188 BBs) and the excellent price, calling is the most prudent, low-variance play to realize my equity.

Check. With As2h on Kc–3c–5h I only have Ace‑high (no club backdoor) plus a gutshot to a 4 and three remaining Aces — some showdown/turn equity vs Claude’s wide button-opening range, but nothing to build a big pot OOP against two opponents. I’m the chip leader and the pot is small (750) relative to stacks, so I want pot control and to let the preflop aggressor show aggression. Plan: check and call small continuation bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3–1/2 pot) to realize equity and see a cheap turn; fold to large, committed‑sized bets or raises that look like a made K/set. If both check through, re‑evaluate on the turn and bet for value or bluff only if I pick up a clear equity improvement (A or 4) or a good read on fold equity.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7s) on a flop of Kc 5h 3c. I am in the Big Blind in a multi-way pot. The Small Blind has checked to me. The board texture is somewhat wet with the club flush draw and connected low cards, but the King hits the pre-flop raiser's range well. My hand is a marginal made hand (underpair to the King, ahead of middle/bottom pair). Leading out (donk betting) here is not advisable as I likely fold out hands I beat (like A-high) and get called or raised by better hands (Kx, better pairs, flush draws). The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Claude on the Button) to see their action. I will check and look to call a small bet or re-evaluate if the action gets heavy.

I have Qc2s on a K-high flop (3c Kc 5h). Both opponents checked to me. This is a good c-bet spot - I was the preflop aggressor and can represent the King. I have the Qc giving me backdoor flush equity. A small bet around 1/3 pot should get folds from missed hands while keeping my bluff cheap.

Flop: I’m SB with As2h on 3c–Kc–5h. Claude (button) opened wide pre and now makes a small c-bet 250 into a 1000 pot (~25% pot). That bet offers ~20% break‑even equity (250/(1000+250)). My Ace‑high has decent showdown value vs a wide BTN range and I have the Ace blocker to some strong Ax combos. I’m deep and the bet is cheap — 3‑betting OOP with A2o is unattractive (often dominated) and folding here surrenders too much equity given the pot odds. Plan: call the 250 (500 total committed including my preflop 250) to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn. If I face a large turn raise or polarized shove (clear K/set value range) I’ll fold; if I improve (an A or a 4) or see a cheap showdown, I’ll continue for value. So I’ll call and see the turn.

I am getting excellent pot odds (5:1) to make this call, needing only about 16.6% equity to continue. The c-bet from the Button was small (1/3 pot), and while the Small Blind's call indicates some strength (likely a King, a decent draw, or a mid-pair), my hand (Pocket Sevens) beats all draws and some lower pairs. I am closing the action on this street, which minimizes risk. Folding a pocket pair on a board with only one overcard for such a small price is exploitable. I will call to see a turn card, hoping to hit a set or get to showdown cheaply if the board runs out safely.

I checked the flop and called Claude’s small raise to keep the pot controlled with Ace‑high (As2h). The turn (8♦) doesn’t improve my hand or remove many of Claude/Pro’s likely value combos (Kx, pairs, club draws that now missed). With two players behind me and deep stacks, leading out OOP with a marginal Ace has poor fold equity and will often get called by better hands. My plan is pot control: check and realize showdown value; call small turn bets but fold to large, polarized bets that represent a made K/set/two‑pair. If both check through I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only bet for value or bluff if I actually improve (A/2/4) or get a good read to fold out better hands.

The turn brings an 8d, making the board 3c Kc 5h 8d. This is an overcard to my pocket 7s, weakening my relative hand strength slightly. I am sandwiched between the Small Blind (who checked) and the Button (the pre-flop aggressor). Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose; better hands like Kx or 8x will call or raise, and worse hands will fold, denying me value. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a lucky 7 on the river. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size and react to the aggressor's action.

Both opponents called my flop c-bet, showing some interest in the pot. I have Qc2s - complete air with no pair, no draw (the backdoor club draw is dead). The 8d turn doesn't help me and likely doesn't hurt their ranges. Firing another barrel into two opponents who've already shown resistance with queen-high is just burning chips. Time to give up and check behind.

River: 3c Kc 5h 8d 6c. I hold As2h — only Ace‑high, no club, no pair or two‑pair; I never improved. Claude opened wide pre and c‑bet small on the flop with both of us calling, then everyone checked the turn, so ranges are wide but include many Kx, one‑pair hands, and some two‑club combos that can make a flush on this river. In a three‑way pot I have virtually no fold equity as a bluff and my Ace‑high is behind any pair or a made flush, so betting risks chips for very little expected gain. The pot (1,500) is small relative to my deep stack, so preserve chips and take showdown. Action: check and show down; fold to any large river jam if faced after my check.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7s) on a final board of 3c Kc 5h 8d 6c. The river card brings a third club, completing potential flush draws, and puts four cards to a straight (3-5-6-8 require a 4 or 7, I have a 7 but 47 makes the straight). 

The action has been passive: Claude (BTN) c-bet the flop small and checked back the turn. Minni (SB) check-called the flop and checked turn and river. 

My hand has marginal showdown value. I beat missed non-club draws, Ace-highs, and lower pairs (3x, 5x). I lose to any King, 8x, 6x, straight, or flush. 

Betting here achieves little: worse hands will fold, and better hands (Kx, flushes) will call or raise. Turning my hand into a bluff is risky in a 3-way pot where opponents have shown stickiness (calling flop). The most profitable line is to check and hope to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate if Claude bets. Given the board texture change with the flush completing, checking is the standard defensive play.

River brings the 6c completing a club flush draw. I have Qc2s - just queen high with no pair. Both opponents checked again. While I have the Qc blocker to nut flushes, bluffing into two opponents who've called the flop bet is -EV. My queen high has essentially no showdown value, but betting accomplishes nothing - they're not folding pairs to a river bet, and any made flush calls. Check behind and concede.

Q♢J♠ on the button is a strong hand to open — lots of playability and good post‑flop vs two defenders when I have position. The pot is only 150 and the cost to enter is 100, so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp and let cheaper multiway pots form. I’ll open-raise to a standard size (250) — big enough to get fold equity and define my range, small enough to keep weaker hands and some dominated hands in and to allow comfortable postflop play. Plan: if I face a small 3‑bet I can call and play postflop in position; I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet/shove depending on size. On favorable flops I’ll c‑bet to continue aggression, otherwise pivot to a check/fold or check‑raise line as needed.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 7c, facing a Button raise to 250 from Minni. Although we are 3-handed and ranges are wider, Q7 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling would require investing 200 more chips into a pot that will likely be heads-up or 3-way, where I will be at a positional disadvantage and often dominated by better Queens or Sevens. The equity realizability of this hand is low. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better spots.

I have ATo in the BB facing a button min-raise. This is a strong hand against a likely wide button opening range. I should 3-bet for value to build the pot and take control. A raise to around 750-800 is appropriate.

I opened to 250 on the button with Q♦J♠ to seize initiative; Claude’s 3‑bet to 750 is a standard size but not huge. QJo offsuit isn’t strong enough to 4‑bet for value, and it’s too good to fold vs a BB 3‑bet — with position and deep stacks I prefer to call and play post‑flop. The pot is ~1050 and I must call 500 (total commitment 750), giving me ~3:1 immediate pot odds and plenty of implied odds to realize equity against a mixed 3‑bet range. Plan: use position — proceed aggressively when I connect (pair/straight/flush draw), control size or fold on dry, hostile boards, and relinquish to a large 4‑bet shove. Calling keeps the range wide and lets me exploit post‑flop.

I flopped middle pair (tens) with top kicker on Jc Tc 6s. This is a decent spot - I'm ahead of many hands in Minni's calling range like smaller pairs, Ax suited, KQ. However, there are draws (club flush draw, straight draws) and hands that beat me (Jx, sets). As the preflop aggressor, I should c-bet for value and protection. A ~50% pot bet of around 750 is appropriate.

Flop is Jc Tc 6s; I hold Js Qd — top pair with a decent kicker. Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands (QQ+/AK/AJ/TT) but his flop stab is small (750 into a 2300 pot), which also fits draws and thin value bets. I have position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand versus a 3‑bettor’s value-heavy range. Calling keeps worse Jx/Tx and bluffs in, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me use position to control the hand. Raising (min to 1500) would mostly fold out my weaker equity and get action from hands that dominate me. Plan: call the 750, evaluate the turn — extract value if he shows weakness or improve, fold to large turn aggression that clearly represents overpairs/two‑pair/sets.

Turn is a blank 2h. I have middle pair with top kicker. After Minni called my flop c-bet, their range includes Jx hands that beat me, flush draws, straight draws, and some floats. Betting again risks getting raised off my equity or inflating the pot when I'm often behind. Checking to pot control is best - I can call a reasonable bet or check back river for showdown value.

Claude 3‑bet preflop and continued with a modest flop stab, so his range is weighted to value (TT+, AK, AQ/AJ, maybe some bluffs/draws). I have J♠Q♦ — top pair with a good kicker — and I’m in position. Claude checked the turn, which is a sign of weakness or pot control rather than strength. This is exactly the spot to extract value and deny equity to club draws and marginal hands that will call (worse Jx, some Tx, weak pairs, and club draws with two clubs). I don’t want to bloat the pot vs overpairs/sets, so I’ll use a medium-sized value bet (~½ pot) to get called by worse and fold pure air. If Claude check‑raises big, that’s usually two‑pair/sets/very strong hands and I’ll strongly consider folding; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and target thin value or pot control. Bet 1,500 into 3,050 pot.

Facing a 1500 bet (50% pot) on the turn with second pair top kicker. Minni's range includes Jx that beats me, flush draws, straight draws, and some floats. I'm getting ~3:1 odds (1500 to win 6050). Folding is too weak with this holding - I have decent showdown value against missed draws. Raising only gets called by better. Call is the best option to see a river cheaply and potentially win at showdown against draws that miss or weaker value bets.

River 8c completes the club flush draw. I have second pair top kicker (tens with ace kicker). The flush completing is bad for me - if I bet, I only get called by better hands (flushes, Jx). Minni showed aggression on the turn after calling my flop bet, suggesting strength or a draw. Now that the flush came in, checking is clearly best to see a cheap showdown. If Minni bets, I'd face a tough decision, but betting myself has no value - worse hands fold, better hands call/raise.

I have top pair (J) with a Queen kicker on Jc Tc 6s 2h 8c. Claude 3‑bet preflop and led small on the flop, then checked the turn and called my 1,500 raise — that line points to medium/strong one‑pair hands, some pocket pairs, and club draws that were chasing. The river brings a third club, which completes any two‑club draws that called the turn (those now beat me), but Claude’s river check looks more like pot control or a missed/medium holding than absolute strength. I want to extract thin value from worse Jx/Tx and other single‑pair hands while avoiding bloating the pot against sets/overpairs/flushes. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/3 pot) to get called by worse and fold pure air; if Claude check‑raises the river I’ll assume the stronger hand (set/flush/overpair) and fold.

River 8c completes the club flush. Minni bets 2000 into 8050 (~25% pot). I have second pair top kicker. Getting about 5:1 odds (2000 to win 10050), I need to be good ~17% of the time. Minni's line (call 3-bet, call flop, bet turn, bet river) is polarized. However: 1) I hold the As which blocks Axcc flush combos, 2) There are busted straight draws (Q9, K9) that might bluff, 3) The small sizing could be thin value or a blocker bet. With these pot odds and blockers, folding second pair top kicker would be too exploitable. Call.

I am on the Button with 3d 2s, which is effectively the worst starting hand in poker. Despite having position, the hand has negligible equity and playability. With 17 hands left and a comfortable stack of ~200bb, there is no need to attempt a loose steal, especially with the chip leader in the Big Blind who can easily defend or 3-bet. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

62o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even though I only need 50 to complete, I'm out of position against Minni who has a much larger stack. This hand has terrible playability and equity. With 17 hands left and a decent stack, no reason to get involved with trash.

I have 6d9d on the button with only 3 players remaining. Suited connectors play well in position, and as the button I should be opening wide against just the blinds. Standard raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play in position postflop.

Claude opened to 250 from the button and I’m in the small blind with K♦6♥. K6o is a weak, easily-dominated hand and I’ll be out of position to both opponents postflop. Calling 200 into a 400 pot (~33% pot odds) doesn’t compensate for my poor equity vs a typical button opening; a tiny re-raise to 400 has little fold equity and a larger 3-bet as a bluff is too spewy with this holding. I’ll preserve my deep stack for better spots and fold the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jh 5c. The Button (Claude) raised to 2.5BB (250) and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 to win a pot of 400, offering me pot odds of roughly 27%. My hand (J5 offsuit) has raw equity of about 35% against a wide button opening range. However, J5o suffers significantly from poor playability and reverse implied odds (hitting a Jack often leaves me dominated), especially when playing out of position against an active opponent like Claude who has shown capability to barrel post-flop. Realizing my equity will be difficult. While the price is tempting, folding is the disciplined and standard play to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

In 3‑handed play the button has a wide opening range — K♥8♥ is a very playable, positionally strong hand (suited king + blocker to AK/AQ). Pot is only 150 and I’m deep (28.5k), so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds. I’ll open‑raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to generate fold equity and discourage marginal calls, but small relative to effective stacks so I can continue postflop in position. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops, apply pressure, and fold to large 3‑bets when out of position against likely tighter ranges.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o). Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300 (3x BB). This is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot against a likely stronger range, and 3-betting is too loose with this holding. Given the stack depths and the late stage of the tournament (Hand 135/150), preserving chips by folding is the prudent decision.

Q5o in BB facing a button min-raise. Getting ~2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a borderline defend - Q5o is marginal but playable against a button open at these odds. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but can see a flop in position vs the min-raise sizing. Calling.

Missed the flop completely with Q5o on Jh Kd 4s. No pair, no draws, just queen high. This board heavily favors the preflop aggressor's range (Kx, Jx, broadway). I should check and likely give up if Minni bets. No reason to bluff into a board that hits their range well.

I have K♥8♥ on Jh Kd 4s — top pair (king) with a decent kicker and a backdoor heart. I raised preflop and am in position against a wide BB calling range (Kx, Qx, Jx, Ax, small pairs, suited connectors). This is a clear spot to c‑bet for value and protection: extract value from worse kings, Jx and medium pairs while denying free cards and charging the few draws. I’ll size around 2/3 pot to get value without overcommitting (keeps weaker calls and misclick bluffs in play). If I get a clear call I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue for value on safe cards, pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression on very strong action).

Completely whiffed this flop with Q5o. No pair, no draws, just queen high on a K-J-4 board that smashes Minni's button raising range. Getting 2.6:1 but have almost no equity - would need to catch runner-runner to improve. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). In a 3-handed game, while Button ranges are wide, J5o is near the bottom of the range and is typically a fold. It has poor playability and is easily dominated. With 14 hands remaining and a comfortable stack, there is no need to force a steal with such a weak hand, especially against active opponents in the blinds. The standard play is to fold.

K2o is a weak hand but heads up against just the BB after Pro folded, I should complete. Raising with K2o is too aggressive and plays poorly against 3-bets. Completing for 50 more to see a flop is the most efficient play - I can hit a king or fold cheaply if I miss.

I'm in the big blind with 5h7d and Pro (button) folded; Claude simply limped (called the small blind). This is effectively a heads-up, limp-pot against a very wide range. 5-7 offsuit is a weak, unconnected hand — not worth building the pot with by raising. I have a big stack (28,800) and will have position postflop (Claude acts first), so the prudent line is to check and see a flop cheaply. If Claude checks I can take a free card or lead on favorable textures; if he bets large I can fold and preserve chips. Raising to 200 here would be unnecessary and often counterproductive with this holding.

I completely missed this flop with K2o - no pair, no draw. The 7-3-6 board is low and connected, potentially hitting Minni's BB range (45, 54, 87, suited connectors). With just K-high I have minimal showdown value. A small stab could work but this board texture favors the BB's range. Checking is prudent - I can fold to aggression or potentially win at showdown if it checks through.

I have top pair (7s) but a very weak 5-kicker (5h7d) on 7c‑3d‑6s. Claude limped preflop and checked the flop — a wide, often passive range that includes many overcards, small pairs and weak connectors. The pot is tiny (200) and both stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that can be dominated by A7/K7 or other 7x. Checking exercises pot control, lets me realize equity (I have a lone gutshot to a 4 and backdoor heart possibilities), and keeps options open: call small bets, fold to heavy aggression, or take a stab on a safe turn card. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn brings Qs, adding a second spade. I still have just K-high with no pair or draw. Minni checked behind on flop, suggesting weakness, but the Queen could have given them a pair. With K2o my showdown value is marginal but not zero. Betting as a bluff risks getting called by any pair (Q, 7, 6, 3) or draws. Checking keeps pot small and I can fold to a bet or potentially win at showdown with K-high if it checks through to river.

I have 7♦5♥ on 7♣-3♦-6♠-Q♠: top pair with a very weak kicker. This is effectively heads‑up vs Claude who limped preflop and has checked the flop and turn — a wide, often passive range (small pairs, undercards, weak Aces, connectors). The pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks, and my hand is vulnerable to Qx, 6x, or any small pair that connects. Betting here risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand and will get called by better hands or shove-worthy hands. I have only a 4 for a straight draw and no strong redraws. Pot control is the correct line: check to keep the pot small, realize equity, and induce river bluffs. Plan for the river: call small/medium bluffs, fold to heavy value aggression, or take a cheap showdown if checked to. Action: check.

River 4c completes a potential 3-4-5-6-7 straight for anyone with a 5. I have K-high which has some showdown value. Minni has checked every street, indicating weakness. Betting as a bluff is pointless since they'll fold worse and call with better. Checking makes sense - my K-high might actually win against missed draws or worse high cards.

The river 4c gives me a 3-4-5-6-7 straight with 5♥7♦ — a very strong, mostly-unbeatable hand on this board. Claude limped preflop and played passively (checked flop and turn), so his range is wide: small pairs, Qx/Ax, connectors and some 5x. Very few hands can beat my straight (only another 5 makes the same straight — ties), so this is a clear value-bet spot. The pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks, so I’ll bet to extract calls from Qx, small pairs and weaker 7x while not overbloating the pot. I size to ~75% pot (150) to get value but keep worse hands and curious callers in. If he jams or raises big, I’ll re-evaluate (it’s likely a chop or an unlikely full house); but against his passive line he should call a bet like this with many worse hands.

Minni checked every street then makes a small 150 into 200 river bet. This pattern typically indicates thin value - they likely have at least a weak pair (Q, 7, 6, 3, or 4). I only have K-high with no pair. Pot odds are ~30% needed to call, but my K-high loses to any pair. Minni wouldn't typically bluff here after showing weakness all hand. Folding is correct.
Button with Q7o in 3-handed play. This is a standard open from the button - Q7o is playable with position. I'm the short stack at 11,300 but still have 113 BBs. Standard raise to 250.

3♣6♣ is a very weak, low suited connector — playable in position or as a limp/call occasionally, but not as a defense OOP facing an unopened raise. Claude has raised to 250 from the button and the big blind still has a decision, so I’m likely to face a multiway pot or aggressive postflop action out of position. Calling 200 for questionable equity (200 into a 400 pot — ~33% break‑even) isn’t attractive given hand strength and table dynamics; a raise to isolate is speculative and unnecessarily bloats the pot with a poor hand. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position or with stronger holdings, preserving my deep stack to exploit later.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s4s facing a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Claude). The Small Blind has folded. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 400, requiring ~27% equity). 94s, while weak, is a suited connector variant that realizes equity well enough to defend against a wide button opening range, especially given our deep effective stacks (over 100 BBs). Calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I have flopped middle pair with a weak kicker on a J-9-6 board. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Claude on the Button), the standard play is to check. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable, and I want to keep the pot manageable. Donk betting would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws. I will check and evaluate Claude's action.

I have Q-high on a J96 board with no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Pro checked from BB. This board connects well with BB's calling range (Jx, 9x, suited connectors). With only queen high and no real equity, a c-bet will often get called by better hands. Checking back for pot control and a free card is the best play here.

On the turn, the board is [6s 9d Jc Qd]. The Queen is an overcard to my pair of Nines, downgrading my hand to third pair. However, Claude checked back the flop, which often indicates a marginal hand or a miss, though the Queen hits a portion of his floating/checking range (like KQ, AQ, QTs). I still have some showdown value against Ace-highs or lower pairs (88, 77). Leading out here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and might isolate me against better ones (Queens, Jacks, or straights). Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and try to reach a cheap showdown. I will check and re-evaluate if Claude decides to bet.

I turned top pair with Q7o on a 6-9-J-Q board. Pro checked twice showing weakness. My queen is likely ahead of their range (underpairs, Jx, 9x, draws). I should bet for value - around 60-70% pot to extract from worse hands while not over-committing with a mediocre kicker.

I hold a pair of Nines (9s4s) on a board of [6s 9d Jc Qd]. The turn Queen is an overcard that downgrades my hand to third pair and connects well with the Button's range (KQ, QJ, AQ, QT) and straight draws (T8, KT). Claude checked back the flop but fired a ~68% pot bet on the turn. While he could be stabbing at the scare card, his range includes many value hands that beat me (Top pair, Second pair, Straights). Out of position with a weak kicker and no significant draw (only 3 spades), my hand is too vulnerable to call a substantial bet. I have better hands in my range to defend with (Jx, Qx, Two Pair). Conserving chips is the prudent play here.

I’m on the button with K♥4♥ — a suited king with decent playability in position. With three players and very deep stacks (I have ~289 BB, SB ~194 BB, BB ~115 BB) this is a hand I want to take the initiative with rather than limp. A standard 3x open (300 total) wins the blinds often, avoids giving opponents cheap odds to see flops, and lets me play postflop last with a suited broadway-type hand. I’ll size to 300 to balance fold equity and pot control; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll usually fold this holding, but versus a single caller I’ll use position to c-bet favorable boards and control the pot on dry textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 6d facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Minni). Suited connectors like 56s are excellent speculative hands to defend with, especially given the deep stack depths (over 100bb effective). The price to call is 250 to win a pot that will be at least 700, offering reasonable direct odds plus significant implied odds if I hit a straight or flush draw. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the low cost relative to my stack (1.3%) makes calling a standard and profitable play compared to folding. 3-betting is an alternative but risks bloating the pot out of position against the chip leader. I will call and evaluate the flop.

Pocket 9s is a premium hand 3-handed. Minni opened to 300 from button, Pro called from SB. I have a strong hand that plays well in a raised pot. I should 3-bet for value to build the pot and potentially isolate. Raising to about 1000 is a good size - roughly 3x the open with a caller.

Claude’s 3‑bet to 1,000 is a big, polarizing sizing, but the extra 700 to see a flop is trivial relative to my 28,650 stack (~2.4%). K♥4♥ is not a 4‑bet candidate — it has some playability and a valuable K blocker in position, plus flush/backdoor equity — so a call to realize equity and use position is the best low‑variance line. Folding would be too tight for the price; a 4‑bet bluff is reckless with this holding and risks isolating me against a stronger range. Postflop plan: if I miss a dry board I’ll check and give up to heavy pressure; if I hit a king or a heart draw I’ll proceed selectively and use position to control pot size. Call 700 more (1000 total).

I am facing a squeeze to 1000 from the Big Blind after the Button called. I have already committed 300 chips. The pot is currently 2300 (1000 from BB + 1000 from BTN + 300 from me), and I need to call 700 more. This gives me direct pot odds of 700/3000, or roughly 23%. My hand (5d 6d) plays well in multi-way pots and has roughly 30-35% equity against typical squeeze and calling ranges. Although I am out of position, the price is attractive enough to peel and look for a strong flop (straights, flushes, or trips). Folding suited connectors getting nearly 4.3:1 implied odds is too tight. I call.

On a Flop of Qs Kd 3h, I have missed completely with 5d 6d, holding only Six-high and a backdoor flush draw. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Claude in the Big Blind) and the original raiser (Minni on the Button), as both their ranges are dense with broadway cards like AK, KQ, and AQ. As the first to act in a multi-way 3-bet pot, leading out would be suicidal given my lack of equity and the likelihood that one of my opponents has connected strongly. My plan is to check and fold to any aggression.

I have 99 on a Qs Kd 3h board. Both opponents called my 3-bet preflop. This board has two overcards to my pair, and against two opponents who called a 3-bet, there's a significant chance one of them connected with K or Q. Pro has checked. With this texture and multiway pot, I should check for pot control - my hand still has showdown value but betting into two players on this board is risky. If I bet and get called or raised, I'll be in a tough spot.

Flop Q♠ K♦ 3♥ — I have K♥4♥: top pair but a very weak kicker and only a backdoor heart. Both opponents checked to me; that shows some weakness or pot‑control but also gives me a chance to seize the initiative. Claude’s preflop 3‑bet polarizes his range (AK/AQ/pp/bluffs) and Pro’s cold‑call range is likely wider, so there are plenty of hands I can get value from (Qx, medium pairs, some draws) but also many hands that beat me (AK, Kx with better kickers, overpairs). With three ways to the flop I prefer a small c‑bet to extract thin value, deny free cards, and keep the pot manageable given my poor kicker. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (1,000). Plan: fold to a large check‑raise (I’m likely dominated), call small resistance cautiously, and if called proceed conservatively on the turn — pot‑control on scary cards, continue for thin value on safe cards and re‑assess vs heavy action.

I am holding 5d 6d on a flop of Qs Kd 3h. I missed the flop completely, having no pair, no direct straight draw, and only a backdoor flush draw. The pot is multi-way (3 players). After I checked and the Big Blind checked, the Button (Minni) bet 1000 into a pot of 3000. This bet signifies strength on a board that hits perceived ranges (Broadway cards) very hard. I have virtually no equity and no reason to continue. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Minni bet 1000 into a 3000 pot after both Pro and I checked. On this KQ3 board with two overcards to my 99, Minni's bet after our checks suggests strength - they likely have a K, Q, or better. Their preflop calling range for a 3-bet includes many hands that connect here (KQ, KJ, AK, AQ, QJ, etc.). Getting 4:1 pot odds, but my equity against their value-heavy betting range is poor. I'm essentially drawing to two outs if behind. Folding is the disciplined play here rather than paying off with an underpair.

I am on the Button with Pocket Sevens (7d 7h) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand that is well ahead of random hands from the blinds. I should open-raise for value and to deny equity to random holdings in the blinds. A standard raise size of 2.5bb (250 chips) has been consistent with the table flow and my previous actions. This size balances the risk/reward, allowing me to steal blinds or play a pot in position with a decent hand. Limping is passive and allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free, while folding is too tight.

32 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a button raise, I have no business calling with this garbage. Even with the small blind already invested, defending this hand would be lighting money on fire. Easy fold.

Holding Tc-2d — a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with poor playability. Pro opened to 250 from the button (SB folded), so I must call 150 into a 400 pot (≈27% pot odds). Versus a BTN opening range this hand is frequently dominated and has low equity and few good turn/river plans out of position. 3-betting as a bluff is a bad look here (no meaningful blockers and I’d be OOP with a poor hand). With a big stack and many hands left, it’s better to preserve chips and wait for a playable spot. Fold.

On the button with T5o, 10 hands left in tournament as the short stack (10,550 vs 30,800 and 18,500). This is a marginal hand but I have position and only need to get through 2 players. Standard button steal attempt with a 2.5x raise makes sense to try picking up blinds.

Claude opened to 250 on the button and I’m in the small blind with J♣6♣. In 3-handed play the button’s opening range is wide, and J6s has decent postflop playability (backdoor clubs and straight redraws) so defending is reasonable. A tiny 3-bet to 400 as a bluff is thin here — J6s doesn’t block enough of the raiser’s strong hands and a small 3-bet will either get called cheaply or force us into awkward OOP spots against two players. Calling 200 more (250 total committed preflop) keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity on flops that hit my suit or J-high, and avoids overcommitting with a marginal hand. Plan: see the flop, continue with clubs/J-high/backdoor equity, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards or a large raise unless I have clear equity.

I am in the Big Blind facing a Button raise and a Small Blind call. The pot is 600 chips (250 from Claude + 250 from Minni + 100 my BB). It costs me 150 more to call, giving me excellent pot odds of 5:1 (20% equity required). My hand, 6d 4d (suited one-gapper), plays well in multi-way pots deep-stacked due to its potential to make straights and flushes (implied odds). Closing the action allows me to realize equity cheaply without the risk of reopening the betting. Folding would be too tight given the price, and raising is unnecessary with a speculative hand out of position. I will call to see the flop.

I called preflop with J♣6♣ to see a flop, but the 9♦-4♥-9♥ board completely missed me: no pair, no heart, only a tiny runner‑runner club backdoor. I'm out of position to two opponents (button opened wide and BB called) so a c‑bet OOP as a bluff is thin — many hands in their ranges (9x, 4x, overpairs, sets) already dominate me. Pot control is the priority: check and see a turn card. If I face a small bet I can consider a call to realize equity vs a wide c‑bet, but I'll fold to heavy pressure since my showdown equity is very low.

I have bottom pair (Fours) on a paired board of 9d-4h-9h in a 3-way pot. Being out of position relative to the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and having a marginal made hand, leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as it likely only gets called by better hands or isolates me against strong ranges. Checking allows me to see what the pre-flop aggressor does. If it checks through, I'm happy to see a free turn. If there is a bet, I can evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the standard and most prudent play to control the pot size.

Flop is 9d-4h-9h with T5 and two hearts giving me a backdoor flush draw. Both opponents checked. This paired board likely missed both callers. Standard c-bet spot to take down the pot - betting small (~1/3 pot) to apply pressure while keeping risk minimal. If raised, I can fold easily.

Board is 9♦-4♥-9♥; I hold J♣6♣ — I missed the flop (no pair, only a very remote backdoor club). Claude (BTN) opened wide preflop and now fires a small c-bet (275 into ~750, ~35% of the pot). Calling 275 into the 1025 pot requires ~21% equity to break even. Versus a wide button c-bet my hand has borderline but acceptable equity at this price, and the call is cheap relative to my 30.6k stack. Raising OOP as a bluff is a poor line (little fold equity, I block few value hands); folding surrenders too much potential equity. Plan: call to keep the pot small and see the turn, fold to significant aggression (check-raise/raise) from either opponent, and only become aggressive if I pair J, hit clubs, or see clear weakness.

I hold bottom pair (Fours) with a backdoor flush draw on a paired board (9-4-9). The initial raiser (Claude) bet small (approx 1/3 pot), and the Small Blind (Minni) called. I am getting excellent pot odds of roughly 5.7:1 (requiring only ~15-17% equity). While being sandwiched in a multi-way pot is not ideal, my hand is too strong to fold for this price given I beat bluffs and flush draws, and the 9s on board reduce the combinations of value hands opponents might have. I will call to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

Check. The turn (2♦) is a blank for J♣6♣ — I still have no pair and there are no clubs on the board (so a club flush was never viable after that flop), only ~6 outs (~12%) to pair on the river. Claude opened wide and barreled the flop; both opponents called, so their continuing ranges contain many hands that already beat me (9x, pocket pairs, some 4x, occasional 2x). I’m out of position to two players and pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to any meaningful turn aggression. I’ll only call a very tiny probe that gives correct pot odds (e.g. under ~300 into the 1,575 pot); otherwise I fold and re-evaluate on the river if both check.

On the turn, the board is 9d-4h-9h-2d. I have picked up a flush draw to go with my bottom pair (Fours). The pot is 1,575. The Small Blind has checked to me. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who bet the flop, and having a hand that has good equity (pair + flush draw) but is not strong enough to bet for value, checking is the correct standard play. I want to see a free river if possible or evaluate the bet sizing if Claude bets again. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or force me into a tough spot if raised.

Turn 2d is a blank. Both opponents checked again, showing weakness. However, I have T-high with no real equity now - my heart backdoor didn't materialize. Both called my flop bet and could be trapping or floating. With only T-high against two opponents, another barrel is risky. I'll take a free card and potentially bluff or show down on river depending on action.

Check. I have J♣6♣ and completely missed: no pair on the flop/turn and the river Q♣ is only a single club (no flush). Betting OOP as a bluff into two opponents who have shown willingness to continue is a very thin line — I have almost no fold equity and I’m likely up against hands that already beat me (9x, any pair, Qx) or will call a small probe. Pot control and a cheap showdown is the correct line: check and fold to value-sized bets on the river; only consider calling a very small river bet if the price is attractive, but with no bet currently I check.

I hold [6d, 4d] on a board of [9d, 4h, 9h, 2d, Qc]. I have Two Pair (Nines and Fours) with a Queen kicker. The action went: I called a small c-bet on the flop, and the turn checked through. The river Queen is an overcard to my pair of 4s and likely connects with the pre-flop raiser's (Claude's) range or the small blind's broadway hands. 

In this multi-way pot, my hand has some showdown value against missed flush draws (hearts from flop, diamonds from turn) and Ace-highs.
