However, betting would likely only be called by better hands (Queens, Nines, pocket pairs > 44) and would fold out the hands I beat. Therefore, betting serves no purpose. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold given the board texture and multi-way action, but checking gives me the best chance to win the pot against missed draws without risking more chips.

River Qc completes the board 9d-4h-9h-2d-Qc. I have T-high with no showdown value. Both opponents checked again. While I could bluff representing the Queen or a 9, bluffing into two opponents who've called my flop bet is risky. Being the short stack (10k vs 30k and 18k) with only 10 hands left, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than risk a multi-way bluff. Checking back and conceding this small pot is the prudent play.

On the button with 2♠4♦ in a 3‑handed spot I have a very weak hand but maximum positional advantage and a large stack. Standard exploitative play is to open-raise to pick up the blinds rather than limp/call and play a difficult postflop spot multiway. I’ll raise to a normal open size (250) to apply pressure and keep the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet (this hand has poor playability), if called use position to c‑bet favorable flops and give up vs heavy resistance. The raise is about stealing dead money and leveraging position, not about playing the hand to showdown.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit facing a button raise from Minni. This is a standard defense situation. My hand is too strong to fold against a wide button opening range, especially given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400+, getting roughly 2:1 immediate odds before BB acts). 3-betting is an option but risky out of position against a deep-stacked chip leader who likely won't fold easily. Calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to realize equity with a decent broadway hand, even though I will be out of position post-flop.

2c8d is a very weak hand. Even with 4:1 pot odds closing the action, this hand has poor playability post-flop. I'm the short stack at the table with 9 hands remaining, so I need to preserve chips for better spots. Folding marginal holdings like 28o is correct here.

I have flopped Top Pair with a good kicker (Queen-Jack) on a board of [Ts 6d Qh]. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni on the Button), the standard and most effective line is to check. This allows Minni to make a continuation bet with her wide button range, which could include many draws (like straight draws with K9, J9, AK) or worse made hands (like Tx or pocket pairs). Donk betting here would likely only get action from hands that beat me or strong draws, while folding out her bluffs. Checking keeps her range wide and allows me to evaluate her action. I intend to call if she bets.

I opened preflop to steal from the button and planned to c-bet dry, favorable flops. Ts-6d-Qh is a relatively dry, high-card flop and Pro checked to me — perfect spot to continue as the preflop aggressor. I have no pair and almost no improvement potential, so this is a pure continuation bet to pick up the pot or fold out weaker holdings. I’ll bet ~50% pot (300) — big enough to get folding equity against Pro’s wide calling range but small relative to my deep stack. If I get significant resistance (a raise) I’ll fold; if called I’ll proceed very cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and opponent action.

I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (Queen-Jack) on a Q-T-6 rainbow board. Facing a standard half-pot continuation bet from the Button (Minni) after checking to the aggressor. My hand is well ahead of Minni's wide button opening range, which includes many worse pairs (Tx, lower pocket pairs) and straight draws (KJ, K9, J9). Folding is incorrect given my hand strength. Raising might isolate me against better hands (AQ, KQ, QT, Sets) while folding out her bluffs. Calling is the best option to keep her range wide, control the pot size out of position, and allow her to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on later streets.

I hold Top Pair (Queens), an Open-Ended Straight Draw (needs a King or Eight), and a Queen-high Flush Draw on a board of [Ts 6d Qh 9d]. The Turn card (9d) is dynamic, completing straights for hands like KJ or 87, and bringing a flush draw. Despite the wet board, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) remains the best line. It protects my checking range, avoids getting raised off my equity if I were to donk bet, and allows me to call a bet comfortably given my massive number of outs and current showdown value. If Minni checks back, I get a free card to realize my equity.

I have nothing — 2♠4♦ with no pair and no realistic draw on Ts 6♦ Qh 9♦. I c-bet flop and was called, so Pro’s range contains many hands with showdown value (Qx, Tx, 9x, middle pairs, or draws). The turn 9♦ doesn’t help my hand and actually makes some opponent holdings (9x, diamond draws) better. A second-barrel bluff here has poor fold equity and risks a call or raise from hands that beat me. With deep stacks I should control the pot and avoid getting bloated or facing a raise with air. Check back, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river (look for a blank to bluff or fold to aggression).

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of [Ts 6d Qh 9d 7c]. The River card (7c) is very coordinated, meaning any 8 makes a straight (6-7-8-9-T). Given the pre-flop action and Minni checking back the Turn, her range is capped but certainly contains many 8x combinations (like T8, 98, 87, J8) that could have checked the turn for pot control or to realize equity. Betting here is thin because worse hands (like Tx or 9x) will likely fold given the scary board, while straights and two-pairs will call or raise. Checking is the best option to control the pot size and try to get to showdown cheaply, potentially catching a bluff if Minni decides to bet missed diamond draws.

I have nothing (2♠4♦) and missed all realistic draws on this board. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop (300) and was called — that line is consistent with Pro having Qx, Tx, 9x, or a medium pair/overcard that wants showdown. Both of us checked the turn; the 7♣ on the river is a blank for my range but completes a straight for any hand containing an 8. With the pot at 1,200 and deep stacks, a river bluff has poor fold equity (Pro can profitably call with many Qx/TX/9x holdings) and creates unnecessary variance. Betting risks getting called or raised by hands that already beat me; checking preserves chips and allows me to fold to aggression. I’ll check back and concede the pot rather than turning air into a bluff in a marginal spot.

I am on the Button with 9s 6h, a statistically weak hand (trash). With 20,300 chips, I am in a comfortable second place with only 8 hands remaining after this. There is no strategic incentive to try to steal the blinds with such a poor hand against the chip leader (Minni) in the Big Blind and a shorter stack (Claude) in the Small Blind who might reshove. Preserving my stack is prioritized over a low-probability steal attempt.

Heads up in the blinds with T8o after Button folded. This is a decent hand to raise against the big blind - has straight potential and playability. With 3 players remaining and 9 hands left, I should be active in these blind vs blind situations. Raising to 250 applies pressure.

Heads-up against the small blind's 250 open, K♥8♠ is a playable hand but it's marginal and often dominated by the SB's raising range. I'm out of position postflop, and a min 3-bet to 400 would have limited fold equity and doesn't solve the OOP problem — a larger 3-bet would be over-committing with a medium hand. The pot (350) gives me ~30% break-even equity to call 150 more, so calling is the lowest-variance, +EV play: I keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and decide based on texture. With 8 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack, I prefer a disciplined call and careful postflop play rather than unnecessary risk preflop.

Flop is Ac 6s Qh - completely missed. I have T-high with no real draws (maybe backdoor straight with J9). This board heavily favors the BB's calling range - they'll have many Ax hands and Qx hands. C-betting into this texture with air is burning chips. Better to check and give up if faced with any aggression.

Board is A♣ 6♠ Q♥; I hold K♥8♠. Claude opened from the SB so his range is polarized toward A‑x, Q‑x, and broadways — this flop hits his range much harder than mine. I have no pair and my K is often dominated by Ax and KQ/KJ combinations; my lone heart is only a weak backdoor. Claude checked the flop, which could be either a control/check‑back or a trap, but betting here has low fold equity and will frequently get called by better hands. Pot control is the priority: stacks are deep relative to the pot and I don’t want to create a spot where I’m OOP and committed with a marginal hand. I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the turn — call small bets if they appear reasonable, fold to large aggression unless I improve, and consider a delayed bluff only on favorable turn runouts (hearts or low cards that miss his range).

Turn 4c changes nothing for me - still have T-high with no real draws. Minni checked behind on the flop which could be weakness or a trap with a medium hand. I have no equity to continue betting as a bluff here. Check and see a free river, potentially fold to any aggression.

I check. I have K♥8♠ on A♣6♠Q♥4♣ with no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (a flush is impossible from here and I only have three kings to pair on the river). Claude opened preflop and has a range heavy in A‑x/Q‑x and broadways; his check on both flop and turn looks like pot control or a missed/medium holding rather than an easy fold target. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and has poor fold equity — he will call with many better hands and I’d be committed with very little equity. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, pot control is the lowest-variance line: check and re-evaluate on the river (call small bets if price is right, fold to large aggression unless I improve, and only look to extract/raise if I pair the K on the river).

River 2d completes a very dry board: Ac 6s Qh 4c 2d. I have T-high - zero showdown value. Both of us checked flop and turn, which typically indicates weak hands. However, Minni's passive line usually means a marginal made hand (small pair, weak Qx, weak Ax) that will call a bet. Bluffing into a passive opponent who has checked twice is usually -EV as they're likely to call with any pair. The pot is only 500 and bluffing would require meaningful chips at risk. Better to accept the loss and check - we might even chop occasionally against K-high type hands.

I have K♥8♠ on A♣6♠Q♥4♣2♦ — no pair and essentially no showdown-improving outs. Claude opened preflop and checked flop/turn/river, a line consistent with A‑x/Q‑x or medium broadway holdings that dominate my king. Betting here has very poor fold equity and will mainly get called by better hands; there’s no realistic thin value to extract. With a healthy stack late in the tournament I prioritize pot control and low variance — check to take it to showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

3-handed on the button with K8o. This is a standard open from the button in a short-handed game. I'm the short stack but still have about 97 BBs. K8o plays well heads-up and I should be opening wide from the button. Standard raise to 2.5x.

5h2h is one of the weakest hands I can have — completely dominated by a standard button open and very poor multi-way and out-of-position. Calling 200 more to see a flop is speculative with almost no realized equity; a light 3-bet bluff is possible but risky here with Pro in the big blind and Claude capable of calling or 4-betting. I’m deep-stacked and can wait for much better spots, so I fold and preserve stack equity for higher EV opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 6h, facing a standard 2.5x raise from Claude on the Button. The Small Blind has folded. The pot odds are approximately 27% (calling 150 into a total pot of 550). While 65o has some connectivity, it is generally too weak to defend profitably against a 2.5x open, especially out of position. Reviewing my recent history, I have been folding unsuited disconnected or low connected hands (like 96o, 95o) in similar spots. Conserving chips is prudent here as I am comfortably in second place with only 7 hands remaining.

9♣4♦ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability post‑flop. Facing 100 to call into a 150 pot and a min‑raise to 200 available, this is not a spot to gamble out of position — a small open here is likely to be 3‑bet or isolated by the shorter big blind, and I don’t want to call or fold equity with such a dominated holding. With a comfortable large stack (29,975) and plenty of hands left, preserve chips and wait for a better spot to apply aggression. Fold.

Current situation: Blind vs Blind, 3-handed, no antes. I am in the Small Blind with 9d8h, an off-suit connector. Minni (Button) has folded. The pot is 150. My stack is very deep (~200BB), and Claude in the BB has ~97BB. 

Strategic analysis:
1.  **Pot Odds & Structure**: Without an ante, the pot is small (1.5BB). The immediate reward for stealing via a raise is lower, and the risk/reward ratio for a steal is worse compared to an ante game. Pot odds to call are 3:1 (50 to win 150), which is attractive for a connected hand like 98o.
2.  **Hand Strength**: 98o has reasonable equity (~47% vs random) and makes straights, but it plays poorly in large pots out of position due to lack of high card value and flush potential compared to suited variants. 
3.  **Position**: Playing OOP is a disadvantage. Raising builds a larger pot where this disadvantage is magnified.
4.  **Opponent Tendencies**: Claude has shown he is capable of defending his BB. Raising might provoke a call (leaving me OOP in a larger pot) or a 3-bet (forcing a fold).

Decision: Completing the blind (limping) is the most balanced play here. It allows me to realize equity cheaply and keeps the pot manageable. I can comfortably check-fold if I miss the flop or call small bets if I connect. Raising is too loose for this specific hand without antes, and folding is slightly too tight given the good price to see a flop.

In BB with T4c. Pro limped from SB, giving me a free option. T4 suited is a weak hand - no reason to build a pot with it. I'll check and see a free flop where I could potentially hit a flush draw or trips.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 8-9 on a 6-7-2 board. In a limped blind-vs-blind pot, checking out of position is the standard play. This allows me to see a free turn if the opponent checks back, or evaluate their bet size if they decide to stab at the pot. Leading out (donk betting) is an option but checking conceals my hand strength and keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to potentially bluff. Given the small pot size and my strong draw, I am comfortable playing passively on the flop to realize my equity.

Flopped nothing - just T-high with a backdoor flush draw. Pro limped and checked, showing weakness, but my hand has minimal equity. With only 200 in the pot and very little to work with, I'll check behind and see a free turn. If I hit a club I can reassess.

Turn card is the 9s, making the board 2d-7h-6c-9s. I have improved significantly to top pair (Nines) with an Eight kicker, while retaining my open-ended straight draw (needs 5 or 10). In a limped blind-vs-blind pot where the opponent checked back the flop, my hand is likely the best. I want to bet to extract value from lower pairs (7x, 6x), pocket pairs, and draws, as well as to deny equity to random overcards that might have checked back the flop. Checking risks giving a free card to a hand that has 6 outs against me or losing value from a passive opponent who would call a bet but not bet themselves. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is appropriate.

I have T-high with only a gutshot straight draw (need an 8 for T-9-8-7-6). That's about 8% equity. Pro's small bet of 125 into 200 gives me 2.6:1 odds, needing ~28% equity. My 8% is nowhere close. With 6 hands left and a solid stack, no reason to chase here. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with 4s 2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having the best position, the hand has extremely low playability and equity. We are deep in the tournament (Hand 145/150) with a healthy stack of 20,300 (203 BB). There is no strategic incentive to attempt a steal with such poor holdings, especially against the chip leader Minni in the Big Blind who can easily defend. The standard and correct play is to fold.
QJs is a strong suited broadway hand. Pro folded, so it's heads up with Minni in the BB. I should raise here for value and to take initiative. Standard raise to 250.

I’m in the big blind with A♣9♥ and Claude (SB) opened to 250 — a standard 2.5x-ish raise. The pot is 350 and I only have to call 150, which gives acceptable pot odds to defend with a hand that plays well heads-up. A9o is not strong enough to 3‑bet for value (I’ll often be dominated by Ax and have to play OOP postflop), and I don’t want to inflate the pot unnecessarily when I’m the table’s biggest stack with 5 hands left. I’ll call to 250 (add 150) to see a flop, preserve stack flexibility, and proceed based on texture and Claude’s postflop actions.

I flopped top two pair (QJ) on Qh Jd 8s - an excellent result. The board has some straight draws (T9, 97) so I need to bet for value and protection. With 500 in the pot, I'll bet around 65-70% to extract value from worse queens, jacks, or draws.

Flop: Qh Jd 8s. I have A♣9♥ — no pair but a clean gutshot to a 10 (4 outs) and 3 Aces that improve to a likely winning top pair. Pot is 850 and Claude’s bet is 350, so calling 350 means I need ~350/(850+350) ≈ 29% equity to break even. Conservatively counting T + A as the primary clean outs gives ~27–29% chance to improve by the river (adding 9x improves this further), so this is borderline but reasonable. Claude opened from the SB and is c‑betting; his range will include many Q/J/T/Kx and bluffs, so I get reasonable implied/fold equity by calling and seeing the turn. I won’t raise — A9 is too weak OOP to turn into a credible bluff/value raise and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand late in the tourney. Plan: call 350, evaluate the turn (if I hit a T/A/9 I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll give up to heavy aggression).

I have top two pair (QJ) on Qh Jd 8s 8d. The 8 pairing is slightly concerning if Minni has an 8 for trips, but unlikely given preflop call and flop action. More likely they have a queen, jack, or straight draw. My hand is still very strong and I should continue betting for value. Pot is 1200, I'll bet around 65% pot to extract value from weaker queens, jacks, and draws while not overcommitting against trips.

Heads‑up vs Claude (SB). I called preflop and the flop, he barrels the turn (Qh Jd 8s 8d) for 800 into a 2000 pot. Folding here would be too nitty given the pot odds: calling 800 into 2000 requires ~28.6% equity. My rough outs are Aces (3) + Tens (4 straight outs) + nines (3) ≈ 10 raw outs (~21.7%), though some outs aren’t fully “clean” (e.g. an Ace vs AQ). Raising to 1600 would be a pure polarizing bluff that bloats the pot with a marginal hand and risks a bigger confrontation; I don’t have enough blockers or a reliable story to force folds from Claude’s value hands. With a big stack advantage and a small price to see the river, the correct line is to call, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the river: fold to heavy action if I miss, extract value (or raise) if I pair the A, make the straight, or otherwise improve.

I have top two pair (QJ) on Qh Jd 8s 8d 4h. The river 4h is a blank. Minni called both flop and turn bets, suggesting they have something - likely a queen, jack, or possibly an 8 for trips. My hand beats all Qx and Jx hands. I should value bet thin here - not too large since only better hands (8x for trips, or very unlikely 44) would call a big bet. A smaller value bet around 30-40% pot should extract value from weaker queens and jacks while minimizing losses if they have trips.

River decision: I have A♣9♥ on Qh Jd 8s 8d 4h — I missed. Claude’s line (SB open → flop c‑bet → turn barrel on a paired board → small river bet of 1000 into 3800) is heavily value‑oriented (lots of Qx/Jx, some two‑pair/trips/sets and occasional very strong pocket pairs). Calling 1000 into 3800 requires ~20.8% equity to break even, but with only Ace‑high I have no further outs and I only beat bluffs and a subset of worse Q/J hands — my real equity vs his continuing range is well below the break‑even threshold. Raising to 2000 would be a pure polarizing bluff (I lack the blockers and a credible story) and could open me to a shove; it’s unnecessary with my large stack and the tournament nearing the end. Action: preserve stack and avoid a low‑EV call. Fold.

4d7s on the button is a very weak hand even 3-handed. With 5 hands left and being the short stack (11,125 vs Minni's 28,525 and Pro's 20,200), I should be selective. This hand has poor playability and limited equity. Folding and waiting for a better spot.

6♣8♣ is a strong, playable hand heads-up — suited connectors with good flop equity and implied odds given very deep stacks. Button folded, so I'm up against only the big blind (Pro). I want to take the initiative rather than just completing and playing a small pot OOP, so open to a standard 2x raise. Size = 200 total is routine: it builds the pot with hands I can play postflop, denies good odds to some of BB's marginal calls, and keeps my range wide. If Pro 3-bets heavily I'll re-evaluate, otherwise I'll c-bet and play aggressively on favorable flops.

Minni has min-raised from the Small Blind to 200. As the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (calling 100 to win a pot of 300). I will also have position on Minni post-flop. While Q3o is a weak hand, the excellent price and positional advantage in a blind-vs-blind scenario justify a defense. My hand has decent equity against a wide stealing range, and the cost to see a flop is minimal relative to stack depths.

I opened to 200 preflop and Pro called, so I'm still the preflop aggressor. Flop A♠ K♣ 6♥ is somewhat hostile to my raising range because it contains both an Ace and a King, but I hold 6♣8♣ — middle pair with a weak kicker and a backdoor club draw. Heads‑up this is a good spot to continue taking the initiative: a half‑pot c-bet pressures pure air/overcards and weak floats, builds the pot when I'm ahead, and keeps the pot manageable when I'm behind. I'll bet ~1/2 pot (200). Plan: if Pro folds I take it down; if called I proceed cautiously on the turn (check or barrel small on safe cards), and if I face a sizable raise I will fold most of the time given the Ace on board and my vulnerable kicker.

The flop comes down As Kc 6h, which is an excellent board for the pre-flop raiser (Minni) as it hits a significant portion of their broadway and Ax range. I hold Q3o, giving me only Queen-high with no draws on this texture. Minni bets 200 into a pot of 400. Although the price is decent, my hand has very little equity and very poor playability on future streets against an opponent's range that connects well with this board. Floating here with just Queen-high and no backdoor potential is negative EV. The best play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I’m on the button with Q♠T♣ in a three‑handed pot — a very good spot to open. Q‑To has solid playability in position and provides useful blockers to some strong QQ/TT/KT combinations. With deep stacks (~288bb) and two opponents in the blinds who defend and open frequently, I want to take the initiative and build a pot in position while also getting fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) — large enough to apply pressure and consistent with the table’s open sizes. Plan: c‑bet versus a single caller and play aggressively in position; be cautious and likely fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet but continue versus small/medium 3‑bets depending on ranges and stack dynamics.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit (K4o), facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Minni). This is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Even though I'm getting decent pot odds, my equity realization will be low, and I risk being dominated or squeezed by the Big Blind. Given that it is hand 147/150 and I have a healthy stack, conserving chips by folding this marginal hand is the best strategy to maximize my final stack size.

8h6c is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a button raise from Minni, I'm getting 2.25:1 odds to call 200. While 86o has some straight potential, it's not suited and I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. With only 4 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 110BB, there's no need to defend with such a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 7c9s, an off-suit one-gapper. We are playing 3-handed with deep stacks (100BB+). While opening ranges on the button in 3-handed play are wide, 97o is at the very bottom or outside of a standard opening range. The Big Blind (Minni) has a massive chip lead and can defend widely or 3-bet effectively, making post-flop play difficult with a weak hand. Given that we are at Hand 148/150, preserving chips by avoiding marginal negative-EV spots is consistent with maximizing the final stack size. A fold is the standard and disciplined play here.

Hand 148/150, heads up vs BB after Pro folded. Q3o is marginal but decent enough for a steal attempt from SB in a heads-up situation. Raising to put pressure on Minni and potentially take down the blinds. If called, I have queen-high which can connect with some boards.

Pro folded — heads-up vs Claude (SB) who opened to 250. I’m in the big blind with J♥8♣: not a premium hand but perfectly defendable heads-up. Pot is 350 and it’s only 150 more to call (250 total). A min-3bet to 400 would be too small to fold him and a larger 3bet would bloat a pot OOP against an opponent who shows postflop aggression; folding is too tight. I’ll call to realize equity, keep my range wide, and evaluate on the flop — if I pair or get a strong draw I’ll apply pressure; if the board is hostile and he fires big I can fold.

Flopped middle pair with Qs on a Qh Ad 8d board. The ace is concerning since Minni defended BB and could easily have Ax. Two diamonds creates a flush draw. With second pair and weak kicker, checking for pot control is prudent - I can call a reasonable bet or bet the turn if checked through. Betting here risks getting raised by Ax or better queens.

Flop Qh Ad 8d — I have Jh8c: second pair (eights) with a fair kicker but two overcards and two diamonds on board make this a vulnerable hand OOP. Claude opened wide preflop and shows frequent post‑flop aggression, but he checked here which signals some weakness or pot control. I want to get value from worse Kx/Jx/Tx and fold out pure bluffs and unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable OOP, so I’ll probe with a half‑pot c-bet. A 250 bet prices out some equity for draws but isn’t so large I’ll be committed if he shows strong resistance — if he check‑raises big I can fold and preserve my stack; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have second pair (queens) with a weak 3 kicker on Qh Ad 8d. Minni's small 250 bet (half pot) after my check could be a weak ace, a draw, or even a bluff. The ace is concerning but folding second pair to a half-pot bet seems too weak. Raising accomplishes little - I'd only get action from better hands. Calling 250 into a 750 pot gives me good odds to see the turn with my made hand.

Minni checked the turn after betting the flop - this could indicate weakness or a trap. I have second pair with a weak kicker on a Qh Ad 8d Ts board. The turn brought a T which adds KJ straight possibilities. With the ace on board and potential draws, my hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value. Betting here only gets called by better hands. Checking behind for pot control makes sense - I can see a free river and make a decision based on Minni's action.

Board Qh Ad 8d Ts; I hold Jh8c — second pair (eights) with a fair kicker. Pot is 1,000 and Claude opened preflop then check‑called my 250 flop probe and now checks the turn. His line looks like pot control or a medium‑strength holding (Ax, Qx, maybe some Kx or pocket pairs) rather than a wide missed draw. The Ten on the turn creates the only real straight scare (9+J combos), but I hold a J which removes some of those combos. Betting here risks bloating the pot OOP against many hands that dominate me (A/Q) or will call/raise, while a small bet likely won’t extract much extra value. With a marginal but decent showdown hand I prefer pot control — check the turn, see how he reacts on the river, and call reasonable river bets as a bluff catcher or fold to large aggression. Action: check and re‑evaluate on the river.

River 5c is a blank. I have second pair (queens) with a weak 3 kicker. Minni bet small on flop then checked turn, suggesting weakness or a missed draw. My hand has showdown value but betting accomplishes little - I'd only get called by better hands (aces, better queens, straights). Checking to showdown is optimal here.

Check. I have Jh8c — second pair (eights) with a weak kicker on Qh Ad 8d Ts 5c. Claude’s line (open preflop, check‑call my 250 flop probe, then check the turn and river) looks like pot control with medium strength hands (Ax, Qx) or missed draws. Betting here OOP bloats the pot and will often get called by hands that beat me (A/Q) while folding out many worse hands; a small thin bet won’t reliably extract value. With a ~1,000 pot and stacks deep relative to the pot, pot control is the +EV line: check to showdown and call small river bets as a bluff‑catcher, folding only to large river jams. Action: check (0).
4c6h is a very weak hand. Even on the button, this is a clear fold. With only 2 hands left and being in 3rd place, I shouldn't waste chips on such a marginal holding against two opponents.

Call 50 more (total commit 100). K6s is a marginal but playable hand heads‑up — suited King with decent equity and backdoor flush potential — and the price is cheap: the pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to see a flop (~4:1 immediate pot odds). With very deep stacks and being out of position post‑flop, I don’t want to enlarge the pot with a marginal hand by raising to 200; calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves flexibility. Plan: see the flop and proceed aggressively if I hit a King or strong draws, otherwise control the pot OOP and fold to heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah 2c. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped. With only 2 hands left in the tournament and facing the chip leader, there is no need to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand like A2o. Checking allows me to see a free flop and realize my equity. Raising would isolate me against a calling range that likely dominates me or has good equity, while checking keeps the variance low.

Flop: Kh Kc 8d and I hold Ks6s — I’ve flopped trips. Claude folded pre‑flop so this is heads‑up vs Pro; only a few very thin combos (pocket 88 or K+8 hands) currently beat me. My preflop plan was to proceed aggressively if I hit a King; this is that spot. I want to extract value from broad calling hands (pocket pairs, Kd combos, weak Kx, Ax, etc.) while keeping the price low enough to induce calls rather than folding out medium-strength hands. Bet half‑pot (100) is the right sizing: builds the pot, charges the few hands that beat me, and keeps future decisions manageable if I face heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high (Ah 2c) on a paired board of Kh Kc 8d. The opponent (Minni) in the Small Blind limped pre-flop and has now led out for a minimum bet of 100 into a pot of 200. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. Given the paired board, it is difficult for the opponent to have connected heavily unless they have a King or an Eight. A min-bet often signifies a weak made hand, a draw, or a blocker bet with air. My Ace-high has decent showdown value here and is ahead of many of her bluffs or semi-bluffs (like QJ, T9, flush draws if any, though rainbow board makes flush draws impossible unless backdoor). Raising turns my hand into a bluff and isolates me against better hands. Folding is too weak given the price. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn and potentially get to showdown cheaply.

I currently hold Ks6s on Kh Kc 8d 9h — I flopped trips and got one caller on the flop, so my opponent’s continuing range is likely medium pocket pairs (QQ–TT), some K combos (very few since only one king remains), maybe A‑x and occasional speculative hands. The 9 on the turn does introduce a small number of new hands that now beat me (pocket 9s) in addition to pocket 8s and the lone remaining king, but those combos are rare. I want to extract value from the many worse hands that will still call (QQ–TT, worse Kx if present, some A‑x) while keeping the pot controlled against the small set of crushing full‑house/quads combos. A modest half‑pot turn bet (~200 into 400) achieves that: it builds value, keeps weaker calls in, and avoids bloating the pot where a check‑raise/full‑house trap could cost a lot. If I face a large raise I’ll put the opponent on a very narrow full‑house/quads range and re‑evaluate, but the default line is a value bet now.

The turn card is the Nine of Hearts (9h), making the board Kh Kc 8d 9h. This card connects well with a Small Blind limping range (containing hands like T9, J9, Q9, 98, or straight draws like QJ/JT). Minni bets 200 into 400. While the price is attractive (3:1), my Ace-high (Ah 2c) likely trails her value range, which includes any 8, any 9, or a King. The likelihood of her double-barreling pure air on this texture is lower than the likelihood of her holding a piece of the board. With only one hand left after this in the tournament and my stack safely in second place, there is no incentive to make a loose hero call for a small pot. I fold to preserve my stack.

Short-handed on the button with deep stacks, open-stealing is the most +EV option. 8♠5♥ is a weak hand but perfectly reasonable to open in 3‑handed play — I have position on both blinds and a lot of fold equity. I’ll make a standard button open to 250 (≈2.5x BB) to balance pressure and pot control. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold this marginal holding; if called I’ll use position to c-bet dry flops and pot-control on dangerous boards. Overall this maximizes our chance to pick up the blinds or play a postflop pot in position with initiative.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I hold King-Six offsuit in the Small Blind. Minni, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 250. In Hand #147, I faced a similar situation with King-Four offsuit against a Minni raise and folded. K6o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, often being dominated by better Kings or Aces in the opener's range. Although the price to call is relatively cheap (200 more into a pot of 400), the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a negative expected value call. Maximizing my stack size at this stage involves avoiding bleeding chips in marginal spots. Therefore, I will fold.

Final hand of the tournament. I have AJs - a premium hand in the big blind facing a button min-raise from Minni. This is a clear 3-bet spot for value. AJs plays well as a 3-bet against a wide button opening range. Standard 3-bet sizing to around 800.

I opened to 250 as planned, but Claude’s 3‑bet to 800 is a large, standard 3‑bet size here and represents a fairly strong/polarized range in 3‑handed play. 8♠5♥ is a weak, poorly‑dominated hand with very limited postflop playability against a 3‑bet — it has poor equity vs Ax/Kx/broadway-heavy 3‑bets and few backdoors to realize. Calling 550 into a 1,100 pot requires ~33% equity (550/(1100+550)=33%), which 8‑5o doesn’t have vs a reasonable 3‑bet range, and 4‑bet bluffing with this holding is overly risky given stacks and opponent tendencies. Preserve the deep stack for better spots — fold and move on.
